topics.title: The ROS Graph
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The ROS Graph

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **ROS Graph** (also called the **Computation Graph**) is the network of all ROS 2 programs (called *Nodes*) that are running at the same time, together with the *Topics* and *Services* that connect them, the *Parameters* they share, and the *Messages* flowing across the graph. In formal KTU 2024 syllabus terms:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The ROS Graph is a peer-to-peer distributed process graph in which independent executable units (Nodes) exchange data through strongly-typed publish/subscribe Topics, request/reply Services, and feedback-based Actions, coordinated by a discovery layer (DDS / ROS Master in ROS 1) and a centralized Parameter Server.**

It represents the **logical runtime topology** of a robotic system — what is communicating with what, on which channel, and at what rate. The graph is **dynamic**: nodes can join or leave at any moment, and the underlying middleware automatically re-routes the communication paths.

## 1.2 Primary Constituents of the ROS Graph

A complete ROS Graph is composed of the following fundamental building blocks:

| # | Building Block | One-Line Purpose |
|---|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Nodes** | Executable processes that perform computation. |
| 2 | **Topics** | Named buses over which nodes exchange *streams* of messages asynchronously. |
| 3 | **Services** | Synchronous request/reply RPC calls for one-shot interactions. |
| 4 | **Messages** | Strongly-typed data structures that travel over topics/services. |
| 5 | **Parameters** | Configuration values shared and managed across nodes. |
| 6 | **Actions** | Long-running, feedback-rich tasks built on top of topics + services. |
| 7 | **Parameter Server** | Centralized key-value store for global configuration (dominant in ROS 1; distributed in ROS 2). |
| 8 | **Discovery / DDS Layer** | Underlying middleware that performs node discovery and message transport. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The ROS Graph as a Multilingual Office Building**
>
> Imagine a giant office building with hundreds of independent workers, each sitting in their own cubicle. No worker can leave their cubicle, but every cubicle has:
> - A **letterbox** (a *Topic*) labeled with a specific subject (e.g., `/camera/image`), and any worker may post letters into it, while any other worker may subscribe to read them.
> - A **phone line** (a *Service*) that accepts a strict request and returns a strict reply — a one-shot conversation.
> - A **shared notice board** (the *Parameter Server*) holding configuration notices.
> - A **head office directory** (the *Discovery layer / DDS*) that helps workers find each other automatically.
>
> Each worker is a **Node**, each letter is a **Message**, and the entire building's social network at any moment is the **ROS Graph**. If a new worker moves in, the directory updates automatically — no wiring needed.

## 1.4 Why the Graph Abstraction Matters in Robotics

Traditional monolithic robot software fails because it cannot be decomposed, scaled, or reused. The ROS Graph enforces a **microservice-style architecture** for robots, providing:
- **Modularity** — replace a perception node without touching motion planning.
- **Language interoperability** — a Python node can seamlessly talk to a C++ node.
- **Heterogeneous hardware integration** — sensors, actuators, and AI models can all sit on the same graph.
- **Distributed execution** — nodes can run on different machines over a LAN.
- **Fault isolation** — a crash in one node does not cascade to others.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Mnemonic — "NTM-SPA":** To remember the components of the ROS Graph, recall **N**odes, **T**opics, **M**essages, **S**ervices, **P**arameters, **A**ctions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Node — The Atom of the ROS Graph

A **Node** is a single-purpose executable process written in a supported language (C++, Python, etc.) that uses ROS client libraries (`rclcpp`, `rclpy`) to participate in the graph.

**Properties of a Node:**
- Performs **one logical function** (e.g., publishing wheel odometry, controlling a motor, running a SLAM algorithm).
- Identified by a **unique graph-level name** within its namespace.
- Contains a set of **Publishers**, **Subscribers**, **Service Servers**, **Service Clients**, **Action Servers**, and **Action Clients**.
- Lifecycle in ROS 2 follows a managed state machine: `unconfigured → configure() → inactive → activate() → active → deactivate() → cleanup() → shutdown()`.

**Why single-purpose?** Because when a node crashes, only that capability is lost — the rest of the robot keeps functioning. This is the *micro-recovery* principle.

## 2.2 Topics — Asynchronous Many-to-Many Streaming

A **Topic** is a named channel identified by a string (e.g., `/cmd_vel`). It is a **strongly-typed bus** carrying messages of one fixed type. Communication is:
- **Asynchronous** — publisher does not wait.
- **Anonymous** — publishers and subscribers do not know each other's identity.
- **Many-to-many** — N publishers can write, M subscribers can read.
- **One-way** — no reply is expected.

### 2.2.1 Quality of Service (QoS) — The ROS 2 Innovation

Unlike ROS 1, ROS 2 topics expose **Quality of Service** policies that govern delivery guarantees. The most important policies are:

| QoS Policy | Symbol | Effect on Graph |
|------------|:------:|-----------------|
| Reliability | RELIABLE / BEST_EFFORT | Guaranteed vs. droppable delivery |
| Durability | TRANSIENT_LOCAL / VOLATILE | Late-joining subscribers get last message or not |
| History | KEEP_LAST(n) / KEEP_ALL | How many messages to buffer |
| Deadline | duration | Max allowed inter-message gap |
| Lifespan | duration | How long a message stays valid |
| Liveliness | AUTOMATIC / MANUAL | How to detect dead publishers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Always match the QoS profile of a publisher and subscriber, otherwise the DDS layer silently filters the connection and students often lose marks by not mentioning this.

## 2.3 Services — Synchronous Request/Reply

A **Service** is a named RPC endpoint defined by a pair of message types: `request` and `response`. It implements:
- **One-to-one** synchronous communication.
- **One-shot transactions** (e.g., "trigger a calibration", "compute inverse kinematics once").

The pair `(name, type)` uniquely identifies a service, where the type is the concatenation of `pkg/srv/<name>.srv`.

## 2.4 Messages — The Typed Data Contracts

A **Message** is the data payload that flows over a topic or a service. It is a `.msg` (for topics) or `.srv` (for services) file composed of primitive fields (`int32`, `float64`, `string`, `bool`) and nested sub-messages. At runtime, messages are serialized using **CDR (Common Data Representation)** for DDS.

## 2.5 Parameters — The Configuration Layer

A **Parameter** is a key-value pair (where the value is typed — `int`, `double`, `string`, `bool`, arrays, or byte arrays) used to configure node behavior without recompiling. In ROS 1, parameters live in a global Parameter Server; in ROS 2, parameters are owned per-node with optional global visibility.

## 2.6 Actions — Long-Running Goal-Oriented Tasks

An **Action** is built on the **publish/subscribe + service** hybrid pattern and is used for tasks that take a long time and require:
- A **Goal** (sent by client).
- Continuous **Feedback** (streamed back).
- A final **Result** (sent at completion).
- Optional **Cancel** capability.

## 2.7 The Discovery / DDS Layer (ROS 2) vs. ROS Master (ROS 1)

| Feature | ROS 1 (Master-based) | ROS 2 (DDS-based) |
|---------|---------------------|--------------------|
| Discovery | Centralized ROS Master + XML-RPC | Distributed via DDS-RTPS multicast |
| Single point of failure | **Yes** (Master down ⇒ graph down) | **No** (peer-to-peer) |
| Real-time support | Limited | Native (with RTPS profiles) |
| QoS control | None | Full policy set |
| Multi-robot | Possible but awkward | Native and scalable |

## 2.8 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical / Symbolic Form | Units / Notes |
|---------|------------------------------|---------------|
| Node name uniqueness constraint | $\exists\, n_i,\, n_j \in V \mid i \neq j \;\Rightarrow\; \text{name}(n_i) \neq \text{name}(n_j)$ | Within the same namespace |
| Topic data rate | $R_t = \dfrac{N_m \cdot S_m}{T_{window}}$ [bytes/s] | $N_m$ messages, $S_m$ bytes each |
| End-to-end latency bound (QoS) | $L_{e2e} \leq D_{deadline}$ | Must satisfy publisher & subscriber `deadline` |
| Pub/Sub fan-in / fan-out | $\deg^{+}(t) = \mid P_t \mid,\ \deg^{-}(t) = \mid S_t \mid$ | Number of publishers / subscribers on topic $t$ |
| Service cardinality | $\mid C_s \mid = \mid \text{clients} \mid,\ \mid S_s \mid = 1$ | Exactly one server (graph invariant) |
| Graph connectivity check | $\text{rank}(A) = N-1$ | $A$ = adjacency matrix of nodes $N$ |
| ROS domain ID isolation (ROS 2) | $0 \leq \text{ROS\_DOMAIN\_ID} \leq 232$ | Nodes on different IDs form disjoint graphs |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough of ROS Graph Mechanics

> Since the ROS Graph is a discrete-event distributed system rather than a continuous physical law, this section performs a **rigorous step-by-step analytical derivation of the communication flow and the graph invariants** that examiners love to test.

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Publisher-to-Subscriber Data Path

We trace one message "Hello" from a publisher node $P$ to a subscriber node $S$ on topic $T$ under QoS profile $Q$.

**Step 1 — Node Initialization**

The node process is launched with the `ros2 run` or `ros2 launch` CLI.

$$
\text{Initialize}(P) \rightarrow \text{CreateContext}() \rightarrow \text{CreateNode}("talker")
$$

The RMW (ROS Middleware) layer establishes a DDS DomainParticipant in the multicast group corresponding to `ROS_DOMAIN_ID`.

**Step 2 — Publisher Construction**

A publisher handle is created with a fixed message type $\tau$ and QoS profile $Q_P$:

$$
P_{handle} = P.\text{create\_publisher}\langle \tau, T, Q_P \rangle
$$

The DDS middleware advertises the publisher's *Writer* endpoint over multicast to all peers in the discovery range.

**Step 3 — Subscriber Construction**

A subscriber node $S$ registers a subscription:

$$
S_{handle} = S.\text{create\_subscription}\langle \tau, T, Q_S \rangle
$$

The DDS middleware advertises the *Reader* endpoint similarly.

**Step 4 — Discovery & Matching (The "Handshake")**

ROS 2 / DDS performs QoS *compatibility matching*. The connection is **established** if and only if:

$$
Q_P.\text{RELIABILITY} \geq Q_S.\text{RELIABILITY}
$$

$$
Q_P.\text{DURABILITY} \geq Q_S.\text{DURABILITY}
$$

That is, the **publisher's guarantees must meet or exceed** the subscriber's minimum requirements. Otherwise:

$$
\text{Connection} = \emptyset \;\;(\text{no data delivered})
$$

**Step 5 — Message Serialization**

The C++/Python message object is converted into CDR-encoded bytes:

$$
B = \text{Serialize}_{CDR}(m,\ \tau) \quad \text{where} \quad m \in \mathcal{M}_\tau
$$

For a message with $k$ fields of sizes $s_1, s_2, \dots, s_k$, the encoded size is:

$$
\mid B \mid = 4 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{align4}(s_i) \;\; \text{bytes}
$$

The 4-byte prefix holds the encapsulation header of CDR.

**Step 6 — Transport**

The serialized buffer is handed to the DDS transport (default UDP multicast, optionally TCP or shared memory intra-host):

$$
T_{xmit} = \frac{\mid B \mid + H_{DDS}}{B_{link}}
$$

where $H_{DDS}$ is the RTPS submessage header (typically 12–20 bytes), and $B_{link}$ is the link bandwidth.

**Step 7 — Reception & Deserialization**

The subscriber's reader thread receives the buffer, runs CDR deserialization, and invokes the user callback:

$$
S.\text{callback}(m) = \text{Deserialize}_{CDR}(B, \tau)
$$

**Step 8 — Latency Computation**

End-to-end latency from publish to callback is:

$$
L_{e2e} = t_{serialize} + t_{queue} + t_{transmit} + t_{deserialize}
$$

For QoS compliance:

$$
L_{e2e} \leq \min(Q_P.\text{deadline}, Q_S.\text{deadline})
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Service Request/Reply Flow

For a synchronous service between client $C$ and server $S_r$ on service name $\Sigma$:

**Step 1** — Server registers: $S_r.\text{create\_service}\langle \tau_{req}, \tau_{res}, \Sigma, h_{srv} \rangle$

**Step 2** — Client issues: $C.\text{call\_async}(\Sigma, m_{req}) \rightarrow \text{Future}$

**Step 3** — The request crosses the graph and server handler executes:

$$
m_{res} = h_{srv}(m_{req})
$$

**Step 4** — Response is returned, future resolved on the client.

The total wall-clock time is:

$$
L_{svc} = 2 \cdot T_{xmit} + T_{handler}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why services are NOT used for sensor data:** They would require the publisher to block, destroying parallelism. Topics (async) are used for streams; services (sync) for one-shot RPCs.

## 3.3 Graph Invariants Examiners Test

| Invariant | Formal Statement |
|-----------|------------------|
| **I-1 (Name Uniqueness)** | No two nodes share the same fully qualified name in one graph. |
| **I-2 (Type Compatibility)** | Every connection on a topic uses the same message type $\tau$. |
| **I-3 (Service Singularity)** | Each named service has exactly one server, but may have many clients. |
| **I-4 (QoS Compatibility)** | A connection is formed only if publisher QoS $\geq$ subscriber QoS. |
| **I-5 (Domain Isolation)** | Nodes in domain $d_1$ cannot communicate with nodes in domain $d_2 \neq d_1$. |

These invariants are derivable by induction over the graph's lifecycle.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Computing a Topic's Bandwidth Requirement

A stereo camera publishes 30 fps, each frame as 2 × `sensor_msgs/Image` of $640 \times 480$ RGB8. Compute the required topic bandwidth.

**Given:**
- Resolution: $W = 640,\ H = 480$
- Channels: $C = 3$ (RGB8 = 1 byte per channel)
- Frame rate: $f = 30$ Hz
- Stereo: 2 images per "frame set"

**Step 1 — Bytes per image:**

$$
S_{img} = W \cdot H \cdot C = 640 \times 480 \times 3 = 921{,}600 \;\text{bytes} = 900 \;\text{KiB}
$$

**Step 2 — Bytes per stereo frame set:**

$$
S_{stereo} = 2 \cdot S_{img} = 1{,}843{,}200 \;\text{bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Per second (30 fps):**

$$
R_t = 30 \times 1{,}843{,}200 = 55{,}296{,}000 \;\text{bytes/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Mbps (×8 / 1,000,000):**

$$
R_t \approx 442.4 \;\text{Mbps}
$$

**Conclusion:** A single 30 fps stereo RGB topic on a 1 Gbps LAN consumes nearly **44 % of link capacity**, justifying use of compression topics and `BEST_EFFORT` QoS with `KEEP_LAST(1)`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level ROS Graph Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram depicts a typical mobile-robot ROS Graph with all six primary elements and their interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph camNode["Camera Driver Node"]
        camPub["Publisher\n/sensor_msgs/Image"]
    end
    subgraph lidNode["LiDAR Driver Node"]
        lidPub["Publisher\n/sensor_msgs/PointCloud2"]
    end
    subgraph odomNode["Odometry Node"]
        odomPub["Publisher\n/nav_msgs/Odometry"]
    end
    subgraph slamNode["SLAM Node"]
        slamSub1["Subscriber\n/scan"]
        slamSub2["Subscriber\n/odom"]
        slamPub["Publisher\n/nav_msgs/Map"]
    end
    subgraph navNode["Navigation Stack Node"]
        navSub["Subscriber\n/map"]
        navSvc["Service Client\n/nav_msgs/GetPlan"]
        navAct["Action Client\n/nav_msgs/NavigateToPose"]
    end
    subgraph ctrlNode["Motion Controller Node"]
        ctrlActSrv["Action Server\n/FollowJointTrajectory"]
        ctrlPub["Publisher\n/cmd_vel"]
    end
    subgraph paramSrv["Parameter Server\nGlobal Config"]
        p1["robot_radius = 0.30 m"]
        p2["max_velocity = 1.50 m/s"]
    end

    lidPub -- "/scan" --> slamSub1
    odomPub -- "/odom" --> slamSub2
    camPub -- "/camera/image_raw" --> slamNode
    slamPub -- "/map" --> navSub
    navSvc -- "GetPlan" --> slamNode
    navAct -- "NavigateToPose" --> ctrlActSrv
    ctrlPub -- "/cmd_vel" --> ctrlNode
    paramSrv -. "shared config" .-> slamNode
    paramSrv -. "shared config" .-> navNode
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid arrows = Topics (async, many-to-many streams).
- Dashed arrows = Services or Actions (request/reply or goal-oriented).
- Dotted arrows from `Parameter Server` = configuration injection.
- Each box is one **Node**; the graph is the union of all nodes and edges at runtime.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

For clarity, the same system can be re-rendered as a block-flow showing the *data plane* and *control plane* separation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph dataPlane["Data Plane  Async Streaming"]
        dp1["Sensor Drivers\nCameras, LiDAR, IMU"]
        dp2["Perception Nodes\nDetection, Segmentation"]
        dp3["Localization Nodes\nAMCL, EKF"]
        dp4["Planning Nodes\nGlobal, Local"]
        dp5["Control Nodes\nPID, MPC"]
    end
    subgraph controlPlane["Control Plane  Sync / Goal"]
        cp1["Service Servers\nCalibration, IK"]
        cp2["Action Servers\nNavigate, Follow"]
    end
    subgraph configPlane["Configuration Plane"]
        cf1["Parameter Server\nYAML / CLI overrides"]
    end

    dp1 -- "/sensors/*" --> dp2
    dp2 -- "/detections" --> dp3
    dp3 -- "/odom, /pose" --> dp4
    dp4 -- "/path" --> dp5
    cf1 -. "/**" .-> dp2
    cf1 -. "/**" .-> dp3
    cf1 -. "/**" .-> dp4
    cp2 -- "goal" --> dp4
    dp4 -- "feedback" --> cp2
    cp1 -- "one-shot" --> dp5
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick:** In a diagram, label the *direction* of every arrow, the *topic name*, and the *message type*. This single habit typically adds 2–3 marks in KTU 2024 scheme evaluations.

## 4.3 Topic/Service/Action Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Need to exchange data between nodes?"] --> q1{"Continuous stream?"}
    q1 -- Yes --> topicUse["Use TOPIC\nasync, many-to-many"]
    q1 -- No --> q2{"Need a reply?"}
    q2 -- No --> topicUse
    q2 -- Yes --> q3{"Long-running with feedback?"}
    q3 -- Yes --> actionUse["Use ACTION\ngoal + feedback + result"]
    q3 -- No --> serviceUse["Use SERVICE\none-shot request/reply"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define the ROS Graph. List its main components.**

**Model Answer:**

The **ROS Graph** is the network of ROS 2 processes (nodes) and the communication channels (topics, services, actions) that connect them, plus shared parameters, all running concurrently and exchanging strongly-typed data.

Main components:
1. **Nodes** — single-purpose executables.
2. **Topics** — asynchronous, many-to-many named buses.
3. **Services** — synchronous request/reply RPCs.
4. **Actions** — long-running goal/feedback/result tasks.
5. **Messages** — strongly-typed data structures.
6. **Parameters** — typed configuration values.

*(3 marks — definition 1 mark, components 2 marks.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]`
**Differentiate between a ROS Topic and a ROS Service in two points each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Topic | Service |
|--------|-------|---------|
| Communication pattern | Asynchronous, one-way | Synchronous, request/reply |
| Cardinality | Many publishers ↔ many subscribers | One server ↔ many clients |
| Use case | Sensor streams, continuous commands | One-shot computations, calibration |
| Blocking | Publisher non-blocking | Client blocks until response |

*(2 points × 1.5 marks each = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE rules, the student answers **one out of two** in Part B. We provide both alternatives below for full practice coverage.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the ROS Graph. List all its elements and describe the role of the master/parameter server. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The ROS Graph architecture consists of three logical planes:

1. **Data plane** — Topics carry streaming messages between perception, localization, planning and control nodes.
2. **Control plane** — Services and Actions handle synchronous RPCs and long-running goal-oriented tasks.
3. **Configuration plane** — The Parameter Server / YAML files expose typed key-value configuration to all nodes.

**Elements:**
- **Nodes** — modular processes identified uniquely.
- **Topics** — typed, named async channels.
- **Services** — typed sync request/reply.
- **Actions** — long-running goal/feedback/result.
- **Messages** — `.msg` / `.srv` / `.action` contracts.
- **Parameters** — typed configuration.
- **Discovery/Master** — keeps the registry of names and types.

**Role of the Master / Parameter Server (ROS 1):**
- The **ROS Master** acts as a **name server** — it stores topic/service registrations and helps nodes find each other.
- The **Parameter Server** runs inside the Master and stores global configuration in a multi-robot-scoped key-value tree.
- Without the Master, ROS 1 nodes cannot discover each other (single point of failure).
- In ROS 2, the Master is **replaced by DDS-RTPS discovery**, making the graph decentralized and fault-tolerant.

*(Block diagram 3 marks, elements 2 marks, master/parameter server 2 marks = 7 marks.)*

**(b) Explain the publish/subscribe mechanism in ROS with a suitable example. Show how a topic is created, a publisher and subscriber are written (in pseudocode or actual code), and how data flows. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The publish/subscribe mechanism is **decoupled and asynchronous**:

- A node creates a **publisher** on a named topic with a fixed message type.
- A (possibly different) node creates a **subscriber** on the same topic and type.
- The middleware (DDS in ROS 2, Master+XMLRPC in ROS 1) discovers both endpoints, matches QoS, and transports serialized messages.

**Conceptual flow (turtle_teleop example):**

```
Keyboard Node ──/turtle1/cmd_vel──▶ turtlesim Node
              (geometry_msgs/Twist)        (moves turtle)
```

**Pseudocode (Python / ROS 2):**

```python
# talker.py — Publisher
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from geometry_msgs.msg import Twist

class Talker(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('talker')
        self.pub = self.create_publisher(Twist, '/turtle1/cmd_vel', 10)
        self.timer = self.create_timer(0.1, self.send_cmd)

    def send_cmd(self):
        msg = Twist()
        msg.linear.x = 2.0
        self.pub.publish(msg)

rclpy.init()
rclpy.spin(Talker())
rclpy.shutdown()
```

```python
# listener.py — Subscriber
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from geometry_msgs.msg import Twist

class Listener(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('listener')
        self.sub = self.create_subscription(
            Twist, '/turtle1/cmd_vel', self.cb, 10)

    def cb(self, msg):
        self.get_logger().info(f'vx = {msg.linear.x}')

rclpy.init()
rclpy.spin(Listener())
rclpy.shutdown()
```

**Data flow steps:**
1. `rclpy.init()` — initialize client library & DDS.
2. `create_publisher()` — registers a *Writer* in DDS discovery.
3. `create_subscription()` — registers a *Reader*.
4. DDS performs handshake, QoS check, and creates a transport association.
5. `publish(msg)` serializes to CDR and sends via UDP multicast (or shared memory).
6. Receiver thread deserializes and calls `cb(msg)`.

*(Concept 2 marks, code 3 marks, flow 2 marks = 7 marks.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**(a) Differentiate between ROS 1 and ROS 2 graph architectures. Explain the role of DDS and QoS in ROS 2 with examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | ROS 1 Graph | ROS 2 Graph |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| Discovery | Centralized Master (XMLRPC) | Distributed via DDS-RTPS |
| Single point of failure | **Yes** | **No** |
| Real-time support | Poor | Native (Connext DDS, Cyclone DDS) |
| QoS | None | Full QoS policy set |
| Multi-robot | Possible, manual | Native via `ROS_DOMAIN_ID` |
| Security | None | SROS 2 / DDS-Security |
| Communication middleware | Custom TCP/UDP | Standardized OMG DDS |

**Role of DDS:**
- **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** is an OMG standard for real-time, decentralized publish/subscribe.
- Each node is a *DomainParticipant*; topics are *Topics*; pubs/subs are *Writers*/*Readers*.
- DDS provides **discovery, transport, serialization, and QoS** uniformly.
- Implementations: Fast DDS (default), Cyclone DDS, RTI Connext DDS.

**Role of QoS — Examples:**
- **Sensor streams (e.g., camera):** `BEST_EFFORT + KEEP_LAST(1) + VOLATILE` → drop stale frames, low latency.
- **Critical commands (e.g., `/cmd_vel`):** `RELIABLE + KEEP_LAST(10) + VOLATILE` → guarantee delivery.
- **Maps (slow-changing):** `RELIABLE + TRANSIENT_LOCAL + KEEP_LAST(1)` → late joiners receive the latest map.

*Rule of thumb:* Publisher must **exceed or meet** subscriber's QoS demands; otherwise the connection is silently dropped.

*(Comparison 3 marks, DDS 2 marks, QoS examples 2 marks = 7 marks.)*

**(b) What are Actions in ROS? Explain the goal–feedback–result pattern with a navigation example. Also write the file structure of an action definition file. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Actions** are the third communication pattern in the ROS Graph, designed for **long-running, preemptable, feedback-rich tasks** that go beyond a simple request/reply. They combine:
- A **service-like Goal** (one-shot request to start).
- A **topic-like Feedback** (continuous progress stream).
- A **service-like Result** (one-shot final reply).
- A **topic-like Cancel** (preemption channel).

**Navigation example:**

```
Navigator ──[Action Goal: NavigateToPose]──▶ Nav2 Stack
       ◀──[Feedback: distance_remaining]──
       ◀──[Feedback: estimated_time_remaining]──
       ◀──[Result: success=True, total_time=42.0s]──
       ──[Cancel: abort=True]──▶ (optional)
```

**File structure of an action definition (`nav2_msgs/action/NavigateToPose.action`):**

```
# Goal
geometry_msgs/PoseStamped target_pose
string behavior_tree

---
# Result
std_msgs/Empty result
int32 error_code
float32 total_elapsed_time

---
# Feedback
float32 distance_remaining
float32 estimated_time_remaining
int16 number_of_recoveries
```

The three sections are separated by `---`. At build time, `rosidl` generates `Goal`, `Result`, `Feedback`, and a `*_Action` message class.

*(Action definition 2 marks, navigation example 3 marks, file structure 2 marks = 7 marks.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks on ROS Graph Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing Topics and Services** — Writing `service = node.create_publisher(...)` is an instant zero. Always state *what pattern* suits the problem *before* coding.
> 2. **Ignoring QoS** — In ROS 2 answers, failing to mention Reliability/Durability when explaining why a topic "doesn't work" costs 2 marks. The correct line is: *"The connection failed because the publisher's `BEST_EFFORT` did not meet the subscriber's `RELIABLE` requirement."*
> 3. **Forgetting namespacing** — A node's fully qualified name is `/<ns>/<name>`; collisions across namespaces are allowed but not within the same.
> 4. **Mixing up Message, Topic, and Type** — A *Message* is the `.msg` data structure; a *Topic* is the bus; the *Type* is the message class. Examiners explicitly test this trichotomy.
> 5. **Skipping the block diagram** — In 7-mark sub-questions, a missing diagram forfeits 2–3 marks. Always include a labeled Mermaid or hand-drawn graph.
> 6. **Not specifying ROS 1 vs ROS 2** — Especially in comparison questions; the KTU 2024 syllabus lists both, so explicitly state the version.
> 7. **Action file structure** — Students often forget the three `---` separators (goal/result/feedback); this is a free 1-mark trap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "The ROS Graph"**

- ☐ The **ROS Graph** = all running nodes + their communication edges (topics, services, actions) + parameters + discovery.
- ☐ **Node** = single-purpose executable, identified by a unique fully-qualified name; has pubs, subs, srvs, actions, params.
- ☐ **Topic** = async, many-to-many, **strongly-typed**; carries `Message`s of one fixed type; no reply.
- ☐ **Service** = sync, request/reply, one server ↔ many clients; for one-shot computations.
- ☐ **Action** = long-running, goal + feedback + result + cancel; used for navigation, manipulation.
- ☐ **Message** = `.msg` file; built-in types include `geometry_msgs/Twist`, `sensor_msgs/Image`, `std_msgs/String`.
- ☐ **Parameter** = typed key-value, set via launch files, YAML, or CLI; supports `int`, `double`, `string`, `bool`, arrays, `byte[]`.
- ☐ **Master (ROS 1)** = name server + parameter server, single point of failure.
- ☐ **DDS (ROS 2)** = decentralized OMG-standard discovery; eliminates master; supports real-time.
- ☐ **QoS** = publisher must satisfy subscriber's profile; common profiles: `BEST_EFFORT+KEEP_LAST(1)` for sensors, `RELIABLE+TRANSIENT_LOCAL+KEEP_LAST(1)` for maps.
- ☐ **Domain isolation** = `ROS_DOMAIN_ID ∈ [0, 232]` partitions one LAN into disjoint graphs.
- ☐ CLI tools to introspect the graph: `ros2 node list`, `ros2 topic list`, `ros2 topic info`, `ros2 service list`, `ros2 action list`, `ros2 param list`, `rqt_graph`.
- ☐ Diagram etiquette: label **every arrow with the topic name AND message type**.
- ☐ KTU 2024 ESE pattern: 3-mark Part A = definitions/comparisons; 14-mark Part B = `(a)` architecture/diagram (7m) + `(b)` mechanism + code (7m).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: roscore
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** roscore

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**`roscore`** is a collection of nodes and programs that form the pre-requisite core system of any ROS (Robot Operating System) based application. Technically, `roscore` is a launch script (typically located at `/opt/ros/<distro>/bin/roscore`) that bootstraps and launches three critical background processes simultaneously:

1. **ROS Master** — A central registration and lookup service.
2. **Parameter Server** — A shared, distributed, multi-variables dictionary.
3. **`rosout` (ROS Logging Node)** — A standardized stdout/stderr relay for all nodes.

The command `roscore` initializes the master URI and binds it to the default XML-RPC port **11311** on the host machine. Until `roscore` is executed and running, no other ROS node can discover peers, register topics, or establish peer-to-peer communication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** `roscore` does **NOT** handle actual data transport between nodes (that is done via TCPROS). It only provides **name resolution, registration, and metadata lookup**, analogous to DNS in networking.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Post Office Switchboard Operator

Imagine a giant automated office building (your robot or computer) where hundreds of employees (ROS Nodes) work in isolated rooms. They do not know each other's names, locations, or phone numbers. Without `roscore`:

- Node A publishing temperature data has no way of finding Node B that wants to subscribe to it.
- A request to "move the robot arm" would float in limbo with no listener.

`roscore` acts like a **central switchboard operator (Telephone Exchange)**:

- **ROS Master** is the operator. When a node says *"Hello, I am publishing topic /temperature"*, the operator registers this fact.
- **Parameter Server** is the building's shared notice board — anyone can pin a note (parameter) and anyone can read it.
- **`rosout`** is the office's announcement system — every shout ("ERROR", "WARN", "INFO") gets logged centrally.

When Node B asks, *"Who is publishing /temperature?"*, the operator connects the call. After the handshake, Node A and Node B communicate **directly** (peer-to-peer), and the operator steps out of the loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Port:** XML-RPC Server runs on **TCP Port 11311** (configurable via `ROS_MASTER_URI`). All discovery traffic uses this port, while actual topic data uses **random ephemeral high ports (typically 1000+)**.

### 1.3 When and Why `roscore` is Required

| Trigger Condition | Is `roscore` required? | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Running a single ROS node | **Yes** | Even one node needs the master for `/clock`, `/rosout`, and parameter lookup. |
| Running `rostopic`, `rosnode`, `rosservice` CLI | **Yes** | All CLI tools query the master. |
| Distributed multi-machine ROS setup | **Yes (on Master host only)** | All remote machines point to one master's URI via `ROS_MASTER_URI`. |
| Pure MATLAB/Python ROS bridges without topic use | **Yes** | ROS client libraries internally ping the master at init. |

### 1.4 System Prerequisites and Configuration

`roscore` requires:
- **Hostname Resolution:** The local machine must be able to resolve its own hostname (`127.0.0.1 <hostname>` in `/etc/hosts` for localhost setups).
- **`ROS_MASTER_URI`:** Defaults to `http://localhost:11311`.
- **`ROS_HOSTNAME` or `ROS_IP`:** Defines the IP advertised to other nodes during discovery.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often skip setting `ROS_IP` in a LAN-based multi-machine setup. The master then advertises `localhost`, and remote nodes can never connect. Always set `export ROS_IP=<your_lan_ip>` **before** launching `roscore`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of `roscore` — Detailed Architecture

#### 2.1.1 ROS Master (`/rosmaster`)
- Implemented in C++ as `rosmaster` package.
- Listens on **XML-RPC port 11311**.
- Maintains an in-memory graph of:
  - **Topic registrations** (Publishers/Subscribers per topic).
  - **Service registrations** (Service names → server URIs).
  - **Node registrations** (Node URI ↔ node name mapping).
- Does **NOT** route data — only provides the URI handshake so nodes connect directly.

#### 2.1.2 Parameter Server (`/parameter_server`)
- A key-value store implemented as part of `rosmaster` (a separate XML-RPC API).
- Default storage: in-memory; can be dumped to YAML via `rosparam dump`.
- Supports data types: `int32, float64, string, bool, list, dict, base64 (xmlrpc)`.
- Operations: `set, get, has, search, delete, load, dump`.
- **Scope:** Global by default; can be namespaced under a node via `~private` syntax (e.g., `~max_speed` resolves to `/<nodename>/max_speed`).

#### 2.1.3 `rosout` Node
- A standard ROS node that subscribes to the `/rosout` topic.
- All other nodes **automatically** publish their `ROS_INFO`, `ROS_WARN`, `ROS_ERROR`, `ROS_FATAL` messages to `/rosout`.
- The `rosout` node then logs these to `~/.ros/log/` directory by default.
- Aggregated logging for debugging across distributed systems.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic (Node Discovery)

The following logic governs every ROS node interaction:

1. **Node Startup:** A node (e.g., `/talker`) calls `ros::init()` (C++) or `rospy.init_node()` (Python).
2. **Master Contact:** The node opens an XML-RPC connection to `ROS_MASTER_URI` (port 11311).
3. **Registration:** It calls `registerPublisher("/chatter", "std_msgs/String", ...)`.
4. **Master Update:** The master stores this in its topic graph.
5. **Subscriber Contact:** Another node (`/listener`) registers as a subscriber of `/chatter`.
6. **Callback Notification:** The master notifies `/talker` about the new subscriber and provides the subscriber's XML-RPC URI.
7. **Direct Handshake:** `/talker` connects to the subscriber's XML-RPC to negotiate a **TCPROS** connection on a random high port.
8. **Data Flow:** Both nodes communicate directly via TCP — the master is bypassed.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Table

| Concept | Value / Protocol | Default Port | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Master XML-RPC | XML-RPC over HTTP | **11311** | Registration, lookup, parameter I/O |
| Topic Data Transport | **TCPROS** (TCP) | Ephemeral (≥ 1000) | Peer-to-peer message streaming |
| Service Calls | **TCPROS** | Ephemeral | Synchronous request/reply |
| Parameter Storage | XML-RPC + YAML | 11311 | Shared configuration |
| Logging Topic | `/rosout` | — | Central log aggregation |
| Default Log Location | `~/.ros/log/` | — | Persisted `rosout` logs |
| Master URI Env Var | `ROS_MASTER_URI` | — | Points to master's network address |
| Host Identity Env Var | `ROS_HOSTNAME` / `ROS_IP` | — | IP/hostname advertised to peers |
| Recommended Distribution | ROS Noetic / ROS 2 Humble (KTU Lab) | — | Check your syllabus edition |

### 2.4 Critical Engineering Distinctions (Exam Gold)

> [!NOTE]
> **`roscore` vs `rosmaster`**
> - `roscore` is the **launcher script** (a shell script).
> - `rosmaster` is the **executable Python module** that the script invokes. They are often used interchangeably in textbooks, but **technically distinct**.

> [!NOTE]
> **ROS 1 vs ROS 2**
> - In ROS 1 (Noetic, Melodic), `roscore` is mandatory.
> - In ROS 2 (Foxy, Humble), there is **NO `roscore`**. Discovery is handled by **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** middleware. KTU 2024 syllabus often tests both — confirm with your module content.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Robotics:** Multi-robot fleets in factories use `roscore` for centralized coordination.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Distributed perception, planning, and control nodes register via the master.
- **Swarm Drones:** Ground control stations use `roscore` for parameter synchronization.
- **Research Labs:** Single `roscore` enables heterogeneous hardware (LiDARs, cameras, IMUs) to share data seamlessly.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Procedure & Walkthrough

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Discovery, Subscription, and Data Flow

Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step protocol** that occurs when two nodes `/pub_node` and `/sub_node` exchange data. This is the most frequently tested analytical sequence in KTU exams.

---

#### **Phase 1: Master Initialization**

**Step 1.1** — Operator executes `roscore` on host H1.

The launcher script performs the following sequence:

$$
\text{roscore} \rightarrow \text{start\_rosmaster} \rightarrow \text{start\_parameter\_server} \rightarrow \text{start\_rosout}
$$

**Step 1.2** — The `rosmaster` process binds TCP socket on port **11311** and waits for incoming XML-RPC requests.

**Step 1.3** — The `/rosout` node is launched, which subscribes to topic `/rosout` and logs messages to:
$$
\text{\texttt{\textasciitilde/.ros/log/}}
$$

---

#### **Phase 2: Publisher Registration**

**Step 2.1** — The publisher node `/pub_node` starts and resolves the master URI from `ROS_MASTER_URI` (default: `http://localhost:11311`).

**Step 2.2** — It opens an XML-RPC connection to the master and invokes:
```
registerPublisher("/chatter", "std_msgs/String", pub_node_uri)
```

**Step 2.3** — The master appends this registration to its in-memory topic table:

$$
T_{topics} \leftarrow T_{topics} \cup \{(\text{"/chatter"}, \text{"std\_msgs/String"}, \text{pub\_uri})\}
$$

**Step 2.4** — Master returns a **success status** and a unique **caller ID** to `/pub_node`.

---

#### **Phase 3: Subscriber Registration and Master-Mediated Handshake**

**Step 3.1** — The subscriber `/sub_node` registers via XML-RPC:
```
registerSubscriber("/chatter", "std_msgs/String", sub_node_uri)
```

**Step 3.2** — The master detects a **topic match** between publisher and subscriber. It appends to its table.

**Step 3.3** — The master sends an XML-RPC notification (callback) to `/pub_node`:
```
publisherUpdate("/chatter", [sub_node_uri])
```

**Step 3.4** — `/pub_node` now knows that a subscriber exists and must establish a **TCPROS** connection.

---

#### **Phase 4: Peer-to-Peer TCPROS Negotiation**

**Step 4.1** — `/pub_node` opens a TCP connection to `sub_node_uri` (on sub_node's XML-RPC port).

**Step 4.2** — `/pub_node` sends a **TCPROS handshake header** (multi-line ASCII):
```
[TCPROS header]
topic: /chatter
md5sum: <hash_of_message_definition>
type: std_msgs/String
callerid: /pub_node
```

**Step 4.3** — `/sub_node` validates the `md5sum` and `type` against its expected values. If compatible, it returns success (`""`).

**Step 4.4** — Both sides are now ready. Binary-serialized messages flow over this TCP socket **directly**, bypassing the master.

---

#### **Phase 5: Parameter Server Operations (Parallel Channel)**

A node can independently query parameters via XML-RPC:

$$
\text{getParam}(\text{"/robot/max\_speed"}) \rightarrow \text{master returns } 1.5
$$

$$
\text{setParam}(\text{"/robot/max\_speed"}, 2.0) \rightarrow \text{master updates store}
$$

If a private namespace is used (`~max_speed`), the resolved global key becomes:
$$
\text{resolved\_key} = \text{"/"} + \text{nodename} + \text{"/max\_speed"}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical/Logical Example

**Problem Scenario:** A KTU lab setup has three nodes — `/camera_driver`, `/image_processor`, `/gui_display`. `/camera_driver` publishes raw images on `/camera/image_raw`. `/image_processor` subscribes and republishes processed images on `/image_processed`. `/gui_display` subscribes to `/image_processed`.

**(a)** Identify the role of `roscore` in this setup.

**Solution:** `roscore` acts as the central registry. It does **not** transport image data. Its role is limited to:
- Registering `/camera_driver` as a publisher of `/camera/image_raw`.
- Registering `/image_processor` as a subscriber and publisher.
- Registering `/gui_display` as a subscriber.
- Mediating URIs between publishers and subscribers.

**(b)** If `/camera_driver` publishes at **30 Hz** with images of size **640×480×3 bytes**, calculate the TCPROS throughput.

**Solution:**

$$
\text{Frame size} = 640 \times 480 \times 3 = 921{,}600 \text{ bytes} \approx 900 \text{ KB}
$$

$$
\text{Throughput} = 30 \times 921{,}600 = 27{,}648{,}000 \text{ bytes/sec}
$$

$$
\text{Throughput in Mbps} = \frac{27{,}648{,}000 \times 8}{10^{6}} \approx 221.18 \text{ Mbit/s}
$$

This shows that for high-bandwidth topics, the peer-to-peer nature of TCPROS is essential — routing this through the master would crash it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Master bottleneck is the reason `roscore` only handles **metadata**. Streaming 221 Mbps of image data through a single Python master would be catastrophic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level `roscore` Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Operator Terminal: roscore command] --> B[roscore Launcher Script]
    B --> C[ROSMaster Process]
    B --> D[Parameter Server Engine]
    B --> E[rosout Logging Node]
    C --> F[XML-RPC Listener :11311]
    D --> F
    F --> G[(Topic / Service / Node Registry)]
    D --> H[(Key Value Parameter Store)]
    E --> I[/rosout Topic Subscriber]
    I --> J[Log Files on Disk]
    K[Publisher Node] -->|XML-RPC Register| F
    F -->|URI Callback| K
    L[Subscriber Node] -->|XML-RPC Register| F
    F -->|URI Callback| L
    K -->|TCPROS Direct| L
```

### 4.2 Detailed Discovery and Data Flow Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Op as Operator
    participant RC as roscore Master
    participant Pub as /pub_node
    participant Sub as /sub_node
    Op->>RC: Launch roscore (bind port 11311)
    RC-->>Op: Ready (xml-rpc server up)
    Pub->>RC: XML-RPC: registerPublisher(/chatter, type, uri)
    RC-->>Pub: status=success, caller_id=/pub_node
    Sub->>RC: XML-RPC: registerSubscriber(/chatter, type, uri)
    RC-->>Sub: status=success, caller_id=/sub_node
    RC->>Pub: XML-RPC callback: publisherUpdate(subscriber_uris)
    Pub->>Sub: TCP Handshake (TCPROS header: topic, md5, type)
    Sub-->>Pub: OK (md5 matched)
    loop Data Streaming
        Pub->>Sub: Binary-serialized std_msgs/String messages
        Sub->>Pub: ACK (TCP)
    end
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture (Master Internals)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Inet[XML-RPC Request Handler :11311] --> Router{Request Type Router}
    Router -->|registerPublisher / Subscriber| TopicReg[Topic Registration Table]
    Router -->|registerService| ServiceReg[Service Registration Table]
    Router -->|getParam / setParam| ParamStore[Parameter Key Value Store]
    Router -->|registerNode| NodeReg[Node URI Database]
    TopicReg --> Notifier[Topic Change Notifier]
    Notifier -->|XML-RPC Callback| Outgoing[URI Callback Dispatcher]
    ServiceReg --> Outgoing
    NodeReg --> Outgoing
    ParamStore --> ParamResp[Parameter Response Builder]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw the architecture of `roscore`," always mark **Port 11311**, distinguish **XML-RPC (control)** from **TCPROS (data)**, and show the **peer-to-peer** connection that bypasses the master after the handshake.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**What is `roscore`? List its three main components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

`roscore` is the core launcher process of the Robot Operating System (ROS) that must be running before any other ROS node can start. It provides naming, registration, and parameter lookup services.

Its three main components are:
1. **ROS Master** — handles name registration and lookup for nodes, topics, and services.
2. **Parameter Server** — a shared key-value store accessible by all nodes.
3. **`rosout`** — a centralized logging node that aggregates log messages from all nodes.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**What is the default port of the ROS Master? What protocol does it use?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The default port of the ROS Master is **11311**, and it uses the **XML-RPC over HTTP** protocol for all control-plane communications (registration, lookup, parameter queries). Actual topic data, however, is exchanged peer-to-peer using the **TCPROS** protocol on separate ephemeral ports.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of `roscore` with a neat block diagram. List its components and describe the role of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a step-by-step sequence diagram, describe how two ROS nodes (`/publisher` and `/subscriber`) discover each other and establish communication through `roscore`. Clearly distinguish between XML-RPC and TCPROS. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Architecture of `roscore` (7 marks):**

`roscore` is a launcher script that bootstraps three background processes required for any ROS system to function. Its architecture can be visualized as follows:

1. **ROS Master (`rosmaster`)** — A central XML-RPC server listening on **port 11311**. It maintains the **topic graph**, **service graph**, and **node registry**. It does not transmit any data; it only stores names and URIs.

2. **Parameter Server** — A key-value store, also implemented within the master. It allows nodes to read/write configuration parameters at runtime using XML-RPC calls. Supports data types such as integers, floats, strings, booleans, lists, and dictionaries.

3. **`rosout` Node** — A standard ROS node that subscribes to the `/rosout` topic. All other nodes automatically publish their logging output to this topic. `rosout` then writes the logs to `~/.ros/log/` directory, enabling centralized debugging.

[Insert diagram from Section 4.1 here]

The ROS Master enables **decoupled peer-to-peer** communication: publishers and subscribers do not need to know each other's addresses beforehand. They register with the master, receive each other's URIs, and then connect directly.

**Part (b) — Discovery Sequence (7 marks):**

1. **Step 1:** The `/publisher` node starts and resolves `ROS_MASTER_URI`. It opens an XML-RPC connection to port 11311.
2. **Step 2:** It invokes `registerPublisher("/chatter", "std_msgs/String", pub_uri)` over XML-RPC. The master stores this entry.
3. **Step 3:** The `/subscriber` node similarly registers via `registerSubscriber("/chatter", "std_msgs/String", sub_uri)`.
4. **Step 4:** The master detects the topic match and sends an XML-RPC callback (`publisherUpdate`) to `/publisher`, providing the subscriber's URI.
5. **Step 5:** `/publisher` now opens a **TCPROS** connection directly to `/subscriber`'s XML-RPC port. It sends a handshake header containing the topic name, message MD5 sum, and type.
6. **Step 6:** `/subscriber` validates the handshake and accepts the connection. The XML-RPC connection is closed for data exchange.
7. **Step 7:** From this point, binary-serialized messages flow **directly over TCP** between the two nodes. The master is no longer involved in data transport.

**Distinction:**
- **XML-RPC:** Used for **control plane** (registration, lookup, parameters). Runs on port 11311.
- **TCPROS:** Used for **data plane** (message streaming). Runs on ephemeral ports directly between nodes.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Differentiate between ROS Master and Parameter Server. Explain the types of parameter scopes with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ROS-based robot has a camera publishing 1920×1080 RGB images at 60 Hz on `/camera/image_raw`. Calculate:
(i) The size of a single image in MB.
(ii) The data rate in MB/s.
(iii) Explain why this traffic cannot be routed through `roscore` and how TCPROS resolves this. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — ROS Master vs Parameter Server (7 marks):**

| Aspect | ROS Master | Parameter Server |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Function | Name registration & lookup for nodes/topics/services | Shared key-value configuration storage |
| Data Stored | Topic names, node URIs, service definitions | Configuration parameters (int, float, string, etc.) |
| Communication | XML-RPC | XML-RPC |
| Scope | Global across all nodes | Global, relative (`/ns/key`), or private (`~key`) |

**Parameter Scopes (with examples for node `/robot1/motor_driver`):**

- **Global Scope:** `/robot/max_speed = 2.0` — accessible by all nodes.
- **Relative Scope:** `motor_driver/max_speed` — resolved under the node's namespace as `/robot1/motor_driver/max_speed`.
- **Private Scope:** `~max_speed` — resolved as `/robot1/motor_driver/max_speed` (using `~` syntax).

Private scope is preferred for node-specific configuration to avoid naming collisions.

**Part (b) — Bandwidth Calculation (7 marks):**

**(i) Single image size:**

$$
\text{Size} = 1920 \times 1080 \times 3 \text{ bytes} = 6{,}220{,}800 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{Size in MB} = \frac{6{,}220{,}800}{1024 \times 1024} \approx 5.93 \text{ MB}
$$

**(ii) Data rate:**

$$
\text{Data rate} = 60 \times 5.93 \approx 355.8 \text{ MB/s} \approx 2.85 \text{ Gbit/s}
$$

**(iii) Why `roscore` cannot route this traffic:**

`roscore` runs as a single Python-based process on port 11311. Routing 2.85 Gbit/s of image data through this single-threaded XML-RPC server would:
- Saturate the master's CPU and memory.
- Introduce massive latency (the master would become a bottleneck).
- Violate ROS's design philosophy of lightweight, distributed peer-to-peer communication.

**How TCPROS resolves this:**

After the initial handshake via the master, the publisher and subscriber establish a **direct TCP connection** on an ephemeral high port. This connection bypasses the master entirely. Data flows peer-to-peer at line speed, enabling high-bandwidth streams (cameras, LiDAR point clouds) to be handled efficiently without overloading `roscore`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Do not** confuse `roscore` with the ROS Master alone. Always mention **all three** components (Master, Parameter Server, `rosout`).
> - **Do not** state that `roscore` transmits message data. It only handles **metadata**. Failure to clarify this costs **2 marks**.
> - **Do not** forget to mention **port 11311** and the **XML-RPC protocol** in any architecture question.
> - **Always** emphasize the **peer-to-peer** nature of TCPROS in numerical bandwidth questions to earn full methodology marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **`roscore` is mandatory** before any ROS 1 node can run.
- It launches **three** processes: **ROS Master**, **Parameter Server**, and **`rosout`**.
- The **ROS Master** listens on **XML-RPC port 11311**.
- `roscore` does **NOT** route message data — it only provides **name resolution** and **URI lookup**.
- Actual message exchange happens via **TCPROS** (peer-to-peer TCP) after an XML-RPC-mediated handshake.
- **Parameter Server** supports **global**, **relative**, and **private (`~`)** scopes.
- **`rosout`** aggregates logs centrally to `~/.ros/log/`.
- **ROS 2** does **NOT** use `roscore** — it uses **DDS** for discovery.
- `ROS_MASTER_URI` and `ROS_IP`/`ROS_HOSTNAME` must be correctly set for distributed setups.
- For high-bandwidth topics (camera, LiDAR), the **peer-to-peer** design is critical to avoid master overload.
- Always include **port 11311**, **XML-RPC**, and **TCPROS** terminology in answers for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: catkin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** catkin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Catkin** is the official **build system** of the Robot Operating System (ROS). It is a set of Python-based extensions to the **CMake** cross-platform build automation tool, designed specifically to satisfy the unique structural, dependency, and packaging requirements of ROS-based robotic software projects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Catkin forms the foundational build infrastructure of ROS. Every ROS package, node, library, and message definition must be compiled, linked, and aggregated through the catkin build pipeline. Without catkin, ROS packages cannot interoperate, discover each other, or resolve message/service/action dependencies.

Technically, catkin is **not** a replacement for CMake — it *sits on top of* CMake and provides additional macros, functions, and conventions (e.g., `catkin_package()`, `find_package(catkin ...)`, `add_message_files()`, `add_service_files()`, `add_action_files()`, `generate_messages()`, `catkin_LIBRARY_DIRS`) that automate the tedious aspects of building distributed, inter-dependent robotic software.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Construction Company of ROS"

Imagine you are building a large **robotic city** (a ROS application). The city has many independent districts (ROS packages) — a camera district, a motor-control district, a navigation district, a vision district. Each district has:
- Its own **blueprint** (CMakeLists.txt + package.xml)
- Its own **construction crew** (source code)
- Its own **delivery schedule** (dependencies)

**Catkin is the master construction company** that:
1. Reads every district's blueprint,
2. Identifies which districts depend on which other districts (e.g., the navigation district needs the map district's libraries),
3. Schedules the construction in the correct order,
4. Lays down a common road network so that all districts can communicate via shared libraries and message headers (the `devel/setup.bash` overlay),
5. Finally hands over a ready-to-run city (the compiled workspace).

Without catkin, every ROS developer would have to manually run `g++` and `gcc` commands, set include paths by hand, and figure out inter-package dependencies on their own — a logistical nightmare for a system built on **modular code reuse**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exam:** `rosbuild` was the *original* build system of ROS (used in early ROS versions like ROS Electric and Fuerte). From **ROS Groovy onwards (2013)**, `catkin` replaced it as the default and is now the *only* supported build system in modern ROS 1 (Melodic, Noetic) and is also used in **ROS 2** (Foxy, Humble, Iron, Jazzy) as the foundation for `ament_cmake` (its successor with similar concepts).

## 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Workspace** | A directory tree that contains the source code of multiple ROS packages. |
| **Catkin Package** | The minimum unit of build/distribution — a folder with `package.xml` and `CMakeLists.txt`. |
| **Source Space (`src/`)** | Where user code and cloned packages live. |
| **Build Space (`build/`)** | Where CMake is invoked and intermediate files are generated. |
| **Devel Space (`devel/`)** | Where built targets (libraries, headers, executables) are installed. |
| **Overlay** | Multiple workspaces chained so newer packages shadow older ones. |
| **Manifest (`package.xml`)** | XML file declaring package metadata, dependencies, and license. |
| **Build Manifest (`CMakeLists.txt`)** | CMake script declaring build targets, dependencies, and exports. |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Conventions in Bold

- **Minimum Python version supported by catkin:** **Python 3.5+** (for ROS Noetic and ROS 2).
- **Default workspace name (convention, not enforced):** **`catkin_ws`**.
- **Recommended number of top-level source packages per workspace:** No hard limit, but **< 100** for sane build times.
- **Standard build tool:** **`catkin_make`** (legacy) or **`catkin build`** (from `catkin_tools`, isolated devel spaces, parallel jobs).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Favourite:** A common KTU question asks, *"List any two differences between `catkin_make` and `catkin build`."* Remember: `catkin build` provides **isolated devel spaces per package** and supports **parallel builds**; `catkin_make` uses a **single shared devel space** and builds serially by default.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Catkin Build Pipeline — Operational Concept

The catkin build process follows a strict, deterministic sequence. Understanding this sequence is **the most exam-relevant piece of catkin theory** at KTU level.

### Step 1 — Workspace Initialization
The user creates a workspace directory tree:
```
~/catkin_ws/
├── src/        # SOURCE SPACE (you put your packages here)
├── build/      # BUILD SPACE  (auto-created)
└── devel/      # DEVEL SPACE  (auto-created)
```
The `src/` folder is the **only manually populated** folder; the other two are created during the first build.

### Step 2 — Configuration Phase
Catkin invokes **CMake** in the `build/` directory. For each package, CMake:
1. Reads the `CMakeLists.txt` file.
2. Reads the `package.xml` manifest.
3. Discovers all packages in the `src/` tree via **recursive directory traversal**.
4. Constructs a **directed acyclic graph (DAG)** of package dependencies.

### Step 3 — Dependency Resolution & Topological Sorting
Catkin sorts the DAG so that any package `P` is built **after** all packages that `P` depends on. This solves the "which library comes first?" problem automatically. The algorithm is essentially **Kahn's algorithm** or **DFS-based topological sort**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why topological sort matters:** Imagine Package A defines a custom message `MySensor.msg`, and Package B's library uses that message. B *cannot* be compiled until A's message headers are generated. Catkin's topological sort guarantees A is built first.

### Step 4 — Message / Service / Action Generation
For each package that defines `.msg`, `.srv`, or `.action` files:
- The **message generation pipeline** runs: `genmsg` → `gencpp`/`genpy`/`genlisp`.
- Language-specific headers/sources are emitted into the `devel/` folder.
- These become include directories for downstream packages.

### Step 5 — Compilation, Linking, Installation
- C/C++ source files are compiled to object files, then linked into libraries or executables.
- Headers are exported to `devel/include/`.
- Shared libraries (`.so`) are exported to `devel/lib/`.
- Python scripts are made executable and installed.

### Step 6 — Environment Setup
A file **`devel/setup.bash`** is generated. Sourcing it sets environment variables so the shell can find packages:
```bash
source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash
export ROS_PACKAGE_PATH=~/catkin_ws/src:/opt/ros/noetic/share
export CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH=~/catkin_ws/devel:/opt/ros/noetic
```

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Catkin Package — The Two Mandatory Files

### (A) `package.xml` — The Manifest
The manifest is an **XML file** describing the package's identity, authorship, license, and dependencies. A minimal example:

```xml
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<package format="2">
  <name>my_robot_pkg</name>
  <version>0.1.0</version>
  <description>My first ROS package</description>
  <maintainer email="student@ktu.ac.in">KTU Student</maintainer>
  <license>BSD</license>
  <buildtool_depend>catkin</buildtool_depend>
  <depend>roscpp</depend>
  <depend>std_msgs</depend>
</package>
```

**Dependency tags (format 2):**
- `<buildtool_depend>` — Tools needed to **build** the package (almost always `catkin`).
- `<depend>` — Generic dependency needed at **build, export, and runtime**.
- `<build_depend>` — Needed only at build time.
- `<exec_depend>` — Needed only at runtime.
- `<test_depend>` — Needed only for unit tests.
- `<doc_depend>` — Needed only for documentation (e.g., `rosdoc_lite`).

### (B) `CMakeLists.txt` — The Build Script
A minimal CMakeLists.txt:

```cmake
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.0.2)
project(my_robot_pkg)

find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS
  roscpp
  std_msgs
)

catkin_package(
  CATKIN_DEPENDS roscpp std_msgs
)

include_directories(${catkin_INCLUDE_DIRS})

add_executable(talker src/talker.cpp)
target_link_libraries(talker ${catkin_LIBRARIES})
```

**Key catkin macros:**
- `find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS ...)` — Declare inter-package build dependencies.
- `catkin_package(...)` — Declare what THIS package exports to downstream packages.
- `add_message_files(...)` — Declare `.msg` files to generate.
- `add_service_files(...)` — Declare `.srv` files to generate.
- `add_action_files(...)` — Declare `.action` files to generate.
- `generate_messages(...)` — Trigger message code generation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Rule of Thumb / Formula |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Workspace size | `src + build + devel` |
| 2 | Mandatory package files | `package.xml` AND `CMakeLists.txt` |
| 3 | Build order | Determined by `find_package(catkin ...)` + topological sort |
| 4 | Header export path | `devel/include/<package_name>/` |
| 5 | Library export path | `devel/lib/` |
| 6 | Default parallel jobs (`catkin_make`) | `-j<NCPU>` |
| 7 | Build time complexity (approx) | $T_{build} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{S_i}{C} + D$ where $S_i$ = package i size, $C$ = cores, $D$ = dependency wait |
| 8 | Overlay depth | **No hard limit** but recommended $\leq 3$ |
| 9 | `catkin_make` invocation | `catkin_make` (uses current dir as workspace) |
| 10 | `catkin build` invocation | `catkin build` (must be inside `src/`'s parent) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The catkin overlay system enables *layered robotics development*. A drone manufacturer can ship a base workspace; research labs can overlay experimental packages on top without modifying the original installation. This is analogous to **Docker image layers** or **Linux chroot overlays** — a critical pattern in modern robotics CI/CD.

## 2.4 Why Catkin Exists — The Engineering Problem It Solves

In a large robotic system (e.g., an autonomous car with 200+ packages):
- Manual compilation would be **error-prone** and **non-reproducible**.
- Cross-package message dependencies are **complex to resolve manually**.
- A central registry of installed packages is required for runtime discovery (used by `rospack`, `rosnode`, `rostopic`).

Catkin provides:
1. **Standardized metadata** (package.xml) — every ROS tool can introspect any package.
2. **Deterministic build order** — no "it works on my machine" failures.
3. **Discovery paths** — `rospack find <pkg>` works because every installed package's path is known.
4. **Message generation pipeline** — language-agnostic IDL → C++/Python/Lisp bindings.
5. **Workspace overlays** — incremental development without polluting the system install.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Mathematical Analysis & Build Order

## 3.1 Derivation of the Catkin Build Order (DAG Analysis)

The catkin build system models the workspace as a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**:

$$G = (V, E)$$

where:
- $V$ = set of packages in the workspace, $V = \{P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_N\}$
- $E$ = set of edges, $E = \{(P_i, P_j) \mid P_j \text{ depends on } P_i\}$

### 3.1.1 Edge Construction (from `find_package` calls)

For each package $P_j$, the CMakeLists.txt contains:

```cmake
find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS P_a P_b P_c)
```

This translates into three directed edges in the DAG:

$$
(P_a, P_j), \quad (P_b, P_j), \quad (P_c, P_j)
$$

meaning "$P_a$ must be built *before* $P_j$".

### 3.1.2 Topological Sort via Kahn's Algorithm

Kahn's algorithm produces a build order $L$ such that for every edge $(P_i, P_j) \in E$, $P_i$ appears *before* $P_j$ in $L$.

**Step 1.** Compute in-degree $d_{in}(v)$ for every vertex $v \in V$.

**Step 2.** Initialize a queue $Q$ with all vertices having $d_{in}(v) = 0$ (packages with no dependencies).

**Step 3.** While $Q$ is not empty:
- Dequeue vertex $u$.
- Append $u$ to list $L$.
- For every edge $(u, v)$: decrement $d_{in}(v)$. If $d_{in}(v) = 0$, enqueue $v$.

**Step 4.** If $\vert L \vert \neq \vert V \vert$, a **circular dependency** exists and the build fails.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

Suppose the workspace contains four packages: `A`, `B`, `C`, `D` with the following dependencies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Package A: depends on} \quad & \{\} \\
\text{Package B: depends on} \quad & \{A\} \\
\text{Package C: depends on} \quad & \{A, B\} \\
\text{Package D: depends on} \quad & \{B\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Edge set:** $E = \{(A,B),\ (A,C),\ (B,C),\ (B,D)\}$

**In-degrees:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{in}(A) &= 0 \\
d_{in}(B) &= 1 \quad \text{(from A)} \\
d_{in}(C) &= 2 \quad \text{(from A, B)} \\
d_{in}(D) &= 1 \quad \text{(from B)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Initial queue:** $Q = [A]$

**Iteration 1:** Dequeue $A$, append to $L$. $L = [A]$.
- Decrement $d_{in}(B)$: $1 \to 0$, enqueue $B$.
- Decrement $d_{in}(C)$: $2 \to 1$.

$Q = [B]$

**Iteration 2:** Dequeue $B$, append to $L$. $L = [A, B]$.
- Decrement $d_{in}(C)$: $1 \to 0$, enqueue $C$.
- Decrement $d_{in}(D)$: $1 \to 0$, enqueue $D$.

$Q = [C, D]$

**Iteration 3:** Dequeue $C$, then $D$. $L = [A, B, C, D]$.

$\vert L \vert = 4 = \vert V \vert$ → **No cycles. Valid build order: A, B, C, D.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** If the examiner asks *"What happens if there is a circular dependency?"*, the answer is: **The build FAILS**, and catkin prints a `Circular dependency detected` error. This is a deliberate safety feature to prevent infinite build loops.

## 3.2 Build Time Estimation Model

A simplified model for the total build time $T_{build}$ of a catkin workspace:

$$
T_{build} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( t_{compile}(P_i) + t_{link}(P_i) \right) + T_{msg\_gen} + T_{overhead}
$$

where:
- $t_{compile}(P_i) = \dfrac{S_i^{cpp}}{C \cdot \bar{v}_{compile}}$ (compilation time scales with source size and inversely with CPU cores)
- $t_{link}(P_i) = \dfrac{S_i^{lib}}{C \cdot \bar{v}_{link}}$
- $T_{msg\_gen}$ is the message generation overhead (small for most projects, dominates for very message-heavy ones)
- $T_{overhead}$ includes CMake configuration time, typically **5–15 seconds** per package

When **parallel builds** are enabled (e.g., `catkin build -j8`), the term $C$ (number of cores) is utilized, but only for packages whose dependencies are already satisfied — catkin cannot parallelize *across* a dependency chain, only *within* a layer of the DAG.

The build time assuming a **fully parallel DAG layer** with $L$ layers and $C$ cores:

$$
T_{build}^{parallel} \approx \sum_{\ell=1}^{L} \max\left( \dfrac{1}{C} \sum_{i \in \text{Layer}_\ell} t_i,\; \max_{i \in \text{Layer}_\ell} t_i \right) + T_{overhead}
$$

For a **single-core serial build** ($C = 1$):

$$
T_{build}^{serial} = \sum_{\ell=1}^{L} \sum_{i \in \text{Layer}_\ell} t_i + T_{overhead}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Insight:** This is why very large ROS workspaces (e.g., the full **ROS Navigation Stack** with 50+ packages) take **10–30 minutes** to build from source. Cloud CI pipelines for robotics (e.g., Industrial CI, Robonomics) leverage **incremental catkin builds** and **caching of build artifacts in Docker layers** to keep CI times manageable.

## 3.3 Message Generation Pipeline — Algebraic View

When a package $P$ contains $M$ message files, the generated artifacts for language $\ell$ are:

$$
A_\ell(P) = \{ h_\ell(m), \ s_\ell(m) \mid m \in M_P \}
$$

where:
- $h_\ell(m)$ = generated header/source file in language $\ell$ (e.g., `MyMsg.h` for C++),
- $s_\ell(m)$ = generated source for serialization/deserialization,
- $M_P$ = set of `.msg` files in package $P$.

For C++ ($\ell = \text{cpp}$):

$$
A_{cpp}(P) = \{ \text{MyMsg.h},\ \text{MyMsg.cpp},\ \text{MyMsgRequest.h},\ \ldots \}
$$

The dependency of downstream package $P_j$ on $P_i$'s messages is then resolved by:

$$
\text{Include path of } P_j = \bigcup_{P_i \in \text{deps}(P_j)} \text{devel/include/}P_i
$$

This is the mechanism that lets `P_j` write `\#include <Pi/MyMsg.h>` and have the compiler find the generated header.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Catkin Workspace Architecture (High-Level Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    USER["User / Developer"]
    CMD["Terminal Command: catkin_make or catkin build"]
    WS["catkin_ws Workspace Root"]
    SRC["src/  Source Space"]
    PKGA["package_A/  with package.xml and CMakeLists.txt"]
    PKGB["package_B/  with package.xml and CMakeLists.txt"]
    PKGC["package_C/  with package.xml and CMakeLists.txt"]
    BUILD["build/  Build Space"]
    CMAKE["CMake Configuration"]
    DAG["Dependency DAG Topological Sort"]
    DEVEL["devel/  Development Space"]
    LIBS["devel/lib/  Shared Libraries .so"]
    HEAD["devel/include/  C++ Headers"]
    PY["devel/lib/python3/  Python modules"]
    SETUP["devel/setup.bash  Environment Script"]
    ROSPKG["rospack find / roscore / rosrun  Runtime tools"]

    USER --> CMD
    CMD --> WS
    WS --> SRC
    WS --> BUILD
    WS --> DEVEL
    SRC --> PKGA
    SRC --> PKGB
    SRC --> PKGC
    BUILD --> CMAKE
    CMAKE --> DAG
    DAG --> DEVEL
    DEVEL --> LIBS
    DEVEL --> HEAD
    DEVEL --> PY
    DEVEL --> SETUP
    SETUP --> ROSPKG
    PKGA -. "find_package dep" .-> PKGB
    PKGB -. "find_package dep" .-> PKGC
```

## 4.2 Catkin Build Pipeline — Detailed Sequence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: User runs catkin_make inside catkin_ws"]
    B["Step 2: CMake invoked inside build/ directory"]
    C["Step 3: CMakeLists.txt of each package is parsed"]
    D["Step 4: package.xml manifests are read"]
    E["Step 5: DAG of package dependencies is constructed"]
    F{"Step 6: Cycle in DAG?"}
    G["ABORT with Circular dependency error"]
    H["Step 7: Perform topological sort of packages"]
    I["Step 8: For each layer of the DAG, in order"]
    J["Step 9: Generate message and service code if .msg and .srv exist"]
    K["Step 10: Compile C++ sources to .o files"]
    L["Step 11: Link .o files into libraries and executables"]
    M["Step 12: Export headers to devel/include"]
    N["Step 13: Export libraries to devel/lib"]
    O["Step 14: Generate devel/setup.bash"]
    P["Step 15: User sources setup.bash to use the workspace"]
    Q["Step 16: ROS runtime tools rospack, roscore discover packages"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F -- Yes --> G
    F -- No --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J
    J --> K
    K --> L
    L --> M
    L --> N
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P
    P --> Q
```

## 4.3 Catkin Package Internal Structure (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    PKG["Catkin Package Root Folder"]
    MAN["package.xml  Manifest Metadata and Dependencies"]
    CML["CMakeLists.txt  Build Script and Targets"]
    INCL["include/pkg_name/  Public C++ Headers"]
    SRC1["src/  C++ Source Files .cpp"]
    SCRIPTS["scripts/  Python or Shell Scripts"]
    MSG["msg/  Message Definitions .msg files"]
    SRV["srv/  Service Definitions .srv files"]
    ACT["action/  Action Definitions .action files"]
    LAUNCH["launch/  ROS Launch Files .launch"]
    CFG["config/  Configuration YAML Files"]
    TEST["test/  Unit Tests"]
    README["README.md  Documentation"]

    PKG --> MAN
    PKG --> CML
    PKG --> INCL
    PKG --> SRC1
    PKG --> SCRIPTS
    PKG --> MSG
    PKG --> SRV
    PKG --> ACT
    PKG --> LAUNCH
    PKG --> CFG
    PKG --> TEST
    PKG --> README
```

## 4.4 Overlaid Workspace Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SYS["System Install: /opt/ros/noetic/share  Base ROS packages"]
    W1["Workspace 1: ~/overlay_ws  Custom robot packages"]
    W2["Workspace 2: ~/catkin_ws  User application packages"]
    SHELL["User Shell Session"]
    ROSLAUNCH["roslaunch / rosrun  Resolved through ROS_PACKAGE_PATH"]

    SYS --> SHELL
    W1 --> SHELL
    W2 --> SHELL
    SHELL --> ROSLAUNCH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Figure 4.4:** The order of entries in `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` determines search priority. In a typical setup:
> ```
> ROS_PACKAGE_PATH = ~/catkin_ws/src : ~/overlay_ws/src : /opt/ros/noetic/share
> ```
> When `rospack find foo` is called, it searches left-to-right and returns the **first match**. This means `~/catkin_ws` overrides `~/overlay_ws`, which overrides the system install. This is the **overlay mechanism** of catkin in action.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]
**Define catkin. Why is it called the build system of ROS?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Catkin is the official build system of the Robot Operating System (ROS). It is a set of Python-based extensions built on top of the CMake cross-platform build automation tool, designed specifically to compile, link, and package ROS nodes, libraries, and message definitions. It is called the *build system of ROS* because every ROS package — comprising source code, message/service files, and configuration — is compiled, dependency-resolved, and integrated into a runnable workspace only through the catkin pipeline. Catkin replaces the older `rosbuild` system and is mandatory in all modern ROS distributions (Groovy onwards). (3 marks)

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]
**List the three top-level directories of a catkin workspace and state the purpose of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A standard catkin workspace contains three top-level directories:
1. **`src/`** — The **source space** where the user places or clones ROS packages. It is the only manually created directory. (1 mark)
2. **`build/`** — The **build space** where CMake is invoked and intermediate compilation files (Makefiles, object files) are stored. (1 mark)
3. **`devel/`** — The **devel (development) space** where built artifacts — shared libraries, generated headers, Python modules, and the `setup.bash` environment script — are installed. (1 mark)

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** As per the 2024 ESE scheme, Module 1 long-answer questions carry 14 marks. Students are given an internal choice (Q1 OR Q2 within the same module). Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each, or a single 14-mark analytical question with multiple sub-asks.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a catkin workspace. List the three standard directories and describe the role of the `devel/setup.bash` file. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the structure of a catkin package. With an example, describe the purpose of the `package.xml` manifest and the `CMakeLists.txt` build script. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — Catkin Workspace Architecture (7 marks):**

A catkin workspace is a directory tree where multiple ROS packages are co-located, built, and made runnable. The convention is to name it `catkin_ws`. The architecture is as follows:

```
~/catkin_ws/
├── src/        ← Source space (user populates this)
├── build/      ← Build space  (auto-created on first build)
└── devel/      ← Devel space  (auto-created on first build)
```

- **`src/` (Source Space):** This is the only directory the developer manually populates. It contains either freshly authored packages or repositories cloned via `git clone` or `wstool`. Each subdirectory under `src/` is expected to be (or contain) a catkin package. (2 marks)

- **`build/` (Build Space):** When the user runs `catkin_make` (or `catkin build`), CMake is invoked in this directory. All intermediate artifacts — `CMakeCache.txt`, `Makefile`s, object files, and CMake-generated configuration — reside here. The user normally does not interact with this folder. (2 marks)

- **`devel/` (Devel Space):** This is the workspace's "output" folder. It contains:
  - `devel/lib/` — built shared libraries (`.so` files) and executables,
  - `devel/include/` — exported C++ headers,
  - `devel/lib/python3/dist-packages/` — Python modules,
  - `devel/setup.bash` — the critical environment script. (2 marks)

**Role of `devel/setup.bash`:** Sourcing this file in a shell sets environment variables such as `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`, `CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH`, and `PYTHONPATH` so that ROS tools (`rospack`, `rosrun`, `roslaunch`, `roscore`) can discover and execute the packages built in this workspace. Without sourcing it, the shell only knows about the system ROS install at `/opt/ros/<distro>`. (1 mark)

**Part (b) — Structure of a Catkin Package (7 marks):**

A **catkin package** is the minimum unit of build and distribution in ROS. It is any directory inside `src/` that contains **both** a `package.xml` file **and** a `CMakeLists.txt` file. (1 mark)

Standard sub-folders inside a package:

| Folder | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `src/` | C++ source files (`.cpp`) |
| `include/<pkg_name>/` | C++ public header files (`.h`) |
| `scripts/` | Python / shell scripts (made executable) |
| `msg/` | Custom `.msg` message definitions |
| `srv/` | Custom `.srv` service definitions |
| `action/` | Custom `.action` action definitions |
