| `launch/` | `.launch` files for starting multiple nodes |
| `config/` | YAML configuration files |
| `test/` | Unit tests (e.g., rostest, gtest) |

(2 marks)

**`package.xml` example and purpose:**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<package format="2">
  <name>my_robot_pkg</name>
  <version>0.1.0</version>
  <description>Controller for my robot arm</description>
  <maintainer email="student@ktu.ac.in">KTU Student</maintainer>
  <license>BSD</license>
  <buildtool_depend>catkin</buildtool_depend>
  <build_depend>roscpp</build_depend>
  <build_depend>std_msgs</build_depend>
  <exec_depend>roscpp</exec_depend>
  <exec_depend>std_msgs</exec_depend>
</package>
```

The `package.xml` declares the package's **name, version, description, maintainer, license, and dependencies**. It is consumed by ROS package-management tools (`rospack`, `rosdep`, `bloom` for release). The dependency tags (`buildtool_depend`, `build_depend`, `exec_depend`, etc.) tell catkin and the OS package manager which other packages must be present. (2 marks)

**`CMakeLists.txt` example and purpose:**

```cmake
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.0.2)
project(my_robot_pkg)
find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS roscpp std_msgs)
catkin_package(CATKIN_DEPENDS roscpp std_msgs)
include_directories(${catkin_INCLUDE_DIRS})
add_executable(arm_controller src/arm_controller.cpp)
target_link_libraries(arm_controller ${catkin_LIBRARIES})
```

The `CMakeLists.txt` is the **build script** that invokes CMake and tells the compiler *how* to build the package. Key macros:
- `find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS ...)` — declares build-time dependencies.
- `catkin_package(...)` — declares what this package *exports* to downstream packages.
- `add_executable(...)` + `target_link_libraries(...)` — builds and links a ROS node.

(2 marks)

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted Model Question]**

**(a)** What is a **catkin workspace overlay**? Explain with a suitable example how multiple workspaces can be chained together. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare `catkin_make` and `catkin build`. Describe the **DAG-based topological sort** algorithm that catkin uses to determine the build order, with a worked example of **4 packages** having the dependency edges: $A \to B$, $A \to C$, $B \to D$, $C \to D$. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — Workspace Overlays (7 marks):**

A **catkin workspace overlay** is the mechanism by which multiple ROS workspaces are layered on top of each other, so that packages in a "higher" workspace shadow those in a "lower" workspace with the same name. (1 mark)

**Why overlays are needed:**
- The **base ROS install** at `/opt/ros/<distro>` contains thousands of core packages.
- A roboticist may want to **modify one of those packages** (e.g., a new version of `navigation`) without uninstalling the system version.
- Solution: clone the modified package into a new workspace, build it, and source its `setup.bash` *after* the system setup. The new package takes precedence due to its position in `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`. (2 marks)

**Chained workspace example:**

```
/opt/ros/noetic/setup.bash         ← Base ROS install
~/robot_base_ws/devel/setup.bash   ← Manufacturer workspace (e.g., TurtleBot3)
/home/student/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash  ← Student's research workspace
```

The user's `.bashrc` typically contains:
```bash
source /opt/ros/noetic/setup.bash
source ~/robot_base_ws/devel/setup.bash
source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash
```

After sourcing all three, the effective `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` is:
```
/home/student/catkin_ws/src : ~/robot_base_ws/src : /opt/ros/noetic/share
```

If a package named `teleop_twist_joy` exists in **both** `robot_base_ws` and `catkin_ws`, the version in `catkin_ws` is used because it appears **earlier** in the path. (4 marks)

**Part (b) — Comparison of `catkin_make` vs `catkin build` and Topological Sort (7 marks):**

**Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Feature | `catkin_make` | `catkin build` (catkin_tools) |
|---|---|---|
| Devel space | **Single shared** for entire workspace | **Isolated per package** |
| Parallelism | Default serial; manual `-jN` | Automatic parallel across DAG layers |
| Source space traversal | Treats all packages as one | Supports multiple source spaces |
| Build profiling | Not built-in | Built-in (`catkin build --summarize`) |
| Selective rebuild | All-or-nothing | Per-package (`catkin build <pkg_name>`) |
| Invocation directory | Anywhere in workspace | Must be in `src/`'s parent |

**DAG Topological Sort — Worked Example (4 marks):**

Given the dependency edges: $A \to B$, $A \to C$, $B \to D$, $C \to D$.

Interpretation: $B$ depends on $A$, $C$ depends on $A$, $D$ depends on both $B$ and $C$.

**Step 1 — Vertex set and in-degrees:**

$$
V = \{A, B, C, D\}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{in}(A) &= 0 \\
d_{in}(B) &= 1 \quad (\text{from } A) \\
d_{in}(C) &= 1 \quad (\text{from } A) \\
d_{in}(D) &= 2 \quad (\text{from } B \text{ and } C)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Initialize queue with zero in-degree vertices:** $Q = [A]$

**Step 3 — Process queue:**

- Pop $A$, output order $L = [A]$. Decrement in-degrees: $d_{in}(B) \to 0$ (enqueue $B$), $d_{in}(C) \to 0$ (enqueue $C$). $Q = [B, C]$.
- Pop $B$, output $L = [A, B]$. Decrement: $d_{in}(D) \to 1$. $Q = [C]$.
- Pop $C$, output $L = [A, B, C]$. Decrement: $d_{in}(D) \to 0$ (enqueue $D$). $Q = [D]$.
- Pop $D$, output $L = [A, B, C, D]$. $Q = [\ ]$.

**Result:** $\vert L \vert = 4 = \vert V \vert$ → No cycles. Valid build order: **$A \to B \to C \to D$** (or $A \to C \to B \to D$ — both are valid topological orders). (4 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing `rosbuild` and `catkin`:** Students often write `rosbuild` in exams for modern ROS versions. Mark deduction: 1 mark. Always specify the **distro name** (e.g., ROS Noetic) when asked.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mention BOTH `package.xml` AND `CMakeLists.txt`:** A folder with only `package.xml` is **not** a valid catkin package. Examiners specifically check for both files. Mark deduction: 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Stating `devel/` is manually created:** It is **auto-created** by catkin during the first build. Only `src/` is user-created. Mark deduction: 0.5 marks.
>
> 4. **Not sourcing `setup.bash`:** Students often build the workspace correctly but forget to mention that the `setup.bash` must be sourced for ROS tools to find the packages. Mark deduction: 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Mixing up dependency tags:** Using `<depend>` for a build-time-only dependency is technically correct (it covers build, export, and exec), but if the question specifically asks for *runtime* dependencies, the correct tag is `<exec_depend>`. Pay attention to wording.
>
> 6. **Not explaining topological sort with a diagram:** The DAG and topological sort together carry 4–5 marks in a 14-mark question. Always draw the DAG, label in-degrees, and show the queue evolution step by step.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Memorize These for KTU ESE**

- ✅ **Catkin = build system of ROS** = **CMake + Python extensions** (not a replacement for CMake).
- ✅ Catkin **replaced** `rosbuild` since **ROS Groovy (2013)**.
- ✅ **Three workspace folders:** `src/` (manual, user code), `build/` (CMake artifacts), `devel/` (output + `setup.bash`).
- ✅ **Two mandatory files per package:** `package.xml` (manifest) and `CMakeLists.txt` (build script).
- ✅ **Topological sort** is used to determine build order based on the **DAG** of `find_package(catkin ...)` calls.
- ✅ **Circular dependencies** cause catkin to **abort** the build — there is no workaround other than redesigning the package boundaries.
- ✅ **`catkin_make`** = single shared devel space, simple, default since ROS Groovy.
- ✅ **`catkin build`** = per-package isolated devel spaces, parallel, requires `python-catkin-tools`.
- ✅ **Message generation:** `.msg`/`.srv`/`.action` → `genmsg` → `gencpp`/`genpy` → headers in `devel/include/`.
- ✅ **Workspace overlays** = chaining `setup.bash` files so newer packages shadow older ones via `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` search order.
- ✅ **Always `source devel/setup.bash`** after a build to make the new packages discoverable.
- ✅ **The `catkin_package()` macro** is what makes a package's headers and libraries *exportable* to downstream packages — without it, other packages cannot link against it.
- ✅ **Format 2 of `package.xml`** (the modern style) uses unified `<depend>` tag plus specific `<build_depend>`, `<exec_depend>`, `<test_depend>`, `<doc_depend>`, `<buildtool_depend>` tags.
- ✅ **ROS 2 successor:** `ament_cmake` is the catkin-equivalent build system for ROS 2; the concepts are very similar but `ament` has stricter isolation.
- ✅ **Build artifacts:** shared libraries in `devel/lib`, headers in `devel/include`, Python modules in `devel/lib/python3/dist-packages`.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, allocate marks roughly as — **Diagram: 2 marks**, **Three folders explained: 3 marks**, **`package.xml` purpose + example: 2 marks**, **`CMakeLists.txt` purpose + example: 2 marks**, **Build pipeline / DAG / topological sort: 4 marks**, **Overlay concept: 1 mark**. Always conclude with a one-line summary of catkin's role.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Workspaces and ROS Packages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Workspaces and ROS Packages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **ROS Workspace (Catkin Workspace):** A ROS workspace is a *directory* on the file system where you modify, build, and install ROS packages. In modern ROS 1 distributions (Noetic) and ROS 2, the build system is **catkin** (ROS 1) or **colcon/ament** (ROS 2). The workspace follows a strict, conventional directory hierarchy that allows the build tool to locate source code, intermediate build files, and the final installable binaries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ROS Package:** A package is the *fundamental unit of software organization* in ROS. It is a directory containing a `package.xml` (the manifest), a `CMakeLists.txt` (or `setup.py` for pure-Python packages), source code, configuration files, launch files, and other related resources. Every executable, library, and tool in ROS is contained within a package.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Think of a **ROS workspace** as the **construction site** of a large housing colony, and a **ROS package** as an individual **house (or a self-contained apartment unit)**.

| Real-World Analogy | ROS Equivalent | Function |
|--------------------|----------------|----------|
| The entire land where houses are built | Workspace (e.g., `~/ros_ws/`) | Container for all development |
| Blueprints for the colony layout | `src/` folder | Where source code "blueprints" live |
| Construction equipment and partially built structures | `build/` folder | Intermediate compilation output |
| Completed, furnished houses ready for occupancy | `devel/` (or `install/`) | Final, runnable binaries |
| An individual house | A ROS package | A reusable, distributable unit of functionality |
| The house's title deed and address | `package.xml` | Manifest with name, version, dependencies |
| The structural engineering plan | `CMakeLists.txt` | Build instructions for the compiler |

> [!TIP]
> Just as you cannot build a house without land, a ROS package must reside *somewhere* within a workspace's `src/` directory to be compiled and used by ROS.

---

## 1.3 The Standard Catkin Workspace Topology

A standard ROS 1 (Noetic) catkin workspace has **four (4)** logical sub-directories. In ROS 2, the `devel` folder is replaced by `install`, but the philosophy is identical.

$$
\text{Catkin Workspace} = \{\text{src}, \text{build}, \text{devel}, \text{logs}\}
$$

- **`src/`** — The **source space**. You create your package source code here. This is the **only** directory you should *manually* edit.
- **`build/`** — The **build space**. Invoked by `catkin_make` or `catkin build`. Stores intermediate CMake/Make cache files.
- **`devel/`** — The **development space**. Holds the compiled binaries, generated headers, and the critical `setup.bash` file you source before running ROS nodes.
- **`logs/`** — Diagnostic logs produced during compilation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a ROS Package — The Operational Breakdown

A ROS package is **not** just a folder. It is a contractually-defined software unit. The ROS build system *refuses* to recognize a folder as a package if the mandatory contract files are missing.

### 2.1.1 Mandatory Files (The "Non-Negotiable Contract")

1. **`package.xml`** — The **manifest file**.
   - Written in **XML**.
   - Declares the package name, version, author, maintainer, license, and crucially, its **dependencies** (both build, export, and run-time).
   - This is the file ROS package managers (`rosdep`, `apt`) read to install dependencies automatically.

2. **`CMakeLists.txt`** — The **build script** (for C++ packages).
   - Written in **CMake** language.
   - Declares the package, finds required ROS components (`find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS ...)`), and specifies executables/libraries to build.

### 2.1.2 Common Optional Components

| Component | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| `src/` | C++ source code (`.cpp`) and headers (`.h`) |
| `scripts/` | Executable Python scripts (with `chmod +x`) |
| `msg/`, `srv/`, `action/` | Custom message, service, and action definitions |
| `launch/` | XML launch files to start multiple nodes simultaneously |
| `config/` | YAML parameter files |
| `urdf/`, `meshes/` | Robot description files and 3D models |
| `include/<pkg_name>/` | Public C++ headers exposed to other packages |

---

## 2.2 The Why & How of the Catkin Build Process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why do we need `catkin_make`?**
> Plain `gcc` cannot resolve ROS-specific includes, find message-generated headers, or link against `roscpp`. `catkin` extends CMake to understand the ROS ecosystem (topics, services, messages) and orchestrates parallel compilation across dozens of interdependent packages.

### 2.2.1 The "Why" Behind Each Build Space
- **`src` (Why keep source separate?):** Cleanliness and version control. You never commit `build/` or `devel/` to Git. A fresh clone only contains `src/`, and a single `catkin_make` recreates everything.
- **`build` (Why a separate cache?):** CMake's incremental build logic relies on comparing source timestamps against the `build/` cache. Keeping it isolated prevents cache poisoning.
- **`devel` (Why a "development" output?):** In ROS 1, the compiled artifacts are placed in `devel/` so you can `source devel/setup.bash` and immediately run your code **without a system-wide install** (no `sudo make install`).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **must-memorize** set of commands and definitions for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 viva and written exams.

| # | Command / Concept | Syntax | Function |
|---|-------------------|--------|----------|
| 1 | Create workspace | `mkdir -p ~/catkin_ws/src` | Creates the standard workspace layout |
| 2 | Initialize workspace | `cd ~/catkin_ws/src && catkin_init_workspace` | Marks `src/` as a catkin workspace (creates `CMakeLists.txt` symlink) |
| 3 | Build workspace | `cd ~/catkin_ws && catkin_make` | Compiles all packages in `src/` |
| 4 | Source overlay | `source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash` | Registers workspace packages with ROS environment |
| 5 | Create package (C++) | `catkin_create_pkg <name> [deps...]` | e.g., `catkin_create_pkg my_robot std_msgs rospy roscpp` |
| 6 | Create package (Python) | Manual creation + `catkin_python_setup()` | Pure-Python packages do not need CMake compilation |
| 7 | Install dependencies | `rosdep install --from-paths src --ignore-src -r -y` | Resolves and installs all `package.xml` dependencies |
| 8 | Find a package | `rospack find <package_name>` | Returns the absolute path of a package |
| 9 | List all packages | `rospack list` or `rospack list-names` | Enumerates packages in the ROS environment |
| 10 | View dependencies | `rospack depends1 <package_name>` | Lists **direct** dependencies |
| 11 | View transitive deps | `rospack depends <package_name>` | Lists **all** recursive dependencies |
| 12 | Manifest file | `package.xml` | Format 1 (legacy) or Format 2 (current) |
| 13 | Build tool | `catkin` (ROS 1) / `colcon` + `ament` (ROS 2) | Underlying build system |
| 14 | Metapackage | A package with `<exec_depend>` on others and an empty `CMakeLists.txt` | Groups related packages for one-line install |
| 15 | License field | `<license>BSD</license>` etc. | **Mandatory** in `package.xml` for release |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

In industrial robotics, the workspace/package model enables:
- **Modular Development:** A perception team, a motion-planning team, and a hardware-interface team can each own separate packages and integrate them via the ROS topic graph without code-level coupling.
- **Reusability:** A well-written SLAM package can be dropped into any robot's workspace, provided the message interfaces match.
- **Continuous Integration (CI):** The `catkin_make --pkg <pkg>` flag enables CI pipelines (e.g., GitHub Actions) to build and test only the changed package, drastically reducing CI time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section walks through the **exact, exhaustive** command sequence to create a workspace, create a package, declare dependencies, and build it. Every step includes the command, the input/output, and the explanation of *why* it works.

---

## 3.1 The Complete Workflow: From Zero to a Running Package

### Step 1 — Create the Workspace Directory Tree

**Command:**
```bash
mkdir -p ~/catkin_ws/src
cd ~/catkin_ws/src
```

**Explanation:**
- `mkdir -p` creates parent directories recursively without error if they exist. This creates the canonical `catkin_ws/src/` pair in one shot.
- The `-p` flag is the **professional** way; without it, running the command twice errors out.

### Step 2 — Initialize the Source Space

**Command:**
```bash
catkin_init_workspace
```

**Output (Expected):**
```
Creating symlink /home/user/catkin_ws/src/CMakeLists.txt pointing to /opt/ros/noetic/share/catkin/cmake/toplevel.cmake
```

**Explanation:**
- This is a *one-time* operation per workspace.
- It creates a `CMakeLists.txt` **symlink** inside `src/` that points to the top-level CMake file shipped with your ROS distribution. This symlink is what tells `catkin_make` "I am a valid catkin source space."

### Step 3 — Build the (Empty) Workspace

**Command:**
```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws/
catkin_make
```

**Output (Truncated):**
```
Base path: /home/user/catkin_ws
Source space: /home/user/catkin_ws/src
Build space: /home/user/catkin_ws/build
Devel space: /home/user/catkin_ws/devel
Install space: /home/user/catkin_ws/install
####
#### Running command: "cmake /home/user/catkin_ws/src -DCATKIN_DEVEL_PREFIX=/home/user/catkin_ws/devel -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=/home/user/catkin_ws/install" in "/home/user/catkin_ws/build"
####
...
[100%] Built target ...
```

**Explanation:**
- `catkin_make` automatically creates the `build/` and `devel/` folders.
- The `devel/` folder now contains a `setup.bash` script that, when sourced, **overlays** this workspace's packages on top of the system ROS installation. This is the **ROS overlay** concept.

### Step 4 — Source the Workspace (CRITICAL)

**Command:**
```bash
source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash
```

**Explanation:**
- This modifies environment variables, principally `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` and `PYTHONPATH`, so that ROS tools (`rosrun`, `roslaunch`, `rospack`) can *find* your packages.
- **Pro Tip for KTU Labs:** Add this command to your `~/.bashrc`:
```bash
echo "source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash" >> ~/.bashrc
```

### Step 5 — Create a New Package

**Command:**
```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws/src
catkin_create_pkg beginner_tutorials std_msgs rospy roscpp
```

**Output (Expected):**
```
Created file beginner_tutorials/package.xml
Created file beginner_tutorials/CMakeLists.txt
Created folder beginner_tutorials/include/beginner_tutorials
Created folder beginner_tutorials/src
```

**Explanation of Arguments:**
- `beginner_tutorials` → The new package name (must be lowercase, underscores only, no spaces).
- `std_msgs` → Standard message types (e.g., `String`, `Int32`).
- `rospy` → Python ROS client library.
- `roscpp` → C++ ROS client library.

These three are auto-injected into `<depend>`, `<build_depend>`, `<exec_depend>`, and `<build_export_depend>` tags inside `package.xml`.

### Step 6 — Customize the `package.xml` (Format 2)

**File:** `~/catkin_ws/src/beginner_tutorials/package.xml`

**Critical Tags (Excerpt):**
```xml
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<package format="2">
  <name>beginner_tutorials</name>
  <version>0.0.0</version>
  <description>The beginner_tutorials package</description>
  <maintainer email="you@todo.todo">Your Name</maintainer>
  <license>BSD</license>
  <buildtool_depend>catkin</buildtool_depend>
  <depend>roscpp</depend>
  <depend>rospy</depend>
  <depend>std_msgs</depend>
</package>
```

**Tag Semantics:**
- `<name>` — Unique package name; must match the directory name.
- `<license>` — **Mandatory**. Common values: `BSD`, `MIT`, `Apache-2.0`, `GPLv3`.
- `<buildtool_depend>` — The tool required to *build* the package (always `catkin`).
- `<depend>` — A shorthand that implies all four dependency types (build, export, exec, doc).

### Step 7 — Build Only Your New Package

**Command:**
```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws
catkin_make -DCATKIN_WHITELIST_PACKAGES="beginner_tutorials"
```

**Explanation:**
- The `-DCATKIN_WHITELIST_PACKAGES` flag tells catkin to **build only** the specified package and ignore others. This is invaluable for large workspaces (e.g., the full `ros_comm` has ~100 packages).
- To reset the whitelist and build everything:
```bash
catkin_make -DCATKIN_WHITELIST_PACKAGES=""
```

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: ROS 1 vs. ROS 2 Workspace Differences

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently asks comparison questions. Memorize this matrix.

| Feature | ROS 1 (Noetic) | ROS 2 (Foxy/Humble) |
|---------|----------------|----------------------|
| Build Tool | `catkin_make` / `catkin build` | `colcon build` |
| Manifest File | `package.xml` (Format 2) | `package.xml` (Format 2/3) + `setup.py` |
| Build Script | `CMakeLists.txt` | `CMakeLists.txt` (C++) or `setup.py` (Python) |
| Source Script | `devel/setup.bash` | `install/setup.bash` |
| Workspace Folders | `src`, `build`, `devel`, `logs` | `src`, `build`, `install`, `log` |
| Init Command | `catkin_init_workspace` | (Not required — any folder with packages works) |
| Default Build | Single-threaded (CMake default) | Parallel via `colcon build --parallel-workers N` |

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of the ROS Package Resolution Algorithm

When you call `rospack find beginner_tutorials`, the following logic executes:

$$
\text{Result} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{N} \text{search}(\text{ROS\_PACKAGE\_PATH}_i, \text{package\_name})
$$

**Where:**
- $N$ = the number of paths listed in the environment variable `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`.
- $\text{search}(P, n)$ = a function that scans directory $P$ for a sub-directory named $n$ containing a valid `package.xml`.

**Step-by-step expansion:**

1. The shell loads `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` from `setup.bash`. Typical content:
   ```
   /opt/ros/noetic/share:/home/user/catkin_ws/src
   ```

2. `rospack` iterates through each path in order (left-to-right).

3. For each path $P_i$, it checks if $P_i / n$ exists AND if $P_i / n / \text{package.xml}$ exists.

4. The **first match** is returned. This is why the **order** of paths in `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` matters — overlay packages (in your `catkin_ws/src`) appear *after* the system path, so they take precedence.

5. If no match is found, `rospack find` exits with code `3` and prints:
   ```
   [rospack] Error: package 'beginner_tutorials' not found
   ```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Trap:** Forgetting to `source` the `setup.bash` file is the **#1** reason a freshly-created package reports as "not found." Always verify with `echo $ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` after sourcing.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Catkin Workspace Directory Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[HOME: ~/catkin_ws] --> B[src/]
    A --> C[build/]
    A --> D[devel/]
    A --> E[logs/]
    B --> F[CMakeLists.txt symlink]
    B --> G[beginner_tutorials/]
    G --> H[package.xml]
    G --> I[CMakeLists.txt]
    G --> J[src/]
    G --> K[include/]
    G --> L[launch/]
    G --> M[CMakeLists_build]
    D --> N[setup.bash]
    D --> O[lib/]
    D --> P[share/]
    D --> Q[include/]
    N --> R[Sourced into ~/.bashrc]
    R --> S[ROS_PACKAGE_PATH updated]
```

**Diagram Walkthrough:**
- The **root** is the workspace home.
- `src/` is the only user-editable space.
- `build/` is auto-generated and holds CMake cache.
- `devel/` holds the compiled outputs and the critical `setup.bash` that exports `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`.
- `logs/` captures compiler diagnostics.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Package Creation & Build Workflow (Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Terminal
    participant FS as File System
    participant CAT as catkin_make
    participant ROS as ROS Environment
    U->>FS: mkdir -p ~/catkin_ws/src
    U->>FS: cd src && catkin_init_workspace
    FS-->>U: CMakeLists.txt symlink created
    U->>CAT: cd .. && catkin_make
    CAT->>FS: Create build/ and devel/
    CAT->>FS: Compile all packages
    FS-->>U: devel/setup.bash ready
    U->>ROS: source devel/setup.bash
    ROS->>ROS: Update ROS_PACKAGE_PATH
    U->>FS: catkin_create_pkg my_pkg std_msgs
    FS-->>U: package.xml + CMakeLists.txt + folders
    U->>CAT: catkin_make (or whitelist)
    CAT-->>U: [100%] Built target my_pkg
    U->>ROS: rospack find my_pkg
    ROS-->>U: /home/user/catkin_ws/src/my_pkg
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Package

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Input[INPUTS]
        SRC[Source Code .cpp .py]
        XML[package.xml Manifest]
        CMAKE[CMakeLists.txt]
    end
    subgraph Core[BUILD ENGINE]
        CATK[catkin Compiler]
        CMAKEENG[CMake Engine]
    end
    subgraph Output[OUTPUTS devel/]
        EXEC[Executables nodes]
        LIB[Shared Libraries]
        HEAD[Generated Headers]
        SETUP[setup.bash Overlay]
    end
    SRC --> CATK
    XML --> CATK
    CMAKE --> CMAKEENG
    CMAKEENG --> CATK
    CATK --> EXEC
    CATK --> LIB
    CATK --> HEAD
    CATK --> SETUP
    SETUP --> ROSENV[ROS Runtime Environment]
```

**Interpretation:** This block diagram abstracts the internal pipeline of a package compilation, showing the inputs (source, manifest, build script), the build engine (catkin + CMake), and the outputs (executables, libraries, headers, and the overlay setup script).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below follow the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Evaluation) pattern: **Part A** (2 marks) and **Part B** (14 marks with internal choice).

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each)

### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Define a ROS package. List its two mandatory files.

**Model Answer:**
A ROS package is the fundamental unit of software organization in ROS, consisting of a directory containing source code, configuration, and build files. Its two mandatory files are:
1. **`package.xml`** — the manifest declaring name, version, dependencies, and license.
2. **`CMakeLists.txt`** — the build script instructing the compiler on how to build the package (for pure-Python packages, a `setup.py` is used instead).

---

### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`** What is the function of the `catkin_make` command?

**Model Answer:**
`catkin_make` is a macro that invokes CMake and Make in the standard catkin build order. It:
1. Compiles all packages found in the `src/` folder.
2. Generates intermediate files in `build/`.
3. Places the final compiled binaries, libraries, and the `setup.bash` overlay script in `devel/`.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the structure of a ROS workspace with a neat diagram. Describe the role of each sub-directory.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a step-by-step procedure, demonstrate how to create a ROS workspace, create a new package named `robot_nav` with dependencies `std_msgs`, `roscpp`, and `rospy`, and build it.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a):**

A ROS catkin workspace is a directory on the file system used to organize, build, and install ROS packages. The standard structure is:

```
~/catkin_ws/
├── src/        # Source space — user-edited package code
├── build/      # Build space — CMake/Make cache
├── devel/      # Development space — compiled outputs + setup.bash
└── logs/       # Build diagnostics
```

**Role of Each Sub-Directory:**

- **`src/`**: Contains the *source code* of all packages. This is the **only** directory a developer should manually modify. The packages live here as sub-directories, each containing a `package.xml` and `CMakeLists.txt`.
- **`build/`**: An *intermediate* space used by CMake to track which files need recompilation. It holds Makefiles, CMake cache, and object files. This folder can be safely deleted to force a clean rebuild.
- **`devel/`**: The *output* space where compiled executables, libraries, and the `setup.bash` overlay script reside. After sourcing `devel/setup.bash`, all packages compiled in this workspace become discoverable by the ROS environment.
- **`logs/`**: Contains diagnostic information emitted by the build tools (e.g., warnings, errors).

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Step 1 — Create the workspace skeleton:**
```bash
mkdir -p ~/catkin_ws/src
cd ~/catkin_ws/src
```

**Step 2 — Initialize the source space:**
```bash
catkin_init_workspace
```

**Step 3 — Build the (empty) workspace:**
```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws
catkin_make
```

**Step 4 — Source the overlay into the current shell:**
```bash
source devel/setup.bash
```

**Step 5 — Create the `robot_nav` package with the specified dependencies:**
```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws/src
catkin_create_pkg robot_nav std_msgs roscpp rospy
```

This command generates the `robot_nav/` directory containing:
- `package.xml` (auto-populated with `<depend>` tags for `std_msgs`, `roscpp`, `rospy`)
- `CMakeLists.txt` (auto-populated with `find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS ...)`)

**Step 6 — Build the new package:**
```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws
catkin_make
```

**Step 7 — Verify the package is registered:**
```bash
rospack find robot_nav
```
Expected output: `/home/<user>/catkin_ws/src/robot_nav`

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is a `package.xml` file? Explain the Format 2 schema of `package.xml` with a sample file.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between ROS 1 (Noetic) and ROS 2 (Humble) workspace systems. Mention the build tools, source script locations, and initialization differences.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a):**

**Definition:**
The `package.xml` file is the **manifest** of a ROS package. It is an XML file that contains metadata about the package — its name, version, author, license, description, and **dependencies** on other packages and build tools. It is the only file that ROS package managers like `rosdep` and `apt` use to identify and install dependencies.

**Format 2 Schema (Current Standard):**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<package format="2">
  <name>my_robot_pkg</name>
  <version>1.0.0</version>
  <description>Controller for differential-drive robot</description>

  <maintainer email="alice@ktu.in">Alice KTU</maintainer>
  <license>BSD</license>
  <author email="bob@ktu.in">Bob KTU</author>

  <buildtool_depend>catkin</buildtool_depend>
  <depend>roscpp</depend>
  <depend>std_msgs</depend>
  <depend>geometry_msgs</depend>

  <exec_depend>rospy</exec_depend>
  <doc_depend>doxygen</doc_depend>
</package>
```

**Key Tags Explained:**

| Tag | Purpose |
|-----|---------|
| `<name>` | Unique package name; must match directory name |
| `<version>` | Semantic version (major.minor.patch) |
| `<description>` | Human-readable purpose of the package |
| `<maintainer>` | Current maintainer (name + email); **required** |
| `<license>` | **Required** for release. Common: `BSD`, `MIT`, `Apache-2.0` |
| `<buildtool_depend>` | The tool used to build the package (typically `catkin`) |
| `<depend>` | Shorthand covering build, build_export, exec, and doc dependencies |
| `<exec_depend>` | Dependencies required at *runtime* only |
| `<doc_depend>` | Tools required to generate documentation (e.g., `doxygen`) |

**Format 1 vs. Format 2:**
- **Format 1** requires four separate tags: `<build_depend>`, `<run_depend>`, `<buildtool_depend>`, and `<test_depend>`.
- **Format 2** simplifies this with the unified `<depend>` tag and a clearer separation of concerns (e.g., `<exec_depend>` for runtime, `<doc_depend>` for documentation).

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b):**

| Parameter | ROS 1 (Noetic) | ROS 2 (Humble / Foxy) |
|-----------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Build Tool** | `catkin_make` or `catkin build` (community fork) | `colcon build` (official, multi-language) |
| **Source Script** | `devel/setup.bash` (overlay for development) | `install/setup.bash` (after `colcon build`) |
| **Initialization** | `catkin_init_workspace` required in `src/` | **No** init command — any directory with packages works |
| **Workspace Folders** | `src/`, `build/`, `devel/`, `logs/` | `src/`, `build/`, `install/`, `log/` |
| **Manifest** | `package.xml` (Format 2) | `package.xml` (Format 2/3) + `setup.py` for Python |
| **Compilation** | CMake + Make (sequential by default) | CMake + Ninja (parallel by default) |
| **Pure-Python Support** | `catkin_python_setup()` macro | Native via `setup.py` and `ament_python` |
| **Middleware** | Custom XML-RPC / TCPROS | DDS (Data Distribution Service) |
| **Multi-machine** | Requires `ROS_MASTER_URI` + manual `ROS_HOSTNAME` setup | Built-in discovery via DDS |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks on "Workspaces and Packages" questions:**
> 1. **Confusing `devel` and `install`:** In ROS 1, the overlay output is `devel/setup.bash`. In ROS 2, it is `install/setup.bash`. Writing the wrong one in a comparison answer is an instant **2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Forgetting to `source setup.bash`:** If a student's lab record or answer sheet shows `rospack find` failing, the examiner will mark it as "incomplete practical understanding." Always show the `source` command.
> 3. **Mixing up `catkin_make` and `catkin_create_pkg`:** The former *builds* an entire workspace; the latter *creates a single package* inside `src/`.
> 4. **Omitting the `<license>` tag:** A `package.xml` without a `<license>` field is considered **invalid** by `bloom-release` (KTU's release tool). Examiners often deduct 1 mark for this omission.
> 5. **Using the old Format 1 manifest:** Format 1 is deprecated. Always use Format 2 with the `<package format="2">` root tag.
> 6. **Wrong package naming convention:** Package names must be **lowercase** with **underscores** only. `My_Package` or `my-package` will fail.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Glance at this list 5 minutes before the exam for last-mile confidence.**

- [ ] A **workspace** is a directory; a **package** is a sub-directory inside `src/`.
- [ ] The canonical workspace structure: `src/`, `build/`, `devel/` (or `install/` in ROS 2), `logs/`.
- [ ] Two **mandatory** files per package: `package.xml` and `CMakeLists.txt`.
- [ ] `catkin_init_workspace` is run **once**, inside `src/`, to create the top-level `CMakeLists.txt` symlink.
- [ ] `catkin_make` is run from the workspace **root**, not from `src/`.
- [ ] `catkin_create_pkg <name> [dep1 dep2 ...]` is run from inside `src/`.
- [ ] After every build, you **must** `source devel/setup.bash` for the new packages to be discoverable.
- [ ] The `<license>` tag in `package.xml` is **mandatory**; common values are `BSD`, `MIT`, `Apache-2.0`.
- [ ] **Format 2** of `package.xml` uses `<depend>` as a shorthand for build/build_export/exec/doc dependencies.
- [ ] `rospack find <pkg>` returns the **absolute path** of a package; `rospack depends1 <pkg>` shows **direct** dependencies.
- [ ] `rosdep install --from-paths src --ignore-src -r -y` resolves and installs all declared dependencies.
- [ ] A **metapackage** is a special package that groups other packages and contains an *empty* `CMakeLists.txt`.
- [ ] **ROS 1 uses `catkin` + `devel/`; ROS 2 uses `colcon` + `install/`.**
- [ ] Never commit `build/`, `devel/`, or `install/` to Git — add them to `.gitignore`.
- [ ] Package names: **lowercase, underscores, no spaces, no hyphens**.
- [ ] The build system for ROS 1 is **CMake** under the hood; `catkin_make` is just a convenience wrapper.

---

> **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — TOPIC: WORKSPACES AND ROS PACKAGES**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: rosrun
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** rosrun

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**`rosrun`** is a fundamental command-line utility of the **Robot Operating System (ROS)** that allows a user to execute a single executable node from a ROS package directly from the shell, without requiring the user to navigate to the package directory or manually configure the executable path. According to the official ROS wiki specifications, `rosrun` locates and runs an executable from a given package by relying on the package's `manifest` (or `package.xml`) and the shell's environment variable `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PBRAT504 – Module 1):**
> The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly lists `rosrun` as a core **ROS Filesystem-Level Concept**, alongside tools like `rospack`, `roscd`, `rosls`, and `roscore`. Mastery of `rosrun` is mandatory for completing the practical lab components of this course.

The standard executable syntax adheres to the **POSIX CLI standard**:

$$ \texttt{rosrun} \; [\texttt{options}] \; \langle package\_name \rangle \; \langle executable\_name \rangle \; [args] $$

Where:
- $\langle package\_name \rangle$ refers to the name of the ROS package (as defined in `package.xml`).
- $\langle executable\_name \rangle$ refers to the actual compiled binary or script inside the `src/` or `scripts/` folder of that package.
- `[args]` are optional arguments passed directly to the node at runtime.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of ROS as a massive **digital warehouse** filled with thousands of toolboxes (called *packages*). Each toolbox contains several individual tools (called *nodes* or *executables*).

- Without `rosrun`, you would have to walk into the warehouse, find the exact aisle, find the exact shelf, find the exact box, open it, and only then take the tool out.
- With `rosrun`, you simply stand at the warehouse entrance and call out: *"Warehouse, get me the `turtlesim_node` tool from the `turtlesim` toolbox!"*

The warehouse manager (the `rosrun` utility) instantly knows where everything is stored and hands you the tool. You never have to know the physical location of the tool.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Analogy:**
> In an industrial PLC control room, an engineer uses a HMI (Human-Machine Interface) to launch a specific control routine. `rosrun` plays the exact same role in ROS — it abstracts the filesystem layer and provides a unified launch point for any executable in the ROS ecosystem.

### 1.3 Standard ROS Metrics

- **Default Package Search Paths:** Built on top of `rospack find` → scans the `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` environment variable.
- **Execution Layer:** User space (no kernel-level privileges required).
- **Default Node Discovery Time:** Typically $\lt 500$ ms on standard hardware (Intel i5/i7 class).
- **Underlying Language:** Written primarily in **Python 2/3** (legacy ROS1) with C++ bindings for performance-critical operations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic – The 5-Phase Execution Pipeline

When a user types `rosrun my_package my_node` in the terminal, ROS performs the following sequence of operations:

1. **Phase 1 – Argument Parsing:** The shell parses the input. `rosrun` recognizes three positional arguments: package, executable, and optional arguments.
2. **Phase 2 – Package Resolution:** The tool calls `rospack find <package_name>` internally to locate the absolute filesystem path of the requested package.
3. **Phase 3 – Manifest Validation:** It validates the `package.xml` (or older `manifest.xml`) to ensure the package exists and is properly registered in the ROS environment.
4. **Phase 4 – Executable Resolution:** It searches within the package's `src/`, `scripts/`, and `lib/` subdirectories for the named executable.
5. **Phase 5 – Process Spawning:** It uses the operating system's process creation API (`execve()` in Linux) to spawn the executable as a new process, passing any optional arguments to it.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this important for KTU exams?**
> The examiner frequently asks: *"What happens internally when you run `rosrun`?"* Memorizing this 5-phase pipeline guarantees full marks on the "Explain the working of rosrun" type questions.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Syntax / Value** | **Description** | **KTU Frequency** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| Command | `rosrun pkg node` | Launches a node from a package | ★★★★★ |
| Help flag | `rosrun -h` | Displays help information | ★★★ |
| Debug flag | `rosrun --debug pkg node` | Prints verbose debug logs | ★★★ |
| Prefix | `rosrun pkg node __name:=newName` | Remaps the node name | ★★★★★ |
| Log level | `rosrun pkg node __log_level:=DEBUG` | Sets ROS logging severity | ★★★★ |
| Param | `rosrun pkg node _param:=value` | Sets a private parameter | ★★★★ |
| Env Variable | `$ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` | Search root for packages | ★★★★ |
| Underlying Tool | `rospack find` | Resolves package paths | ★★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind `rosrun`:**
> Before `rosrun` was introduced, developers had to manually `cd` into the package's `src/` folder and run `./executable_name` every single time. This was tedious, error-prone, and did not scale. `rosrun` solved this by introducing **environment-based path resolution**, a concept now standard in DevOps (cf. Docker `PATH` resolution).

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

In professional robotics, `rosrun` is the **first tool used during development and debugging**. Engineers use it to:
- Test individual nodes in isolation before integrating them into a `roslaunch` file.
- Inject custom parameters at runtime without recompiling the code.
- Remap topic names on the fly to test interoperability between heterogeneous robots.
- Conduct unit testing on specific algorithm nodes (e.g., a path planner run in simulation).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the `rosrun` Internal Call Chain

Let us mathematically trace the call chain. We define the following variables:

- $P$ = the input package name (string)
- $N$ = the input node/executable name (string)
- $A$ = the list of additional arguments
- $E$ = the environment variable `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`
- $\mathcal{F}(x)$ = the `rospack find` resolution function

**Step 1: Input Acceptance**
The shell receives the command and forwards it to the `rosrun` binary.

$$\texttt{Input} = \{ P, N, A \}$$

**Step 2: Path Resolution**
The `rosrun` script invokes the underlying `rospack` utility to resolve the absolute path of the package.

$$\text{Path}_{pkg} = \mathcal{F}(P, E)$$

This step involves searching all directories listed in `$ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`. If $P$ is not found in any directory, the function returns a `None` value, and `rosrun` aborts with an error.

**Step 3: Executable Verification**
Once $\text{Path}_{pkg}$ is obtained, the system checks for the executable $N$ in standard subdirectories.

$$\text{Path}_{exe} = \text{Path}_{pkg} \cup \{ \text{"src/"}, \text{"scripts/"}, \text{"lib/"} \} \cup N$$

If the file exists and has executable permissions (`chmod +x`), it is selected.

**Step 4: Argument Forwarding**
The optional arguments $A$ are appended to the execution call.

$$\text{Command}_{final} = \text{Path}_{exe} \; \Vert \; A$$

The `$\Vert$` operator here denotes string concatenation in shell execution context.

**Step 5: Process Execution**
The system spawns the process. In Linux, this corresponds to a `fork()` followed by an `execve()` system call.

$$\text{Process}_{new} = \text{fork}() \rightarrow \text{execve}(\text{Command}_{final})$$

The parent shell process waits for the child node to terminate, while the node itself begins communicating over the ROS middleware.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Explanation:**
> The above derivation demonstrates that `rosrun` is essentially a **path resolver + process spawner wrapper**. It does not add any ROS-specific runtime behavior; that is handled by the `rospy` or `roscpp` client libraries *inside* the node itself.

### 3.2 Worked Example – Launching `turtlesim_node`

**Problem Statement:**
Launch the `turtlesim_node` executable from the `turtlesim` package, set the background color parameter to red (R=255, G=0, B=0), and remap the node name to `my_robot_turtle`.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify package and node names.
- Package: $P = \texttt{turtlesim}$
- Node: $N = \texttt{turtlesim\_node}$

**Step 2:** Identify the remapping syntax. ROS uses special "double-underscore" parameters for remapping.

$$\texttt{\_\_name} := \texttt{my\_robot\_turtle}$$

**Step 3:** Identify the private parameter syntax (single underscore prefix for node-private params).

$$\texttt{\_background\_r} := 255$$

**Step 4:** Assemble the final command.

```bash
rosrun turtlesim turtlesim_node __name:=my_robot_turtle _background_r:=255 _background_g:=0 _background_b:=0
```

**Step 5:** Verify execution.
- Open a new terminal and run `rosnode list`.
- Expected output: `/my_robot_turtle` (confirming the remap worked).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of `rosrun`

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete data flow when a user invokes `rosrun` from the command line, mapping all internal interactions with the ROS Filesystem Layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userInput([User Terminal Input]) --> argParse[Phase 1: Argument Parser]
    argParse --> pkgResolver[Phase 2: Package Path Resolver]
    pkgResolver --> envLookup{{Lookup ROS_PACKAGE_PATH Env Variable}}
    envLookup --> rospackCall[Call rospack find internally]
    rospackCall --> pathReturn[Return Absolute Package Path]
    pathReturn --> manifestCheck[Phase 3: Manifest Validator]
    manifestCheck -->|Valid package.xml found| exeSearch[Phase 4: Executable Search]
    manifestCheck -->|Invalid| errThrow[Throw Package Not Found Error]
    exeSearch --> dirScan[Scan src/ scripts/ lib/ subdirectories]
    dirScan -->|Executable located| permsCheck[Check Executable Permissions]
    permsCheck -->|chmod +x confirmed| procSpawn[Phase 5: Process Spawner]
    permsCheck -->|Permission denied| errPerm[Throw Permission Denied Error]
    procSpawn --> forkSys[Linux fork System Call]
    forkSys --> execSys[Linux execve System Call]
    execSys --> nodeLive([Node Running on ROS Network])
    nodeLive --> topicComm[Publishes Subscribes to ROS Topics]
    nodeLive --> paramComm[Reads Writes ROS Parameters]
    nodeLive --> serviceComm[Offers Calls ROS Services]
```

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram – Interaction with `roscore`

`rosrun` itself does not start `roscore`; the user must start it separately. The following sequence diagram shows the dependency.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant R as rosrun CLI
    participant RP as rospack Library
    participant FS as Linux Filesystem
    participant RC as roscore Master
    participant N as Target Node
    U->>R: Types rosrun my_pkg my_node
    R->>RP: rospack find my_pkg
    RP->>FS: Scan ROS_PACKAGE_PATH
    FS-->>RP: Return /home/user/catkin_ws/src/my_pkg
    RP-->>R: Absolute path resolved
    R->>FS: Verify my_node executable
    FS-->>R: Confirmed
    R->>N: fork and execve my_node
    N->>RC: Register as new node on master
    RC-->>N: URI assigned, handshake complete
    N-->>U: Node running, awaiting topics
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow – Error Handling Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[rosrun invoked] --> B{Package exists?}
    B -->|No| E1[Error: ResourceNotFound]
    B -->|Yes| C{Executable exists?}
    C -->|No| E2[Error: ExecutableNotFound]
    C -->|Yes| D{Executable bit set?}
    D -->|No| E3[Error: PermissionDenied]
    D -->|Yes| F{roscore running?}
    F -->|No| E4[Warning: No master, node may fail to register]
    F -->|Yes| G[Node launched successfully]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define `rosrun` and write its general syntax.*

**Model Answer:**
`rosrun` is a ROS command-line tool used to execute a specific node (executable) from a specified ROS package without needing to navigate to the package's directory. It relies on the `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` environment variable to locate packages.

**General Syntax:**
`rosrun [options] <package_name> <executable_name> [args]`

**Example:** `rosrun turtlesim turtlesim_node`

**Marks Breakdown:** Definition (2 marks) + Syntax (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between `rosrun` and `roslaunch` in ROS.*

**Model Answer:**

| **Feature** | `rosrun` | `roslaunch` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Purpose | Launches a **single** node | Launches **multiple** nodes |
| Input | Package + executable name | A `.launch` XML file |
| Parameter setting | Inline via command line | Defined inside the launch file |
| Use case | Testing/debugging a single node | Starting a full robotic system |
| Master required | Yes (must run `roscore` first) | Yes (can auto-start master) |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Exam Pattern Note:** Module 1 ESE questions carry 14 marks and offer an internal choice. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the internal working of the `rosrun` command with a neat block diagram. List the different options available with `rosrun`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The `rosrun` command operates in **5 phases**:

1. **Argument Parsing:** The shell parses the package name, executable name, and any optional arguments.
2. **Package Resolution:** It calls `rospack find <package>` internally to resolve the absolute path of the package from the `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` environment variable.
3. **Manifest Validation:** It checks the `package.xml` to ensure the package is valid and registered.
4. **Executable Resolution:** It searches the `src/`, `scripts/`, and `lib/` subdirectories of the package for the named executable.
5. **Process Spawning:** It uses the Linux `fork()` and `execve()` system calls to launch the executable as a new process.

**Available Options:**

| **Option** | **Description** |
| :--- | :--- |
| `-h`, `--help` | Shows help message |
| `--debug` | Prints debug information |
| `--prefix <prefix>` | Adds a prefix to the command |
| `__name:=<name>` | Remaps the node name |
| `__log_level:=<level>` | Sets logging severity |
| `__ip:=<ip>` | Sets the ROS_IP address |
| `_param:=value` | Sets a private parameter |

**(b)** Write a step-by-step procedure to launch the `turtlesim_node` using `rosrun`, and demonstrate how to remap its name and change the background color to red using command-line parameters. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Ensure `roscore` is running in a separate terminal.

```bash
roscore
```

**Step 2:** Source the ROS setup script (if not already done).

```bash
source /opt/ros/<distro>/setup.bash
```

**Step 3:** Verify the package is accessible.

```bash
rospack find turtlesim
```

**Step 4:** Launch the node with remapping and private parameters. The color channels in `turtlesim` are exposed as private parameters `_background_r`, `_background_g`, and `_background_b`.

```bash
rosrun turtlesim turtlesim_node __name:=my_turtle _background_r:=255 _background_g:=0 _background_b:=0
```

**Step 5:** Verify in another terminal.

```bash
rosnode list
```
**Expected Output:** `/my_turtle`

This confirms the remap worked. The background of the turtlesim window will now appear red.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of the `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` environment variable in the execution of `rosrun`. What happens if this variable is not set correctly? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`:**
`ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` is a colon-separated list of directories that tells ROS where to search for packages on the filesystem. When `rosrun` is invoked, it delegates the package lookup to the `rospack` library, which scans every directory in `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` looking for a folder matching the requested package name.

**Default Contents (Typical Setup):**
```
/opt/ros/noetic/share
/home/user/catkin_ws/src
```

**Consequences of Incorrect / Unset `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`:**
- If unset, `rosrun` will fail with `ResourceNotFound: <package_name>`.
- If pointing to wrong directories, `rosrun` will not find user-created packages.
- The system will still find system-wide packages installed via `apt`, since these are registered in `/opt/ros/...`.

**Fix:** Re-source the workspace.
```bash
source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash
echo $ROS_PACKAGE_PATH
```

**(b)** Explain the difference between **node remapping** and **topic remapping** in ROS. Provide an example using `rosrun` for both. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Node Remapping:**
Changes the **name of the node itself** as it registers with the ROS master. Uses the `__name:=` syntax.

**Example:**
```bash
rosrun turtlesim turtlesim_node __name:=robot1
```
The node now appears as `/robot1` instead of `/turtlesim`.

**Topic Remapping:**
Changes the **names of topics** that the node publishes or subscribes to. Uses the `<original_topic>:=<new_topic>` syntax.

**Example:** A node that subscribes to `/cmd_vel` can be remapped to subscribe to `/robot1/cmd_vel`:
```bash
rosrun teleop_twist_keyboard teleop_twist_keyboard.py cmd_vel:=/robot1/cmd_vel
```

**Key Difference:**

| **Aspect** | Node Remapping | Topic Remapping |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Target | The node's own name | Topic names in the graph |
| Syntax prefix | `__name:=` | No prefix (direct topic name) |
| Effect on `rosnode list` | Changes the name shown | No change to node name |
| Use case | Running multiple instances of same node | Connecting heterogeneous nodes |

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the double underscore:** Students often write `rosrun pkg node _name:=x` (single underscore). This is **incorrect** — node name remap uses `__name` (double underscore), while private parameters use a single `_` prefix. Mixing these up will fail the command at runtime and cost you 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Not starting `roscore`:** `rosrun` does not start the master. If the examiner asks you to "launch a node," always mention that `roscore` must be running first. Skipping this step costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing `rosrun` with `roslaunch`:** Writing a `.launch` file when asked about `rosrun` is an instant 0 for that sub-question. Read the verb carefully: *run* (single) vs *launch* (multiple).
> 4. **Wrong path resolution mention:** Examiners expect you to mention `rospack find` explicitly when explaining the internal working. Omitting this costs 2 marks.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **`rosrun` is a single-node launcher**, part of the ROS **Filesystem-Level** tools.
- **Syntax:** `rosrun [options] <package_name> <executable_name> [args]`
- **Internal dependency:** Uses `rospack find` to resolve package paths.
- **Environment variable:** `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` controls search roots.
- **Remapping syntax:** `__name:=<new_name>` for nodes, `<topic>:=<new_topic>` for topics.
- **Private parameters:** Single underscore prefix, e.g., `_param:=value`.
- **Requires `roscore`:** The master must be running before `rosrun` can register a node.
- **Cannot start multiple nodes:** For multi-node systems, use `roslaunch` with a `.launch` file.
- **Top exam tip:** Always draw the 5-phase pipeline diagram in "internal working" questions — it guarantees full marks.
- **Frequent command:** `rosrun rqt_graph rqt_graph` is the most commonly used debug tool launched via `rosrun`.
- **Linux dependency:** Heavily relies on `fork()`/`execve()` for process spawning.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Names, Namespaces, and Remapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Names, Namespaces, and Remapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are "Names" in ROS 2?

In the Robot Operating System, every **graph resource** — every node, topic, service, action, parameter, and launch file entry — is identified by a string called a **Graph Resource Name**. These names are the *only* way the ROS 2 graph (managed by the DDS layer) can discover, address, and connect computational elements to one another.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> A **Graph Resource Name** is a UTF-8 string used to uniquely identify a node, topic, service, or parameter within the ROS computation graph. It is the ROS-level equivalent of a *Uniform Resource Identifier (URI)* in computer networks.

### 1.2 The Three Categories of Names

ROS 2 strictly classifies every name into one of three forms based on its **first character**:

| Form | First Character | Scope | Typical Use |
|------|-----------------|-------|-------------|
| **Relative Name** | Letter, digit, or underscore (no leading `/`) | Bound to the **current namespace** | Default form for nodes/topics |
| **Global Name** | Begins with `/` | Absolute; ignores any current namespace | Cross-system references |
| **Private Name** | Begins with `~` (tilde) | Replaced by the **node's own namespace** | Node-specific parameters/topics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** Understanding the difference between *relative*, *global*, and *private* names is **the most frequently tested concept** in this module. A relative name `"cmd_vel"` is NOT the same as the global name `"/cmd_vel"` — they refer to completely different graph resources once a node is launched inside a namespace.

## 1.3 What Is a Namespace?

A **namespace** in ROS is a hierarchical prefix that groups related graph resources under a common logical umbrella. Namespaces partition the ROS graph into smaller, isolated sub-graphs — much like directories partition a file system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> A **Namespace** in ROS is a hierarchical identifier (string of characters separated by `/`) that acts as a logical container for graph resources. The separator character is the **forward slash `/`**, identical to a UNIX path.

**Key Properties of Namespaces:**
- Hierarchical (tree-structured).
- Created *implicitly* when a node is launched with a namespace.
- The empty/global namespace is represented by a single `/`.
- Sub-namespaces are formed by string concatenation with `/`.

## 1.4 What Is Remapping?

**Remapping** is the runtime mechanism that allows a user to **rename** a graph resource *without* modifying the source code. It is applied at launch time, after the name has been parsed but before the resource is registered with the DDS layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> **Remapping** is the substitution of one graph resource name for another at runtime, performed using the CLI syntax `_original_name:=new_name` (single underscore) or special node-control syntax `__name:=` (double underscore for the node's own name, namespace, log directory, etc.).

---

## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

Imagine a **large apartment complex** with multiple towers:

| ROS Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|-------------|--------------------|
| **Node** | An individual apartment |
| **Topic** | A postal address (mailbox) |
| **Namespace** | The building/tower name (e.g., `"TowerA"`) |
| **Global Name** (`/MailRoom`) | A shared facility accessible from anywhere |
| **Private Name** (`~rent`) | The apartment's own utility bill — only that resident can see it |
| **Remapping** (`/TowerA/MailRoom := /MailRoom_HQ`) | A forwarding address set up at the post office |
| **Relative Name** (`MailRoom`) | Saying "the local mailroom" — meaning depends on which building you are in |

### 1.6 Allowed Character Set (Strict Rule)

A valid graph resource name must satisfy the following character rules:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Character Rule:** The first character must be an **alphabetic letter (a–z, A–Z)**, a tilde (`~`), or a forward slash (`/`). Subsequent characters may additionally include digits (0–9) and underscores (`_`). **No spaces, no hyphens, no special symbols** (`!@#$%^&*+=\`, etc.) are allowed.

**Examples of valid names:**
- `velocity_controller`
- `/robot1/camera/image_raw`
- `~max_speed`
- `_cmd_vel`

**Examples of INVALID names:**
- `1turtle` (starts with digit)
- `cmd-vel` (hyphen not allowed)
- `cmd vel` (space not allowed)
- `cmd@vel` (special char not allowed)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Many students write topic names like `cmd-vel` (kebab-case) — this is **invalid** in ROS. Use `cmd_vel` (snake_case) instead. This single mistake causes the DDS discovery layer to silently reject the topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Graph Resource Name

A complete graph resource name has the following **structural anatomy**:

$$\text{Name} \;=\; \underbrace{\text{prefix}}_{\text{global / or private }\sim \text{ or empty}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{namespace\_path}}_{\text{zero or more segments separated by /}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{base\_name}}_{\text{the actual resource identifier}}$$

For example, in the name `/robot1/sensors/camera/image_raw`:
- **Prefix:** `/` (global)
- **Namespace path:** `robot1/sensors/camera`
- **Base name:** `image_raw`

## 2.2 Three Resolution Rules — The Heart of Module 1

When a node uses a name string, ROS resolves it through a deterministic algorithm:

### Rule 1: Private Name Resolution (Tilde Prefix)
$$\text{``}\sim x\text{''} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{``} \langle \text{node's namespace} \rangle / x \text{''}$$

If a node named `/bot/camera` references `~exposure`, it resolves to `/bot/camera/exposure`.

### Rule 2: Relative Name Resolution (No Prefix)
$$\text{``} x \text{''} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{``} \langle \text{node's namespace} \rangle / x \text{''}$$

This is **identical** to private resolution in effect, but the semantic intent is different: relative names are meant to be *shared* within a namespace, while private names (`~`) are intended to be *unique* to a single node.

### Rule 3: Global Name Resolution (Leading Slash)
$$\text{``}/x\text{''} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{``}/x\text{''} \quad (\text{no change, always absolute})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** A node inside namespace `/robot1` that publishes to `cmd_vel` will publish on `/robot1/cmd_vel`, **NOT** `/cmd_vel`. To reach the global topic, the publisher must use the **explicit** global name `/cmd_vel`.

## 2.3 Namespace Hierarchy (Tree View)

Namespaces form a strict tree structure. Below is a sample of a multi-robot ROS graph:

```
/                              ← Global (root) namespace
├── /turtlesim                 ← Sub-namespace
│   ├── /turtlesim/turtle1     ← Topic inside turtlesim
│   ├── /turtlesim/turtle2
│   └── /turtlesim/parameter_server
├── /robot1                    ← Sub-namespace for Robot 1
│   ├── /robot1/camera_node
│   ├── /robot1/lidar_node
│   └── /robot1/cmd_vel
└── /robot2                    ← Sub-namespace for Robot 2
    ├── /robot2/camera_node
    └── /robot2/cmd_vel
```

> [!NOTE]
> Two robots can each have their own `/camera_node` because they live in *different* namespaces (`/robot1` and `/robot2`). The same `base_name` is allowed in different namespaces.

## 2.4 Remapping — Complete Syntax Reference

### 2.4.1 Standard Resource Remapping (Single Underscore)

| Syntax | Effect |
|--------|--------|
| `_name:=new_name` | Remap any resource called `name` to `new_name` |
| `cmd_vel:=velocity_command` | Remap topic `cmd_vel` → `velocity_command` |
| `turtle1/cmd_vel:=cmd_vel` | Remap the **sub-topic** inside a namespace |
| `/absolute_topic:=new_topic` | Remap a global topic |

### 2.4.2 Node-Control Remapping (Double Underscore)

These are special **reserved** keys that affect the *node itself*, not its resources:

| Reserved Key | Purpose | Example |
|--------------|---------|---------|
| `__name` | Override the node's own name | `__name:=my_turtle` |
| `__ns` | Override the node's namespace | `__ns:=my_namespace` |
| `__log` | Override the log file location | `__log:=~/my_logs` |
| `__ip` | Override the publisher IP | `__ip:=192.168.1.10` |
| `__hostname` | Override advertised hostname | `__hostname:=robot1.local` |
| `__master` | Override the ROS master URI | `__master:=http://...` |
| `__params` | Override parameter file path | `__params:=my_config.yaml` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction:** Single underscore `_` = remap a *resource*. Double underscore `__` = control the *node itself*. Mixing them up is a common KTU exam error.

## 2.5 Remapping Resolution Order

The remapping engine applies rules in a **strict priority order**:

1. **Command-line remappings** (highest priority — passed to `ros2 run` or `ros2 launch`).
2. **Launch-file remappings** (override defaults declared in code).
3. **Hard-coded names** in source code (lowest priority — used as defaults if no remap exists).

If multiple remap rules target the same name, **the most specific match wins** (a longer namespace-prefixed match beats a shorter one).

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Rule | Mnemonic |
|---------|------|----------|
| Leading `/` | **Global** — ignores all namespaces | "Slash = Stop pushing the namespace" |
| Leading `~` | **Private** — replaced by the node's namespace | "Tilde = This node's home" |
| No prefix | **Relative** — appended to current namespace | "No symbol = inherit current home" |
| `_x:=y` | Remap resource `x` to `y` | "Single underscore = single resource" |
| `__x:=y` | Control the node's special key `x` | "Double underscore = double importance" |
| Valid char set | `[a-zA-Z/~][a-zA-Z0-9_/]*` | "Alpha first, then alpha-num-underscore-slash" |
| Invalid char | Hyphen, space, `@`, `#`, `$`, `!` | "Kebab-case is not allowed" |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

In real robotics deployments, namespaces and remapping are **mission-critical**:

- **Multi-robot systems:** Two identical TurtleBots are launched in `/tb3_0` and `/tb3_1`. Their identical source code works in both because namespacing isolates them.
- **Hardware abstraction:** A simulator (`/sim/scan`) and a real LIDAR (`/lidar/scan`) are interchanged via a single remap line — no code change required.
- **Testing & CI:** Remapping lets test harnesses redirect `cmd_vel` to a mock recorder during automated tests.
- **Multi-master bridging:** Global names (`/foo`) cross namespace boundaries during bridge operations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section formalizes the **name resolution algorithm** that ROS applies internally. The derivation is exhaustive and follows KTU Module 1 syllabus depth.

## 3.1 Formal Problem Statement

> **Given:** A node `N` launched in namespace $\text{ns}(N)$, referencing a graph resource using the string $S$.
> **Find:** The fully resolved absolute name $S^*$ that the DDS layer will use for discovery.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Resolution Algorithm

Let $S$ be the input name string. The algorithm proceeds as follows:

### Step 1: Check for Remapping Rules

Before any resolution, ROS scans all active remap rules. Let $\mathcal{R}$ be the set of remap pairs $(S_i \rightarrow T_i)$ passed via CLI or launch file.

$$
S' = \begin{cases}
T_k & \text{if } \exists \, k \text{ such that } S = S_k \text{ in } \mathcal{R} \\
S & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Explanation:** The remap lookup is performed first because the user may have replaced the name entirely (e.g., the source code uses `cmd_vel` but CLI remaps it to `velocity_command`).

### Step 2: Check for Private Name (Tilde Prefix)

$$
\text{If } S'[0] = \text{`}\sim\text{'} \text{ then} \quad S' \;\leftarrow\; \text{``}\text{''} \;+\; \text{ns}(N) \;+\; \text{``}\!/\text{''} \;+\; S'[1:]
$$

**Explanation:** Strip the leading `~` and prepend the node's namespace followed by a `/`.

### Step 3: Check for Global Name (Leading Slash)

$$
\text{If } S'[0] = \text{``}\!/\text{''} \text{ then} \quad S^* = S' \quad \text{(return immediately)}
$$

**Explanation:** Global names are absolute; no further resolution is needed.

### Step 4: Apply Relative Name Resolution

$$
\text{If } \text{ns}(N) = \text{``}\!/\text{''} \text{ (root):} \quad S^* = \text{``}\!/\text{''} + S'
$$

$$
\text{Otherwise:} \quad S^* = \text{``}\!/\text{''} + \text{ns}(N) + \text{``}\!/\text{''} + S'
$$

**Explanation:** Prepend the node's namespace to the relative name. The leading `/` is added to make the result an absolute name.

### Step 5: Normalize Multiple Slashes (Optional Cleanup)

$$
S^* \;\leftarrow\; \text{NormalizeSlashes}(S^*)
$$

ROS collapses consecutive `/` into a single `/` and removes trailing `/` (except for the root `/`).

## 3.3 Worked Example A — Private Name

**Setup:** Node `N` is launched with `__ns:=/robot1`. Inside its source code, it advertises a service using the string `"~get_status"`.

**Step-by-step trace:**

| Step | Operation | Resulting String |
|------|-----------|------------------|
| Input | Raw string in code | `~get_status` |
| Step 1 | No remap matches | `~get_status` |
| Step 2 | Tilde detected — strip and prepend `ns(N)="robot1"` | `robot1/get_status` |
| Step 3 | No leading slash, so not global | `robot1/get_status` |
| Step 4 | Add leading `/` | `/robot1/get_status` |
| Step 5 | Normalize | `/robot1/get_status` |

**Final resolved service name:** `/robot1/get_status`

## 3.4 Worked Example B — Global Name with Remapping

**Setup:** Node code uses `"scan"`. Launched with remap: `scan:=laser_scan`. Node namespace is `/robot1/sensors`.

| Step | Operation | Resulting String |
|------|-----------|------------------|
| Input | Raw string in code | `scan` |
| Step 1 | Remap rule `scan := laser_scan` found → apply | `laser_scan` |
| Step 2 | No tilde | `laser_scan` |
| Step 3 | No leading slash | `laser_scan` |
| Step 4 | Prepend `/robot1/sensors` | `/robot1/sensors/laser_scan` |
| Step 5 | Normalize | `/robot1/sensors/laser_scan` |

## 3.5 Worked Example C — Global Name (Bypasses Namespace)

**Setup:** Node `N` in namespace `/robot1` uses the string `"/clock"`.

| Step | Operation | Resulting String |
|------|-----------|------------------|
| Input | Raw string in code | `/clock` |
| Step 1 | No remap matches | `/clock` |
| Step 2 | Leading `/`, not tilde | `/clock` |
| Step 3 | **Global — return immediately** | `/clock` |
| Step 4 | (Skipped) | — |
| Step 5 | (Skipped) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice:** Even though the node is in `/robot1`, the global name `/clock` resolves to `/clock` — **NOT** `/robot1/clock`. This is the *only* way to "escape" a namespace in ROS.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Resolution Outcomes

The following 3×3 matrix exhaustively covers all combinations of **name-type** × **node-namespace**:

| Name Type in Code | Node ns = `/` | Node ns = `/robot1` | Node ns = `/a/b/c` |
|-------------------|---------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| `"topic"` (relative) | `/topic` | `/robot1/topic` | `/a/b/c/topic` |
| `"~topic"` (private) | `/topic` | `/robot1/topic` | `/a/b/c/topic` |
| `"/topic"` (global) | `/topic` | `/topic` | `/topic` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation from the matrix:** Relative and private names have **identical** resolution behavior when the node has a namespace. The difference is purely *semantic* (intent of the programmer), not mechanical.

## 3.7 Multiple-Resource Remap Trace

**Setup:** A node subscribes to three topics: `"front_laser"`, `"rear_laser"`, `"/diagnostics"`. CLI: `front_laser:=scan rear_laser:=/sensors/rear __ns:=/tb3`.

| Step | Input | After Remap | After Resolution |
|------|-------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | `front_laser` | `scan` | `/tb3/scan` |
| 2 | `rear_laser` | `/sensors/rear` | `/sensors/rear` (global) |
| 3 | `/diagnostics` | `/diagnostics` | `/diagnostics` (global) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Namespace Tree of a Multi-Robot System

This diagram shows how namespaces partition the global ROS graph.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootN["GLOBAL NS /"]
    simN["turtlesim"]
    rob1N["robot1"]
    rob2N["robot2"]
    
    t1N["turtlesim turtle1"]
    t2N["turtlesim turtle2"]
    r1camN["robot1 camera node"]
    r1lidarN["robot1 lidar node"]
    r1velN["robot1 cmd vel topic"]
    r2camN["robot2 camera node"]
    r2velN["robot2 cmd vel topic"]
    
    rootN --> simN
    rootN --> rob1N
    rootN --> rob2N
    
    simN --> t1N
    simN --> t2N
    rob1N --> r1camN
    rob1N --> r1lidarN
    rob1N --> r1velN
    rob2N --> r2camN
    rob2N --> r2velN
```

> [!NOTE]
> The tree is **strictly hierarchical**. The same *base name* (`camera_node`, `cmd_vel`) appears multiple times because each lives in a **separate namespace branch**. The DDS layer treats them as completely independent resources.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Name Resolution Flowchart

This flow-chart implements the algorithm derived in Section 3.2.

```mermaid
graph TD
    startS["START: Name string S"]
    remapS["Step 1: Apply Remap Rules"]
    remapCheck{"Remap match found?"}
    remapYes["Replace S with new name"]
    remapNo["Keep S unchanged"]
    tildeS["Step 2: Check leading tilde"]
    tildeCheck{"Starts with tilde?"}
    tildeYes["Strip tilde, prepend node namespace"]
    tildeNo["Continue"]
    slashS["Step 3: Check leading slash"]
    slashCheck{"Starts with slash?"}
    slashYes["Return S as global immediately"]
    slashNo["Step 4: Relative resolution"]
    relYes["Prepend node namespace and add leading slash"]
    finalS["FINAL: Resolved absolute name S*"]
    
    startS --> remapS
    remapS --> remapCheck
    remapCheck -- Yes --> remapYes
    remapCheck -- No --> remapNo
    remapYes --> tildeS
    remapNo --> tildeS
    tildeS --> tildeCheck
    tildeCheck -- Yes --> tildeYes
    tildeCheck -- No --> slashS
    tildeYes --> slashS
    slashS --> slashCheck
    slashCheck -- Yes --> slashYes
    slashCheck -- No --> relYes
    slashYes --> finalS
    relYes --> finalS
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Remapping Block Architecture

This diagram shows how remapping sits *between* the source code and the DDS discovery layer.

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA["Source Code with hardcoded name"]
    cliA["Command Line Remap Rules"]
    launchA["Launch File Remap Rules"]
    engineA["ROS Remapping Engine"]
    resolveA["Name Resolution Algorithm"]
    ddsA["DDS Discovery Layer"]
    finalA["Registered Graph Resource"]
    
    srcA --> engineA
    cliA --> engineA
    launchA --> engineA
    engineA --> resolveA
    resolveA --> ddsA
    ddsA --> finalA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Remapping Engine** sits *before* the DDS layer. This is why remapping can redirect a topic at runtime without restarting or recompiling the node.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Effect of a Remap on Topic Wiring

This diagram shows how a remap changes the actual subscriber-publisher connection.

```mermaid
graph LR
    pubA["Publisher Node Code uses name cmd_vel"]
    remapCmd["Remap cmd vel to velocity command"]
    resolvedPub["Resolved Publisher Topic /robot1/velocity_command"]
    
    subA["Subscriber Node Code uses name cmd_vel"]
    remapCmd2["No remap applied to subscriber"]
    resolvedSub["Resolved Subscriber Topic /robot1/cmd_vel"]
    
    mismatch["CONNECTION FAILS: topics do not match"]
    
    pubA --> remapCmd
    remapCmd --> resolvedPub
    subA --> remapCmd2
    remapCmd2 --> resolvedSub
    resolvedPub -.-> mismatch
    resolvedSub -.-> mismatch
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall (KTU 2024):** If a publisher is remapped but its subscriber is not (or vice-versa), the DDS layer cannot perform discovery and the two nodes **silently fail to connect**. This is the single most common debugging issue in ROS namespace/remap questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between global, relative, and private names in ROS 2 with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Name Type | Syntax | Resolution Rule | Example (in ns `/robot1`) |
|-----------|--------|-----------------|---------------------------|
| **Global** | Begins with `/` | Absolute; bypasses current namespace | `/cmd_vel` → `/cmd_vel` |
| **Relative** | No prefix | Prepended with current namespace | `cmd_vel` → `/robot1/cmd_vel` |
| **Private** | Begins with `~` | Tilde replaced by node's own namespace | `~status` → `/robot1/nodeX/status` |

*(+1 mark per correct row, +0 for full-mark diagram or extra example)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**Explain the difference between the remap syntax `_name:=new_name` and `__name:=new_name`.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **`_name:=new_name`** (single underscore) — Remaps a *graph resource* (topic, service, parameter) named `name` to `new_name`. Example: `_cmd_vel:=velocity_command`. (1.5 marks)
