The following Mermaid block diagram shows the functional interaction between a Publisher Node, the ROS Master, and two Subscriber Nodes, including the message wire format. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters, and labels contain no markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["PUBLISHER NODE P STARTED"] --> A2["CALL ADVERTISE TOPIC CMD VEL GEOMETRY MSGS TWIST Q 10"]
    A2 --> A3["MASTER REGISTERS PUBLISHER IN TOPIC REGISTRY"]
    A3 --> A4["SUBSCRIBER S1 ADVERTISES MATCHING TOPIC"]
    A3 --> A5["SUBSCRIBER S2 ADVERTISES MATCHING TOPIC"]
    A4 --> A6["MASTER NOTIFIES P WITH SUBSCRIBER S1 URI"]
    A5 --> A7["MASTER NOTIFIES P WITH SUBSCRIBER S2 URI"]
    A6 --> A8["P CONNECTS TO S1 VIA TCPROS HANDSHAKE"]
    A7 --> A9["P CONNECTS TO S2 VIA TCPROS HANDSHAKE"]
    A8 --> A10["MAIN LOOP FILLS MSG AND CALLS PUBLISH MSG"]
    A9 --> A10
    A10 --> A11["SERIALIZE MSG TO CDR BINARY BUFFER"]
    A11 --> A12["WRITE BUFFER TO SOCKET PER SUBSCRIBER"]
    A12 --> A13["S1 DESERIALIZES AND CALLS CALLBACK"]
    A12 --> A14["S2 DESERIALIZES AND CALLS CALLBACK"]
    A13 --> A15["SUBSCRIBER PROCESSES DATA"]
    A14 --> A15
```

**How to read this diagram:** Boxes prefixed with `A` describe the *advertisement / connection phase* (steps A1 through A9). The hub step A10 is the per-iteration publish call inside the publisher's main loop. Steps A11–A12 are serialization and socket I/O. Steps A13–A15 show the symmetric actions on the subscriber side.

---

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram: Single Message Publish Round-Trip

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PubNode as PUBLISHER NODE
    participant Master as ROS MASTER
    participant SubNode as SUBSCRIBER NODE
    participant Socket as TCPROS SOCKET

    PubNode->>Master: REGISTER PUBLISHER CMD VEL TWIST
    Master-->>PubNode: ACK WITH PUBLISHER URI
    SubNode->>Master: SUBSCRIBE CMD VEL
    Master->>PubNode: NOTIFY NEW SUBSCRIBER URI
    Master->>SubNode: REPLY WITH PUBLISHER URI
    PubNode->>Socket: CONNECT TO SUBSCRIBER
    Socket->>SubNode: TCPROS HANDSHAKE HEADER
    SubNode-->>Socket: HANDSHAKE ACK
    loop MAIN LOOP AT F HZ
        PubNode->>PubNode: BUILD MESSAGE OBJECT
        PubNode->>Socket: SERIALIZE AND WRITE
        Socket->>SubNode: DELIVER BYTES
        SubNode->>SubNode: DESERIALIZE AND DISPATCH CALLBACK
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Reference Skeleton: Minimal ROS 1 Publisher (C++)

> [!NOTE]
> The following is a *minimal, syntactically correct* C++ publisher using `roscpp` — included only to anchor the abstract concepts above in canonical ROS practice. It is not a Python algorithm and is appropriate for this CS-engineering course.

```cpp
#include "ros/ros.h"
#include "std_msgs/String.h"

int main(int argc, char **argv) {
    ros::init(argc, argv, "talker_node");
    ros::NodeHandle nh;
    ros::Publisher pub = nh.advertise<std_msgs::String>("chatter", 10);
    ros::Rate loop_rate(10); // 10 Hz

    int count = 0;
    while (ros::ok()) {
        std_msgs::String msg;
        msg.data = "hello world " + std::to_string(count++);
        pub.publish(msg);
        ros::spinOnce();
        loop_rate.sleep();
    }
    return 0;
}
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Define a Publisher in ROS. How does it differ from a Subscriber?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *Publisher* is a node that **transmits messages on a named, typed topic** by first advertising the topic to the ROS Master and then calling `publish()` whenever new data is available. It is the *producer* in the publish/subscribe paradigm.

A *Subscriber* is the symmetric counterpart: it **declares interest in a topic** via `subscribe()`, registers a callback, and passively *receives* messages pushed by matching publishers.

| Aspect | Publisher | Subscriber |
|--------|-----------|------------|
| Direction of data | Outgoing | Incoming |
| Initiates contact with Master? | Yes (advertise) | Yes (subscribe) |
| Has a callback? | No | Yes |
| Data flow | Push (fire-and-forget) | Pull (callback-driven) |

(1 mark for definition of publisher, 1 mark for subscriber, 1 mark for difference table.)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`

**What is a "latched topic" in ROS? When would you use one?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *latched topic* is a topic for which the publisher instructs the Master / subscriber to **cache the most recently published message** so that any subscriber that connects *after* publication will still immediately receive that last message.

**Use cases:** Publishing slowly-changing but important state information such as the robot's TF transform, map data, or current joint calibration. New subscribers should not have to wait for the next publish cycle to obtain the current state.

(1 mark definition, 1 mark mechanism, 1 mark use case.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Part B questions feature an *internal choice*. Both alternatives are provided below; in the actual exam you answer **one**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete publishing pipeline** in ROS, starting from node initialization up to message delivery at a subscriber. Clearly label the **ROS Master**, **Publisher Node**, **Subscriber Node**, **Topic Name**, **Message Type**, and **Transport Layer**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A robot's wheel-odometry node publishes `nav_msgs/Odometry` messages. Each serialized message is **256 bytes** long. The node runs at **50 Hz**. Compute:
1. The publish period $T$.
2. The bandwidth $B$ in KB/s.
3. The bandwidth in **Mbps**.
4. Comment on whether this load is acceptable for a standard 100 Mbps Ethernet link in a multi-node robot. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Node startup (1 mark):** The publisher process launches, parses command-line args, calls `ros::init()`, and acquires a `NodeHandle`.
2. **Advertise call (1 mark):** `advertise<nav_msgs::Odometry>("/odom", 10)` registers the intent to publish on topic `/odom` with queue size 10.
3. **Master registration (1 mark):** The Master adds the publisher to its topic→publishers map keyed by topic name and message type.
4. **Subscriber handshake (1 mark):** When a subscriber advertises the same topic/type, the Master notifies both endpoints with each other's URI.
5. **Transport setup (1 mark):** A TCPROS connection (3-way handshake + connection header) is opened per subscriber.
6. **Main loop publish (1 mark):** In the loop, the publisher constructs the message, calls `pub.publish(msg)`; the roscpp middleware serializes the message to a binary buffer.
7. **Delivery (1 mark):** The bytes are written to each subscriber's socket. Each subscriber deserializes and dispatches to its callback.

A Mermaid block diagram of the same flow is given in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $S = 256$ bytes, $f = 50$ Hz.

**1. Publish Period (1.5 marks):**

$$
T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{50} = 0.02\ \text{s} = 20\ \text{ms}
$$

**2. Bandwidth in bytes/sec (2 marks):**

$$
B = S \cdot f = 256 \times 50 = 12{,}800\ \text{bytes/s}
$$

Convert to KB/s (1 KB = 1024 bytes):

$$
B = \frac{12{,}800}{1024} = 12.5\ \text{KB/s}
$$

**3. Bandwidth in Mbps (1.5 marks):**

$$
B = 12{,}800\ \text{bytes/s} \times 8\ \text{bits/byte} = 102{,}400\ \text{bps} = 0.1024\ \text{Mbps}
$$

**4. Comment (2 marks):**

A load of **0.1024 Mbps** is roughly **0.1 % of a 100 Mbps link's capacity**. Even if **20** such nodes run concurrently, the total is only ~2 Mbps — completely acceptable. Odometry messages are tiny and well-suited to high-frequency publication over standard Ethernet.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Topic-based publishing** and **Service-based RPC** in ROS. Cover: communication pattern, synchronicity, Master involvement, suitability for continuous sensor streams, and coupling. (7 marks)

**(b)** A LiDAR node publishes at **20 Hz** to a navigation stack. The serialized message size is **$S = 12{,}000$ bytes**. The queue size is **5**, and the subscriber falls behind so that **2 messages are dropped every 100 publishes**. Compute the **drop ratio** $D$, the **effective throughput** $\eta$, and discuss one engineering remedy. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

| Property | Topic (Publisher) | Service |
|----------|-------------------|---------|
| Pattern (1) | Many-to-many, anonymous | One-to-one request/reply |
| Synchronicity (1) | Asynchronous | Synchronous |
| Master role (1) | Routes publisher↔subscriber URIs | Routes service client↔server URIs |
| Sensor stream suitability (2) | **Excellent** — natural for continuous data | **Poor** — designed for discrete queries |
| Coupling (2) | **Loose** — peers need not coexist in time | **Tight** — both endpoints must be alive for the call to succeed |

(Use the **Analytical Matrix** from Section 3.3 as the reference.)

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $f_p = 20$ Hz, $S = 12{,}000$ bytes, $q = 5$, $f_{\text{drop}} = 2$ per 100 publishes.

**1. Drop Ratio (2.5 marks):**

$$
D = \frac{2}{100} \times 100\% = 2\%
$$

**2. Effective Throughput (2.5 marks):**

$$
\eta = 100\% - D = 98\%
$$

Alternatively via the conservation law $\eta + D = 100\%$.

**3. Engineering Remedy (2 marks):**

Several valid remedies exist; any one is acceptable:
- **Reduce publish rate** to a level the subscriber can sustain.
- **Increase queue size** so transient bursts are absorbed.
- **Use ROS 2 QoS with `BEST_EFFORT`** reliability if missing the latest message is acceptable.
- **Optimize the subscriber callback** to process messages faster (e.g., move heavy work to a separate thread).
- **Use `message_filters` or `approximate_sync`** to coalesce multiple sensor inputs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up `publish()` with `advertise()`.** Many students write a publisher without calling `advertise()` first and lose 2 marks outright. The advertise call is what registers the publisher with the Master.
> 2. **Forgetting to call `ros::spinOnce()` or `ros::spin()`.** Without it, subscriber callbacks (and shutdown signals) will never be processed. Examiners explicitly check for this in any code-based question.
> 3. **Unit confusion in bandwidth problems.** Converting KB/s ↔ Mbps is the most common error. Remember: $1$ byte $= 8$ bits, and $1$ MB $= 1024 \times 1024$ bytes (or use $1000$ if the question specifies SI).
> 4. **Confusing "topic" with "service".** Topics are *asynchronous and stream-oriented*; services are *synchronous and request/reply*. Examiners will deduct marks if you conflate them.
> 5. **In Markdown, avoid raw `\vert` in tables** — use $\vert$ or $\mid$ as instructed to prevent table-rendering failures in the answer sheet.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Publishing to a Topic**

- A **Publisher** is a node that **advertises** a topic and **publishes** typed messages to it.
- Topics are **named buses** that implement **many-to-many, asynchronous, anonymous** communication.
- The **ROS Master** (ROS 1) or **DDS Discovery** (ROS 2) acts as the name service matching publishers to subscribers.
- Publishing requires **two API calls**: `advertise()` (once) and `publish()` (per message).
- **Publish rate** $f$ (Hz) and **period** $T$ (s) are reciprocals: $T = 1/f$.
- **Bandwidth** consumed by a single publisher: $B = S \cdot f$, with $S$ in bytes.
- **Queue size** $q$ bounds the outgoing buffer; overflow causes message drops.
- **Latched topics** cache the last message for late subscribers — useful for slowly-changing state.
- **Quality of Service (QoS)** in ROS 2 governs reliability, durability, deadline, and lifespan policies.
- Topics are **decoupled in time and space**; subscribers and publishers do not need to coexist to communicate (with latching).
- **Serialization** (CDR / custom binary) is what makes C++ and Python nodes interoperable.
- Common message types: `std_msgs/String`, `geometry_msgs/Twist`, `sensor_msgs/LaserScan`, `sensor_msgs/Image`, `nav_msgs/Odometry`.
- The `ros::Rate` object (roscpp) / `rclpy_rate` (rclpy) enforces loop frequency, preserving a steady publish rate.
- Network bandwidth, not CPU, is usually the bottleneck for **multi-camera, multi-LiDAR** systems — pre-compute and budget it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subscribing to a Topic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Subscribing to a Topic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **Robot Operating System (ROS)**, **Subscribing to a Topic** is the asynchronous, many-to-many inter-process communication mechanism by which a **ROS Node** registers its interest in receiving data of a specific **Message Type** published on a named **Topic**. The subscription is realized through a registered **callback function** that is automatically invoked by the ROS client library (`roscpp` / `rospy`) every time a new message arrives on that topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PBRAT504 - Module 1):**
> A subscriber is one half of the **Topic-based Publish/Subscribe messaging pattern**. Unlike the ROS Service (synchronous, 1-to-1, request/reply), a topic is **asynchronous**, **anonymous**, and **many-to-many** — many publishers and many subscribers can coexist on the same topic without knowing each other's identities.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Radio Station"

Imagine a **radio station** broadcasting on **107.5 FM**:

| ROS Concept | Radio Analogy | Plain English |
|---|---|---|
| **Topic** (`/turtle1/cmd_vel`) | The FM frequency (107.5) | The named "channel" data travels on |
| **Publisher** | The radio station's transmitter | The entity *pushing* data out |
| **Subscriber** | Your car radio tuned to 107.5 | The entity *listening* for data |
| **Message** | The song being broadcast | The actual payload of information |
| **Callback function** | The speaker playing the audio | What *runs* when a message arrives |
| **`roscore`** | The broadcasting tower / airwaves | The message bus connecting everyone |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a radio does not know *who* is listening, a ROS Publisher has **no knowledge** of its Subscribers. Conversely, a Subscriber does not know *who* is publishing. The **ROS Master** (`roscore`) facilitates the initial connection, but data flows peer-to-peer via **XMLRPC** and **TCPROS** protocols.

## 1.3 Why Subscription Matters in Robotics

A mobile robot, for example, has **dozens of sensors** (LIDAR, IMU, cameras, wheel encoders) that constantly produce data. Rather than each sensor node directly calling functions inside a motor-control node (tight coupling, brittle), each sensor simply **publishes** to a topic, and any node that needs the data — path planner, obstacle avoider, data logger, GUI visualizer — simply **subscribes**. This **decouples** the system, allowing components to be developed, replaced, and tested independently.

## 1.4 Core Terminology Callout

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Vocabulary for KTU Exams:**
> - **Node:** An executable process that uses ROS to communicate.
> - **Master:** The `roscore` service registry that enables nodes to find each other.
> - **Topic:** A named bus over which messages flow (e.g., `/camera/image_raw`).
> - **Message:** A strictly-typed data structure defined in a `.msg` file.
> - **Publisher / Subscriber:** The two endpoints of topic-based communication.
> - **Callback:** A function executed in response to an incoming message.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Step Subscription Lifecycle

Subscribing to a topic in ROS follows a strict, well-defined lifecycle. Understanding each step is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** in KTU ESE.

### Step 1 — Node Initialization
The process must first be registered as a ROS node. The `init()` function contacts the **ROS Master**, obtains the node's unique network address, and registers it. Without a registered node, subscriptions are **silently rejected**.

**Why it matters:** The Master acts as a **Name Service** (similar to DNS). A subscription request is meaningless without a node URI to deliver the message to.

### Step 2 — Subscription Registration
The `subscribe()` call performs three sub-actions:
1. Looks up the **topic name** in the Master's registry.
2. Advertises the subscriber's interest using the topic's **MD5-sum** to enforce strict message-type compatibility.
3. Negotiates a **TCP connection** (or UDP) with one or more publishers using the **TCPROS** protocol.

> [!NOTE]
> **Type Safety:** ROS uses an **MD5 checksum** of the `.msg` file. If a publisher and subscriber use the same message type name but different field definitions, ROS will refuse the connection and log an `md5sum mismatch` error. This prevents silent data corruption.

### Step 3 — Message Reception & Callback Dispatch
The ROS client library opens a **persistent socket** with the publisher. Data is pushed in a **queue**. Whenever the application calls `spin()` (or `spinOnce()` in C++), the library dequeues messages and invokes the user's **callback function** for each.

## 2.2 The Subscriber Object — Internal Data Flow

| Internal Component | Function | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Subscription Queue** | Buffers incoming messages | If `queue_size = 1` and callback is slow, old messages are **dropped** |
| **TCP Socket** | Carries serialized bytes from publisher | Uses **TCPROS** (default) or **UDPROS** (low-latency) |
| **Deserializer** | Converts bytes back into a Message object | Uses the message's MD5 + serialized format |
| **Callback Dispatcher** | Invokes the user-defined function | May be **single-threaded** or use a **callback queue** |
| **Spin Loop** | Pumps the dispatcher | Without `spin()`, callbacks **never execute** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Subscription Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table. KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions frequently ask for the **default values**, **units**, and **significance** of each parameter.

| Parameter | Typical C++ Name | Typical Python Name | Default Value | Purpose / Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Topic name | `topic` | `name` | (None) | The named channel to listen on |
| Message type | `msg_type` | `data_class` | (None) | Strictly-typed message class |
| Queue size | `queue_size` | `queue_size` | **C++: undefined (warns), Python: 1** | How many messages to buffer |
| Callback function | `callback` | `callback` | (None) | Function called on each message |
| TCP vs UDP | (compiled-in) | (compiled-in) | **TCP** | Reliability vs latency tradeoff |

## 2.4 Mathematical Model of Message Flow

Let us define the subscription system as a discrete-time queueing model — a favorite KTU **analytical** question type.

$$ \lambda = \text{Message arrival rate (messages/sec)} $$

$$ \mu = \text{Callback processing rate (messages/sec)} $$

$$ \rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} \quad \text{(Server utilization factor)} $$

**Three regimes:**

$$ \rho < 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Stable: queue drains, no data loss} $$

$$ \rho = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Critical: queue grows without bound in worst case} $$

$$ \rho > 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Unstable: messages dropped after queue fills} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** This is the same M/M/1 queueing model used in telecommunications, manufacturing, and CPU scheduling. In a 60 Hz camera pipeline ($\lambda = 60$, $\mu = 80$ Hz), $\rho = 0.75$ — comfortably stable. Drop it to $\mu = 50$ Hz and the system saturates, causing **dropped frames** in the visual SLAM pipeline.

## 2.5 Synchronous vs Asynchronous Spinning

ROS provides two paradigms for invoking callbacks:

1. **Single-threaded spinning:** All callbacks execute on the main thread, serialized. Simplest, easiest to reason about, but a slow callback **blocks** all others.
2. **Multi-threaded / Async spinning (`AsyncSpinner`, `MultiThreadedSpinner`):** Callbacks run on a pool of $N$ threads. Necessary for parallel sensor processing, but the programmer must add **mutexes** to protect shared state.

## 2.6 KTU Conceptual Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - A subscriber **cannot** know how many publishers exist on its topic.
> - If a publisher starts *after* a subscriber subscribes, ROS performs an automatic **"last-call" handshake** via the Master.
> - The topic name **must** start with `/` for an absolute name (otherwise it is relative to the node's namespace).
> - In **ROS 2**, the subscription object exposes a callback handle that can be **destroyed** to unsubscribe dynamically — ROS 1 lacks this graceful feature.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section builds a **complete, runnable subscriber** in both `rospy` (Python) and `roscpp` (C++), explicitly tracing every line to its theoretical purpose.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Subscriber in Python (`rospy`)

**Engineering problem:** Create a ROS node that listens to a turtle simulator's pose stream (`/turtle1/pose`) and prints the x-coordinate to the terminal in real time.

**Step-by-step construction:**

**Line 1 — Shebang and imports:**

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import rospy
from turtlesim.msg import Pose
```

*Line 1* (`#!/usr/bin/env python3`): The **shebang** declares the interpreter. ROS launch files rely on this to execute the file directly.

*Line 2* (`import rospy`): Loads the **Python ROS client library**. Without this, no ROS API calls are available.

*Line 3* (`from turtlesim.msg import Pose`): Imports the `Pose` **message class** generated from `Pose.msg`. This class is *strictly typed* — it has fields `x`, `y`, `theta`, `linear_velocity`, `angular_velocity`, each as `float32`.

> [!NOTE]
> The `Pose` class lives at `turtlesim/msg/Pose.msg`. When you run `catkin_make` (or `colcon build` in ROS 2), this `.msg` is compiled into Python/C++ source code and made importable.

**Line 2 — Define the callback function:**

```python
def pose_callback(msg):
    rospy.loginfo("Turtle x = %.2f", msg.x)
```

*Logic:* The function receives one argument — the deserialized message object. Each time a new `Pose` arrives, ROS automatically calls this function. We extract `msg.x` (a `float32`) and log it. The `%.2f` format specifier limits output to 2 decimal places — important in KTU exams for clean answers.

**Line 3 — Node initialization:**

```python
rospy.init_node('turtle_pose_subscriber', anonymous=True)
```

*Logic:* Registers the process with the ROS Master under the name `turtle_pose_subscriber`. The `anonymous=True` flag appends a random integer suffix (e.g., `_38291...`) so that **multiple instances of the same script can run simultaneously** without a name-collision crash. This is a common KTU viva question.

**Line 4 — The actual subscription registration:**

```python
sub = rospy.Subscriber('/turtle1/pose', Pose, pose_callback, queue_size=10)
```

*Logic breakdown:*

| Argument | Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `'/turtle1/pose'` | Absolute topic name. The leading `/` makes it global, not relative. |
| 2 | `Pose` | Message class. ROS computes its **MD5** to enforce type compatibility. |
| 3 | `pose_callback` | The function to invoke on each message. |
| 4 | `queue_size=10` | Buffer up to 10 messages. If the callback is slow, the 11th incoming message drops the oldest. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The line `sub = ...` is **non-blocking**. It returns immediately. The actual data reception happens asynchronously in the background, driven by the spin loop below.

**Line 5 — The spin (event loop):**

```python
rospy.spin()
```

*Logic:* This is the **heart** of every subscriber. It blocks the main thread indefinitely, polling the incoming-message queue and dispatching each message to the registered callback. **Without `rospy.spin()`, your callback will never execute**, even though the subscription is registered. This is the **#1 mistake** KTU students make.

**Full, runnable script — `pose_subscriber.py`:**

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import rospy
from turtlesim.msg import Pose

def pose_callback(msg):
    rospy.loginfo("Turtle x = %.2f, y = %.2f, theta = %.2f",
                  msg.x, msg.y, msg.theta)

def pose_subscriber():
    rospy.init_node('turtle_pose_subscriber', anonymous=True)
    sub = rospy.Subscriber('/turtle1/pose', Pose,
                           pose_callback, queue_size=10)
    rospy.spin()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    try:
        pose_subscriber()
    except rospy.ROSInterruptException:
        pass
```

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Subscriber in C++ (`roscpp`)

The C++ version is the **preferred** form for production robotics due to its deterministic memory layout and real-time suitability.

**Step-by-step construction — `pose_subscriber.cpp`:**

```cpp
#include <ros/ros.h>
#include <turtlesim/Pose.h>

void poseCallback(const turtlesim::Pose::ConstPtr& msg)
{
    ROS_INFO("Turtle x = %.2f, y = %.2f, theta = %.2f",
             msg->x, msg->y, msg->theta);
}

int main(int argc, char** argv)
{
    ros::init(argc, argv, "turtle_pose_subscriber");
    ros::NodeHandle nh;

    ros::Subscriber sub = nh.subscribe("/turtle1/pose", 10, poseCallback);

    ros::spin();
    return 0;
}
```

**Line-by-line derivation:**

| Line | C++ Construct | Theoretical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `#include <ros/ros.h>` | ROS C++ client library header | Provides `ros::init`, `NodeHandle`, `Subscriber`, `spin` |
| `#include <turtlesim/Pose.h>` | Generated message header | Defines the `turtlesim::Pose` struct |
| `void poseCallback(const turtlesim::Pose::ConstPtr& msg)` | Callback signature | `ConstPtr` is a `boost::shared_ptr<const Pose>` — zero-copy, thread-safe |
| `ros::init(argc, argv, "...")` | Node initialization | Parses ROS-remapped arguments and contacts the Master |
| `ros::NodeHandle nh;` | The handle to the node | All publishers/subscribers are created *through* a `NodeHandle` |
| `nh.subscribe("/turtle1/pose", 10, poseCallback)` | Subscription registration | `10` is the queue size. **No anonymous flag** in C++ — handled differently via `ros::init_options::AnonymousName` |
| `ros::spin();` | Event loop | Blocks until `ros::shutdown()`. Internally calls `ros::ok()` in a tight loop |
| `ros::spinOnce()` | Non-blocking variant | Used in custom main loops when the node must perform other periodic work |

> [!NOTE]
> **ConstPtr pattern explained:** Passing the message as a `const`-pointer avoids copying the data into the callback's stack frame. For a 1 MB camera image at 30 Hz, this prevents a **30 MB/sec** memory-copy bottleneck — a critical concept in real-time robotics.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Hidden Algebra of the MD5 Handshake

When a subscriber registers, the following exchange occurs:

$$ \text{Subscriber} \xrightarrow{\text{TCP}} \text{Master} : \text{"register, topic=/turtle1/pose, type=Pose, md5=X"} $$

$$ \text{Master} \xrightarrow{\text{TCP}} \text{Subscriber} : \text{"publisher URI=Y, type=Pose, md5=X"} $$

$$ \text{Subscriber} \rightarrow \text{Publisher} : \text{"TCPROS connection request, md5=X"} $$

$$ \text{Publisher} \rightarrow \text{Subscriber} : \text{"md5 matches, sending data"} $$

If at **any step** the MD5 does not match, the connection is **refused** and an error is logged. This is why ROS is **type-safe** end-to-end.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Error Diagnosis (High-Yield for KTU)

| Symptom | Probable Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Callback never fires | `rospy.spin()` / `ros::spin()` missing | Add the spin call |
| `md5sum mismatch` | Mismatched `.msg` versions between packages | Re-build the workspace |
| `Connection refused` | Publisher not running | Start the publisher first |
| Messages dropped silently | `queue_size=1` and slow callback | Increase `queue_size` |
| Topic not visible in `rostopic list` | Namespace mismatch | Verify leading `/` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Publisher → Master → Subscriber Registration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Subscriber Node Init] --> B[rospy.init_node or ros::init]
    B --> C[rospy.Subscriber or nh.subscribe]
    C --> D{Topic Exists in Master?}
    D -- No --> E[Master Waits for Publisher]
    E --> F[Publisher Registers]
    F --> G[Master Notifies Subscriber of Publisher URI]
    D -- Yes --> G
    G --> H[TCPROS Handshake with MD5 Check]
    H --> I{MD5 Match?}
    I -- No --> J[Log md5sum Mismatch Error]
    I -- Yes --> K[Persistent TCP Socket Established]
    K --> L[Publisher Streams Messages]
    L --> M[Queue Buffers Messages]
    M --> N[spin Triggers Callback Dispatch]
    N --> O[User Callback Executes with Msg]
    O --> M
```

## 4.2 Many-to-Many Fan-out Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Camera Publisher Node] --> T((Topic: /camera/image_raw))
    P2[Simulated Camera Node] --> T
    T --> S1[Image Processor Node]
    T --> S2[Data Logger Node]
    T --> S3[RViz Visualizer]
    T --> S4[ML Perception Node]
    S5[Another Publisher] --> T2((Topic: /cmd_vel))
    T2 --> M1[Motor Controller Node]
    T2 --> M2[Simulator Bridge]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Subscriber

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Publisher_Process
        PData[Sensor Data Producer]
        PSerial[Message Serializer]
        PSock[TCPROS Socket Sender]
    end

    subgraph ROS_Master
        Reg[(Topic Registry and MD5 Lookup)]
    end

    subgraph Subscriber_Process
        RSock[TCPROS Socket Receiver]
        RQueue[Incoming Message Queue queue_size N]
        RDeser[Message Deserializer]
        RDisp[Callback Dispatcher]
        RCall[User Defined Callback Function]
        RSpin[rospy.spin or ros::spin Event Loop]
    end

    PData --> PSerial --> PSock
    PSock <-->|TCP| RSock
    Subscriber_Process -.register interest.-> Reg
    Publisher_Process -.register URI.-> Reg
    Reg -.URI lookup.-> Subscriber_Process
    RSock --> RQueue --> RDeser --> RDisp
    RSpin --> RDisp
    RDisp --> RCall
    RCall -.returns.-> RSpin
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The solid arrows are the **data path**. The dotted arrows are the **registration/lookup** path. The Master is involved only at registration time; **all real data flows peer-to-peer** between the publisher's sender socket and the subscriber's receiver socket. This minimizes Master load and latency.

## 4.4 Sequence Diagram of a Single Message

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Pub as Publisher Node
    participant Sub as Subscriber Node
    participant CB as Callback Function
    participant Q as Message Queue

    Pub->>Sub: TCPROS Header (topic, type, md5)
    Sub-->>Pub: ACK (md5 verified)
    Pub->>Sub: Serialized Message Bytes
    Sub->>Q: Enqueue Message
    Note over Sub: spin polls queue
    Sub->>CB: invoke callback(msg)
    CB-->>Sub: returns
    Sub->>Q: dequeue
    Pub->>Sub: Next Message Bytes
    Sub->>Q: Enqueue
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define a ROS **Subscriber**. List the **mandatory arguments** required to create a subscriber in `rospy`.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Subscriber** is a registered listener on a named topic that receives messages asynchronously through a user-defined callback function. The `rospy.Subscriber` constructor requires **four mandatory arguments**:

1. `name` — the topic name as a string (e.g., `'/turtle1/pose'`)
2. `data_class` — the imported message class (e.g., `Pose`)
3. `callback` — the function to be invoked on each message arrival
4. `queue_size` — integer buffer capacity (default 1 in Python, must be specified in C++)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget that `queue_size` is **required in C++** but **defaults to 1 in Python**. A common 0.5 mark deduction comes from conflating these defaults.

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Explain the role of `rospy.spin()` in a subscriber node. What happens if it is omitted?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

`rospy.spin()` is a blocking call that enters an **infinite event loop**, repeatedly polling the incoming message queue and dispatching each message to its corresponding callback. It is the **engine** that drives asynchronous message processing.

If **omitted**, the script reaches the end of `main()` and exits. The subscription is registered with the Master, but the callback is **never invoked** because the event loop is never entered. The node terminates immediately, producing no output.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Publisher-Subscriber architecture** in ROS. Compare it with the **Service-Client** model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Python program using `rospy` to subscribe to the topic `/turtle1/pose` (message type `turtlesim.msg.Pose`) and log the `x` and `y` coordinates to the terminal. Explain each section of your code. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer to Question A

#### Part (a) — Publisher-Subscriber Architecture (7 marks)

The **Publisher-Subscriber (Pub/Sub) model** in ROS is an **asynchronous, many-to-many** communication pattern suitable for continuous data streams such as sensor readings, robot states, and actuator commands.

**Key characteristics:**

1. **Topics as named buses:** A topic is a string identifier (e.g., `/camera/image_raw`) over which messages flow.
2. **Decoupling:** Publishers and subscribers are **anonymous** to each other. The ROS Master handles discovery.
3. **Asynchronous delivery:** Messages are pushed into a queue and processed when the subscriber is ready.
4. **Many-to-many:** Multiple publishers and multiple subscribers can co-exist on the same topic.
5. **Typed messages:** Each topic has a fixed `.msg` type enforced by MD5 checksums.

**Pub/Sub vs Service/Client — Comparative Analysis:**

| Feature | Pub/Sub (Topic) | Service/Client |
|---|---|---|
| Communication pattern | Asynchronous | Synchronous (request/reply) |
| Cardinality | Many-to-many | One-to-one |
| Use case | Continuous streams | Discrete computations |
| Latency | Buffered (queue) | Immediate blocking |
| Example | LIDAR scan data | Compute inverse kinematics |
| Callback | Triggered on receipt | Triggered on response |

#### Part (b) — Python Subscriber Program (7 marks)

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import rospy
from turtlesim.msg import Pose

def pose_callback(msg):
    rospy.loginfo("Position: x = %.2f, y = %.2f", msg.x, msg.y)

def turtle_pose_subscriber():
    # Step 1: Initialize the node
    rospy.init_node('turtle_pose_subscriber', anonymous=True)

    # Step 2: Register the subscription
    sub = rospy.Subscriber('/turtle1/pose', Pose,
                           pose_callback, queue_size=10)

    # Step 3: Enter the event loop
    rospy.spin()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    try:
        turtle_pose_subscriber()
    except rospy.ROSInterruptException:
        pass
```

**Code explanation:**

- **Line 3-4:** Import the ROS Python client and the auto-generated `Pose` message class.
- **Line 6-7:** The callback receives the deserialized message and logs `x`, `y` with 2-decimal precision.
- **Line 10:** `init_node` registers with the Master; `anonymous=True` allows multiple instances.
- **Line 13-14:** `Subscriber` registers interest on `/turtle1/pose`. Queue of 10 absorbs bursts.
- **Line 17:** `spin()` blocks indefinitely, dispatching messages to the callback.
- **Line 21-22:** Exception handler cleanly catches Ctrl-C interrupts.

### Question B — Choice 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Describe the **internal data flow** of a ROS subscriber from the TCP socket to the user callback. Include the role of the message queue and the spin loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ROS camera node publishes images at **30 Hz** to a topic subscribed by an image-processing callback that takes **40 ms** per image. Determine whether the system is stable. If unstable, suggest two engineering remedies. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer to Question B

#### Part (a) — Internal Data Flow (7 marks)

The flow consists of **five stages**:

1. **Socket Reception:** The subscriber maintains a **persistent TCP socket** to each publisher. Bytes arrive asynchronously at the OS kernel buffer.
2. **Deserialization:** A dedicated I/O thread reads the bytes, parses the **TCPROS header** (length-prefixed), and reconstructs the message object using the message class's deserializer.
3. **Queue Insertion:** The deserialized message is pushed to the back of a **bounded queue** of size `queue_size`. If the queue is full, the **oldest** message is dropped (FIFO with overwrite).
4. **Spin Loop Poll:** The main thread (or a `CallbackQueue` thread) running `rospy.spin()` / `ros::spin()` calls `callOne()` in a loop, which checks for available messages.
5. **Callback Dispatch:** When a message is available, the spin loop invokes the user-registered callback, passing a `const` pointer to the message.

This pipeline decouples **I/O** from **processing**, which is essential for real-time robotics.

#### Part (b) — Stability Analysis (7 marks)

**Given:**

$$ \lambda = 30 \text{ messages/sec (arrival rate)} $$

$$ \mu = \frac{1}{0.040 \text{ s}} = 25 \text{ messages/sec (processing rate)} $$

**Compute utilization factor:**

$$ \rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{30}{25} = 1.20 $$

**Conclusion:** Since $\rho = 1.20 > 1$, the system is **unstable**. The arrival rate exceeds the processing rate, so the queue grows without bound until `queue_size` is reached, after which messages are **dropped continuously**.

**Two engineering remedies:**

1. **Reduce arrival rate:** Downsample the camera to **20 Hz** (e.g., `ros::Rate r(20);` in publisher) so that $\lambda' = 20 < \mu = 25$, giving $\rho' = 0.80$ — stable.
2. **Increase processing rate:** Optimize the callback (e.g., use OpenCV with GPU acceleration, reduce image resolution) so that $\mu' = 40$ Hz, giving $\rho = 0.75$ — stable.
3. **Alternative remedy:** Use a **multi-threaded spinner** with a thread pool of $N = 2$ workers and `queue_size = 1`, processing the latest image and discarding stale frames (real-time perceptual systems often prefer freshness over completeness).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In the stability problem, students frequently compute $\rho$ incorrectly by inverting the formula. Write $\rho = \lambda / \mu$ explicitly. (**−1 mark**)
> - In the code questions, omitting the `try/except rospy.ROSInterruptException` block is a common deduction. (**−0.5 mark**)
> - Always include the **leading `/`** in absolute topic names. Relative names cause namespace bugs that are hard to debug. (**−0.5 mark**)
> - Do **not** confuse `spin()` with `spinOnce()`. The former blocks forever; the latter is non-blocking and used in custom main loops. (**−1 mark**)

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Subscription is asynchronous and many-to-many** — the foundation of ROS's modularity.
- The four **mandatory** subscriber arguments are: **topic name, message class, callback, queue size**.
- `rospy.spin()` / `ros::spin()` is **mandatory** — without it, no callback ever fires.
- The `queue_size` parameter governs **memory vs freshness** tradeoff; default in Python is 1, must be specified in C++.
- **MD5 handshake** enforces type-safety between publishers and subscribers.
- **TCPROS** is the default transport; UDPROS is the low-latency alternative.
- For **C++**, use `ConstPtr` (zero-copy `boost::shared_ptr`) for performance.
- **Multi-threaded spinning** (`AsyncSpinner`) is required when callbacks are slow or independent.
- Mathematical stability condition: **$\rho = \lambda / \mu < 1$** for lossless operation.
- In **ROS 2**, subscribers can be **dynamically destroyed** using the callback handle — a feature absent in ROS 1.
- Always end a `rospy` script with a `try/except rospy.ROSInterruptException` block for clean Ctrl-C handling.
- The `anonymous=True` flag is a clean way to spawn multiple instances of the same subscriber without name clashes.
- **Topic remapping** at launch time allows the same code to subscribe to different topics in different deployments.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Latched Topics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Latched Topics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Latched Topic** in the Robot Operating System (ROS) is a special category of publish–subscribe communication channel in which the **publisher node retains the most recently transmitted message** in the ROS Master (ROS 1) or Discovery Server / DDS layer (ROS 2). When any **new subscriber** joins the network, the master automatically pushes the cached message to that subscriber without requiring the publisher to re-transmit.

Mathematically, for a latched topic $\mathcal{T}$ with message buffer size $n = 1$:

$$
M_{\text{cached}}(t) = \lim_{t' \to t^{-}} P_{\mathcal{T}}(t')
$$

where $P_{\mathcal{T}}(t')$ is the last published payload before time $t$. A new subscriber $S_{\text{new}}$ connecting at time $t_c$ instantly receives $M_{\text{cached}}(t_c)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Latched topics are classified under *Asynchronous Communication Patterns* in Module 1, alongside regular topics, services, and actions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **digital notice board** in a college corridor:
- The Principal (**Publisher**) puts up a notice: *"Holiday on Friday."*
- A student (**Subscriber**) walks past **two hours later**.
- The notice board **still shows** the last notice, so the student sees it immediately.

> A **latched topic** behaves exactly like this notice board — the latest message stays *“stuck”* on the wall until a new one replaces it.

**Non-latched (regular) analogy:** A **radio broadcast** — if you tune in late, you miss what was said earlier.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Identifier in ROS 1:** The third boolean argument of `advertise()` — the *latch* flag.
> **Key Identifier in ROS 2:** Durability QoS policy set to `TRANSIENT_LOCAL`.

| Aspect | Regular Topic | Latched Topic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Message retention | None | Last message retained |
| Late subscriber receives history | No | Yes (only the last message) |
| Use case | Sensor streams | Static configuration data |
| Bandwidth impact | Continuous | One-shot on connect |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Working Principle

The behaviour of a latched topic follows a deterministic sequence:

1. **Advertise Phase** — The publisher declares a topic as latched by passing `latch = true` (ROS 1) or by setting `Durability = TRANSIENT_LOCAL` (ROS 2).
2. **Publication Phase** — The publisher sends a message; the ROS Master / DDS participant **stores** it in a single-slot cache.
3. **Subscription Phase** — A new subscriber registers interest. The Master detects this event and **automatically dispatches** the cached payload.
4. **Update Phase** — Any subsequent publish *overwrites* the previous cached value atomically.

## 2.2 The "Why" — Design Rationale

- **Eliminates race conditions** in startup sequencing (subscribers do not need to wait for the next publish cycle).
- **Reduces redundant publishing** for slow-changing data (e.g., URDF, static transforms).
- **Ensures deterministic initialization** — every node that needs a parameter or a map can be sure to receive it exactly once at startup.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** In mobile robotics, latched topics are the standard mechanism for publishing the robot's `tf_static`, the `map` server output, and the robot description (`robot_description`). These are slow-changing and absolutely critical at boot time.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | ROS 1 Representation | ROS 2 Representation | Boundary / Limit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Latch flag | `latch=true` (3rd arg) | `Durability QoS = TRANSIENT_LOCAL` | Must be paired with `RELIABLE` reliability |
| Cache depth | $n = 1$ message | $n = 1$ by default (depth=1) | Multi-message latching uses `keep_last(N)` |
| Late delivery | Guaranteed on connect | Guaranteed on discovery | $t_{\text{delivery}} \leq t_{\text{discovery}} + \epsilon$ |
| Publish frequency | Arbitrary | Arbitrary | $f_{\text{publish}} \to 0$ allowed (static data) |

### 2.3.1 Latency Equation for Late Subscribers

For a subscriber that joins at time $t_s$ and receives the cached message at time $t_r$:

$$
\Delta t_{\text{latch}} = t_r - t_s \quad \text{where} \quad \Delta t_{\text{latch}} \approx \mathcal{O}(\text{network RTT})
$$

For a regular topic, the subscriber would have to wait until the **next** publish:

$$
\Delta t_{\text{regular}} = t_{\text{next\_publish}} - t_s \quad \text{where} \quad \mathbb{E}[\Delta t_{\text{regular}}] = \frac{1}{2 f_{\text{publish}}}
$$

**Engineering utility:** Latched topics decouple the publisher's publish rate from the subscriber's startup time — a critical property in distributed robotic swarms and modular robot architectures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Implementation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 ROS 1 Implementation (C++)

The `latch` flag is the **third positional argument** in the `advertise()` template method of the `NodeHandle`.

```cpp
#include <ros/ros.h>
#include <std_msgs/String.h>

int main(int argc, char** argv) {
    ros::init(argc, argv, "latched_publisher_node");
    ros::NodeHandle nh;

    // Argument 1: topic name
    // Argument 2: queue size
    // Argument 3: latch flag  <-- KEY DIFFERENCE
    ros::Publisher pub = nh.advertise<std_msgs::String>(
        "/robot/description", 
        1,        // queue size
        true      // <-- LATCH ENABLED
    );

    std_msgs::String msg;
    msg.data = "URDF: 6-DOF Manipulator, payload 2kg";

    ros::Rate loop_rate(1); // 1 Hz - but the value is latched for late joiners
    while (ros::ok()) {
        pub.publish(msg);
        ros::spinOnce();
        loop_rate.sleep();
    }
    return 0;
}
```

**Step-by-step logic explanation:**
- **Line `nh.advertise<...>(...)`**: Registers a publisher on topic `/robot/description` with queue size 1 and latch enabled.
- **`true` flag**: Tells the ROS Master to **store** the last published message in its connection cache.
- **`loop_rate(1)`**: Publishes once per second, but late subscribers will still get the data immediately upon connection.

## 3.2 ROS 2 Implementation (C++) with QoS

In ROS 2, latching is achieved through the **Durability QoS policy** set to `TRANSIENT_LOCAL`.

```cpp
#include <rclcpp/rclcpp.hpp>
#include <std_msgs/msg/string.hpp>

class LatchedPublisher : public rclcpp::Node {
public:
    LatchedPublisher() : Node("latched_publisher_node") {
        rclcpp::QoS qos_profile(1);
        qos_profile.transient_local();  // <-- EQUIVALENT TO LATCH
        qos_profile.reliable();         // <-- MANDATORY PAIRING

        publisher_ = this->create_publisher<std_msgs::msg::String>(
            "/robot/description", qos_profile);

        auto msg = std_msgs::msg::String();
        msg.data = "URDF: 6-DOF Manipulator, payload 2kg";
        publisher_->publish(msg);
    }
private:
    rclcpp::Publisher<std_msgs::msg::String>::SharedPtr publisher_;
};

int main(int argc, char** argv) {
    rclcpp::init(argc, argv);
    rclcpp::spin(std::make_shared<LatchedPublisher>());
    rclcpp::shutdown();
    return 0;
}
```

## 3.3 ROS 2 Implementation (Python) with QoS

```python
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from rclpy.qos import QoSProfile, DurabilityPolicy, ReliabilityPolicy
from std_msgs.msg import String

class LatchedPublisher(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('latched_publisher_node')
        qos = QoSProfile(
            depth=1,
            durability=DurabilityPolicy.TRANSIENT_LOCAL,  # Latch equivalent
            reliability=ReliabilityPolicy.RELIABLE
        )
        self.publisher_ = self.create_publisher(String, '/robot/description', qos)
        msg = String()
        msg.data = "URDF: 6-DOF Manipulator, payload 2kg"
        self.publisher_.publish(msg)

def main(args=None):
    rclpy.init(args=args)
    rclcpp_node = LatchedPublisher()
    rclpy.spin(rclcpp_node)
    rclpy.shutdown()
```

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | ROS 1 (C++) | ROS 2 (C++/Python) | Analogy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Latch enable mechanism | `latch=true` boolean | `DurabilityPolicy.TRANSIENT_LOCAL` | Notice board pin |
| Required reliability | Inherited default | Must set `RELIABLE` | Postal registered mail |
| Caching layer | ROS Master | DDS Participant | Post office |
| Cache size | Exactly 1 (last msg) | Configurable `keep_last(N)` | Whiteboard capacity |
| Trigger for delivery | Subscriber XMLRPC registration | DDS discovery handshake | Visitor signing register |
| Failure mode | Master crash = loss of cache | DDS persistence if configured | Eraser falls on notice |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common board question asks to compare ROS 1 latch mechanism with ROS 2 QoS durability. The conceptual answer is that **latching is an application-level convenience in ROS 1, but a first-class QoS policy in ROS 2** (DDS-native).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Behavioural Flow of a Latched Topic

The diagram below illustrates the temporal sequence of message caching and late-subscriber delivery:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Publisher Node Boots] -->|advertise with latch=true| B[ROS Master / DDS]
    B -->|Registers latched topic| C[Master Cache: slot_size=1]
    A -->|publish msg_A: URDF v1.0| C
    C -->|Stores msg_A| D[Cache updated: msg_A]
    A -->|publish msg_B: URDF v1.1| C
    C -->|Overwrites with msg_B| E[Cache updated: msg_B]
    F[Subscriber Node Boots LATE] -->|subscribe /robot/description| B
    B -->|Detects new subscriber| E
    E -->|Auto-push msg_B immediately| F
    F -->|Receives latest data| G[Subscriber initialized]

    classDef publisher fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
    classDef master fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef cache fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef subscriber fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    class A publisher
    class B master
    class C,D,E cache
    class F,G subscriber
```

## 4.2 Regular Topic vs Latched Topic — Side-by-Side Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REG["Regular Topic - No Caching"]
        P1[Publisher] -->|stream of msgs| M1[Master]
        M1 -.->|missed msgs lost| S1[Late Subscriber]
    end

    subgraph LAT["Latched Topic - Cache Size 1"]
        P2[Publisher] -->|publishes msgs| M2[Master]
        M2 -->|stores last| K2[(Cache: msg_last)]
        K2 -->|auto-push on connect| S2[Late Subscriber]
    end

    classDef pubnode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085
    classDef subnode fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    classDef masnode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    classDef cachenode fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    class P1,P2 pubnode
    class S1,S2 subnode
    class M1,M2 masnode
    class K2 cachenode
```

## 4.3 QoS Policy Stack for Latching in ROS 2

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[QoS Profile for Latched Topic] --> B[Reliability: RELIABLE]
    A --> C[Durability: TRANSIENT_LOCAL]
    A --> D[History: keep_last depth=1]
    A --> E[Deadline: default]
    A --> F[Lifespan: default]

    B --> G[Guaranteed delivery to all matched subscribers]
    C --> H[Late joiners get stored samples]
    D --> I[Cache holds only the latest message]
    E --> J[No time-based expiry enforced]
    F --> K[Sample valid indefinitely]

    classDef root fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41
    classDef policy fill:#bee5eb,stroke:#0c5460
    classDef effect fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460
    class A root
    class B,C,D,E,F policy
    class G,H,I,J,K effect
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Define a latched topic in ROS. How is it implemented in ROS 1?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** A latched topic is a publish-subscribe channel in which the publisher's most recent message is retained by the ROS Master and automatically pushed to any new subscriber that connects.
- **(1 mark)** In ROS 1, it is enabled by passing `latch = true` as the third argument of `NodeHandle::advertise()`.
- **(1 mark)** Example signature: `ros::Publisher pub = nh.advertise<std_msgs::String>("/topic", 1, true);`

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *List any two real-world robotic scenarios where latched topics are preferred over regular topics.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Publishing the **robot description (URDF)** to `robot_description` — late-joining visualization nodes (e.g., RViz) must receive the kinematic model immediately.
- **(1 mark)** Broadcasting **static transforms** (`/tf_static`) for sensor mounts — the geometry is fixed and rarely changes.
- **(1 mark)** Publishing a **pre-built occupancy grid map** from the `map_server` — any navigation stack that starts later must get the current map on connect.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **internal mechanism of latched topics in ROS 1**. How does the XMLRPC-based Master handle message caching? Discuss the role of `connection_cb`. **— 7 marks**

**Model Answer:**

1. **(2 marks)** The publisher, upon calling `advertise(name, queue, latch=true)`, sends an XMLRPC call to the ROS Master. The Master registers a **publisher record** with a flag `latch = 1` in its internal `TopicManager` data structure.
2. **(2 marks)** Each `publish()` call causes the publisher's `Publication` object to invoke the Master's `registerPublisher` and `publish` APIs. The Master stores the **last serialized message bytes** in a `latch` field attached to the publisher's slot.
3. **(1 mark)** When a new subscriber calls `subscribe()`, the Master invokes the publisher's `connection_cb` (connection callback). This callback transmits the **cached serialized message** over the TCPROS handshake *before* normal streaming begins.
4. **(1 mark)** A subsequent `publish()` call updates the cache atomically; only the most recent message is kept (queue depth = 1).
5. **(1 mark)** Limitation: this mechanism is **ROS Master-dependent** — if the Master crashes, the cache is lost. This motivated the shift to DDS-native QoS in ROS 2.

**(b)** Write a **complete ROS 2 C++ publisher node** that advertises a latched `std_msgs/String` topic. Specify the exact QoS profile. Justify each QoS choice. **— 7 marks**

**Model Answer:**

```cpp
#include <rclcpp/rclcpp.hpp>
#include <std_msgs/msg/string.hpp>

int main(int argc, char** argv) {
    rclcpp::init(argc, argv);
    auto node = std::make_shared<rclcpp::Node>("latched_pub");
    
    rclcpp::QoS qos(1);
    qos.reliable();          // (i)
    qos.transient_local();   // (ii)
    
    auto pub = node->create_publisher<std_msgs::msg::String>(
        "/robot/status", qos);
    
    auto msg = std::msg::String();
    msg.data = "OPERATIONAL";
    pub->publish(msg);
    rclcpp::spin(node);
    rclcpp::shutdown();
}
```

**Justifications (1 mark each for the first 4 points; remaining 3 marks for explanation):**
- **(i) `reliable()`**: Mandated by the DDS specification for `TRANSIENT_LOCAL` durability — guarantees eventual delivery.
- **(ii) `transient_local()`**: Equivalent to ROS 1's latch — DDS participant stores the sample for late-joining subscribers.
- **Depth = 1**: Stores only the latest sample to minimize memory footprint.
- **No deadline/lifespan set**: Default values allow the message to live indefinitely until overwritten.
- **Practical effect**: Any `ros2 topic echo /robot/status` run *after* the publisher exits will still receive the last value.
- **Engineering note**: A subscriber MUST also request `TRANSIENT_LOCAL` durability to receive latched data — compatibility is a *request vs. offer* negotiation.
- **Failure case**: If the subscriber requests `VOLATILE` durability, DDS will not deliver the latched sample, even though the publisher has it cached.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Sample Paper]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **latched topics, services, and actions** in ROS. Construct a comparison matrix and identify the ideal use case for each. **— 7 marks**

**Model Answer — Comparison Matrix (5 marks for table, 2 marks for justification):**

| Dimension | Latched Topic | Service | Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Communication pattern | Pub–Sub (1-to-many, async) | RPC (1-to-1, sync) | Pub–Sub + feedback (async) |
| Response | None | Single response | Stream of feedback + result |
| Blocking | Non-blocking | Blocking on client | Non-blocking |
| Late-joiner support | Yes (last msg) | No | No |
| Pre-emption | N/A | N/A | Yes (cancel goal) |
| Ideal use case | Static map, URDF | Spawn robot, compute IK | Navigate to goal, grasp object |
| ROS 2 mechanism | QoS `TRANSIENT_LOCAL` | Service server/client | Action server/client + QoS |

**Justification (2 marks):** Latched topics excel at **one-shot data dissemination to many late subscribers**; services are best for **request–response computations** with a definitive answer; actions are mandatory for **long-running tasks that need progress feedback and pre-emption** (e.g., MoveIt's `ExecuteTrajectory`).

**(b)** Design a publisher–subscriber system for a **mobile robot fleet manager** that uses latched topics to broadcast the list of active robots. Provide the publisher code, subscriber code, and explain why latching is essential here. **— 7 marks**

**Model Answer:**

**System Design (2 marks):**
The fleet manager publishes a `RobotList` message every 10 seconds. Each robot's onboard monitor subscribes to this topic **at boot** and may restart at any time. Latching ensures that a robot that boots up *between* two publishes still gets the current fleet roster immediately.

**Publisher Code — ROS 2 Python (2 marks):**

```python
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from rclpy.qos import QoSProfile, DurabilityPolicy, ReliabilityPolicy
from my_msgs.msg import RobotList

class FleetPublisher(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('fleet_publisher')
        qos = QoSProfile(
            depth=1,
            durability=DurabilityPolicy.TRANSIENT_LOCAL,
            reliability=ReliabilityPolicy.RELIABLE
        )
        self.publisher_ = self.create_publisher(RobotList, '/fleet/active', qos)
        self.timer = self.create_timer(10.0, self.publish_fleet)
    
    def publish_fleet(self):
        msg = RobotList()
        msg.robots = ['robot_01', 'robot_02', 'robot_03']
        self.publisher_.publish(msg)
```

**Subscriber Code — ROS 2 Python (2 marks):**

```python
class FleetMonitor(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('fleet_monitor')
        qos = QoSProfile(
            depth=1,
            durability=DurabilityPolicy.TRANSIENT_LOCAL,
            reliability=ReliabilityPolicy.RELIABLE
        )
        self.subscription = self.create_subscription(
            RobotList, '/fleet/active', self.listener_callback, qos)
    
    def listener_callback(self, msg):
        self.get_logger().info(f'Active fleet: {msg.robots}')
```

**Why latching is essential (1 mark):**
Without latching, a robot that restarts after a publish event would have to wait up to 10 seconds for the next publish cycle, leaving it **temporarily blind** to its peers. Latching eliminates this gap, giving **deterministic startup behaviour** critical for safety in multi-robot systems.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to set `RELIABLE` reliability** when using `TRANSIENT_LOCAL` in ROS 2 — DDS rejects incompatible QoS combinations silently. **−2 marks**
> 2. **Confusing `latch` with `queue_size`** — students sometimes write `latch=10` thinking it stores 10 messages. The latch flag is **boolean**; queue size is a separate parameter. **−1 mark**
> 3. **Omitting the subscriber-side QoS** — even with a latched publisher, a `VOLATILE` subscriber will **not** receive cached samples. Always mention *request-offer negotiation*. **−2 marks**
> 4. **Mixing up latched topics with services** — a common conceptual error in viva. Latched topics are *push-based continuous*; services are *synchronous request-response*. **−1 mark**
> 5. **Forgetting to include `<rclcpp/qos.hpp>`** in ROS 2 C++ code — compile-time deduction error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Latched Topics**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Latched topics retain the **last published message** and auto-deliver it to **new subscribers** on connection.
- ⭐ **ROS 1 API:** Third argument of `advertise()` is the latch flag (`true` to enable).
- ⭐ **ROS 2 API:** Achieved via QoS policy `DurabilityPolicy.TRANSIENT_LOCAL` (must pair with `RELIABLE`).
- ⭐ **Cache depth:** By default, **1 message** (the most recent); can be increased via `keep_last(N)`.
- ⭐ **Subscribers must opt-in:** A `TRANSIENT_LOCAL` subscriber receives latched data; a `VOLATILE` subscriber does not.
- ⭐ **Common use cases:** `robot_description` (URDF), `map` (occupancy grid), `/tf_static`, fleet rosters, calibration parameters.
- ⭐ **Not suitable for:** High-frequency sensor data (LiDAR point clouds, camera streams) — these are *streaming* data and latching would be wasteful.
- ⭐ **Architectural shift:** ROS 1 = application-level latch (Master-mediated); ROS 2 = DDS-native QoS (decentralized).
- ⭐ **Failure mode:** ROS 1 Master crash = cache loss; ROS 2 DDS persistence = configurable durability.
- ⭐ **Memory cost:** Single-slot cache; negligible for typical robot applications (< few MB).
- ⭐ **Cross-language note:** Python and C++ publishers can both produce latched topics — the protocol is language-agnostic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defining Message Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Defining Message Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **Robot Operating System (ROS 2)**, a **Message Type** is a strictly typed, language-agnostic interface description that defines the **structure**, **field names**, and **data types** of the information exchanged between ROS 2 nodes. These descriptions are written in plain-text files with the extension **`.msg`**, which are processed by the ROS interface generator (`rosidl`) to produce equivalent type-safe data classes in multiple target languages (C++, Python, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from Syllabus Context):**
> A message type is a user-defined data contract that serializes structured data (using **CDR – Common Data Representation** by default) and is part of the **ROS Interface Definition Language (IDL)** family, alongside `.srv` (services) and `.action` (actions).

The three pillars of any ROS 2 message type are:
1. **Primitive Field Types** (e.g., `int32`, `float64`, `string`, `bool`)
2. **Structural Modifiers** (e.g., fixed-length or variable-length arrays)
3. **Header Inclusion** (optional `std_msgs/Header` for timestamping & coordinate frame)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Envelope" Intuition

Imagine a multinational courier company that delivers packages between offices:

| Courier Concept | ROS 2 Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Standardized shipping form | `.msg` file |
| Form fields (weight, dimensions, address) | Field declarations in `.msg` |
| Barcode/Tracking Number | Auto-generated hash / message MD5 |
| Sorting machines that read the form | `rosidl_generator` code generators |
| Actual physical package | Serialized CDR payload on the wire |

**Why is this important?** Just as a courier cannot deliver a package without knowing the sender's format, **ROS 2 nodes cannot communicate** without a pre-agreed message schema. The `.msg` file acts as a **machine-readable contract** that every node must respect — otherwise the build system rejects the code, ensuring type safety at compile time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** You do **not** write the message in C++ or Python first. You write a **declarative `.msg` file**, and the ROS 2 build system automatically generates the language bindings for you.

---

## 1.3 Physical & Standard Constants in Message Serialization

> [!NOTE]
> The following default constants govern how every message is transmitted:
> - **Default Serialization Format:** CDR (Common Data Representation, OMG Standard)
> - **Maximum Practical Field Size:** No hard KTU limit, but recommended **≤ 1 MB** to avoid DDS throughput issues
> - **Default Header Frame ID Length:** `string` (no fixed length — variable)
> - **Endianness:** Little-endian (matches x86/ARM architectures, OMG CDR compliant)

These defaults are non-negotiable across the ROS 2 ecosystem and apply to **every** user-defined message type.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a `.msg` File

A `.msg` file consists of **field declarations** written one per line. Each field uses the syntax:

```
<data_type> <field_name>
```

The structure of a message type can be formally expressed as a tuple:

$$M = \langle F_1, F_2, F_3, \dots, F_n \rangle$$

where each field $F_i$ is itself defined as:

$$F_i = \langle T_i, N_i, C_i \rangle$$

with:
- $T_i$ = **Data Type** (primitive, complex, or reference to another message)
- $N_i$ = **Field Name** (valid identifier)
- $C_i$ = **Constraints** (e.g., array length, default value)

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Type Cheat Sheet

### Table 2.1: Primitive Field Types (HIGH FREQUENCY in KTU exams)

| Category | Data Type | Size (Bytes) | Range / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Boolean | `bool` | 1 | `true` or `false` |
| Integer | `int8` | 1 | $-2^7$ to $2^7-1$ |
| Integer | `uint8` | 1 | $0$ to $2^8-1$ (also used for `byte`) |
| Integer | `int16` | 2 | $-2^{15}$ to $2^{15}-1$ |
| Integer | `uint16` | 2 | $0$ to $2^{16}-1$ |
| Integer | `int32` | 4 | $-2^{31}$ to $2^{31}-1$ |
| Integer | `uint32` | 4 | $0$ to $2^{32}-1$ |
| Integer | `int64` | 8 | $-2^{63}$ to $2^{63}-1$ |
| Integer | `uint64` | 8 | $0$ to $2^{64}-1$ |
| Float | `float32` | 4 | IEEE 754 single precision |
| Float | `float64` | 8 | IEEE 754 double precision |
| String | `string` | variable | UTF-8 encoded, up to **memory limit** |
| Time | `time` | 8 | Seconds + nanoseconds (sec, nanosec) |
| Duration | `duration` | 8 | Signed time interval |

### Table 2.2: Structural Modifiers

| Modifier | Syntax | Example | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fixed-length array | `type[N] name` | `int32[3] coords` | RGB pixel, XYZ vector |
| Variable-length array | `type[] name` | `int32[] samples` | LiDAR scan data |
| Bounded string | `string<=N name` | `string<=10 id` | Short identifiers |
| Constant | `type NAME=value` | `int32 MAX=100` | Compile-time constants |
| Nested message | `pkg/Type name` | `geometry_msgs/Pose pose` | Composite data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The syntax `type[]` declares an **unbounded** variable-length array, while `type[N]` declares a **fixed-length** array of exactly $N$ elements. Examiners frequently test this distinction.

---

## 2.3 Why Do We Need Message Types? (The "Why")

1. **Decoupling**: Publishers and subscribers don't need to share a header — they only need to share the `.msg` definition.
2. **Type Safety**: The compiler catches mismatches at build time, not runtime.
3. **Language Interoperability**: A C++ publisher can send to a Python subscriber seamlessly because both generated from the same `.msg`.
4. **Serialization Efficiency**: CDR encoding ensures compact, deterministic wire format.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of Message Types |
|---|---|
| Autonomous Vehicles | `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2` for LiDAR data streaming |
| Industrial Robotics | `control_msgs/JointTrajectoryControllerState` for motion feedback |
| Medical Robotics | Custom `surgical_msgs/ForceFeedback` for haptic feedback |
| Aerial Drones (UAV) | `mavros_msgs/State` for flight controller telemetry |
| Space Robotics (NASA) | `nav_msgs/Odometry` for planetary rover localization |

In each case, the **message type acts as the universal translator** across heterogeneous hardware, making it the **backbone of modular robotic systems**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Message Definition Workflow

The complete lifecycle of defining a message type in ROS 2 follows a **5-stage pipeline**. We derive each stage formally.

---

### **Stage 1: Package Selection**

Every `.msg` file must live inside a **ROS 2 package** (a `ament_cmake` or `ament_python` build unit). The package must declare runtime dependencies on the interface generator.

For a C++ package, the relevant `<buildtool_depend>` entries in `package.xml` are:

```xml
<buildtool_depend>rosidl_default_generators</buildtool_depend>
<exec_depend>rosidl_default_runtime</exec_depend>
<member_of_group>rosidl_interface_packages</member_of_group>
```

---

### **Stage 2: Creating the `msg` Directory**

Inside the package root, the build system looks for a fixed folder name:

$$\text{Package Root} \supseteq \text{\texttt{msg/}} \text{ directory}$$

**Conversion Logic (Textual):**
- The `ament_cmake` build system recursively scans the `msg/` folder at build time.
- Each `.msg` file found is treated as an interface input.
- Files with other extensions are ignored.

---

### **Stage 3: Authoring the `.msg` File**

Let us derive a **custom message type** for a simple temperature sensor node.

**File:** `msg/TemperatureReading.msg`

```idl
# Timestamp + coordinate frame
std_msgs/Header header

# Primary sensor value
float64 celsius

# Quality-of-service indicator
bool is_calibrated

# Fixed-length 3-axis accelerometer
float64[3] accelerometer_xyz

# Up to 5 diagnostic codes
string<=5[] error_codes
```

**Field-by-field analysis:**

| Line | Field | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | `header` | `std_msgs/Header` | Adds timestamp + frame_id |
| 5 | `celsius` | `float64` | Temperature in °C |
| 8 | `is_calibrated` | `bool` | Sensor health flag |
| 11 | `accelerometer_xyz` | `float64[3]` | 3-axis IMU data |
| 14 | `error_codes` | `string<=5[]` | Variable array, max 5 strings each ≤5 chars |

**Mathematical Representation of the Message:**

$$M_{\text{Temp}} = \langle H, T, B, A_{3}, E_{\le 5} \rangle$$

where:
- $H$ = Header (compound)
- $T \in \mathbb{R}$ (float64)
- $B \in \{0, 1\}$ (bool)
- $A_3 \in \mathbb{R}^3$ (fixed-length array)
- $E_{\le 5}$ = variable array of up to 5 bounded strings

---

### **Stage 4: Build Configuration (`CMakeLists.txt`)**

For C++ packages, the following must be added **before** the `ament_package()` call:

```cmake
find_package(rosidl_default_generators REQUIRED)

rosidl_generate_interfaces(${PROJECT_NAME}
  "msg/TemperatureReading.msg"
  DEPENDENCIES std_msgs
)
```

**Logic of Each Line:**

1. `find_package` → Locates the `rosidl` generator toolchain.
2. `rosidl_generate_interfaces` → Registers the `.msg` for code generation.
3. `DEPENDENCIES std_msgs` → Resolves the `Header` cross-reference.

---

### **Stage 5: Code Generation Output**

When you run `colcon build`, the generator emits language-specific bindings.

| Output Artifact | Location | Language |
|---|---|---|
| `temperature_reading.hpp` | `build/.../msg/` | C++ Header |
| `temperature_reading.cpp` | `build/.../msg/` | C++ Implementation |
| `temperature_reading.py` | `install/.../msg/` | Python Module |
| `temperature_reading.idl` | `build/.../rosidl_adapter/` | Intermediate IDL |

**Final In-Memory Structure (Conceptual):**

$$M_{\text{Temp}} \xrightarrow{\text{rosidl}} \text{Type-Safe Struct/Class}$$

This means: **at runtime, the message occupies**:

$$S_{\text{bytes}} = S_{\text{header}} + 8 + 1 + 24 + (\text{len}(E_1) + \dots + \text{len}(E_n))$$

where $S_{\text{header}} = 8 + \text{len}(\text{frame\_id})$ bytes.

---

## 3.2 Verification of Type Safety

Given two nodes with mismatched `.msg` files (e.g., one expects `int32` and the other sends `float32`), the **build system** halts with:

```
ERROR: ... types ... differ
```

This **compile-time guarantee** is the primary engineering advantage of defining message types explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Message Definition Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Developer Authors .msg File]) --> B[Place in msg/ Directory]
    B --> C[Update package.xml]
    B --> D[Update CMakeLists.txt]
    C --> E[colcon build Invocation]
    D --> E
    E --> F[rosidl_default_generators Scans Interface]
    F --> G[CDR IDL Generation]
    G --> H1[C++ Header File .hpp]
    G --> H2[Python Module .py]
    G --> H3[C Implementation .c]
    H1 --> I1[Compile with C++ Node]
    H2 --> I2[Import in Python Node]
    I1 --> J1[Publisher Node]
    I2 --> J2[Subscriber Node]
    J1 --> K([DDS Middleware Wire Transmission])
    J2 --> K
    K --> L([Type-Safe Topic Communication])

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture — Internal Structure of a `.msg` File

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 1
    block:msgfile["Custom Message: TemperatureReading.msg"]
        A1["Line 1: std_msgs/Header header (TIMESTAMP + FRAME)"]
        A2["Line 2: float64 celsius (PRIMARY SENSOR)"]
        A3["Line 3: bool is_calibrated (HEALTH FLAG)"]
        A4["Line 4: float64[3] accelerometer_xyz (FIXED ARRAY)"]
        A5["Line 5: string lessEqual 5 lessThan error_codes (BOUNDED VAR ARRAY)"]
    end

    block:codegen["Generated Language Bindings"]
        B1["C++ Struct: TemperatureReading { Header header; double celsius; bool is_calibrated; std::array lessDouble 3 greaterThan acc; ... }"]
        B2["Python Class: class TemperatureReading: self.header = Header() self.celsius = 0.0 self.is_calibrated = False self.accelerometer_xyz = [0.0, 0.0, 0.0] ..."]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `lessDouble 3 greaterThan` notation in the diagram is a visual artifact of the ASCII rendering — the actual generated C++ uses `std::array<double, 3>`.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence — Publisher/Subscriber Contract

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dev as Developer
    participant Build as colcon build
    participant Gen as rosidl_generator
    participant Pub as Publisher Node
    participant Sub as Subscriber Node
    participant DDS as DDS Middleware

    Dev->>Build: Define TemperatureReading.msg
    Build->>Gen: Invoke interface generator
    Gen-->>Pub: Generate TemperatureReading.hpp
    Gen-->>Sub: Generate temperature_reading.py
    Pub->>Pub: Instantiate msg object
    Pub->>DDS: Publish serialized CDR payload
    DDS->>Sub: Deliver to matching subscribers
    Sub->>Sub: Deserialize + populate fields
    Note over Pub,Sub: Type Safety Guaranteed by Shared .msg Contract
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**
**Define a message type in ROS 2. Mention the file extension used and the toolchain that processes it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **message type** in ROS 2 is a declarative interface definition that specifies the structure, field names, and data types of information exchanged between nodes.

- **File extension:** `.msg`
- **Toolchain:** `rosidl_default_generators` (part of the ROS 2 Interface Definition Language pipeline)
- **Default serialization:** CDR (Common Data Representation)

*(Award 1 mark each for the three points.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any six primitive field types supported by ROS 2 message definitions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. `bool`
2. `int32`
3. `float64`
4. `string`
5. `time`
6. `uint8`

*(Award 0.5 marks per correctly listed type, 6 × 0.5 = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **anatomy of a ROS 2 message definition file** with a suitable example. Clearly distinguish between **fixed-length** and **variable-length arrays** with syntax. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **complete workflow** of defining a custom message type — from `.msg` file creation to language binding generation. Include the role of `package.xml` and `CMakeLists.txt`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

The **anatomy** of a `.msg` file consists of:

1. **Field declarations** (one per line): `data_type field_name`
2. **Comments** prefixed with `#`
3. **Constants** using `type NAME=value`
4. **Cross-package references** using `pkg/Type name`
5. **Optional header** for timestamping

**Example: `msg/GPSFix.msg`**

```idl
# GPS sensor reading
std_msgs/Header header
float64 latitude
float64 longitude
float32 altitude
uint8 satellites_visible
int32[3] position_xyz
```

**Distinction Table:**

| Aspect | Fixed-length Array | Variable-length Array |
|---|---|---|
| Syntax | `int32[3] data` | `int32[] data` |
| Memory | Pre-allocated | Dynamically resized |
| Wire Size | Deterministic | Variable |
| Use Case | RGB pixels, rotation matrices | Point clouds, scan arrays |

*(Award 4 marks for anatomy + example, 3 marks for array distinction.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

The workflow consists of **5 stages**:

1. **Create package** using `ros2 pkg create --build-type ament_cmake my_robot_msgs`.
2. **Create `msg/` directory** and author the `.msg` file.
3. **Update `package.xml`** with `rosidl_default_generators` and `rosidl_default_runtime` dependencies.
4. **Update `CMakeLists.txt`** using `rosidl_generate_interfaces(${PROJECT_NAME} "msg/GPSFix.msg")`.
5. **Build with `colcon build`** — this triggers `rosidl` to produce:
   - C++ headers in `build/.../include/`
   - Python modules in `install/.../local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/`
