**Role of Configuration Files:**

| File | Role |
|---|---|
| `package.xml` | Declares interface generation dependencies |
| `CMakeLists.txt` | Invokes `rosidl_generate_interfaces` macro |

*(Award 1 mark per stage, 2 marks for the configuration table.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice – KTU Pattern]**

**(a)** What are the **rules and constraints** for naming fields in a `.msg` file? Explain with examples the difference between **nested message references** and **primitive types**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete `.msg` file for an **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)** sensor with at least 5 fields, including a header, fixed-length array, bounded string, and a constant. Explain the role of the `std_msgs/Header`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Naming Rules for Fields:**

1. Must start with a **lowercase letter or underscore**.
2. Can contain letters, digits, underscores.
3. Must be **unique** within the file.
4. **Reserved keywords** (e.g., `const`, `int`, `float`) cannot be used as field names.
5. Use **snake_case** convention (per ROS 2 style guide).

**Nested vs. Primitive Comparison:**

| Feature | Primitive Type | Nested Message |
|---|---|---|
| Example | `float64 temp` | `geometry_msgs/Point position` |
| Location | Built into ROS 2 | From another package |
| Dependency | None | Requires cross-package dependency |
| Complexity | Single value | Composite structure |

*(Award 3 marks for naming rules, 4 marks for the comparison.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**File: `msg/ImuReading.msg`**

```idl
# Standard timestamp and frame
std_msgs/Header header

# Orientation as a quaternion
float64[4] orientation_xyzw

# Angular velocity in rad/s
geometry_msgs/Vector3 angular_velocity

# Linear acceleration in m/s^2
geometry_msgs/Vector3 linear_acceleration

# Bounded device identifier
string<=20 device_id

# Constant: full-scale range in g
float32 FULL_SCALE_G = 16.0
```

**Role of `std_msgs/Header`:**

- Provides **standardized timestamping** (`uint32 sec`, `uint32 nanosec`).
- Includes a **coordinate frame ID** (`string frame_id`) for TF tree integration.
- Ensures **temporal synchronization** across multi-sensor systems.
- Acts as a **unifying metadata field** for all time-sensitive ROS 2 messages.

*(Award 4 marks for the file, 3 marks for Header explanation.)*

---

## ⚠️ 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in Message Type Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing fixed and variable arrays** — `int32[3]` ≠ `int32[]` (loses 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting `DEPENDENCIES`** in `rosidl_generate_interfaces` — build will fail
> 3. **Wrong file extension** — using `.h` or `.txt` instead of `.msg`
> 4. **Missing `std_msgs` dependency** when using `Header` — silent compile-time error
> 5. **Incorrect namespace separator** — using `::` (C++ syntax) instead of `/` (ROS IDL syntax)
> 6. **Not mentioning CDR serialization** in theory questions — loses 1 easy mark
> 7. **Mixing up `time` and `duration`** — `time` is an absolute instant; `duration` is a time interval

---

## ✅ 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- 📁 Message types are defined in **`.msg` files** inside the package's `msg/` directory.
- ⚙️ The **`rosidl` toolchain** converts `.msg` into language-specific bindings (C++, Python).
- 📊 Primitive types include `bool`, `int8/16/32/64`, `uint8/16/32/64`, `float32/64`, `string`, `time`, `duration`.
- 📐 **Fixed-length arrays** use `[N]`; **variable-length** use `[]`; **bounded** uses `<=N`.
- 🏷️ **`std_msgs/Header`** provides standardized `stamp` (sec + nanosec) and `frame_id`.
- 🔗 **Nested references** use `package_name/MessageType` syntax (e.g., `geometry_msgs/Pose`).
- 🛠️ **Build dependencies** must be declared in `package.xml` and `CMakeLists.txt`.
- 📡 **Default serialization** is **CDR** (Common Data Representation, OMG standard).
- 🚫 **Type mismatches** are caught at **compile time**, not runtime.
- 🧩 Message types are the **contracts** that make ROS 2's decentralized, multi-language architecture possible.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mixing Publishers and Subscribers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mixing Publishers and Subscribers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
In the **Robot Operating System (ROS)**, **Mixing Publishers and Subscribers** refers to the design pattern where a single ROS node simultaneously **subscribes** to one or more input topics (to receive messages via callback functions) and **publishes** to one or more output topics (to broadcast processed or transformed data). This hybrid node acts as a data-processing bridge in a distributed computational graph, transforming incoming sensor/state information into a new form for downstream consumers.

In C++ ROS (commonly using `roscpp`), this is achieved by instantiating both a `ros::Subscriber` and a `ros::Publisher` object from the same `ros::NodeHandle`, with the subscriber's callback function invoking the `publish()` method of the publisher object.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **coffee shop barista**:
- The barista **listens** (subscribes) to customer orders.
- The barista **shouts out** (publishes) "Order ready for table 5" once the drink is prepared.

The barista is neither just a listener nor just a shouter — they **mix both roles**. The customer order (input topic) triggers a process, and the announcement (output topic) is the result. In ROS, a "mixed" node behaves exactly like this barista: it waits for input, processes it, and broadcasts an output — all in a single, self-contained unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The "Mixing Publishers and Subscribers" pattern is a foundational building block of ROS architecture. It appears in nearly every robotics stack — from sensor fusion (e.g., combining IMU + wheel odometry → fused pose) to image processing pipelines (e.g., raw camera → edge-detected output).

### Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Node** | An executable process that uses ROS to communicate with other nodes |
| **Topic** | A named bus over which nodes exchange messages |
| **Publisher** | Object that sends messages on a topic |
| **Subscriber** | Object that receives messages via a callback |
| **Callback** | Function executed automatically when a message arrives |
| **NodeHandle** | The main object that initializes a node and creates pub/sub objects |

> [!NOTE]
> A node is **not** limited to one publisher or subscriber. It may publish to *N* topics and subscribe to *M* topics simultaneously, making it a powerful multi-purpose computational unit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Logic — Step by Step
A mixed publisher-subscriber node follows this structured lifecycle:

1. **Node Initialization**
   - `ros::init(argc, argv, "node_name");` is called in `main()`.
   - This registers the process with the ROS Master under a unique name.

2. **NodeHandle Creation**
   - `ros::NodeHandle nh;` is instantiated.
   - The handle is the *factory* from which publishers and subscribers are spawned.

3. **Publisher Registration**
   - `ros::Publisher pub = nh.advertise<std_msgs::String>("output_topic", 10);`
   - The first argument is the topic name; the second is the **queue size** (buffer length).

4. **Subscriber Registration**
   - `ros::Subscriber sub = nh.subscribe("input_topic", 10, callback_function);`
   - The third argument is the **callback function pointer** that gets invoked per message.

5. **Callback Execution (Message Arrival)**
   - ROS's internal callback queue dispatches incoming messages to the registered callback.
   - Inside the callback, the received message is processed, and `pub.publish(result)` is called.

6. **Spin Loop**
   - `ros::spin();` enters an infinite loop, servicing all incoming callbacks.
   - Without spinning, the subscriber will **never** receive data.

### The "Why" Behind the Pattern

- **Decoupling of Concerns:** Producers and consumers of data do not need to know each other's existence.
- **Modularity:** A complex robotics pipeline is split into small, testable, mixed nodes.
- **Parallelism:** Multiple mixed nodes can run in parallel, distributed across CPU cores or even machines.
- **Reusability:** The same input topic can feed many mixed nodes; the same output topic can be consumed by many subscribers.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Expression / API Call | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Subscriber callback signature | `void callback(const std_msgs::String::ConstPtr& msg)` | Pass by `ConstPtr` to avoid copying |
| Publisher creation | `ros::Publisher p = nh.advertise<T>("topic", queue_size);` | `T` is the message type |
| Subscriber creation | `ros::Subscriber s = nh.subscribe("topic", queue_size, &Class::callback, this);` | Class member needs `this` |
| Publish a message | `pub.publish(msg);` | Called inside callback or main loop |
| Spin (process callbacks) | `ros::spin();` or `ros::spinOnce();` | `spinOnce()` for manual loops |
| Rate of execution | `ros::Rate loop_rate(10);` | **10 Hz** is a common safe default |
| NodeHandle namespace | `ros::NodeHandle nh("~");` | `~` denotes **private namespace** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Conventions used in ROS:**
> - Frequencies are in **Hertz (Hz)**
> - Queue size is a dimensionless integer (typically **1, 10, or 100**)
> - Topic names use forward slashes `/` as hierarchy delimiters
> - All times internally tracked in **seconds** and **nanoseconds**

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Mixed Node |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Subscribes to raw LiDAR scan, publishes filtered obstacle map |
| **Industrial Robots** | Subscribes to joint angles, publishes inverse-dynamics torques |
| **Drones** | Subscribes to IMU + GPS, publishes fused pose estimate |
| **Service Robots** | Subscribes to speech text, publishes motion commands |
| **Agricultural Robots** | Subscribes to camera feed, publishes weed-detection bounding boxes |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Analytical Derivation: Message Flow Through a Mixed Node

We derive the logical transformation that a mixed node performs. Let the input message stream be $M_{in}(t)$ arriving on topic $T_{in}$, and the output message stream be $M_{out}(t)$ published on topic $T_{out}$.

**Step 1: Define the input arrival model**

Let messages arrive at the node with timestamps $t_1, t_2, t_3, \ldots, t_n$. The inter-arrival time is:

$$
\Delta t_i = t_{i+1} - t_i
$$

For a continuous sensor source, we typically assume a constant publish rate $f_s$ (in Hz), so:

$$
\Delta t_i = \frac{1}{f_s} = T_s
$$

**Step 2: Define the callback processing function**

The callback function $C(\cdot)$ operates on the input message and produces an output. Formally:

$$
M_{out} = C(M_{in})
$$

For example, if the input is a `String` message containing a number, and the output is the number doubled:

$$
C(x) = 2x
$$

**Step 3: Define the publication rate at the output**

If the mixed node republishes on every input, the output rate equals the input rate:

$$
f_{out} = f_{in}
$$

However, if the mixed node uses a `ros::Rate` timer in addition to callbacks (a *hybrid* pattern), the output rate becomes:

$$
f_{out} = f_{timer} = \frac{1}{T_{timer}}
$$

**Step 4: Time delay through the node**

The end-to-end latency $L$ of a message through a mixed node is:

$$
L = t_{publish,out} - t_{arrive,in}
$$

In the best case (immediate callback dispatch), this approaches the callback execution time:

$$
L \approx T_{callback} = \text{execution time of } C(\cdot)
$$

For a doubling operation on a 64-bit number:

$$
L \approx O(1) \text{ in CPU cycles}
$$

**Step 5: Queue accumulation condition**

Messages accumulate in the subscriber's queue if the callback cannot keep up. The condition for stable operation (no message loss) is:

$$
T_{callback} < T_s
$$

Substituting $T_s = \frac{1}{f_s}$:

$$
T_{callback} \cdot f_s < 1
$$

If this inequality is violated, the queue overflows and ROS drops old messages (FIFO discard by default).

> [!NOTE]
> The `queue_size` parameter passed to `subscribe()` bounds the buffer. If `queue_size = 10`, only the latest **10** unprocessed messages are retained.

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A mixed node subscribes to a LiDAR scan topic at **40 Hz**, and inside the callback it applies a 2D grid-mapping transformation. The callback takes on average **20 ms** to execute. The subscribe queue is set to **5**. Determine:
1. The maximum sustainable rate.
2. Whether the queue will overflow.
3. The required minimum CPU speed to avoid loss.

**Step 1: Compute sustainable rate**

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{callback}} = \frac{1}{0.020} = 50 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2: Compare with incoming rate**

$$
f_{in} = 40 \text{ Hz} < f_{max} = 50 \text{ Hz}
$$

Therefore, the system is **stable**, and the queue will **not** overflow under steady-state conditions.

**Step 3: Required throughput**

The CPU must process **40 messages per second**, each taking **20 ms** of compute. This yields a CPU utilization of:

$$
U = f_{in} \cdot T_{callback} = 40 \cdot 0.020 = 0.80 = 80\%
$$

So the CPU must have at least **80%** headroom for this single node. A multi-core system is recommended when running multiple such nodes in parallel.

### Analytical Matrix

| Scenario | Input Rate | Callback Time | Output Rate | Stable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Image processing (light) | 10 Hz | 5 ms | 10 Hz | ✅ Yes |
| Image processing (heavy) | 30 Hz | 50 ms | 30 Hz (throttled) | ✅ Yes (with throttle) |
| Point cloud filtering | 20 Hz | 60 ms | 20 Hz | ❌ Unstable — must throttle |
| IMU dead-reckoning | 100 Hz | 1 ms | 100 Hz | ✅ Yes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Data Flow Through a Mixed Publisher-Subscriber Node

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sensor Source Node] -->|Publishes on /scan| B[ROS Topic /scan]
    B -->|Delivers message| C[Mixed Node]
    C -->|Callback processes data| D[Transforms / filters]
    D -->|Publishes on /filtered_scan| E[ROS Topic /filtered_scan]
    E -->|Delivers message| F[Consumer Node]
    G[ros::spin] -->|Services callback queue| C
```

### Diagram 2: Internal Architecture of a Mixed Node

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Main[main function] --> Init[ros::init]
    Init --> NH[Create NodeHandle nh]
    NH --> Sub[Create Subscriber on /input]
    NH --> Pub[Create Publisher on /output]
    Sub --> CB[Register callback_function]
    CB --> Proc[Process incoming message]
    Proc --> Out[Publish result to /output]
    NH --> Spin[ros::spin]
    Spin --> Loop[Infinite callback loop]
    Loop --> CB
```

### Diagram 3: Class-Based Mixed Node Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ClassNode [MixedNode Class]
        A[Constructor: advertise and subscribe]
        B[Private: ros::NodeHandle nh]
        C[Private: ros::Publisher pub]
        D[Private: ros::Subscriber sub]
        E[Method: inputCallback]
    end
    TopicIn[/input_topic] --> D
    E --> Proc[Transform data]
    Proc --> C
    C --> TopicOut[/output_topic]
```

### Diagram 4: Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Block1[Input Topic /input_topic] --> Block2[Subscriber Queue - size 10]
    Block2 --> Block3[Callback Function C]
    Block3 --> Block4[Data Processing Pipeline]
    Block4 --> Block5[Publisher Queue - size 10]
    Block5 --> Block6[Output Topic /output_topic]
    Block7[ROS Master] -.->|Registers publishers and subscribers| Block1
    Block7 -.->|Registers publishers and subscribers| Block6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The dotted lines from the **ROS Master** represent out-of-band registration, while the solid arrows represent actual message data flow. The Master does **not** see message contents — it only connects publishers to subscribers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*What is meant by a "mixed publisher-subscriber node" in ROS? Give one practical example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A mixed publisher-subscriber node is a single ROS node that simultaneously subscribes to one or more input topics and publishes to one or more output topics. The subscriber's callback function processes incoming messages and calls the publisher's `publish()` method to send the transformed data downstream.

**Example:** A node that subscribes to `/camera/image_raw` and publishes the result of an edge-detection filter to `/camera/image_edges`.

*(Breakdown: 1 mark definition, 1 mark mechanism, 1 mark example)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State the role of `ros::spin()` in a mixed publisher-subscriber node. What happens if it is omitted?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
`ros::spin()` enters an infinite loop that processes the ROS callback queue, dispatching incoming messages to their registered subscriber callbacks. Without `ros::spin()` (or `ros::spinOnce()` in a manual loop), the subscriber callbacks will **never** be invoked, and the node will only be able to publish — it will be effectively a "dead" listener.

*(Breakdown: 1 mark role, 1 mark consequence, 1 mark alternative — `spinOnce`)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the architecture of a mixed publisher-subscriber node in ROS. List the key API calls used to construct such a node. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a mixed ROS node that subscribes to a topic `/sensor_data` of type `std_msgs::Float64` and publishes the *squared* value on topic `/sensor_squared`. Show the complete structure (C++ pseudocode accepted) and explain the data flow. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Architecture and API explanation (7 marks):**

A mixed publisher-subscriber node contains the following components, executed in order:

1. `ros::init(argc, argv, "node_name");` — initializes the node with the ROS Master.
2. `ros::NodeHandle nh;` — creates the handle used to create pub/sub objects.
3. `ros::Publisher pub = nh.advertise<T>("output_topic", queue_size);` — registers as a publisher.
4. `ros::Subscriber sub = nh.subscribe("input_topic", queue_size, callback);` — registers as a subscriber and binds the callback.
5. `ros::spin();` — enters the callback-dispatch loop.

**Block diagram:**

```
   /input_topic
        │
        ▼
  [Subscriber Queue]  →  [Callback Function C(x)]
                                   │
                                   ▼
                          [Publisher Queue]
                                   │
                                   ▼
                              /output_topic
```

**Key API calls** (recall, 2 marks):
- `advertise<T>()`, `subscribe()`, `publish()`, `ros::spin()`

*(Breakdown: 2 marks block diagram, 3 marks step-by-step API, 2 marks for flow explanation)*

**(b) Mixed node design — squaring node (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Define the callback that takes the input, squares it, and publishes.

**Step 2:** Wire the subscriber and publisher to the same `NodeHandle`.

**Step 3:** Run `ros::spin()` to keep the node alive.

**Conceptual C++ structure (board-acceptable pseudocode):**

```
#include <ros/ros.h>
#include <std_msgs/Float64.h>

ros::Publisher pub;

void inputCallback(const std_msgs::Float64::ConstPtr& msg)
{
    std_msgs::Float64 output;
    output.data = msg->data * msg->data;   // square the input
    pub.publish(output);                   // publish result
    ROS_INFO("Received: %f, Published: %f", msg->data, output.data);
}

int main(int argc, char** argv)
{
    ros::init(argc, argv, "squaring_node");
    ros::NodeHandle nh;
    pub = nh.advertise<std_msgs::Float64>("/sensor_squared", 10);
    ros::Subscriber sub = nh.subscribe("/sensor_data", 10, inputCallback);
    ros::spin();
    return 0;
}
```

**Data flow explanation (3 marks):**
- The publisher object `pub` is declared as a **global** so the callback can access it.
- Each time a `Float64` message arrives on `/sensor_data`, the callback computes $y = x^2$ and publishes it on `/sensor_squared`.
- The mathematical transformation is:

$$
y = f(x) = x^2
$$

- The output topic rate equals the input rate, assuming the callback completes within the inter-message interval $T_s = \frac{1}{f_s}$.

*(Breakdown: 2 marks structure, 2 marks explanation, 2 marks mathematical mapping, 1 mark for correct global declaration of publisher)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Differentiate between a pure publisher node, a pure subscriber node, and a mixed publisher-subscriber node. State two real-world robotics scenarios where a mixed node is essential. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ROS mixed node subscribes to topic `/imu` (published at 50 Hz) and publishes to topic `/pose`. The callback averages 15 ms. The subscriber queue size is 1. Determine: (i) the maximum sustainable callback rate, (ii) whether the queue overflows under steady state, and (iii) the CPU utilization for this single node. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Differentiation and use-cases (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Pure Publisher | Pure Subscriber | Mixed Node |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ros::Publisher` | Yes | No | Yes |
| `ros::Subscriber` | No | Yes | Yes |
| `ros::spin()` needed? | No (uses `Rate` loop) | Yes | Yes |
| Direction | Sends only | Receives only | Both |
| Example | Sensor driver | Display / logger | Filter, transformer, fusion |

**Two real-world scenarios (2 marks each):**
1. **Sensor Fusion:** Subscribes to `/imu/data` and `/gps/fix`, fuses them, and publishes the result on `/estimated_pose`. This is the cornerstone of robot localization (e.g., robot_localization package).
2. **Vision Pipeline:** Subscribes to `/camera/image_raw`, runs a CNN inference, publishes detected object bounding boxes on `/detections`.

*(Breakdown: 3 marks for table, 2 marks per scenario)*

**(b) Numerical analysis (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Input rate $f_{in} = 50$ Hz
- Callback time $T_{callback} = 15$ ms $= 0.015$ s
- Queue size = 1

**(i) Maximum sustainable callback rate (2 marks):**

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{callback}} = \frac{1}{0.015} \approx 66.67 \text{ Hz}
$$

**(ii) Queue overflow check (3 marks):**

The inter-arrival time at the input is:

$$
T_s = \frac{1}{f_{in}} = \frac{1}{50} = 0.020 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

Stability condition:

$$
T_{callback} < T_s \implies 15 \text{ ms} < 20 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark
$$

The system is **stable** under steady state; the queue (size 1) is **sufficient** to handle the rate, since each message is processed before the next arrives.

**(iii) CPU utilization (2 marks):**

$$
U = f_{in} \times T_{callback} = 50 \times 0.015 = 0.75 = 75\%
$$

The single mixed node consumes **75%** of one CPU core. On a typical 4-core robot computer, this leaves ample headroom for other nodes.

*(Breakdown as indicated above)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to declare the publisher as a global variable** when used inside a class or free function — causes *compilation error* and **0 marks** for that sub-question.
> 2. **Calling `publish()` outside a callback without `spin()`** — the subscriber will be dead. Examiners check whether you mention `ros::spin()` for **1 free mark**.
> 3. **Confusing `advertise()` (publisher) with `subscribe()`** — these are easily swapped. Always re-read the API signature in the answer.
> 4. **In numerical questions, forgetting units** — state Hz and ms explicitly. Marks are deducted for unit ambiguity.
> 5. **Wrong queue size in derivation** — recall the formula $f_{max} = 1/T_{callback}$, not the queue size. The queue size is a buffer, not a rate parameter.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **mixed node** = one NodeHandle + at least one Publisher + at least one Subscriber.
- ✅ The callback function is the **heart** of a mixed node — it receives, transforms, and re-publishes.
- ✅ `ros::spin()` (or `ros::spinOnce()` in a loop) is **mandatory** for any node with a subscriber.
- ✅ The publisher used inside a callback must be **accessible** — declare it globally or as a class member.
- ✅ Stability condition: $T_{callback} < T_s = \frac{1}{f_{in}}$. Violation ⇒ queue overflow ⇒ message loss.
- ✅ ROS is **type-safe** — you cannot publish a `Float64` to a `String` topic. The advertised and subscribed message types must match.
- ✅ The **ROS Master** does not see message contents — it only matches publisher and subscriber topics.
- ✅ Queue size is a **buffer dimension**, not a rate. Typical values: **1, 10, 100**.
- ✅ Common mixed-node patterns in KTU labs: **image filtering**, **sensor fusion**, **PID controllers**, **path smoothing**.
- ✅ Always include `#include <ros/ros.h>` and the relevant message header (e.g., `<std_msgs/Float64.h>`).
- ✅ Use `ROS_INFO()`, `ROS_WARN()`, `ROS_ERROR()` for debugging — these are board-acceptable in exam answers.
- ✅ Avoid **callback blocking** — long computations should be moved to separate threads using `AsyncSpinner`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defining a Service
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Defining a Service

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **Robot Operating System (ROS 2)**, a **Service** is a strictly synchronous, request-response communication paradigm built on top of the underlying **Data Distribution Service (DDS)** middleware. A service is defined by a pair of strongly-typed messages: a **Request** message and a **Response** message, bundled together inside a single `.srv` description file.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner Phrasing):**
> "A ROS 2 Service is a one-to-one, bidirectional, blocking communication mechanism in which a **Client** node sends a single **Request** to a **Service Server** node, which processes the request and returns exactly one **Response**. Unlike Topics, services are designed for **transactional, deterministic, and non-streaming** data exchange."

The three mandatory structural elements of any ROS 2 service are:

| # | Element | Purpose |
|---|---------|---------|
| 1 | **`.srv` file** | Declares the data contract (Request fields + Response fields separated by `---`) |
| 2 | **Service Server Node** | Advertises the service, waits, and executes the callback |
| 3 | **Service Client Node** | Creates the client, sends request, blocks until response arrives |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Waiter**
>
> Think of a **Service** like ordering food at a restaurant:
> - You (the **Client**) read the menu (the **`.srv` file**) and know exactly what you can ask for (Request) and what you will get back (Response).
> - The waiter takes your order, goes to the kitchen, and the chef (the **Server callback function**) prepares it.
> - The waiter **waits** at your table with the food — you cannot leave until the dish arrives. This blocking behaviour is what makes a service *synchronous*.
> - If a new customer arrives, the waiter handles them too — services are still designed for many clients, one at a time per request.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not use a Topic?**
> Topics are like a **radio broadcast** — continuous, asynchronous, fire-and-forget. Services are like a **telephone call** — you call, the other side picks up, talks back, and you hang up. Topics suit sensor streams; Services suit commands like "open gripper", "compute inverse kinematics", or "save map".

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics

- **QoS Reliability**: Services in ROS 2 use **RELIABLE** QoS by default — the request/response is guaranteed to be delivered exactly once.
- **Transport**: Default middleware is **DDS-RTPS** (Real-Time Publish-Subscribe); CycloneDDS, FastDDS, and RMW are the standard implementations.
- **Blocking timeout**: Clients can specify a wait time using `wait_for_service()`; default block is **infinite** (controlled by executor).
- **Concurrency**: A single service server can handle **many sequential clients**, but each request is processed atomically by the callback unless multithreaded executors are used.
- **Naming convention**: Service names follow the same rules as topics — slash-prefixed hierarchical names like `/turtle1/set_pen`, with a max length of **255 bytes**.
- **Standard constants**: `rclpy` / `rclcpp` default timeout for `wait_for_service` ≈ **5.0 seconds** when explicitly passed; `INTRA_PROCESS` communication is **not** supported for services in the same way as topics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Anatomy of a ROS 2 Service

The lifecycle of a service call can be broken down into **6 logical phases**:

1. **Definition Phase** — The developer creates a `.srv` file inside a ROS 2 package under the `srv/` directory.
2. **Build Phase** — `colcon build` invokes `rosidl_generator_c` / `rosidl_generator_cpp` / `rosidl_generator_py` to produce strongly-typed Request and Response classes.
3. **Server Instantiation** — The server node uses `rclpy.create_service()` (Python) or `node->create_service()` (C++) to register the callback.
4. **Client Discovery Phase** — The client node uses `create_client()` and calls `wait_for_service()` to confirm the server is alive via DDS discovery.
5. **Request Transmission** — The client calls `async_send_request()` (Python) or `async_send_request()` (C++ future-based) to dispatch the request.
6. **Callback Execution & Response** — The server's callback runs, populates the response object, and returns it. The client's future resolves and the data is consumed.

## 2.2 The `.srv` File Structure

A service description file uses a **three-hyphen separator** (`---`) to split the **Request** (top) from the **Response** (bottom). Variable typing follows ROS 2 IDL conventions identical to message files.

```text
# Example: AddTwoInts.srv
int64 a          <-- REQUEST field
int64 b          <-- REQUEST field
---
int64 sum        <-- RESPONSE field
```

Built-in primitive types available include: `bool`, `byte`, `char`, `float32`, `float64`, `int8`, `int16`, `int32`, `int64`, `uint8`, `uint16`, `uint32`, `uint64`, `string`, `wstring`. Complex types like `geometry_msgs/msg/Point` can also be embedded.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Formula / Cheat Table)

| Parameter / Concept | Value / Rule | KTU Board Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Service file extension | `.srv` | Frequently asked (2 marks) |
| Separator in `.srv` | `---` (three hyphens) | Direct exam question |
| Default QoS for services | **RELIABLE** | Distinguishes from topics |
| Default middleware | **DDS / RTPS** | KTU 2024 syllabus highlight |
| Sync mode | **Synchronous (blocking client)** | High-yield definition point |
| Relationship | **1 Server ↔ N Clients (one request at a time)** | Architecture diagram |
| Callback signature (C++) | `void cb(const Request::SharedPtr, Response::SharedPtr)` | Code-level question |
| Client wait method | `wait_for_service(timeout_sec=1.0)` | 3-mark practical question |
| Service discovery | Via **DDS Participants** | Theory |
| Default build generator | `rosidl_default_generators` | `package.xml` / `CMakeLists.txt` |
| Srv constants (max name) | **255 bytes** | Rare trivia |
| Concurrency model | Single-threaded by default; `MultiThreadedExecutor` for parallel | Advanced KTU question |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Services are the workhorse for **deterministic robot commands** — setting joint angles, triggering calibration routines, asking for the current pose of a manipulator, computing IK/FK, and saving/loading maps in navigation2. Anywhere you need an "ask-and-answer" interaction in a robot stack, you use a service.

## 2.4 Service vs Topic vs Action — The Trinity of ROS Communication

| Feature | **Topic** | **Service** | **Action** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pattern | Pub/Sub, async | Req/Resp, sync | Goal/Result/Feedback, async |
| Cardinality | N : N | 1 : N (one req at a time) | 1 : N (long-running) |
| Streaming | Yes (continuous) | No (single shot) | Yes (with feedback) |
| Cancellation | N/A | N/A | Yes |
| Use case | Sensor data | Commands, queries | Navigation, manipulation |
| Built on | DDS Publisher/Subscriber | DDS Requester/Replier | Built on **Topics + Services** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pro-tip for the board exam:** When asked "Why use a service over a topic?", answer: *"Topics lack acknowledgement and a defined response — services guarantee a one-to-one reply, making them suitable for transactional robot operations like state queries and discrete commands."*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 End-to-End Derivation: From Concept to Runnable Service

We will now derive the **complete chain** of code and configuration needed to deploy a working ROS 2 service. We will use the canonical **`AddTwoInts`** example because it is the most-tested service in KTU practicals.

### Step 1 — Package Creation Logic

We initialize a ROS 2 package named `py_srvcli` with the official ament_python build type. The derivation logic is:

$$\text{Package} = \text{ament\_python} + \text{rosidl\_default\_generators} + \text{rclpy}$$

The terminal-level derivation is:

```bash
ros2 pkg create py_srvcli --build-type ament_python --dependencies rclpy example_interfaces
```

**Explanation of flags:**

- `--build-type ament_python` selects the Python build system (vs `ament_cmake` for C++).
- `--dependencies rclpy example_interfaces` auto-injects these into `package.xml`.

### Step 2 — The `.srv` File (Definition Layer)

We create the file `srv/AddTwoInts.srv`. Algebraically, the type contract is:

$$
\text{AddTwoInts} : (\text{int64}, \text{int64}) \rightarrow \text{int64}
$$

$$
\text{where } \text{Request} = \{a : \mathbb{Z}_{64}, b : \mathbb{Z}_{64}\}, \quad \text{Response} = \{\text{sum} : \mathbb{Z}_{64}\}
$$

### Step 3 — Update `package.xml`

The build system must declare the `rosidl_default_generators` and `rosidl_default_runtime` member packages. Without these two `<exec_depend>` tags, `colcon build` will fail to generate the Python types for the `.srv` file.

### Step 4 — Update `setup.py`

We must add the line `'resource_packages=["py_srvcli"]'` inside `setup()` so that the generated `srv` types become importable. The justification:

$$
\text{Import path} = \text{py\_srvcli.srv.AddTwoInts\_request / AddTwoInts\_response}
$$

### Step 5 — The Server Node (Provider Layer)

The server node is constructed using **3 derived sub-steps**:

1. **Initialize the ROS 2 client library** $rclpy.init()$
2. **Create the node and register the service** using the equation:

$$
S_{\text{server}} = \text{node.create\_service}(\text{type}, \text{name}, \text{callback})
$$

3. **Enter the spin loop** so the callback can be invoked by the executor

The full Python derivation is:

```python
from example_interfaces.srv import AddTwoInts
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node

class MinimalService(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('minimal_service')
        self.srv = self.create_service(AddTwoInts, 'add_two_ints', self.add_two_ints_callback)

    def add_two_ints_callback(self, request, response):
        response.sum = request.a + request.b
        self.get_logger().info(f'Incoming request: a={request.a}, b={request.b}')
        return response

def main(args=None):
    rclpy.init(args=args)
    node = MinimalService()
    rclpy.spin(node)
    rclpy.shutdown()
```

**Algebraic translation of the callback:**

$$
\text{response.sum} \leftarrow f(a, b) = a + b, \quad \forall\, (a, b) \in \mathbb{Z}_{64} \times \mathbb{Z}_{64}
$$

### Step 6 — The Client Node (Requester Layer)

The client derivation follows the **Future-based asynchronous pattern**:

$$
\text{Future} = \text{client.async\_send\_request}(\text{request})
$$

$$
\text{Response} = \text{Future.result}(\text{timeout})
$$

The full derivation is:

```python
from example_interfaces.srv import AddTwoInts
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node

class MinimalClientAsync(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('minimal_client_async')
        self.cli = self.create_client(AddTwoInts, 'add_two_ints')
        while not self.cli.wait_for_service(timeout_sec=1.0):
            self.get_logger().info('Service not available, waiting again...')
        self.req = AddTwoInts.Request()

    def send_request(self, a, b):
        self.req.a = a
        self.req.b = b
        self.future = self.cli.call_async(self.req)
        rclpy.spin_until_future_complete(self, self.future)
        return self.future.result()
```

**Wait-loop derivation:**

$$
\text{wait\_for\_service} : \text{bool} = \bigvee_{i=1}^{N} \bigl[\text{DDS\_discover}(\text{server}) == \text{True}\bigr]
$$

The loop continues until the DDS participant for the service server is discovered or the node is shut down.

### Step 7 — The Build & Run Sequence

```bash
cd ~/ros2_ws
colcon build --packages-select py_srvcli
. install/setup.bash
ros2 run py_srvcli service_server
ros2 run py_srvcli service_client 3 7
```

**Expected terminal derivation (output of the service call):**

$$
\text{Result} = 3 + 7 = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \texttt{Result of add\_two\_ints: 10}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Service Definition Pitfalls

| # | Mistake | Correct Form | Marks Lost if Wrong |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Using `---` separator in a `.msg` file | `---` is **only** for `.srv` | -2 |
| 2 | Forgetting `wait_for_service()` in client | Always block until service exists | -3 |
| 3 | Declaring both Request and Response on the same side of `---` | Request **above**, Response **below** | -2 |
| 4 | Not adding `rosidl_default_runtime` to `<exec_depend>` | Required for runtime Python imports | -3 |
| 5 | Calling `spin()` after `spin_until_future_complete` | Causes redundant executor conflict | -2 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Service Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

The diagram below maps the **end-to-end data flow** of a ROS 2 service call, from the `.srv` contract definition to the final response delivery. It is intentionally designed to satisfy KTU board expectations of clarity in a 7-mark architecture question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Developer writes AddTwoInts.srv] --> B[rosidl_generator_py compiles types]
    B --> C[Python module py_srvcli.srv.AddTwoInts generated]
    C --> D[Server node imports Request Response classes]
    C --> E[Client node imports Request Response classes]
    D --> F[create_service registers callback with DDS participant]
    F --> G[DDS discovery advertises service to network]
    E --> H[create_client + wait_for_service polls DDS]
    H --> I{DDS server found?}
    I -- no --> J[Log waiting, retry every 1.0 s]
    I -- yes --> K[Client builds Request object with a and b]
    K --> L[async_send_request dispatches to DDS]
    L --> M[DDS routes request to Server callback queue]
    M --> N[Server callback executes response.sum = a plus b]
    N --> O[Response object returned to DDS layer]
    O --> P[DDS delivers Response to Client future]
    P --> Q[Future.result returns response to user code]
    Q --> R[Client logs Result of add two ints equals 10]
```

## 4.2 Srv File Anatomy — Visual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SrvFile["AddTwoInts.srv"]
        direction TB
        REQ["REQUEST ZONE Above the three hyphens"]
        SEP["--- Three hyphen separator ---"]
        RES["RESPONSE ZONE Below the three hyphens"]
        REQ --> SEP --> RES
    end
    subgraph REQFIELDS["Request fields"]
        RA["int64 a"]
        RB["int64 b"]
    end
    subgraph RESFIELDS["Response fields"]
        RS["int64 sum"]
    end
    REQFIELDS --> REQ
    RESFIELDS --> RES
```

## 4.3 Service Lifecycle — State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Uninitialized
    Uninitialized --> Configured : rclpy init
    Configured --> Discovered : wait for service returns true
    Discovered --> RequestSent : async send request
    RequestSent --> Processing : server callback executing
    Processing --> ResponseReceived : future resolves
    ResponseReceived --> [*]
    Discovered --> Timeout : discovery timeout exceeded
    Timeout --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Communication Topology — Service vs Topic Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TopicModel["Topic Model Many to Many async"]
        T1["Publisher A"] --> TB["Topic /scan"]
        T2["Publisher B"] --> TB
        TB --> S1["Subscriber 1"]
        TB --> S2["Subscriber 2"]
        TB --> S3["Subscriber 3"]
    end
    subgraph ServiceModel["Service Model One to One sync"]
        C1["Client X"] -- req --> SV["Service Server"]
        C2["Client Y"] -- req --> SV
        SV -- resp --> C1
        SV -- resp --> C2
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a ROS 2 service. Mention the role of the `.srv` file.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **ROS 2 service** is a synchronous, request-response communication mechanism between nodes, where a **client** sends a structured **request** to a **server**, and the server replies with a structured **response** (3 marks — 1 mark per element: client, request, response).

The **`.srv` file** is the interface description file that defines the data contract of the service. It is divided into two halves by a `---` separator: the upper half declares the request fields and the lower half declares the response fields (3 marks — must include `---` and Request/Response split).

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a ROS 2 topic and a ROS 2 service. Give one example use case for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Topic | Service |
|---|---|---|
| Communication | Asynchronous, one-way | Synchronous, two-way |
| Data flow | Continuous stream | Single discrete request/reply |
| Use case | Publishing LiDAR scans (`/scan`) | Querying robot pose (`/get_pose`) |

(3 marks — 1 for asynchrony distinction, 1 for streaming distinction, 1 for valid example of each)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 🔵 QUESTION A — 14 Marks
> `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a ROS 2 service. List the steps to **define a service** in a Python-based ROS 2 package, including the contents of the `.srv` file, `package.xml`, `setup.py`, server node, and client node. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write the complete Python implementation of a service server and service client for an `AddTwoInts` service. Show the expected output when the client sends `a = 4, b = 6`. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution (a) — 7 marks
The architecture of a ROS 2 service is **synchronous** and **request-response** based. The block diagram (drawn in the exam) should contain: **Client node → DDS middleware → Server node**, with Request flowing left-to-right and Response flowing right-to-left.

**Steps to define a service in a Python ROS 2 package (1 mark per step, 7 marks total):**

1. Create the package using `ros2 pkg create` with the `ament_python` build type and `rclpy` dependency.
2. Create the `srv/` directory inside the package and add the `.srv` file.
3. Write the `.srv` file separating Request and Response with `---`.
4. Update `package.xml` to add `<build_depend>rosidl_default_generators</build_depend>` and `<exec_depend>rosidl_default_runtime</exec_depend>`.
5. Update `setup.py` to add `'resource_packages=["package_name"]'` so the srv types are installed.
6. Implement the **server node** using `create_service()` and the **client node** using `create_client()` + `call_async()`.
7. Build with `colcon build` and run using `ros2 run`.

#### Solution (b) — 7 marks
The complete Python implementation is reproduced below (matches the derivation in Section 3.1):

**Server node** (`service_server.py`) — 3.5 marks:

```python
from example_interfaces.srv import AddTwoInts
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node

class MinimalService(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('minimal_service')
        self.srv = self.create_service(AddTwoInts, 'add_two_ints', self.add_two_ints_callback)
    def add_two_ints_callback(self, request, response):
        response.sum = request.a + request.b
        self.get_logger().info(f'Incoming: a={request.a}, b={request.b}')
        return response

def main(args=None):
    rclpy.init(args=args)
    rclpy.spin(MinimalService())
    rclpy.shutdown()
```

**Client node** (`service_client.py`) — 3.5 marks:

```python
from example_interfaces.srv import AddTwoInts
import rclpy, sys
from rclpy.node import Node

class MinimalClient(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('minimal_client')
        self.cli = self.create_client(AddTwoInts, 'add_two_ints')
        while not self.cli.wait_for_service(timeout_sec=1.0):
            self.get_logger().info('Waiting for service...')
    def send(self, a, b):
        req = AddTwoInts.Request(); req.a = a; req.b = b
        fut = self.cli.call_async(req)
        rclpy.spin_until_future_complete(self, fut)
        return fut.result()

def main(args=None):
    rclpy.init(args=args)
    node = MinimalClient()
    res = node.send(int(sys.argv[1]), int(sys.argv[2]))
    node.get_logger().info(f'Result: {res.sum}')
    rclpy.shutdown()
```

**Expected output** when `a = 4, b = 6`:
- Server terminal: `Incoming: a=4, b=6`
- Client terminal: `Result: 10` (since $4 + 6 = 10$).

---

### 🟢 QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
> `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Alt]`
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **lifecycle of a ROS 2 service call**, from `.srv` definition to the final response, including the role of **DDS discovery**, `wait_for_service()`, and the **future-based asynchronous client pattern**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare **Topics, Services, and Actions** as the three communication mechanisms of ROS 2. For each, give a real robot example and justify why that mechanism is most appropriate. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution (a) — 7 marks
The lifecycle has **six distinct phases** (1 mark + explanation, totalling 7 marks):

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Developer authors the `.srv` file describing Request and Response fields, separated by `---`. The file is placed in the `srv/` subdirectory of an ament package.
2. **Code Generation (1 mark):** During `colcon build`, the `rosidl_default_generators` tool reads the `.srv` and emits strongly-typed Python (`add_two_ints.srv._add_two_ints_request`) and C++ classes for both Request and Response.
3. **Server Registration (1 mark):** The server node calls `create_service()`, which registers a DDS **Replier** participant advertising the service name on the RTPS network.
4. **Client Discovery via DDS (1 mark):** When the client calls `wait_for_service()`, the underlying DDS layer performs participant discovery. If the server is not yet online, the function blocks and periodically re-polls (default 1-second cadence).
5. **Request Dispatch (1 mark):** Once discovered, the client calls `async_send_request()` to push the request onto the DDS data path. This returns a `Future` object, and the call is **non-blocking**.
6. **Callback Execution and Response (2 marks):** The server's executor invokes the callback, which mutates the response object. The response is serialized and sent back over DDS. The client's future resolves, and `spin_until_future_complete()` or `future.add_done_callback()` consumes the result.

#### Solution (b) — 7 marks

| Mechanism | Pattern | Cardinality | Robot Example | Justification (1.5 marks each) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Topic** | Async publish/subscribe | N : N | LiDAR scan stream on `/scan` | Continuous, high-frequency, lossy-tolerant sensor data is naturally streamed; a request-response would be wasteful. |
| **Service** | Sync request/response | 1 : N (one req at a time) | Query current joint state on `/get_joint_state` | Discrete, deterministic query; client needs an answer before it can plan the next motion. |
| **Action** | Async goal/feedback/result | 1 : N (long-running) | Navigate to a goal pose in Nav2 | Long-running mission (minutes) that needs periodic feedback and cancellability, which a service cannot provide. |

(7 marks — 1.5 per row of justification, total 6; + 1 mark for clean comparison and valid example per row.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks — KTU Board 2024 Pattern:**
> - **Forgetting `wait_for_service()` (–3 marks):** The examiner will run your client. If it crashes because the server is not yet up, full marks are not awarded. Always include a `while not cli.wait_for_service()` polling loop.
> - **Swapping Request and Response fields in the `.srv` file (–2 marks):** The convention is **Request above `---`, Response below**. Reversing this leads to a build failure or wrong field population.
> - **Missing `rosidl_default_runtime` in `package.xml` (–2 marks):** Without this `<exec_depend>`, the client code cannot import the generated srv module at runtime. This is a common silent failure.
> - **Not using `rclpy.spin_until_future_complete()` (–2 marks):** The future is asynchronous; without spinning the executor until completion, the client will receive `None` from `future.result()`.
> - **Confusing Service with Action (–3 marks):** Services are **synchronous and non-cancellable**; if asked for a long-running task with feedback, the correct answer is **Action**, not Service.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] A **Service** in ROS 2 is a **synchronous, request-response** communication between **one client** and **one server** (with N clients supported serially).
- [ ] The **`.srv` file** uses `---` to separate **Request fields (above)** from **Response fields (below)**.
- [ ] The **build system** requires `rosidl_default_generators` (build) and `rosidl_default_runtime` (exec) dependencies.
- [ ] **Server side**: use `create_service(Type, name, callback)` and `rclpy.spin(node)`.
- [ ] **Client side**: use `create_client(Type, name)` → `wait_for_service()` → `async_send_request()` → `future.result()`.
- [ ] The **default QoS for services is RELIABLE** — no message loss is acceptable.
- [ ] Services are built on top of **DDS Requester/Replier** pattern, not on raw UDP topics.
- [ ] **Cannot be cancelled** mid-execution — for cancellable long-running tasks, use **Actions**.
- [ ] **Cannot stream** continuous data — for sensors, use **Topics**.
- [ ] The canonical teaching example is **`AddTwoInts`** (Request: `a`, `b`; Response: `sum`).
- [ ] **Service names** are slash-delimited, max 255 bytes, and must match exactly between client and server.
- [ ] For **multithreaded handling of multiple service requests**, use `MultiThreadedExecutor` from `rclpy.executors`.
- [ ] **CLI debugging**: use `ros2 service list`, `ros2 service type <name>`, `ros2 service call <name> <type> <args>` to introspect services at runtime.
- [ ] In KTU 2024 exams, always draw a **block diagram** of client → DDS → server for full architecture marks.
- [ ] **Remember the math**: $f(a,b) = a + b$ is the simplest service contract — show its algebraic form when asked to derive.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementing a Service
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Implementing a Service

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the Robot Operating System (ROS), a **Service** is a strictly **synchronous**, **request/reply** communication paradigm implemented over a dedicated, named message bus. A service is defined by a pair of strongly-typed messages — the **Request** and the **Response** — bundled into a single `.srv` file. A node offering a capability exposes a **Service Server**, while a node that wishes to use that capability instantiates a **Service Client**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"A ROS service is a synchronous, bidirectional communication mechanism whereby a client node sends a strictly typed request message to a server node, which performs a computation and returns a typed response. Services are used for long-running, blocking calls or for situations requiring a definitive reply (unlike topics, which are asynchronous and one-way)."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Think of a **service** as a **restaurant kitchen ticket system** in a hotel:

1. A **waiter (client node)** writes an order on a slip of paper — *the slip has a fixed format (the `.srv` file): appetizer, main course, drink*.
2. The waiter **hands the slip to the kitchen counter** and **stands there waiting** (the `client.call()` line *blocks* the client until the reply arrives).
3. The **chef (server node)** picks up the slip, cooks the food (executes the callback), and writes the bill back on the same slip.
4. The waiter takes the slip back, reads the bill (the response), and proceeds.

If the chef does not exist (no server), the waiter's program would crash with an error — that is why we use `rospy.wait_for_service()` or persistent connections in C++.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **`.srv` file** | Text file separating Request and Response fields with `---` |
| **`advertiseService()`** | C++ API to register a node as a service server |
| **`serviceClient()`** | C++ API to instantiate a client handle |
| **`ServiceProxy`** | Python object that mimics a function call to the server |
| **`call()`** | Blocking call; sends request, waits for response |
| **`exists()`** | Checks whether a service is currently advertised |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exams, *synchronous* and *blocking* are the two adjectives examiners love to associate with services. If you remember nothing else, remember: **"Services are synchronous and blocking; Topics are asynchronous and fire-and-forget."**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Anatomy of a Service

A complete service implementation in ROS requires **four** independent artifacts:

1. **Service Definition File (`.srv`)** — a plain-text file in the `srv/` folder of a ROS package.
2. **Server Node** — registers the callback that processes the request.
3. **Client Node** — crafts the request object and invokes `call()`.
4. **Build System Glue** — `CMakeLists.txt` (for C++) / `setup.py` (for Python) must declare the service generator.

The `.srv` file is split by a line containing exactly three hyphens:

```text
# Request fields (sent client → server)
int64 a
int64 b
---
# Response fields (sent server → client)
int64 sum
```

When the build system processes this file, it auto-generates a language-specific class (e.g., `AddTwoInts.h` in C++ or `AddTwoInts.py` in Python) containing three classes: `Request`, `Response`, and the parent service class.

## 2.2 The "Why" — When to Use a Service vs. a Topic

| Property | Topic (Pub/Sub) | Service (Req/Rep) |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Many-to-many | One-to-one |
| **Timing** | Asynchronous, non-blocking | Synchronous, blocking |
| **Use Case** | Continuous sensor streams | Discrete queries, computations, triggers |
| **Failure Handling** | Message dropped if no subscriber | Client receives `False` on failure |
| **Bandwidth** | High (continuous) | Low (on-demand) |

## 2.3 The Latency Model of a Service Call

The total wall-clock time for one round-trip service call is the sum of three measurable intervals:

$$
T_{\text{rtt}} \;=\; T_{\text{prop\_req}} \;+\; T_{\text{exec}} \;+\; T_{\text{prop\_res}}
$$

Where:

- $T_{\text{prop\_req}}$ — time for the XML-RPC request to travel from client to server (negligible on local host, $\sim 1$–$5\text{ ms}$).
- $T_{\text{exec}}$ — time the server's callback spends computing the response.
- $T_{\text{prop\_res}}$ — time for the response to travel back to the client.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A service connection is **persistent** (created once, reused), so $T_{\text{prop\_req}}$ is paid **only on the first call**. Subsequent calls amortize this cost. Examiners may test this conceptual detail.

## 2.4 KTU Formula & Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / API | Meaning | Typical Value / Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| `$T_{\text{rtt}}$` | Round-trip service latency | $2 \text{ ms}$ to $2000 \text{ ms}$ |
| `$N_{\text{req}}$` | Number of in-flight requests allowed | 1 (strictly synchronous) |
| `advertiseService(name, type, callback)` | Registers a server | Returns `ros::ServiceServer` |
| `serviceClient(name, persistent=false)` | Creates a client | Returns `ros::ServiceClient` |
| `client.call(srv)` | Blocks until response or failure | Returns `bool` |
| `rospy.wait_for_service(name, timeout=...)` | Blocks until server appears | Throws on timeout |

> [!WARNING]
> The default `persistent` flag for `serviceClient()` is **`false`**. Each call opens a new XML-RPC connection. For high-frequency calls (e.g., >10 Hz), use `serviceClient("name", true)` to keep the connection open. **This is a frequent KTU board question.**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Service-based architecture is the backbone of industrial robotics:
- **MoveIt!** uses services to compute inverse-kinematics solutions.
- **Navigation Stack** uses services to clear costmaps or save maps.
- **Gripper control** uses a service to "open" or "close" — a discrete action, not a stream.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Implementation Walkthrough (C++ + Python)

This module assumes the package `beginner_tutorials` is already created with `catkin_create_pkg`.

## 3.1 Step 1 — Create the `.srv` File

Navigate to the package's `srv` folder and create the file `AddTwoInts.srv`:

```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws/src/beginner_tutorials
mkdir srv
cd srv
touch AddTwoInts.srv
```

Add the following content to `AddTwoInts.srv`:

```text
int64 a
int64 b
---
int64 sum
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `---` separator is **mandatory**. The build system (`add_service_files()` in `CMakeLists.txt` and the `srv` declaration in `package.xml`) uses it to split the file into request and response halves.

## 3.2 Step 2 — Modify `package.xml`

Two dependencies are required so that the message generator can compile the `.srv` file into native C++/Python classes.

```xml
<build_depend>message_generation</build_depend>
<exec_depend>message_runtime</exec_depend>
```

## 3.3 Step 3 — Modify `CMakeLists.txt`

Add the following directives inside the existing `CMakeLists.txt`:

```cmake
add_service_files(
  FILES
  AddTwoInts.srv
)

generate_messages(
  DEPENDENCIES
  std_msgs
)
```

Also, uncomment/ensure the `catkin_package()` call lists `message_runtime`:

```cmake
catkin_package(
  CATKIN_DEPENDS message_runtime ...
)
```

## 3.4 Step 4 — Write the C++ Server (`add_two_ints_server.cpp`)

```cpp
#include "ros/ros.h"
#include "beginner_tutorials/AddTwoInts.h"

bool handle_add(beginner_tutorials::AddTwoInts::Request  &req,
                beginner_tutorials::AddTwoInts::Response &res)
{
  res.sum = req.a + req.b;
  ROS_INFO("request: x=%ld, y=%ld", (long int)req.a, (long int)req.b);
  ROS_INFO("sending back response: [%ld]", (long int)res.sum);
  return true;
}

int main(int argc, char **argv)
{
  ros::init(argc, argv, "add_two_ints_server");
  ros::NodeHandle n;

  ros::ServiceServer service = n.advertiseService("add_two_ints", handle_add);
  ROS_INFO("Ready to add two ints.");
  ros::spin();

  return 0;
}
```

### Line-by-Line Logic

| Line | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `#include "beginner_tutorials/AddTwoInts.h"` | Header generated from the `.srv` file |
| `bool handle_add(...)` | The callback that runs each time a request arrives |
| `res.sum = req.a + req.b` | The actual "work" the service performs |
| `n.advertiseService("add_two_ints", handle_add)` | Registers the server under the name `add_two_ints` |
| `ros::spin()` | Enters the event loop, waiting for callbacks |

## 3.5 Step 5 — Write the C++ Client (`add_two_ints_client.cpp`)

```cpp
#include "ros/ros.h"
#include <cstdlib>
#include "beginner_tutorials/AddTwoInts.h"

int main(int argc, char **argv)
{
  ros::init(argc, argv, "add_two_ints_client");
  if (argc != 3) {
    ROS_INFO("usage: add_two_ints_client X Y");
    return 1;
  }

  ros::NodeHandle n;
  ros::ServiceClient client = n.serviceClient<beginner_tutorials::AddTwoInts>("add_two_ints");

  beginner_tutorials::AddTwoInts srv;
  srv.request.a = atoll(argv[1]);
  srv.request.b = atoll(argv[2]);

  if (client.call(srv)) {
    ROS_INFO("Sum: %ld", (long int)srv.response.sum);
  } else {
    ROS_ERROR("Failed to call service add_two_ints");
    return 1;
  }

  return 0;
}
```

## 3.6 Step 6 — Add Executables in `CMakeLists.txt`

```cmake
add_executable(add_two_ints_server src/add_two_ints_server.cpp)
target_link_libraries(add_two_ints_server ${catkin_LIBRARIES})
add_dependencies(add_two_ints_server beginner_tutorials_gencpp)

add_executable(add_two_ints_client src/add_two_ints_client.cpp)
target_link_libraries(add_two_ints_client ${catkin_LIBRARIES})
add_dependencies(add_two_ints_client beginner_tutorials_gencpp)
```

## 3.7 Step 7 — Build and Run

```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws
catkin_make
source devel/setup.bash

# Terminal 1
roscore
# Terminal 2 — start the server
rosrun beginner_tutorials add_two_ints_server
# Terminal 3 — invoke the client
rosrun beginner_tutorials add_two_ints_client 4 5
```

Expected output on the client terminal:

```text
[ INFO] [1680000000.000000]: Sum: 9
```

## 3.8 The Python Equivalent

### Server (`add_two_ints_server.py`)

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
from beginner_tutorials.srv import AddTwoInts, AddTwoIntsResponse
import rospy

def handle_add_two_ints(req):
    rospy.loginfo("Returning [%s + %s = %s]" % (req.a, req.b, (req.a + req.b)))
    return AddTwoIntsResponse(req.a + req.b)

def add_two_ints_server():
    rospy.init_node('add_two_ints_server')
    s = rospy.Service('add_two_ints', AddTwoInts, handle_add_two_ints)
    rospy.loginfo("Ready to add two ints.")
    rospy.spin()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    add_two_ints_server()
```

### Client (`add_two_ints_client.py`)

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
import sys
import rospy
from beginner_tutorials.srv import AddTwoInts, AddTwoIntsRequest

def add_two_ints_client(x, y):
    rospy.wait_for_service('add_two_ints')
    try:
        add_two_ints = rospy.ServiceProxy('add_two_ints', AddTwoInts)
        resp = add_two_ints(AddTwoIntsRequest(x, y))
        return resp.sum
    except rospy.ServiceException as e:
        rospy.logerr("Service call failed: %s" % e)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) == 3:
        x = int(sys.argv[1]); y = int(sys.argv[2])
    else:
        x, y = 4, 5
    print("Requesting %s+%s" % (x, y))
    print("%s + %s = %s" % (x, y, add_two_ints_client(x, y)))
```

## 3.9 Exhaustive Verification — Verify the Service is Live

Use the built-in command-line tools to validate without writing code:

```bash
rossrv list | grep AddTwoInts
rossrv show beginner_tutorials/AddTwoInts
rosservice list
rosservice type /add_two_ints | rossrv show
rosservice call /add_two_ints 10 5
```

The last command will return:

```text
sum: 15
```

This is the **fastest possible debug loop** and is often asked in KTU viva voce.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of Service Communication

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Client Node main] --> B[Create ServiceClient object]
    B --> C[Populate srv.request]
    C --> D{Service exists?}
    D -- No --> E[Client.call returns false]
    D -- Yes --> F[Serialize request to XML-RPC]
    F --> G[ROS Master routes to server]
    G --> H[Server Callback handle_add runs]
    H --> I[Compute res.sum = req.a + req.b]
    I --> J[Serialize response]
    J --> K[Response travels back to Client]
    K --> L[Client.call returns true]
    L --> M[Client reads srv.response.sum]
```

## 4.2 Service Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unadvertised
    Unadvertised --> Advertised: advertiseService called
    Advertised --> Processing: Request received
    Processing --> Advertised: Response sent
    Advertised --> Unadvertised: Node shutdown / unadvertise
    Processing --> Unadvertised: Node crash
```

## 4.3 File / Build Dependency Graph

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PkgXml[package.xml] --> Catkin[catkin_make]
    Cmake[CMakeLists.txt] --> Catkin
    SrvFile[srv/AddTwoInts.srv] --> MsgGen[message_generation]
    MsgGen --> CppHeader[AddTwoInts.h]
    MsgGen --> PyModule[AddTwoInts.py]
    CppHeader --> ServerBin[add_two_ints_server]
    CppHeader --> ClientBin[add_two_ints_client]
    PyModule --> ServerPy[add_two_ints_server.py]
    PyModule --> ClientPy[add_two_ints_client.py]
    Catkin --> ServerBin
    Catkin --> ClientBin
```

## 4.4 Sequence Diagram of a Single Call

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client Node
    participant M as ROS Master
    participant S as Server Node
    C->>M: lookupService("add_two_ints")
    M-->>C: returns server URI
    C->>S: TCP/XML-RPC: srv.request {a=4, b=5}
    S->>S: handle_add_two_ints callback executes
    S-->>C: XML-RPC: srv.response {sum=9}
    C->>C: client.call() returns true
    Note over C,S: T_rtt = T_prop_req + T_exec + T_prop_res
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]`**
Differentiate between a ROS **Topic** and a ROS **Service** in terms of communication pattern, synchronicity, and a suitable use-case for each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Topic | Service |
|---|---|---|
| **Communication Pattern** | Publish/Subscribe (many-to-many) | Request/Reply (one-to-one) |
| **Synchronicity** | Asynchronous, non-blocking | Synchronous, blocking |
| **Use Case** | Streaming LiDAR point clouds at 40 Hz | Computing inverse-kinematics on demand |

> **Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for each row of the table. Examiners expect a clean comparison.

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]`**
What is the role of the `---` separator inside a `.srv` file? Why is `message_generation` declared as a build dependency in `package.xml`?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- The `---` separator (1 mark) divides the `.srv` file into the **Request** fields (above) and the **Response** fields (below). The build system uses it to generate two separate classes (`Request` and `Response`) along with the parent service class.
- `message_generation` (2 marks) is a build dependency because the `.srv` file is **not** a runnable artifact by itself; it must be **processed at compile time** to generate the C++/Python header files (`AddTwoInts.h` / `AddTwoInts.py`) that the source code includes. Without it, the `#include` directive in the server/client code will fail.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

> **KTU Rule:** In the End-Semester Examination (ESE), Module 2 questions appear in **Part B** with an internal choice. You must answer **only one** of the two alternatives. Each question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 2, Q4a/4b]`**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **service communication model** in ROS. Show how a client node sends a request, how the server node processes it, and how the response is returned. Mention the role of the ROS Master. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Write the complete C++ code to create a ROS service called `/compute_area` that takes a `float64 radius` as input and returns `float64 area` as output. Show the `.srv` file, the server code, the client code, and the required `CMakeLists.txt` entries. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Service Communication Model (7 Marks)**

A ROS service follows a strict **request/reply** protocol:

1. **Service Definition (1 mark):** A `.srv` file declares two message blocks — Request and Response — separated by `---`. Example: `int64 a`, `int64 b`, `---`, `int64 sum`.
2. **Registration (1 mark):** The server node calls `advertiseService("name", callback)`. The ROS Master stores the mapping of service name → server URI.
3. **Lookup (1 mark):** The client calls `serviceClient("name")`, which internally asks the ROS Master for the server's URI.
4. **Synchronous Call (2 marks):** `client.call(srv)` serializes the request via XML-RPC and sends it to the server. The client's thread **blocks** until the response arrives.
5. **Callback Execution (1 mark):** The server's `handle_*` function runs, populates `srv.response`, and returns `true`. The response is sent back over the same persistent XML-RPC connection.
6. **Return & Use (1 mark):** `client.call()` returns `true`, and the client reads `srv.response.<field>`. If the server is unreachable, `call()` returns `false` after a timeout (default 1 second, configurable via `serviceClient(..., persistent=false)`).

```
Client → [srv.request] → Server → callback → [srv.response] → Client
                ↑_________________________↓  (persistent TCP/XML-RPC)
```

**(b) `/compute_area` Implementation (7 Marks)**

**`srv/ComputeArea.srv` (1 mark):**
```text
float64 radius
---
float64 area
```

**Server (`compute_area_server.cpp`) (3 marks):**
```cpp
#include "ros/ros.h"
#include "beginner_tutorials/ComputeArea.h"

bool handle(beginner_tutorials::ComputeArea::Request  &req,
            beginner_tutorials::ComputeArea::Response &res)
{
  res.area = 3.14159 * req.radius * req.radius;
  ROS_INFO("Area = %f", res.area);
  return true;
}

int main(int argc, char **argv)
{
  ros::init(argc, argv, "compute_area_server");
  ros::NodeHandle n;
  ros::ServiceServer s = n.advertiseService("compute_area", handle);
  ros::spin();
  return 0;
}
```

**Client (`compute_area_client.cpp`) (2 marks):**
```cpp
#include "ros/ros.h"
#include "beginner_tutorials/ComputeArea.h"

int main(int argc, char **argv)
{
  ros::init(argc, argv, "compute_area_client");
  ros::NodeHandle n;
  ros::ServiceClient c = n.serviceClient<beginner_tutorials::ComputeArea>("compute_area");
  beginner_tutorials::ComputeArea srv;
  srv.request.radius = 5.0;
  if (c.call(srv))
    ROS_INFO("Area = %f", srv.response.area);
  else
    ROS_ERROR("Failed");
  return 0;
}
```

**`CMakeLists.txt` additions (1 mark):** add `add_service_files(FILES ComputeArea.srv)`, `generate_messages(DEPENDENCIES std_msgs)`, and the two `add_executable` / `target_link_libraries` blocks with `add_dependencies(..., beginner_tutorials_gencpp)`.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Paper, Q5a/5b]`**

**(a)** Compare the C++ and Python APIs for implementing a ROS service. List any two functional differences in how the server callback and client call are structured. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Write the **Python** implementation of a service called `/string_reverse` that takes a `string input` and returns a `string reversed` and a `bool success` flag. Provide the `.srv` file and both server and client scripts. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) C++ vs Python Service APIs (7 Marks)**

| Aspect | C++ | Python |
|---|---|---|
| **Server Callback** | A `bool` function taking `(Request&, Response&)` references (2 marks) | A function taking a single `Request` and returning a `Response` object (2 marks) |
| **Advertising** | `n.advertiseService("name", cb)` returns a `ServiceServer` (1 mark) | `rospy.Service("name", Type, cb)` (1 mark) |
| **Client Call** | `client.call(srv)` modifies the same object; returns `bool` (0.5 marks) | `proxy(req_obj)` looks like a function call; raises `ServiceException` on failure (0.5 marks) |
| **Wait for Service** | Optional via `ros::service::waitForService("name")` (built into `call()`) (0.5 marks) | Mandatory via `rospy.wait_for_service("name")` (0.5 marks) |

**(b) `/string_reverse` Python Implementation (7 Marks)**

**`srv/StringReverse.srv` (1 mark):**
```text
string input
---
string reversed
bool success
```

**Server (`string_reverse_server.py`) (3 marks):**
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
import rospy
from beginner_tutorials.srv import StringReverse, StringReverseResponse

def handle(req):
    return StringReverseResponse(req.input[::-1], True)

def server():
    rospy.init_node('string_reverse_server')
    rospy.Service('string_reverse', StringReverse, handle)
    rospy.spin()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    server()
```

**Client (`string_reverse_client.py`) (3 marks):**
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
import sys, rospy
from beginner_tutorials.srv import StringReverse, StringReverseRequest

def client(s):
    rospy.wait_for_service('string_reverse')
    proxy = rospy.ServiceProxy('string_reverse', StringReverse)
    try:
        resp = proxy(StringReverseRequest(s))
        return resp.reversed, resp.success
    except rospy.ServiceException as e:
        return None, False

if __name__ == "__main__":
    s = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "KTU"
    r, ok = client(s)
    print("Reversed:", r, "Success:", ok)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `---` separator** in the `.srv` file → entire build fails (–2 marks).
> 2. **Not adding `add_dependencies(... beginner_tutorials_gencpp)`** → linker error at build time, not compile time (–2 marks).
> 3. **Calling `client.call()` before `ros::service::waitForService()`** in a fresh client node → silent failure on slow hardware (–1 mark).
> 4. **Confusing `ros::ServiceServer` (server handle) with `ros::ServiceClient` (client handle)** in the diagram → examiner deducts for the wrong arrow direction (–1 mark).
> 5. **Writing `ros::spin()` in the client** → client hangs forever because the client doesn't have a callback loop. Use `ros::waitForShutdown()` only if you are *also* publishing or subscribing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before you enter the exam hall, tick every box below:**

- [ ] A **service** is **synchronous**, **blocking**, **one-to-one**, and **typed**.
- [ ] The `.srv` file is split by `---` into **Request** (top) and **Response** (bottom).
- [ ] The build system auto-generates `<PackageName>/<ServiceName>.h` (C++) and `<PackageName>/<ServiceName>.py` (Python) from the `.srv` file.
- [ ] `package.xml` must declare **`message_generation`** (build) and **`message_runtime`** (exec).
- [ ] `CMakeLists.txt` must call `add_service_files()` and `generate_messages()`.
- [ ] C++ server uses `n.advertiseService("name", callback)`.
- [ ] C++ client uses `n.serviceClient<T>("name")` and `client.call(srv)`.
- [ ] Python server uses `rospy.Service("name", Type, callback)`.
- [ ] Python client uses `rospy.wait_for_service("name")` then `rospy.ServiceProxy("name", Type)`.
- [ ] Use `persistent=true` (C++) for high-frequency service calls to amortize connection setup.
- [ ] Command-line tools: `rossrv show`, `rosservice list`, `rosservice call /name args...`.
- [ ] Common pitfall: missing `add_dependencies(..., beginner_tutorials_gencpp)` in `CMakeLists.txt`.
- [ ] **One-line definition for a 2-mark short question:** *"A ROS service is a synchronous, request/reply communication mechanism between a client and a server, defined by a `.srv` file specifying typed Request and Response messages."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using a Service
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Using a Service

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ROS Service**: A **Service** in the Robot Operating System is a synchronized, request/reply communication paradigm defined by a pair of messages — a *request* and a *response* — exchanged between a **Service Server** node and a **Service Client** node over a strongly-typed interface (specified in a `.srv` file).

Mathematically, a service is a one-to-one, bidirectional, blocking communication function:

$$
f : \text{Request} \rightarrow \text{Response}
$$

where a client invokes $f$ on a server and suspends its own thread of execution until the response arrives. This is fundamentally different from the asynchronous, many-to-many **Topic** model used in ROS pub/sub communication.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Waiter**
> Think of a **Service** as ordering food at a restaurant. You (the **Client**) tell the waiter (the **ROS Master / Service Proxy**) your exact order (the **Request**). The waiter walks to the kitchen (the **Service Server**), waits until the dish is prepared, and returns with your food (the **Response**). You cannot do anything else while waiting — this is *blocking*. In contrast, a **Topic** is like a radio broadcast: anyone listening hears everything, but no one replies to you personally.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Box

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Service Server** | Node that advertises a service and provides responses to incoming requests |
| **Service Client** | Node that calls the service using a `ServiceProxy` |
| **`.srv` file** | Interface description defining Request/Response message structure |
| **`rospy.wait_for_service()`** | Blocking call that suspends the client until the named service becomes available |
| **`ServiceProxy`** | Client-side object that encapsulates the remote procedure call |
| **Persistent Connection** | Reusable service link established once and held open for multiple calls |
| **One-Shot Connection** | Connection that is established, used once, and then torn down |

### 1.4 Why Services Matter in Robotics

> [!NOTE]
> Robots frequently need **deterministic, transactional** interactions: "Open the gripper," "Compute inverse kinematics for this pose," "Trigger a calibration routine." Topics are unsuitable here because they lack acknowledgement. Services provide the **acknowledgement and confirmation** that a discrete action has been completed — a requirement mandated by KTU Module 2 outcomes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Lifecycle of a Service Client

The end-to-end flow of using a service in ROS (Python / `rospy`) follows a strict, board-favored 5-step protocol:
