**Step 7 — Preemption (cancellation) math:**

If a cancel request arrives at time $t_c$ after the server has already published $F_{t_c}$ feedback samples, the server must:

$$F_{t_c} \to \text{CancelAck} \to \text{TerminalState} = \text{CANCELED}$$

The cancel acknowledgement must arrive at the client within:

$$R_{\text{cancel}} \le T_{\text{goal}} = 10{,}000 \text{ ms}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation note:** The above code listings are fully aligned with the official ROS 2 Iron/Jazzy tutorial baseline. They are *intentionally* the minimum viable pair (client + server) and demonstrate the full action lifecycle from goal submission to terminal state.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Action Primitives in `rclpy` / `rclcpp`

| Operation | Python (rclpy) | C++ (rclcpp) | Returns |
|---|---|---|---|
| Construct client | `ActionClient(node, type, name)` | `rclcpp_action::Client<NodeT>` | Handle |
| Wait for server | `client.wait_for_server()` | `client->wait_for_action_server()` | `bool` |
| Send goal | `client.send_goal_async(g, fb_cb)` | `client->async_send_goal(g, opts)` | `Future` |
| Get result | `goal_handle.get_result_async()` | `goal_handle->async_get_result()` | `Future` |
| Cancel goal | `goal_handle.cancel_goal_async()` | `goal_handle->async_cancel_goal()` | `Future` |
| Publish feedback | `goal_handle.publish_feedback(fb)` | `goal_handle->publish_feedback(fb)` | `void` |
| Mark success | `goal_handle.succeed()` | `goal_handle->succeed(result)` | `GoalResponse` |
| Mark aborted | `goal_handle.abort()` | `goal_handle->abort(result)` | `GoalResponse` |
| Mark canceled | `goal_handle.canceled()` | `goal_handle->canceled(result)` | `GoalResponse` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Action Client–Server Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Action Client Node] -->|send_goal_async| B[DDS Domain]
    B -->|Goal Topic| C[Action Server Node]
    C -->|Goal Handle Accepted| B
    B -->|Goal Response| A
    C -->|publish_feedback| B
    B -->|Feedback Topic| A
    A -->|cancel_goal_async| B
    C -->|succeed or abort or canceled| B
    B -->|Result Topic| A
    A -->|spin executor| AE[rclpy spin or rclcpp spin]
    C -->|spin executor| CE[rclpy spin or rclcpp spin]
```

### 4.2 Action State Machine — Client Side

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unconfigured
    Unconfigured --> Inactive : ActionClient constructed
    Inactive --> Sent : send_goal_async called
    Sent --> Accepted : server accepts goal
    Sent --> Aborted : server rejects goal
    Accepted --> Executing : server starts processing
    Executing --> Executing : feedback received
    Executing --> Succeeded : publish final result OK
    Executing --> Canceling : cancel request issued
    Executing --> Aborted : server aborts
    Canceling --> Canceled : server acknowledges cancel
    Succeeded --> [*]
    Aborted --> [*]
    Canceled --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Three-File Compilation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Fibonacci dot action file] --> B1[rosidl generate]
    B1 --> C1[Goal msg type]
    B1 --> C2[Result msg type]
    B1 --> C3[Feedback msg type]
    C1 --> D1[Action Client Library]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C1 --> D2[Action Server Library]
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D2
    D1 --> E1[User Application]
    D2 --> E1
```

### 4.4 Goal Lifecycle Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client as Action Client
    participant Server as Action Server
    Client->>Server: 1. send_goal_async
    Server-->>Client: 2. goal response accepted
    loop Feedback loop until terminal
        Server-->>Client: 3. publish_feedback
    end
    Server-->>Client: 4. result with status SUCCEEDED
    Note over Client,Server: User may inject cancel_goal_async at any point
    Client->>Server: 5. cancel_goal_async (optional)
    Server-->>Client: 6. terminal status CANCELED
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*With respect to ROS 2, list any three differences between a **Service** and an **Action**. Why is an action preferred for autonomous robot navigation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Service | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous — client *blocks* until reply. | Asynchronous — client gets a *future*; can do other work. |
| 2 | No feedback during execution. | Continuous feedback via callback. |
| 3 | Cannot be preempted/canceled once accepted. | Cancellable via `cancel_goal_async` at any time. |

**Why preferred for navigation:** Autonomous navigation (e.g., `nav2 NavigateToPose`) is a *long-running, safety-critical* task. The robot must continuously stream its progress (distance to goal, time-to-arrival), and a human operator must be able to **cancel** the mission mid-way (e.g., obstacle detected, battery low). Services cannot provide either capability; actions can. **(1 mark for reasoning.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
*Explain the purpose of the three sections separated by `---` in a ROS 2 `.action` file. Give one example of an action used in the `turtlesim` package.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Section 1 (before first `---`):** Defines the **Goal** — what the client requests. **(1 mark)**
2. **Section 2 (between first and second `---`):** Defines the **Result** — the one-shot terminal outcome. **(1 mark)**
3. **Section 3 (after second `---`):** Defines the **Feedback** — periodic intermediate updates. **(1 mark)**

**Example:** `turtlesim/action/RotateAbsolute` — goal: `theta` (desired orientation), result: `delta` (total rotation performed), feedback: `remaining` (radians yet to rotate).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model QP]*

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Describe the **seven-state action client lifecycle** in ROS 2. Use a labeled diagram to show the transitions, and state the four possible **terminal states**.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Write the `.action` specification for a `NavigateToPose` action that accepts a 2D goal coordinate $(x, y)$ and an orientation $\theta$, and streams `(distance_remaining, eta_seconds)` as feedback, with a final `(success, total_time)` as result. Briefly explain the C++ and Python client code stubs to call this action.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Seven-state action client lifecycle (7 marks):**

The ROS 2 action client walks through these states:

1. **Unconfigured** — `ActionClient` instantiated but not yet usable.
2. **Inactive** — Client is ready, no goal sent yet.
3. **Sent** — `send_goal_async()` has been invoked, awaiting server response.
4. **Accepted** — Server replied with a `GoalHandle` and started working.
5. **Executing** — Server is computing/publishing; `feedback_callback` fires periodically.
6. **Canceling** — `cancel_goal_async()` was issued; server is winding down.
7. **Terminal states** (4): `SUCCEEDED`, `CANCELED`, `ABORTED`, `UNKNOWN`.

(Refer to the state machine in Section 4.2 of these notes for the full diagram — *5 marks for the description, 2 marks for the labeled diagram and the 4 terminal states*.)

**(b) Action specification and code stubs (7 marks):**

`NavigateToPose.action`:

```text
float64 x
float64 y
float64 theta
---
bool success
float64 total_time
---
float64 distance_remaining
float64 eta_seconds
```

**Explanation (1 mark per element):**
- **Goal:** The 2D target pose $(x, y, \theta)$ the robot must reach.
- **Result:** Boolean success flag and total traversal time in seconds.
- **Feedback:** Live distance-to-goal (meters) and ETA (seconds).

**Python stub (rclpy) (3 marks):**

```python
from rclpy.action import ActionClient
from my_robot_interfaces.action import NavigateToPose

client = ActionClient(node, NavigateToPose, 'navigate_to_pose')
client.wait_for_server()
goal = NavigateToPose.Goal()
goal.x, goal.y, goal.theta = 5.0, 3.0, 1.57

def feedback_cb(fb):
    node.get_logger().info(f'{fb.feedback.distance_remaining:.2f} m left')

future = client.send_goal_async(goal, feedback_callback=feedback_cb)
```

**C++ stub (rclcpp) (3 marks):**

```cpp
#include "rclcpp_action/rclcpp_action.hpp"
#include "my_robot_interfaces/action/navigate_to_pose.hpp"

using NavigateToPose = my_robot_interfaces::action::NavigateToPose;
using GoalHandle = rclcpp_action::ClientGoalHandle<NavigateToPose>;

auto client = rclcpp_action::create_client<NavigateToPose>(node, "navigate_to_pose");
client->wait_for_action_server();

NavigateToPose::Goal goal_msg;
goal_msg.x = 5.0; goal_msg.y = 3.0; goal_msg.theta = 1.57;

auto send_goal_options = rclcpp_action::Client<NavigateToPose>::SendGoalOptions();
send_goal_options.feedback_callback = [](GoalHandle::SharedPtr,
                                          const std::shared_ptr<const NavigateToPose::Feedback> fb) {
    RCLCPP_INFO(node->get_logger(), "%.2f m left", fb->distance_remaining);
};

auto goal_handle_future = client->async_send_goal(goal_msg, send_goal_options);
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model QP — Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Compare and contrast the **three ROS 2 communication primitives** — *Topics*, *Services*, and *Actions* — across the axes of *synchronous vs. asynchronous*, *feedback support*, *cancelability*, and *cardinality* (1-to-1, 1-to-many, many-to-many). Give one concrete robotics use case for each.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Draw the action client-server interaction diagram with **all five channels** (goal, result, feedback, cancel, status) labeled. Explain what happens when a client calls `cancel_goal_async()` while the server is in the `EXECUTING` state.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison table (7 marks):**

| Primitive | Sync / Async | Feedback | Cancelable | Cardinality | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Topic** | Async (fire-and-forget) | None inherent (only via the message payload itself) | N/A | Many-to-many | LiDAR point cloud streaming (`/scan`) |
| **Service** | Sync (blocks client) | None | No | 1-to-1 | Request current robot pose (`/get_pose`) |
| **Action** | Async (non-blocking, future-based) | Continuous via callback | Yes, via `cancel_goal_async` | 1 client ↔ 1 server (per goal) | Move arm to joint target (`MoveGroup`) |

(*Each primitive gets 2 marks for the table row + 1 mark aggregate for "use case" examples → 7 marks total*.)

**(b) Interaction diagram and cancel semantics (7 marks):**

The full 5-channel interaction is depicted in Section 4.4 of these notes (sequence diagram). The five channels are: **goal, feedback, result, cancel, status**. *(3 marks for the labeled diagram.)*

**Cancel semantics while in EXECUTING (4 marks):**
1. Client calls `goal_handle.cancel_goal_async()` — non-blocking, returns a future. *(1 mark)*
2. A `cancel` message is published to the cancel topic. *(1 mark)*
3. Server's `execute_callback` receives a cancellation signal (typically polled via `goal_handle->is_canceling()` in C++ or by registering a `cancel_callback` in Python). *(1 mark)*
4. Server invokes `goal_handle.canceled(result)` and publishes terminal state `CANCELED`. The client's `get_result_async()` future resolves with `status = STATUS_CANCELED`. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. **Confusing "Result" with "Feedback":** Result is a *single terminal* message. Feedback is *periodic*. Do not list `feedback` as a "result" in your answer.
> 2. **Forgetting the four terminal states:** Many students only list `SUCCEEDED`. The complete set is `SUCCEEDED`, `CANCELED`, `ABORTED`, `UNKNOWN` — losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Missing the cancel channel:** The action mechanism uses *five* DDS topics, not three. The cancel and status channels are often forgotten.
> 4. **Writing service code in action questions:** The `service.call(req)` pattern is *wrong* for actions. You must use `send_goal_async`, then `get_result_async`. Mixing them costs 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Omitting the `wait_for_server()` call:** Without it, the client may send a goal before the server is up, leading to a silent failure. Examiners specifically look for this line.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (pin to your study wall):**

- ☐ An **Action** is the ROS 2 primitive for *long-running, goal-oriented* tasks with feedback and cancel support.
- ☐ The `.action` file has **three sections** separated by `---` → **Goal | Result | Feedback** (in that order).
- ☐ An action uses **five DDS topics** under the hood: `/_action/send_goal`, `/_action/get_result`, `/_action/feedback`, `/_action/cancel_goal`, and the implicit status channel.
- ☐ The **client-side state machine has 7 states**; the **4 terminal states** are `SUCCEEDED`, `CANCELED`, `ABORTED`, `UNKNOWN`.
- ☐ Always call `client.wait_for_server()` *before* `send_goal_async()` to avoid silent failures.
- ☐ The client is **non-blocking** — it returns a `Future`/`goal_handle` and uses callbacks or `spin_until_future_complete()`.
- ☐ Three callback hooks to know: `feedback_callback`, `goal_response_callback`, and `result_callback`.
- ☐ Actions are implemented in `rclpy.action.ActionClient` (Python) and `rclcpp_action::Client<T>` (C++).
- ☐ Standard actions in ROS 2: `nav2_msgs/action/NavigateToPose`, `moveit_msgs/action/MoveGroup`, `turtlesim/action/RotateAbsolute`, `control_msgs/action/FollowJointTrajectory`.
- ☐ Actions sit on top of DDS, using `RELIABLE` QoS and `VOLATILE` durability (unlike services which use `TRANSIENT_LOCAL`).
- ☐ Preemption: send `cancel_goal_async()` — the server *must* check the cancel flag and transition to `CANCELED` terminal state.
- ☐ Exam mantra: *"Action = Goal + Feedback + Result + Cancelability, all asynchronous."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementing a More Sophisticated Action Server
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Implementing a More Sophisticated Action Server ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the **Robot Operating System (ROS 2 / ROS 1)** communication paradigm, an **Action Server** is a long-running, asynchronous, goal-oriented communication primitive that follows the **client–server action protocol** standardized within `rclcpp_action` (C++) or `rclpy.action` (Python). A *more sophisticated* action server extends the basic single-threaded, single-goal pattern to incorporate: - **Preemption and cancellation** logic (interrupting an in-flight goal) - **Continuous feedback streaming** (e.g., percentage completion, distance remaining, sensor telemetry) - **Multi-threading** (so feedback publishing is not blocked by heavy I/O) - **Goal policy handling** — `REJECT` vs `ACCEPT_AND_EXECUTE`, handling overlapping goals - **State machine integration** — explicit transition between `PROCESSING`, `PREEMPTING`, `SUCCEEDED`, `ABORTED`, `CANCELED` - **Custom action message definitions** with multi-field goals, results, and feedback. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this topic maps directly to **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Implement ROS nodes, topics, services, and actions for robot software architecture."* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pizza Delivery Tracker" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition (Plain English):** > Think of ordering a pizza from a delivery app. > 1. You **place an order (Goal)** — "Deliver a Margherita pizza to Flat 5B." > 2. The restaurant sends you **live updates (Feedback)** — "Pizza is being prepared → Out for delivery → 2 km away → Arrived." > 3. If you cancel after the pizza is out, the driver **stops** the task and the order is **CANCELED**. > 4. When delivered, you get a final **Result** — "Order delivered, total bill ₹450." > 5. If the pizza was burnt, you get an **ABORTED** status, not a success. A *sophisticated* action server is exactly this — a task manager that: - Accepts a goal, - Streams progress, - Listens for cancellation, - Returns a definitive result, - And runs all of this *without freezing other system operations*. ### 1.3 Why a "More Sophisticated" Server? A naive action server (e.g., the `SimpleActionServer` in `rospy` tutorials) executes one goal at a time, blocks its callback thread, and ignores preemption. In **real industrial robotics** (manipulator pick-and-place, autonomous navigation, drone missions), the robot must: | Requirement | Naive Server | Sophisticated Server | |---|---|---| | Cancel ongoing goal | ❌ | ✅ Preemption callback | | Publish continuous feedback | ❌ One-shot | ✅ Periodic, throttled | | Handle overlapping goals | ❌ Crash | ✅ Goal policy + queue | | Non-blocking execution | ❌ | ✅ `MultiThreadedExecutor` | | Structured state reporting | ❌ | ✅ `GoalStatus` enum | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Questions in ESE (End Semester Evaluation) frequently test the *callback trio* — `handle_accepted`, `execute_callback`, and `handle_accepted`/`cancel_callback` — and the goal state transitions. Memorize the **8 ROS 2 action states**: `UNKNOWN, ACCEPTED, EXECUTING, CANCELING, SUCCEEDED, CANCELED, ABORTED, REJECTED`. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Action Communication Triad An ROS action is defined by **three message slots** declared in a `.action` file: ```text # MyTask.action # === GOAL === (what the client requests) int32 task_id float64 target_distance --- # === RESULT === (final terminal output, sent ONCE) float64 total_time bool mission_success --- # === FEEDBACK === (periodic streaming output) float64 percent_complete float64 current_velocity ``` > [!NOTE] > The `---` separator is **syntactic sugar** that the ROS build system (`colcon` / `catkin`) parses into three separate message files internally. This is *exactly* how it is asked in KTU theory questions. ### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step A sophisticated action server's lifecycle unfolds as follows: 1. **Node Initialization** — Instantiate the action server with an action type, node handle, and callback trio. 2. **Goal Reception** — Client sends a goal; `GoalCallback` evaluates it (validate inputs, check feasibility). 3. **Goal Acceptance / Rejection** — Server returns `GoalResponse.ACCEPT` or `GoalResponse.REJECT`. 4. **Goal Execution Handoff** — `ExecuteCallback` is invoked *asynchronously* (in a separate thread by default in ROS 2). 5. **Periodic Feedback Loop** — Inside `execute_callback`, server computes progress and publishes `feedback` messages. 6. **Preemption Watch** — A separate `CancelCallback` (in ROS 2, the standard pattern uses `goal_handle->canceling()` polling inside `execute_callback`) checks if cancellation was requested. 7. **Terminal State** — The goal transitions to one of: `SUCCEEDED`, `ABORTED`, or `CANCELED`, followed by a final `Result` message. ### 2.3 Multi-Threading Architecture ROS 2 actions inherently decouple the *goal-handling* thread from the *execution* thread. The **executor's threading model** is the critical lever: $$\text{Total Parallel Slots} = N_{\text{callback threads}} \times \text{Topics/Services/Actions served}$$ The two canonical executors are: | Executor | Behavior | Use-Case | |---|---|---| | `SingleThreadedExecutor` | Serial callback processing | Deterministic debugging, simple nodes | | `MultiThreadedExecutor(N)` | N parallel OS threads, shared mutex | Sophisticated action servers w/ heavy I/O | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cheat-Sheet Table for the Exam Hall** | # | Concept / Formula | Symbol | Meaning / Value | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Action Message Triplet | $(G, F, R)$ | Goal, Feedback, Result | | 2 | Goal Acceptance Predicate | $A(g)$ | Boolean: server policy decision | | 3 | Feedback Frequency | $f_{fb}$ | $\text{Hz}$; throttled by `rclcpp::Rate` | | 4 | Preemption Token | $P(t)$ | $\in \{0, 1\}$; $1$ = cancel requested | | 5 | Goal Terminal Set | $\mathcal{T}$ | $\{SUCCEEDED, ABORTED, CANCELED\}$ | | 6 | State Transition Function | $\delta(s, e) \rightarrow s'$ | Current state $s$, event $e$, next state $s'$ | | 7 | Thread Pool Size | $N_t$ | Typically = CPU cores, max $\leq 16$ | | 8 | Goal Queue Policy | $\pi_g$ | `REJECT`, `ACCEPT_AND_OVERRIDE`, `ACCEPT_AND_DEFER` | | 9 | Feedback Delay Bound | $\Delta t \leq \frac{1}{f_{fb}}$ | Hard real-time guarantee | | 10 | Action Package Name | `my_robot_interfaces` | Convention: `_interfaces` | > **Note on notation:** The vertical bar $\vert$ is used here to denote cardinality/condition (e.g., $s \in \mathcal{T}\vert_{\text{terminal}}$), strictly avoiding the markdown-breaking `|` symbol. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMR):** Nav2's `navigate_to_pose` action server is a textbook sophisticated server — accepts pose goals, streams feedback (current pose, time remaining), supports cancellation when the operator presses "Stop." - **Manipulator Arms:** `MoveIt 2` action server `MoveGroup` accepts joint trajectories, publishes execution feedback, and aborts on collision. - **Drone Swarms:** A swarm action server coordinates many agents under one goal, with each agent reporting feedback. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — From a Service Call to an Action Call Consider a robot that must travel 100 m. We will formally derive *why* a service is insufficient and an action is mandatory. **Step 1 — Service Limitation Analysis** A service uses a synchronous request–reply: $$T_{\text{service}} = T_{\text{request}} + T_{\text{processing}} + T_{\text{reply}}$$ If $T_{\text{processing}} \gg T_{\text{deadline}}$, the call **blocks** the client. **Step 2 — Action Decoupling** An action separates the three time constants: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{goal\_send}} &\ll T_{\text{feedback\_loop}} \\ T_{\text{feedback\_loop}} &\gg T_{\text{result\_return}} \end{aligned} $$ The action is essentially a **two-phase commit** with a streaming middle phase. **Step 3 — Goal State Finite State Machine (FSM)** Let $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4, s_5, s_6, s_7\}$ be the set of all 8 goal states. The transition function $\delta$ is: $$ \begin{aligned} \delta(s_0,\, \text{GOAL\_RCVD}) &= s_1 \quad (\text{UNKNOWN} \rightarrow \text{ACCEPTED}) \\ \delta(s_1,\, \text{START}) &= s_2 \quad (\text{ACCEPTED} \rightarrow \text{EXECUTING}) \\ \delta(s_2,\, \text{CANCEL}) &= s_3 \quad (\text{EXECUTING} \rightarrow \text{CANCELING}) \\ \delta(s_3,\, \text{STOP}) &= s_5 \quad (\text{CANCELING} \rightarrow \text{CANCELED}) \\ \delta(s_2,\, \text{DONE}) &= s_4 \quad (\text{EXECUTING} \rightarrow \text{SUCCEEDED}) \\ \delta(s_2,\, \text{ERROR}) &= s_6 \quad (\text{EXECUTING} \rightarrow \text{ABORTED}) \\ \delta(s_0,\, \text{REJECT}) &= s_7 \quad (\text{UNKNOWN} \rightarrow \text{REJECTED}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — State Invariant** At any time $t$, exactly one of the following invariants holds: $$ \forall t, \; \text{count}(s \in \mathcal{T}) = 1 \;\; \text{where} \;\; \mathcal{T} = \{s_4, s_5, s_6, s_7\} $$ That is, **the goal terminates in exactly one terminal state**. **Step 5 — Feedback Throttling Derivation** To avoid flooding DDS (Data Distribution Service) with feedback, we throttle: $$ f_{fb} = \min\left(f_{\text{user}}, \frac{B_{\text{DDS}}}{S_{\text{msg}}}\right) $$ where $B_{\text{DDS}}$ is the available DDS bandwidth (typically **10 Mbps** on Wi-Fi) and $S_{\text{msg}}$ is the feedback message size. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Naive vs Sophisticated Action Server | Attribute | Naive Server | Sophisticated Server | |---|---|---| | `accept_callback` | Always returns `ACCEPT` | Validates goal, returns `REJECT` if invalid | | `execute_callback` | Linear, no preemption check | Polls `cancel_requested` flag inside loop | | `cancel_callback` | None | Explicitly handles cancel transitions | | Feedback Publishing | Single-shot at end | Periodic loop with `Rate(10)` | | Threading | Single-threaded | `MultiThreadedExecutor(4)` | | Error Handling | `throw` uncaught | Catches exceptions, returns `ABORTED` | | Result Return | Always success | Conditional: success/abort/cancel | | Goal Stacking | Not supported | `GoalPolicy::ACCEPT_AND_DEFER` | | Logging | None | `RCLCPP_INFO(node->get_logger(), ...)` | ### 3.3 Worked Example — Driving a Robot 100 m **Problem:** Implement (in pseudocode) a sophisticated action server that drives a robot to cover 100 m, publishing 10 Hz feedback, supporting cancellation, and timing out at 60 s. **Solution Walkthrough:** **Step 1 — Initialize rate and start time** $$ t_0 = \text{now}() , \quad \Delta t = t - t_0, \quad \text{rate} = 10 \text{ Hz} $$ **Step 2 — Main execution loop with termination logic** ``` rclcpp::Rate loop_rate(10); auto start = this->now(); auto feedback = std::make_shared(); auto result = std::make_shared(); while (rclcpp::ok()) { // Check for cancellation request if (goal_handle->is_canceling()) { goal_handle->canceled(result); RCLCPP_INFO(... "Goal CANCELED at distance %f", traveled); return; } // Check timeout auto elapsed = this->now() - start; if (elapsed.seconds() > 60.0) { result->mission_success = false; goal_handle->abort(result); return; } // Publish feedback feedback->percent_complete = (traveled / 100.0) * 100.0; feedback->current_velocity = 0.5; goal_handle->publish_feedback(feedback); // Increment distance traveled += 0.05; if (traveled >= 100.0) { result->total_time = elapsed.seconds(); result->mission_success = true; goal_handle->succeed(result); return; } loop_rate.sleep(); } ``` **Step 3 — Mathematical verification of feedback frequency** $$ T_{\text{loop}} = \frac{1}{f_{fb}} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1 \text{ s} $$ $$ N_{\text{feedback}} = \left\lfloor \frac{T_{\text{total}}}{T_{\text{loop}}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{100/0.5}{0.1} \right\rfloor = 2000 \text{ messages} $$ This satisfies real-time streaming without overwhelming DDS. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Sophisticated Action Server ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Client Node] -->|1. send_goal| B[Action Server Node] B --> C{GoalCallback} C -->|ACCEPT| D[ExecuteCallback Thread] C -->|REJECT| E[REJECTED State] D --> F[Feedback Publisher
Rate 10Hz] D --> G[State Machine Engine] G -->|is_canceling| H[Cancel Handler] G -->|timeout| I[ABORTED] G -->|success| J[SUCCEEDED] H --> K[CANCELED] I --> L[Result Publisher] J --> L K --> L L -->|2. result| A F -->|3. feedback| A D --> M[MultiThreadedExecutor
Threads 4] M --> D ``` ### 4.2 Goal State Transition Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> UNKNOWN UNKNOWN --> ACCEPTED: goal received UNKNOWN --> REJECTED: policy REJECT ACCEPTED --> EXECUTING: start EXECUTING --> SUCCEEDED: completion ok EXECUTING --> ABORTED: error thrown EXECUTING --> CANCELING: cancel request CANCELING --> CANCELED: cleanup done SUCCEEDED --> [*] ABORTED --> [*] CANCELED --> [*] REJECTED --> [*] ``` ### 4.3 Component Interaction Map (Feedback + Preemption) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as Action Client participant S as Action Server participant E as Executor C->>S: send_goal (target=100m) S-->>C: goal_response ACCEPT activate S loop Every 100ms S->>C: feedback (50%, vel=0.5) end C->>S: cancel_goal S-->>C: cancel_response ACCEPT S->>C: result (CANCELED, time=4.2s) deactivate S Note over E: MultiThreadedExecutor
4 threads, 1 active ``` ### 4.4 Callback Triad — Internal Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CB [Callback Triad] A1[handle_accepted] --> A2[execute_callback] A2 --> A3[handle_canceling implicit polling] end A1 -.validates.-> P1[Goal Policy] A2 -.streams.-> P2[Feedback Topic] A3 -.transitions.-> P3[Cancel State] P1 --> R[GoalResponse] P2 --> FB[FeedbackMessage] P3 --> RS[ResultMessage] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *List the three message components of a ROS action and explain the role of each in long-running task execution.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Goal (1 mark):** The initial request sent from the client. It contains the parameters of the task (e.g., target distance, target pose). It is sent *once*. - **Feedback (1 mark):** Periodic intermediate updates streamed from the server to the client during execution. It allows the client to monitor progress (e.g., percentage complete, sensor data). - **Result (1 mark):** The terminal output sent *once* when the goal ends, indicating success, abort, or cancel. It carries the final outcome of the task. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Mention any three advantages of an action server over a service in ROS.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Asynchronous execution (1 mark):** The client is not blocked during long-running tasks; it can continue other work. 2. **Feedback streaming (1 mark):** Continuous progress updates are provided, which a service cannot offer. 3. **Preemption support (1 mark):** The goal can be canceled mid-execution; services do not allow this once the call has been dispatched. --- ### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Choice (14 Marks) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Define an action server. With a neat diagram, explain the client–server interaction of an action server in ROS 2. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write the algorithm (pseudocode) for a sophisticated action server that drives a robot 100 m, publishes feedback at 10 Hz, supports cancellation, and aborts on timeout. Explain how the `MultiThreadedExecutor` improves responsiveness. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution **(a) Part Solution (7 marks):** - **Definition (2 marks):** An action server is a long-running, goal-oriented communication node that implements the ROS 2 action protocol. It accepts goals, streams feedback, supports preemption, and returns a definitive result. - **Client–Server Interaction (4 marks):** 1. Client sends `Goal` message. 2. Server's `GoalCallback` evaluates and returns `ACCEPT` or `REJECT`. 3. Upon accept, `ExecuteCallback` is invoked in a separate thread. 4. Server periodically publishes `Feedback` messages. 5. Server returns `Result` upon completion (with terminal state: `SUCCEEDED`, `ABORTED`, or `CANCELED`). - **Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to **Section 4.1** above — the block architecture. **(b) Part Solution (7 marks):** - **Pseudocode (4 marks):** Refer to the worked example in **Section 3.3**. - **MultiThreadedExecutor explanation (3 marks):** - A `SingleThreadedExecutor` runs all callbacks sequentially; if one blocks (e.g., reading I/O), feedback publishing stalls. - `MultiThreadedExecutor(4)` spawns **4 OS threads**, each capable of processing callbacks in parallel. - In the action server, the **execute thread**, **feedback publisher**, **service callbacks**, and **timer callbacks** can all run concurrently without head-of-line blocking. - **Critical:** care must be taken to use mutexes or atomic flags when sharing state between threads (e.g., the `goal_handle` pointer). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** What is a custom action message? Write a sample `.action` file for a "Firefighting Mission" action that has a goal specifying fire location, feedback reporting water-level percentage, and a result reporting success status and extinguished area. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the goal state machine in ROS 2. List all goal states and the conditions that cause transitions between them. How does a sophisticated action server handle the transition from `EXECUTING` to `CANCELED`? **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution **(a) Part Solution (7 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** A custom action message is a user-defined `.action` file in ROS 2 that defines the goal, result, and feedback fields specific to a robot's task. It is compiled by the `rosidl` middleware interface generator into language-specific types (C++/Python). **Sample `.action` file (5 marks):** ```text # FirefightingMission.action # === GOAL === geometry_msgs/Point fire_location float32 mission_duration_limit --- # === RESULT === bool fire_extinguished float32 area_covered_sqm float32 total_water_used --- # === FEEDBACK === float32 water_level_percent float32 distance_to_fire string current_phase # "APPROACHING" / "SPRAYING" / "RETURNING" ``` - **Goal (1.5 marks):** `fire_location` (Point) + duration limit. - **Result (1.5 marks):** Success boolean + area + water usage. - **Feedback (2 marks):** Real-time water level, distance, phase string. **(b) Part Solution (7 marks):** **State Machine (4 marks):** Refer to the FSM derivation in **Section 3.1, Step 3**. The 8 states are: `UNKNOWN, ACCEPTED, EXECUTING, CANCELING, SUCCEEDED, CANCELED, ABORTED, REJECTED`. **Transition conditions (2 marks):** | From | To | Trigger | |---|---|---| | UNKNOWN | ACCEPTED | `goal_response(ACCEPT)` | | UNKNOWN | REJECTED | `goal_response(REJECT)` | | ACCEPTED | EXECUTING | `execute_callback` begins | | EXECUTING | CANCELING | `cancel_request = true` | | CANCELING | CANCELED | Cleanup complete | | EXECUTING | SUCCEEDED | Goal logic returns success | | EXECUTING | ABORTED | Exception or unrecoverable error | **Handling `EXECUTING → CANCELED` (1 mark):** Inside the `execute_callback`, the server polls `goal_handle->is_canceling()` (or `goal_handle->cancel_requested` in older APIs). When `true`, it stops the robot, calls `goal_handle->canceled(result)`, and exits the loop. The result is sent once, and the goal transitions to `CANCELED`. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the `---` separator** in `.action` files → marks deducted (0.5–1 per error). > 2. **Confusing Service and Action:** Writing "Actions are synchronous" — they are *asynchronous*. (-2 marks) > 3. **Omitting the Cancel state:** Students often list only 4 states instead of 8. (-2 marks) > 4. **Not using `goal_handle->publish_feedback(...)` syntax:** Some students write `pub->publish(feedback)` directly, missing the goal-handle binding. (-1 mark) > 5. **Skipping thread-safety discussion** when `MultiThreadedExecutor` is involved. (-1 mark) > 6. **In diagrams:** not labeling the `---` separators between Goal/Result/Feedback boxes in a `.action` diagram — a common KTU pet peeve. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - [ ] **Action Triplet:** Goal, Feedback, Result — defined in `.action` file with `---` separators. - [ ] **Eight Goal States:** UNKNOWN, ACCEPTED, EXECUTING, CANCELING, SUCCEEDED, CANCELED, ABORTED, REJECTED. - [ ] **Four Terminal States:** SUCCEEDED, CANCELED, ABORTED, REJECTED. - [ ] **Callback Triad (ROS 2):** `handle_accepted`, `execute_callback`, `cancel_callback` (or polling-based cancel check). - [ ] **MultiThreadedExecutor:** enables concurrent callback processing; use mutexes for shared state. - [ ] **Preemption:** the ability of the client to cancel an in-flight goal via the `cancel_goal` service call. - [ ] **Feedback Throttling:** use `rclcpp::Rate(10)` to avoid DDS flooding; $f_{fb} \leq B_{DDS} / S_{msg}$. - [ ] **Goal Policies:** REJECT, ACCEPT_AND_DEFER, ACCEPT_AND_OVERRIDE. - [ ] **Custom Action Files:** placed in `my_robot_interfaces/action/MyTask.action`; build with `rosidl_default_generators`. - [ ] **Action vs Service:** Actions are for *long-running* tasks; services are for *quick request/reply*. - [ ] **Action vs Topic:** Actions are goal-oriented and acknowledge termination; topics are one-way continuous streams with no terminal state. - [ ] **Default Executor:** ROS 2 default uses `SingleThreadedExecutor`; switch to `MultiThreadedExecutor` for sophisticated servers. - [ ] **Real-World Examples:** Nav2 `navigate_to_pose`, MoveIt 2 `MoveGroup`, turtle sim `draw_square` action. - [ ] **Build System:** `colcon build --packages-select my_robot_interfaces` to generate action headers. - [ ] **Timeout Handling:** Use `this->now() - start_time > max_duration` to trigger `goal_handle->abort(result)`.

topics.title: Using the More Sophisticated Action
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Using the More Sophisticated Action

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Robot Operating System (ROS), a **More Sophisticated Action** refers to an advanced, long-running, asynchronous, and preemptible communication pattern built on top of the `actionlib` (ROS 1) or `rclpy_action` / `rclcpp_action` (ROS 2) frameworks. Unlike a simple Service (which blocks until completion and offers no feedback) or a Topic (which is fire-and-forget with no confirmation), a sophisticated Action implements a **distributed goal-oriented state machine** that exchanges three distinct message types:

1. **Goal** — sent from the *Action Client* to the *Action Server* to initiate a task.
2. **Feedback** — streamed continuously from the Server back to the Client during execution.
3. **Result** — delivered from the Server to the Client exactly once upon termination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** A "More Sophisticated Action" implies the usage of the **full action protocol** — including asynchronous goal handling, continuous feedback streaming, preemption, and result reporting. The KTU examiner expects students to articulate the *Goal–Feedback–Result (GFR) triad* explicitly.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Pizza Delivery Tracker 🍕

Imagine ordering a pizza through a mobile app:

- **Service Call (old way):** You place the order, and the app freezes with a spinning wheel until the pizza arrives 30 minutes later. You have no idea where the delivery boy is.
- **Topic (pub/sub):** The shop broadcasts "pizzas made" continuously. You cannot ask for *your* pizza specifically.
- **Sophisticated Action (new way):** You submit a `Goal` ("deliver one Margherita to my address"). The app gives you a **live map (Feedback)** updating the driver's location every 10 seconds. The driver can call you to say "I'm stuck in traffic" (Preemption). Eventually, you get a **Result** — "Delivered" or "Failed: Out of stock."

This is exactly how a sophisticated Action works in ROS: a robot arm might receive a goal to "pick up a cup," stream joint-angle feedback as it moves, allow the user to cancel mid-motion, and finally report success or failure.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded for Quick Recall)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Action Client** | The node that *sends goals* and *receives feedback/results*. |
| **Action Server** | The node that *accepts goals*, *executes them*, and *publishes feedback*. |
| **Goal Handle** | A unique object tracking one in-flight goal. |
| **Preemption** | The act of cancelling an already-accepted, running goal. |
| **State Machine** | Internal `PENDING → ACTIVE → (PREEMPTED / SUCCEEDED / ABORTED)`. |
| **Callback** | A user-defined function invoked on Goal, Feedback, or Result events. |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard metric in ROS actionlib: feedback publish rate is typically **1 Hz to 10 Hz**, configurable per server implementation. The actionlib protocol uses 5 internal states standardized by ROS.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Goal–Feedback–Result (GFR) Communication Triad

A more sophisticated action is defined by **three concurrent ROS topics and one service**, all auto-generated from an `.action` file:

```text
.action file declares:
    # Goal        (sent Client → Server)
    # ---         (separator)
    # Result      (sent Server → Client, once)
    # ---         (separator)
    # Feedback    (sent Server → Client, streamed)
```

**Example `.action` definition (Fibonacci sequence — the canonical ROS tutorial):**
```
int32 order
---
int32[] sequence
---
int32[] partial_sequence
```

### 2.2 The Seven Internal States of a ROS Action

The state machine inside an Action Server transitions through these states:

| State | Trigger |
|---|---|
| **PENDING** | Goal received, server has not yet started processing. |
| **ACTIVE** | Server is actively working on the goal. |
| **PREEMPTED** | Goal was successfully cancelled by client. |
| **SUCCEEDED** | Goal completed successfully. |
| **ABORTED** | Server failed to complete the goal. |
| **REJECTED** | Server refused the goal at submission time. |
| **RECALLING / RECALLED** | Goal cancelled before entering ACTIVE state. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This state machine is what makes actions *robust* for industrial robotics — a welding arm, for example, must be able to safely *abort* mid-weld if a sensor detects overheating, and ROS actions handle this natively without the programmer building the state machine from scratch.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Type |
|---|---|---|
| Goal Message Count | **1** (per task) | Discrete |
| Result Message Count | **0 or 1** (terminal) | Discrete |
| Feedback Rate | $f_{fb} \in \left[1,\ 10\right]$ | **Hz** |
| Total Communication Channels | **N = 3 topics + 1 cancel service** | Integer |
| State Transition Count | **Max 7 states** | Finite State Machine |
| Preemption Latency | $t_{preempt} \leq \Delta t_{feedback}$ | Seconds |
| Async Concurrency | **True (non-blocking I/O)** | Boolean |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** When writing $\vert x \vert$ in markdown tables, always use `\vert` instead of the pipe character `|` to avoid breaking the table parser.

### 2.4 Why "More Sophisticated"?

A **simple action** (as introduced in basic tutorials) typically:
- Sends a goal
- Waits for a single result
- Provides no preemption

A **more sophisticated action** adds:
1. ✅ **Callback-based asynchronous handling** (no thread blocking).
2. ✅ **Continuous feedback streaming** for progress monitoring.
3. ✅ **Preemption support** (graceful mid-task cancellation).
4. ✅ **Multiple concurrent goals** (managed via unique `GoalHandle` IDs).
5. ✅ **Result-aware terminal callbacks** distinguishing SUCCEEDED vs ABORTED.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs):** Navigate to a goal, stream battery/path feedback, allow preemption if a new higher-priority goal arrives.
- **Industrial Manipulators:** Execute a welding trajectory with seam-tracking feedback.
- **Drone Swarms:** Coordinate multi-drone delivery with continuous position feedback.
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** Smooth motion preemption when surgeon releases pedal.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Action Communication Topology

Let us formally derive the **minimum number of ROS primitives** required to instantiate a sophisticated action.

**Step 1 — Topic Count Derivation**

A sophisticated action must support bidirectional, typed communication. Each direction needs a distinct message flow:

$$
N_{topics} = N_{goal} + N_{feedback} + N_{result} = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* The Goal flows Client→Server, the Feedback flows Server→Client, and the Result flows Server→Client. Three independent ROS topics are required to decouple these streams.

**Step 2 — Service Count Derivation**

For preemption, the client must request cancellation through a service (because cancellation requires *acknowledgement*, not broadcast):

$$
N_{services} = N_{cancel} = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Total Communication Primitives**

$$
N_{total} = N_{topics} + N_{services} = 3 + 1 = 4
$$

> *Conclusion:* Every sophisticated ROS action uses **exactly 4 underlying ROS communication primitives** (3 topics + 1 service), all auto-generated by the `actionlib`/`rclcpp_action` middleware from a single `.action` definition file.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Simple Service vs. Topic vs. Sophisticated Action

| Parameter | Service | Topic | **Sophisticated Action** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Communication Pattern | Synchronous 1-to-1 | Asynchronous many-to-many | **Asynchronous 1-to-1 with state** |
| Feedback During Execution | ❌ None | ❌ None | ✅ **Continuous streaming** |
| Preemption | ❌ Not supported | ❌ Not applicable | ✅ **Native support** |
| Result Reporting | ✅ (in response) | ❌ None | ✅ **Typed terminal result** |
| Blocking Behavior | ✅ Blocks caller | ❌ Non-blocking | ✅/❌ **Configurable (async/sync)** |
| Multi-goal Concurrency | ❌ | N/A | ✅ **GoalHandle-per-goal** |
| State Machine | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ **7-state FSM** |
| Suitable for Long Tasks | ❌ (timeout risk) | ✅ | ✅ **Designed for long tasks** |

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step: Python (rclpy) Implementation Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Import the action client class**

$$
\texttt{from rclpy.action import ActionClient}
$$

**Step 2 — Instantiate the client inside a Node**

$$
\texttt{self.\_action\_client} = \texttt{ActionClient}(\texttt{self},\ \texttt{Fibonacci},\ \texttt{'fibonacci'})
$$

**Step 3 — Wait for the server (non-blocking on the executor)**

$$
\texttt{self.\_action\_client.wait\_for\_server()}
$$

**Step 4 — Build a Goal Message**

$$
g = \texttt{Fibonacci.Goal}()\ ;\ g.order = 10
$$

**Step 5 — Send Asynchronously with Three Callbacks**

$$
\texttt{future} = \texttt{self.\_action\_client.send\_goal\_async}(g,\ \texttt{feedback\_cb}=\texttt{feedback})
$$

> *The three callbacks are:*
> - **Goal Response Callback:** fired when server accepts/rejects.
> - **Feedback Callback:** fired every feedback message.
> - **Result Callback:** fired when goal terminates (success/failure/cancel).

**Step 6 — Handle the Result**

$$
r = \texttt{result.result} \quad \Rightarrow \quad r.sequence
$$

This is the *complete* control flow. Each step is non-blocking, which is the heart of "sophistication."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Action Client–Server Interaction Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant nodeC as Action Client Node
    participant nodeS as Action Server Node
    participant ctrl as Robot Hardware

    nodeC->>nodeS: 1. send_goal_async(GoalMsg)
    nodeS-->>nodeC: 2. goal_response (ACCEPTED / REJECTED)
    nodeS->>ctrl: 3. begin execution

    loop Continuous Feedback Stream
        nodeS->>nodeC: 4. feedback_cb(FeedbackMsg)
    end

    alt Goal Succeeds
        nodeS->>nodeC: 5a. result_cb(SUCCEEDED, ResultMsg)
    else Goal Aborts
        nodeS->>nodeC: 5b. result_cb(ABORTED, ResultMsg)
    else Client Preempts
        nodeC->>nodeS: 5c. cancel_goal_async()
        nodeS->>nodeC: 5d. result_cb(PREEMPTED, ResultMsg)
    end
```

> *Read this left-to-right and top-to-bottom. Note the **loop** block for streaming feedback and the **alt** block for the three possible terminal outcomes — this is the visual essence of a sophisticated action.*

---

### 4.2 Internal State Machine of an Action Server

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PENDING : goal received
    PENDING --> ACTIVE : server accepts
    PENDING --> REJECTED : server refuses
    PENDING --> RECALLED : client cancels before start
    RECALLED --> [*]
    REJECTED --> [*]

    ACTIVE --> SUCCEEDED : task completed OK
    ACTIVE --> ABORTED : task failed
    ACTIVE --> PREEMPTED : client cancels during run

    SUCCEEDED --> [*]
    ABORTED --> [*]
    PREEMPTED --> [*]

    RECALLING --> RECALLED : cancellation confirmed
    RECALLING --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `RECALLING` state is a transient sub-state used when a client cancels a goal *before* the server has transitioned it to `ACTIVE`. KTU examiners love asking the difference between **PREEMPTED** (cancelled *during* execution) and **RECALLED** (cancelled *before* execution).

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLIENT["ACTION CLIENT NODE"]
        goalBuilder["Goal Message Builder"]
        goalSender["send_goal_async"]
        fbHandler["Feedback Callback"]
        resultHandler["Result Callback"]
        cancelSender["cancel_goal_async"]
    end

    subgraph MIDDLEWARE["ROS 2 ACTION MIDDLEWARE rclcpp_action"]
        topicGoal["/fibonacci/_action/send_goal"]
        topicFB["/fibonacci/_action/feedback"]
        topicRes["/fibonacci/_action/result"]
        srvCancel["/fibonacci/_action/cancel_goal"]
    end

    subgraph SERVER["ACTION SERVER NODE"]
        goalListener["Goal Listener"]
        executor["Task Executor Loop"]
        stateMachine["7-State FSM Tracker"]
        preemptionHandler["Preemption Watchdog"]
    end

    goalBuilder --> goalSender
    goalSender --> topicGoal
    topicGoal --> goalListener
    goalListener --> stateMachine
    stateMachine --> executor
    executor --> topicFB
    topicFB --> fbHandler
    executor --> topicRes
    topicRes --> resultHandler
    cancelSender --> srvCancel
    srvCancel --> preemptionHandler
    preemptionHandler --> stateMachine
```

> *This block diagram shows the internal separation of concerns. The **client** is purely event-driven (callbacks), the **server** owns the **state machine**, and the **middleware** is the 4-primitive transport layer (3 topics + 1 service) — exactly as derived in Section 3.1.*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Q1]**

**Q1.** *List the three message types exchanged in a ROS action and briefly state the direction of flow for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Goal** — flows from Action Client to Action Server (1 mark).
2. **Feedback** — flows from Action Server to Action Client, streamed continuously during execution (1 mark).
3. **Result** — flows from Action Server to Action Client, sent exactly once upon goal termination (1 mark).

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q2]**

**Q2.** *Why is a sophisticated action preferred over a ROS Service for long-running robot tasks?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A Service is **synchronous and blocking**; the client thread is frozen until the response arrives, risking timeout for long tasks (1 mark).
- A Service provides **no intermediate feedback**, so the user cannot monitor progress (1 mark).
- A Service offers **no preemption**; once invoked, the task cannot be gracefully cancelled (1 mark).
- A sophisticated Action addresses all three limitations via async callbacks, continuous feedback, and built-in preemption.

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Question A]**

**Question A (14 marks):**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a sophisticated ROS Action. Clearly identify the Action Client, Action Server, the underlying ROS primitives, and the direction of message flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the seven internal states of an Action Server with a state-transition diagram. Differentiate between PREEMPTED and RECALLED states. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Action Architecture (7 marks):**

A sophisticated ROS Action consists of two primary nodes communicating over **four ROS primitives** (3 topics + 1 cancel service) auto-generated from a single `.action` definition file (1 mark).

**Components:**

- **Action Client Node:** Maintains a `GoalHandle` for each in-flight goal. Registers three callbacks — `goal_response_callback`, `feedback_callback`, and `result_callback` (1 mark).
- **Action Server Node:** Implements a 7-state Finite State Machine (FSM). On goal acceptance, it spawns a worker thread/timer that executes the task and publishes feedback (1 mark).
- **Middleware Primitives** (auto-generated):
  - `/<action_name>/_action/send_goal` — Topic (Client→Server) (1 mark)
  - `/<action_name>/_action/feedback` — Topic (Server→Client, streamed) (1 mark)
  - `/<action_name>/_action/result` — Topic (Server→Client, terminal) (0.5 mark)
  - `/<action_name>/_action/cancel_goal` — Service (Client→Server) (0.5 mark)

**Workflow:** Client sends a `Goal` message → Server responds with `ACCEPTED/REJECTED` → If accepted, server begins execution and streams `Feedback` messages at a configured rate (typically 1–10 Hz) → On completion (or cancellation), server publishes a single terminal `Result` message indicating `SUCCEEDED / ABORTED / PREEMPTED` (1 mark). A block diagram illustrating this flow earns the final architecture mark (1 mark) — refer to Section 4.3 of these notes.

**(b) Seven Internal States (7 marks):**

The Action Server's internal FSM is governed by the `actionlib`/`rclcpp_action` state machine. The seven states and their transitions are:

| # | State | Transition Trigger | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **PENDING** | Goal received; awaiting server processing | 0.5 |
| 2 | **ACTIVE** | Server accepts and begins work | 0.5 |
| 3 | **SUCCEEDED** | Task completed without error | 0.5 |
| 4 | **ABORTED** | Task failed unrecoverably | 0.5 |
| 5 | **PREEMPTED** | Client cancelled a goal *during* execution | 0.5 |
| 6 | **RECALLING** | Client cancelled a goal *before* ACTIVE | 0.5 |
| 7 | **RECALLED** | Cancellation of a PENDING goal confirmed | 0.5 |

**PREEMPTED vs RECALLED (2 marks):**

- **PREEMPTED** is the terminal state reached when the client sends `cancel_goal` *after* the server has transitioned the goal to **ACTIVE**. The server attempts a graceful shutdown, stops the actuator, and publishes a terminal `Result` with status `PREEMPTED` (1 mark).
- **RECALLED** is the terminal state reached when the client cancels a goal while it is still in **PENDING** (i.e., the server has not yet started work). The server simply removes the goal from its queue and publishes a terminal `Result` with status `RECALLED`. The transient state `RECALLING` exists to allow the server to acknowledge cancellation (1 mark).

> *A state-transition diagram earns 1 additional mark — refer to Section 4.2.*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Question B — Alternative Choice]**

**Question B (14 marks):**

**(a)** Describe the asynchronous callback mechanism of a ROS 2 Action Client. Why is this preferred over the synchronous `send_goal()` call for sophisticated applications? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a Fibonacci action server in pseudocode that streams partial sequences as feedback and supports preemption. Show the goal-handle lifecycle. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Asynchronous Callback Mechanism (7 marks):**

In ROS 2 (`rclcpp_action` / `rclpy_action`), the modern API exposes **non-blocking** methods that return a `Future` object, eliminating the need for the client thread to spin-wait (1 mark).

**Three Async Methods:**

1. `send_goal_async(goal_msg, feedback_cb=None)` — Returns a `GoalHandle` future. Invokes the *Goal Response Callback* when the server accepts/rejects (2 marks).
2. `get_result_async(goal_handle)` — Returns a future resolving to the `Result` message. Invokes the *Result Callback* upon terminal state (SUCCEEDED/ABORTED/PREEMPTED) (2 marks).
3. `cancel_goal_async(goal_handle)` — Returns a future resolving to a `CancelResponse` indicating whether the server honored the cancellation (1 mark).

**Why preferred over synchronous `send_goal()` (1 mark):**

The synchronous call **blocks the calling thread** until the server responds, which:
- Starves the ROS executor of cycles, preventing other callbacks (e.g., sensor data) from firing.
- Cannot coexist with multiple concurrent goals on a single client node.
- Is unsuitable for real-time systems where determinism is required.

The async pattern delegates work to the **executor** (MultiThreadedExecutor or default), enabling **true concurrency** and **event-driven** design.

---

**(b) Fibonacci Action Server — Pseudocode (7 marks):**

```text
Class FibonacciActionServer(Node):
    def __init__():
        self._server = ActionServer(
            self, Fibonacci,
            'fibonacci',
            execute_callback=self.execute_cb,
            goal_callback=self.goal_cb,
            handle_accepted_callback=self.handle_accepted_cb,
            cancel_callback=self.cancel_cb
        )

    def goal_cb(goal_request):              # 1 mark
        ACCEPT if (1 <= goal_request.order <= 50) else REJECT

    def handle_accepted_cb(goal_handle):    # 1 mark
        goal_handle.execute()               # spawns executor thread

    def cancel_cb(goal_handle):             # 1 mark
        return CancelResponse.ACCEPT        # graceful preemption

    def execute_cb(goal_handle):            # 4 marks
        sequence = [0, 1]
        feedback = Fibonacci.Feedback()
        feedback.partial_sequence = sequence

        for i in range(1, goal_handle.request.order):
            if goal_handle.cancel_requested:        # PREEMPTION check
                goal_handle.canceled()
                result = Fibonacci.Result()
                result.sequence = sequence
                return result                        # terminal PREEMPTED

            sequence.append(sequence[-1] + sequence[-2])
            feedback.partial_sequence = sequence
            goal_handle.publish_feedback(feedback)   # STREAM feedback
            time.sleep(1.0)                          # 1 Hz rate

        goal_handle.succeed()
        result = Fibonacci.Result()
        result.sequence = sequence
        return result                                # terminal SUCCEEDED
```

**Goal-Handle Lifecycle (annotated):**
- `PENDING` (in queue) → `ACTIVE` (after `handle_accepted`) → `SUCCEEDED / ABORTED / PREEMPTED` (terminal).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing PREEMPTED with ABORTED:** PREEMPTED is *user-initiated cancellation*; ABORTED is *server-side failure*. Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are conflated.
> 2. **Forgetting to call `goal_handle.publish_feedback()`:** Without this, the client never receives progress updates — a sophisticated action *requires* feedback streaming.
> 3. **Blocking the executor with `time.sleep()` inside a single-threaded executor:** This freezes ALL node callbacks. Use `MultiThreadedExecutor` or `ReentrantCallbackGroup`.
> 4. **Returning the result object without calling `succeed()` / `abort()` / `canceled()` first:** The terminal status MUST be set before returning, else the action hangs in ACTIVE state.
> 5. **Mixing up the `.action` file separators:** The order is `Goal --- Result --- Feedback`. Examiners check the file format in code-trace questions.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ A **Sophisticated Action** uses **3 topics + 1 service** under the hood.
- ✅ The **GFR triad** = Goal (Client→Server), Feedback (Server→Client, streamed), Result (Server→Client, once).
- ✅ **7 internal states**: PENDING, ACTIVE, SUCCEEDED, ABORTED, PREEMPTED, RECALLING, RECALLED.
- ✅ **PREEMPTED** = cancellation *during* ACTIVE; **RECALLED** = cancellation *during* PENDING.
- ✅ **Async callbacks** (`send_goal_async`, `get_result_async`, `cancel_goal_async`) are non-blocking.
- ✅ A single `GoalHandle` uniquely tracks one in-flight goal — supports concurrent goals.
- ✅ Feedback rate is configurable; standard is **1–10 Hz**.
- ✅ The `.action` file format: `GoalType --- ResultType --- FeedbackType`.
- ✅ Always call `goal_handle.succeed()` / `abort()` / `canceled()` **before** returning the result.
- ✅ For preemption, periodically check `goal_handle.cancel_requested` inside the execute loop.
- ✅ Use `MultiThreadedExecutor` if the action work blocks the main thread.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When a KTU question says *"compare action with service/topic"*, immediately draw the **Analytical Matrix from Section 3.2** and the **Block Diagram from Section 4.3**. Examiners reward visual answers generously (1–2 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subsystems - Actuation: Mobile Platform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Subsystems - Actuation: Mobile Platform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔧 Module 3 — Subsystems: Actuation (Mobile Platform)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Actuation Subsystem (Mobile Platform):** The actuation subsystem of a mobile robot platform is the *electromechanical energy-conversion layer* responsible for converting stored electrical (or chemical) energy into *controlled mechanical motion* of the robot's locomotion structure (wheels, tracks, legs, thrusters, or articulated joints), thereby producing the **linear and angular velocities** required for navigation in the operating environment.

In the context of KTU's *Robot Operating System* framework, the mobile platform is treated as a *base* that interacts with the higher-level ROS control stack through standardized message interfaces (e.g., `/cmd_vel`, `/odom`, `/joint_states`). The actuation subsystem is the **physical hardware endpoint** that closes the loop between ROS-level velocity commands and the actual movement of the robot in the world frame.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Body & a Car Drivetrain:**
> Think of the actuation subsystem as the *muscles and tendons* of the robot. Just like a human brain (your "ROS node") decides to walk, but the muscles (the "actuators") actually pull the bones (the "chassis and wheels") to produce motion, the actuation subsystem physically executes every motion command. In a car, the engine + transmission + wheels = the actuation subsystem. In a mobile robot, the **motor + gearbox + wheel/tracks + power electronics** = the actuation subsystem.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Parameters

The following engineering quantities are the **primary metrics** by which any mobile platform actuation is evaluated in KTU board examinations:

- **No-Load Speed (RPM or rad/s)** — Maximum theoretical wheel speed.
- **Stall Torque (N·m)** — Maximum torque the motor can supply at zero speed.
- **Nominal Torque & Nominal Speed** — Continuous operating point.
- **Gear Ratio (N:1)** — Trade-off between torque and speed.
- **Wheel Radius (r, in meters)** — Determines linear velocity.
- **Wheelbase (L) and Track Width (W)** — Geometric constraint parameters.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** — Number of independent actuators.
- **Payload Capacity (kg)** — Maximum additional mass supported.
- **Power Consumption (W)** — Energy drawn per unit time.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Hint:** Always quote *both* linear and angular units together (e.g., m/s AND rad/s). A common error is to report only one of them, leading to a loss of 1–2 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of Mobile Platform Actuation

The actuation subsystem of a mobile platform can be broken down into **four functional layers**, each of which is a potential KTU short-answer or 7-mark sub-question:

| Layer | Function | Typical Hardware | ROS Interface |
|------|----------|------------------|---------------|
| **L1 — Power Source** | Stores/supplies energy | Li-Po / Li-ion battery, BEC | `/battery_state` |
| **L2 — Motor Driver** | Regulates current/voltage to motor | H-Bridge (BTS7960), VESC, ODrive | UART/CAN/PWM |
| **L3 — Actuator (Motor + Gearbox)** | Converts electrical → mechanical | DC, BLDC, Servo, Stepper | Encoder feedback |
| **L4 — Locomotion Element** | Contacts ground to produce motion | Wheel, Track, Leg, Propeller | `joint_state` |

## 2.2 Mobile Platform Locomotion Taxonomy

A KTU-favourite classification question is the **type of mobile platform**. The four canonical classes are:

- **Wheeled (Holonomic & Non-Holonomic)** — Most common, ROS `diff_drive_controller` is built for this.
- **Tracked (Skid-Steer)** — Used in rough terrain (TurtleBot 4, military UGVs).
- **Legged (Multi-Point Contact)** — Humanoid, quadruped (Spot, ANYmal).
- **Aerial / Marine / Hybrid** — Drones, AUVs.

### 2.2.1 Wheeled Mobile Robot (WMR) Sub-Types

1. **Differential Drive (DD)** — Two independently driven wheels + castor.
2. **Ackermann Steering (Car-Like)** — One steering + one drive (or 4WD).
3. **Synchronous Drive (Synchro)** — All wheels rotate and steer together.
4. **Omnidirectional (Mecanum / Omni-wheel)** — 3 or 4 wheels allowing full 2D planar motion.

## 2.3 Motor Selection Logic (Why and How)

The "Why" behind motor selection in a mobile platform is governed by three competing constraints:

- **Torque demand** → load + acceleration + incline.
- **Speed demand** → mission max velocity.
- **Power/Energy budget** → battery life & thermal limits.

The "How" follows the standard DC motor selection algorithm:

1. Compute required linear force $F = m a + \mu m g$ (rolling + incline + acceleration).
2. Convert to wheel torque $\tau_w = F \cdot r$ where $r$ is wheel radius.
3. Compute motor torque using gear ratio $N$: $\tau_m = \tau_w / (N \cdot \eta)$ where $\eta$ is drivetrain efficiency.
4. Verify motor speed: $\omega_m = N \cdot v / r$ must lie *below* the no-load speed.
5. Verify continuous power: $P = \tau_m \cdot \omega_m \le P_{rated}$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **high-yield** — at least one formula from this table appears in every KTU ESE paper for this module.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Linear velocity from wheel | $v = \omega \cdot r$ | m/s | Wheel angular velocity × radius |
| 2 | Wheel torque from force | $\tau_w = F \cdot r$ | N·m | Force × moment arm |
| 3 | Motor torque with gear ratio | $\tau_m = \tau_w \,/\,(N \cdot \eta)$ | N·m | Includes drivetrain efficiency |
| 4 | Motor speed from wheel speed | $\omega_m = N \cdot \omega_w$ | rad/s | Gearing up speeds up motor |
| 5 | DD angular velocity (robot frame) | $\omega = (v_R - v_L) / W$ | rad/s | $W$ = track width |
| 6 | DD linear velocity (robot frame) | $v = (v_R + v_L) / 2$ | m/s | Average of two wheels |
| 7 | Instantaneous Centre of Rotation (ICR) radius | $R = v / \omega$ | m | Distance from robot to turning point |
| 8 | Curvature of path | $\kappa = \omega / v = 1/R$ | 1/m | Inverse radius of turn |
| 9 | Tractive force | $F_t = m \cdot a + \mu_r m g \cos\theta + m g \sin\theta$ | N | Force to move on slope $\theta$ |
| 10 | Power required | $P = F_t \cdot v = \tau \cdot \omega$ | W | Mechanical output power |
| 11 | No-load current power | $P_0 = V \cdot I_0$ | W | Power lost when motor idles |
| 12 | Wheel slip condition | $\lambda = (v - \omega r) / v$ | dimensionless | $\lambda = 0$ means pure rolling |
| 13 | Ackermann steering angle | $\delta = \arctan(L / R)$ | rad | $L$ = wheelbase |
| 14 | Energy of battery | $E = V \cdot C$ (in Wh) | Wh | $C$ in Ah |
| 15 | Battery runtime | $t = C / I_{avg}$ | h | Capacity ÷ average current draw |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used in ROS nodes such as `diff_drive_controller` (computes wheel velocities from `/cmd_vel`), `robot_localization` (EKF over `/odom`), and `nav2` (planner converts global path into feasible $v, \omega$ commands respecting the curvature constraint $\kappa \le \kappa_{max}$).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Industrial AGVs** | Differential or Ackermann drive for warehouse automation (Amazon Kiva, Fetch Robotics). |
| **Autonomous Cars** | Ackermann with drive-by-wire; the actuation layer is governed by the `vehicle_control` ROS 2 stack. |
| **Service Robots** | Differential drive (TurtleBot, Husarion ROSbot) — uses `ros2_control` hardware interface. |
| **Search & Rescue UGVs** | Tracked skid-steer for rough terrain (e.g., PackBot, Jackal). |
| **AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robots)** | Mecanum / omni-wheel for tight warehouse aisles. |
| **Planetary Rovers** | Rocker-bogie suspension + 6-wheel Ackermann-steered (NASA's Perseverance, Curiosity). |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Differential Drive Inverse Kinematics

**Given:** Desired robot body linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ in the world (or robot) frame.
**Find:** Required left and right wheel angular velocities $\omega_L, \omega_R$.

### Step 1 — Geometric Setup

Consider a differential-drive mobile robot with:
- Two drive wheels of radius $r$, separated by a track width $W$.
- Velocities along the ground at the wheel contact patches are $v_L$ (left) and $v_R$ (right).
- The robot's instantaneous centre of rotation (ICR) lies on the line connecting the two wheels, at a perpendicular distance $R$ from the wheelbase midpoint.

### Step 2 — Relate Wheel Velocities to ICR

For a rigid body rotating about the ICR with angular velocity $\omega$:

$$v_L = \omega \cdot (R - W/2)$$

$$v_R = \omega \cdot (R + W/2)$$

These follow from the fact that points further from the rotation centre move faster ($v = \omega \cdot d$).

### Step 3 — Solve for $\omega$ and $R$

Subtracting the two equations:

$$v_R - v_L = \omega \cdot W$$

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{W}$$

Adding the two equations:

$$v_R + v_L = 2 \omega \cdot R$$

### Step 4 — Derive Forward Kinematics

Substituting the expression for $\omega$ into the addition step:

$$v_R + v_L = 2 \left( \frac{v_R - v_L}{W} \right) R$$

$$R = \frac{W (v_R + v_L)}{2 (v_R - v_L)}$$

The linear velocity of the robot (defined as the velocity of the midpoint between the wheels) is:

$$v = \omega \cdot R = \frac{v_R - v_L}{W} \cdot \frac{W (v_R + v_L)}{2 (v_R - v_L)} = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$$

This completes the forward kinematics.

### Step 5 — Derive Inverse Kinematics

We invert the relations. Given desired $(v, \omega)$:

$$v_L = v - \omega \cdot \frac{W}{2}$$

$$v_R = v + \omega \cdot \frac{W}{2}$$

The corresponding wheel angular velocities (used by motor controllers):

$$\omega_L = \frac{v_L}{r} = \frac{v - \omega W/2}{r}$$

$$\omega_R = \frac{v_R}{r} = \frac{v + \omega W/2}{r}$$

### Step 6 — Verification / Sanity Check

If $v = 0$ and $\omega \ne 0$ (pure rotation about robot centre): $v_L = -\omega W/2$ and $v_R = +\omega W/2$. The two wheels move in opposite directions with equal magnitude — physically correct for in-place rotation.

If $\omega = 0$ and $v \ne 0$ (pure translation): $v_L = v_R = v$, both wheels move in the same direction with the same speed — physically correct for straight-line motion.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always show **both** forward and inverse kinematics. A 14-mark question on differential drive typically gives 5 marks for forward kinematics, 5 for inverse kinematics, and 4 for the analytical/numerical part.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Motor Torque & Power from Vehicle Dynamics

**Given:** A mobile robot of mass $m = 50$ kg, wheel radius $r = 0.1$ m, gear ratio $N = 30$, drivetrain efficiency $\eta = 0.85$, target acceleration $a = 0.5$ m/s², maximum incline $\theta = 10°$, coefficient of rolling friction $\mu_r = 0.02$.

**Find:** Required motor torque, motor speed, and peak power.

### Step 1 — Total Tractive Force Required

The force needed to propel the robot must overcome three components:

$$F_{inertia} = m \cdot a$$

$$F_{roll} = \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$$

$$F_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$

The total tractive force (sum of all three):

$$F_t = m a + \mu_r m g \cos\theta + m g \sin\theta$$

### Step 2 — Numerical Evaluation

Substituting values with $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$F_{inertia} = 50 \times 0.5 = 25 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{roll} = 0.02 \times 50 \times 9.81 \times \cos(10°) = 0.02 \times 50 \times 9.81 \times 0.9848 \approx 9.66 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{grade} = 50 \times 9.81 \times \sin(10°) = 50 \times 9.81 \times 0.1736 \approx 85.16 \text{ N}$$

Therefore:

$$F_t = 25 + 9.66 + 85.16 \approx 119.82 \text{ N}$$

### Step 3 — Convert Force to Wheel Torque

$$\tau_w = F_t \cdot r = 119.82 \times 0.1 = 11.982 \text{ N·m}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Motor Torque

$$\tau_m = \frac{\tau_w}{N \cdot \eta} = \frac{11.982}{30 \times 0.85} = \frac{11.982}{25.5} \approx 0.470 \text{ N·m}$$

### Step 5 — Motor Speed for $v_{max} = 1.5$ m/s

Wheel angular velocity:

$$\omega_w = \frac{v}{r} = \frac{1.5}{0.1} = 15 \text{ rad/s}$$

Motor angular velocity after gearing:

$$\omega_m = N \cdot \omega_w = 30 \times 15 = 450 \text{ rad/s}$$

Convert to RPM:

$$\omega_{RPM} = 450 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} \approx 4297 \text{ RPM}$$

### Step 6 — Required Peak Mechanical Power

$$P_{mech} = \tau_m \cdot \omega_m = 0.470 \times 450 = 211.5 \text{ W}$$

Add 20% safety margin for transient loads and efficiency of motor controller (~95%):

$$P_{rated} = 1.25 \times P_{mech} \approx 264.4 \text{ W}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A standard ROSbot / TurtleBot uses motors in the 50–150 W range. The 264 W figure derived above indicates that this 50 kg platform requires *industrial-grade* actuators, not hobby motors.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ackermann Steering Geometry

### Step 1 — Setup

For a car-like (Ackermann) robot with wheelbase $L$ (distance between front and rear axles), when the vehicle follows a circular path of radius $R$ measured from the rear axle's midpoint, the **inner** and **outer** front-wheel steering angles are:

$$\delta_i = \arctan\left(\frac{L}{R - T/2}\right)$$

$$\delta_o = \arctan\left(\frac{L}{R + T/2}\right)$$

where $T$ is the front track width.

### Step 2 — Mean Steering Angle (Practical Approximation)

For ROS planning, a single effective steering angle is used:

$$\delta = \arctan\left(\frac{L}{R}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Curvature Bound

The maximum curvature is determined by the mechanical steering limit $\delta_{max}$:

$$\kappa_{max} = \frac{1}{R_{min}} = \frac{\tan(\delta_{max})}{L}$$

This is enforced by the ROS 2 `nav2` controller as a *kinematic constraint* on the path.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mobile Platform Comparison

| Parameter | Differential Drive | Ackermann | Mecanum (Omni) | Tracked (Skid) |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| **DOF in ground plane** | 2 (x, θ) | 2 (x, θ) | 3 (x, y, θ) | 2 (x, θ) |
| **Holonomic?** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **In-place rotation?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Limited |
| **Lateral motion?** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Yes | ❌ |
| **Typical ROS Controller** | `diff_drive_controller` | `ackermann_steering_controller` | `mecanum_drive_controller` | `diff_drive_controller` (skid) |
| **Wheel slip tolerant?** | Low | Low | High (designed for slip) | High |
| **Terrain suitability** | Smooth indoor | Roads | Flat warehouse | Rough outdoor |
| **Energy efficiency** | High | High | Moderate (slip losses) | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Holonomic vs Non-Holonomic:** A mobile platform is *holonomic* if the number of controllable DOFs equals the total DOFs. Differential drive and Ackermann are non-holonomic because they cannot move sideways. Mecanum and Swedish-wheel platforms are holonomic.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Mobile Platform Actuation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pwr1[Battery Pack 24V LiPo] --> pwr2[Voltage Regulator 5V 3.3V]
    pwr1 --> pwr3[Motor Driver VESC / ODrive]
    pwr3 --> m1[BLDC Motor Left]
    pwr3 --> m2[BLDC Motor Right]
    m1 --> g1[Gearbox N:1]
    m2 --> g2[Gearbox N:1]
    g1 --> w1[Left Drive Wheel radius r]
    g2 --> w2[Right Drive Wheel radius r]
    w1 --> fb1[Encoder Left]
    w2 --> fb2[Encoder Right]
    fb1 --> pwr3
    fb2 --> pwr3
    fb1 --> ros1[ros2_control Hardware Interface]
    fb2 --> ros1
    ros1 --> ros2[Diff Drive Controller Node]
    ros2 --> ros3[cmd_vel Subscriber]
    ros3 --> nav1[Nav2 Planner BT]
    nav1 --> ros3
    pwr2 --> ros1
    pwr2 --> ros2
    pwr2 --> sb[Safety Stop E Stop Circuit]
    sb --> pwr3
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — ROS ↔ Actuation Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Nav2 Planner] -->|cmd_vel v omega| B[Diff Drive Controller]
    B -->|joint command| C[ros2_control HW Interface]
    C -->|PWM UART CAN| D[Motor Driver]
    D --> E[BLDC Motor]
    E --> F[Gearbox]
    F --> G[Wheel]
    G --> H[Encoder]
    H -->|feedback| C
    C -->|joint state| B
    B -->|odom| A
```

## 4.3 Mobile Platform Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MP[Mobile Platform] --> W[Wheeled]
    MP --> T[Tracked]
    MP --> L[Legged]
    MP --> S[Specialty Aerial Marine]
    W --> DD[Differential Drive]
    W --> AC[Ackermann]
    W --> SY[Synchro Drive]
    W --> OM[Omnidirectional Mecanum]
    DD --> H1[Non Holonomic]
    AC --> H1
    SY --> H2[Quasi Holonomic]
    OM --> H3[Holonomic]
    T --> SK[Skid Steer]
    L --> H4[Quadruped]
    L --> H5[Humanoid Bipedal]
    S --> H6[Aerial Quadrotor]
    S --> H7[Marine AUV USV]
```

## 4.4 Motor Driver ↔ Motor ↔ Encoder Wiring Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Power Chain
        BAT[Battery 24V] --> FUSE[Fuse and E Stop] --> DRV[3 Phase Inverter]
        DRV --> M[3 Phase BLDC Motor]
    end
    subgraph Feedback Chain
        M --> ENC[Hall Sensor or Quadrature Encoder]
        ENC --> MCU[STM32 / ESP32 MCU]
        MCU --> DRV
        MCU -->|Serial UART| ROS[ROS2 Hardware Interface Node]
    end
    ROS -->|cmd_vel| MCU
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Learning Aid:** These four diagrams together capture (1) the *power flow* from battery to wheels, (2) the *closed-loop control* through ROS 2, (3) the *taxonomy of mobile platforms*, and (4) the *electrical wiring* of a single BLDC motor driver. Together they form a complete mental model of mobile platform actuation as required by KTU 2024.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the actuation subsystem of a mobile robot platform. List any four key performance parameters used to evaluate it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **actuation subsystem** of a mobile robot platform is the electromechanical assembly that converts stored energy (typically electrical energy from a battery) into controlled mechanical motion at the locomotion elements (wheels, tracks, legs, or thrusters) to produce translation and rotation of the robot in its environment. (1.5 marks)

Four key performance parameters are:
1. **Stall Torque** (N·m) — maximum torque the motor can deliver at zero speed.
2. **No-Load Speed** (RPM) — maximum wheel speed with no load.
3. **Gear Ratio** — trade-off between torque and speed.
4. **Power Consumption** (W) — energy drawn per unit time. (1.5 marks)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Compare holonomic and non-holonomic mobile platforms. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A mobile platform is **holonomic** if the number of *controllable* degrees of freedom (DOF) equals the total DOF in the plane, allowing instantaneous motion in any direction. A **non-holonomic** platform has fewer controllable DOFs than total DOFs and is constrained by non-integrable velocity relations. (2 marks)

- *Holonomic example:* **Mecanum-wheel robot** — can translate sideways.
- *Non-holonomic example:* **Differential-drive robot** (e.g., TurtleBot) — cannot move sideways. (1 mark)

---

## 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A — Differential Drive Mobile Robot (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**
