**A differential-drive mobile robot has wheel radius $r = 0.08$ m and track width $W = 0.35$ m. The robot must move with linear velocity $v = 0.4$ m/s and angular velocity $\omega = 0.5$ rad/s.**

**(a) Derive the forward and inverse kinematics of the differential drive robot, and compute the required left and right wheel angular velocities $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$ for the given $v$ and $\omega$.** (7 marks)

**(b) If each motor can supply a maximum torque of $0.5$ N·m before gearing and is connected through a gear ratio of $N = 25$ with drivetrain efficiency $\eta = 0.9$, determine the maximum total tractive force the robot can produce. State any assumptions.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Kinematic Derivation & Numerical Computation — 7 marks**

**Forward Kinematics Derivation (4 marks):**

For a differential drive, let $v_L, v_R$ be the contact-patch velocities. The robot rotates about the instantaneous centre of rotation (ICR). By rigid-body kinematics:

$$v_L = \omega \left( R - \frac{W}{2} \right), \quad v_R = \omega \left( R + \frac{W}{2} \right)$$

Subtracting:

$$v_R - v_L = \omega W \;\Rightarrow\; \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{W}$$

Adding and using $v = \omega R$:

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$$

**Inverse Kinematics (2 marks):**

Given desired $v$ and $\omega$:

$$v_L = v - \frac{\omega W}{2}, \quad v_R = v + \frac{\omega W}{2}$$

$$\omega_L = \frac{v_L}{r}, \quad \omega_R = \frac{v_R}{r}$$

**Numerical Evaluation (1 mark):**

$$v_L = 0.4 - \frac{0.5 \times 0.35}{2} = 0.4 - 0.0875 = 0.3125 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_R = 0.4 + \frac{0.5 \times 0.35}{2} = 0.4 + 0.0875 = 0.4875 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\omega_L = \frac{0.3125}{0.08} = 3.906 \text{ rad/s} \quad (\approx 37.3 \text{ RPM})$$

$$\omega_R = \frac{0.4875}{0.08} = 6.094 \text{ rad/s} \quad (\approx 58.2 \text{ RPM})$$

---

**(b) Maximum Tractive Force — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Motor torque before gearing** (given): $\tau_m = 0.5$ N·m.

**Step 2 — Torque transmitted to wheel after gearing:**

$$\tau_w = \tau_m \cdot N \cdot \eta = 0.5 \times 25 \times 0.9 = 11.25 \text{ N·m (per wheel)}$$

**Step 3 — Total tractive force from both wheels:**

$$F_t = \frac{2 \tau_w}{r} = \frac{2 \times 11.25}{0.08} = 281.25 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Assumptions stated (2 marks):**
- Pure rolling contact (no slip, $\lambda = 0$).
- Both wheels contribute equally (symmetric mass distribution).
- Drivetrain efficiency is constant at $\eta = 0.9$ (no load dependence).
- Static analysis only (no inertial term).

**Final Answer:** $F_{t,max} \approx 281.25$ N.

---

### 🔹 Question B — Ackermann-Steered Mobile Robot (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**A car-like mobile robot has wheelbase $L = 0.6$ m, front track width $T = 0.4$ m, and maximum steering angle $\delta_{max} = 30°$. The robot must follow a circular path.**

**(a) Derive the expression for the inner and outer Ackermann steering angles, and compute the minimum turning radius $R_{min}$ for the robot.** (7 marks)

**(b) If the robot has four in-wheel BLDC motors each rated 200 W and operates on a 24V, 10 Ah battery, estimate the theoretical maximum runtime in hours under continuous full-power operation, and identify the limiting factor in real-world deployment.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Ackermann Derivation & Min Turning Radius — 7 marks**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

For a vehicle with wheelbase $L$ and front track $T$, the inner wheel must turn *more* sharply than the outer wheel so that both wheel axles intersect at a common point on the rear-axle extension (the ICR). By similar triangles:

$$\tan(\delta_i) = \frac{L}{R - T/2}, \quad \tan(\delta_o) = \frac{L}{R + T/2}$$

**Minimum turning radius (2 marks):**

The minimum radius occurs when the *outer* wheel is at its maximum steering angle, $\delta_o = \delta_{max}$:

$$R_{min} = \frac{L}{\tan(\delta_{max})} - \frac{T}{2}$$

**Numerical Evaluation (1 mark):**

$$R_{min} = \frac{0.6}{\tan(30°)} - \frac{0.2}{1} = \frac{0.6}{0.5774} - 0.2 = 1.039 - 0.2 = 0.839 \text{ m}$$

---

**(b) Battery Runtime & Limiting Factor — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Total power draw:**

$$P_{total} = 4 \times 200 \text{ W} = 800 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Total current drawn from 24V source:**

$$I_{total} = \frac{P_{total}}{V} = \frac{800}{24} \approx 33.33 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical runtime (3 marks):**

$$t = \frac{C}{I_{total}} = \frac{10 \text{ Ah}}{33.33 \text{ A}} \approx 0.30 \text{ h} = 18 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4 — Limiting factor in real deployment (3 marks):**

In practice, the *Peukert effect* reduces usable capacity at high discharge rates; the *depth of discharge* is typically limited to 80% for Li-ion longevity; thermal throttling of BLDC drivers further reduces sustained output. Therefore the *practical* runtime is **40–50% less** than the theoretical figure. Additionally, mechanical losses (gearbox friction, rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag) and the auxiliary load (ROS computer, sensors, Wi-Fi) further limit the runtime to **~10–12 minutes** at full power.

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in differential drive:** Many students write $F_t = \tau_w / r$ for *one* wheel but forget that differential drive has *two* driving wheels. Always multiply by 2. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Using wheel radius in the wrong place:** A common error is to write $\tau = F \cdot W$ (using track width) instead of $\tau = F \cdot r$ (using wheel radius). The radius is the lever arm from the wheel centre to the ground. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Ignoring the efficiency $\eta$:** Many students drop the drivetrain efficiency from the formula, leading to an *over-spec'd* motor. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Confusing forward and inverse kinematics:** The forward kinematics is *measurement* (encoders → odometry), and inverse kinematics is *command* (cmd_vel → wheel speeds). Mixing them up costs 3–4 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to state assumptions:** In numerical problems, you *must* state "pure rolling, no slip, symmetric load" etc. (–1 to –2 marks)

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Actuation subsystem** = the physical "muscles" that produce motion; it sits *below* the ROS control layer and *above* the locomotion element.
- ✅ **Mobile platform types:** Wheeled (DD, Ackermann, Synchro, Omni), Tracked, Legged, Specialty (Aerial, Marine).
- ✅ **Differential drive** is the most common ROS test-bed. Memorize both forward and inverse kinematics — they are *high-yield* for KTU.
- ✅ **Core formulas (must be memorized):**
  - $v = \omega r$
  - $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/W$
  - $v = (v_R + v_L)/2$
  - $\tau_m = F r / (N \eta)$
  - $F_t = ma + \mu_r mg \cos\theta + mg \sin\theta$
  - $P = \tau \omega$
  - $\delta = \arctan(L/R)$ (Ackermann)
- ✅ **Holonomic platforms** can move sideways (Mecanum); **non-holonomic** platforms cannot (DD, Ackermann).
- ✅ **Wheel slip** condition $\lambda = (v - \omega r)/v$ is critical for traction analysis — set $\lambda = 0$ for ideal rolling.
- ✅ **Motor selection algorithm:** Force → Torque → Speed → Power. Apply safety factor of 1.2–1.5×.
- ✅ **ROS interface:** The `diff_drive_controller` consumes `/cmd_vel` and produces `/odom` and `/joint_states`.
- ✅ **Common pitfalls to avoid:** forgetting factor of 2 for two wheels, mixing wheel radius with track width, dropping efficiency $\eta$, and confusing forward vs. inverse kinematics.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision:** Draw the differential-drive wheel layout on a sheet, label $r$, $W$, $v_L$, $v_R$, and the ICR. Then write the four kinematic equations beside it. This single diagram covers 70% of typical 14-mark questions on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Actuation: Manipulator Arm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Actuation: Manipulator Arm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Actuation** in the context of a robotic manipulator arm refers to the mechanism by which electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or other forms of energy are converted into **mechanical motion** at the robot's joints, thereby producing controlled movement of the end-effector in the workspace. A robotic manipulator arm is a **serial kinematic chain** of rigid links interconnected by actuated joints, where each joint's motion is governed by a dedicated actuator under closed-loop or open-loop control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A **Manipulator Arm** is a mechanically constructed, multi-degree-of-freedom (DOF) articulated mechanical chain that, when supplied with energy through its actuators, can position and orient its end-effector in 3D Cartesian space to perform industrial, service, or research tasks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Arm

Think of a manipulator arm as a **mechanical replica of the human arm**:

- The **shoulder, elbow, and wrist** correspond to **revolute joints** (rotational actuators).
- The **upper arm, forearm, and palm** correspond to **rigid links**.
- The **muscles** that pull the bones correspond to **actuators** (motors, cylinders).
- The **brain and nervous system** correspond to the **ROS controller nodes** that send velocity/torque commands via `/joint_trajectory_controller` topics.
- The **fingertip** corresponds to the **end-effector** (gripper, tool).

When you want to pick up a cup, your brain sends signals to your muscles (actuators), which contract and pull on bones (links) to move your hand. Similarly, in ROS, a MoveIt planner computes joint angles and the controllers actuate motors to achieve those angles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics** (Bolded KTU Standard Metrics):
> - **Payload Capacity:** measured in **kg** or **N** (typically 1–1000 kg for industrial arms).
> - **Reach / Workspace Radius:** measured in **mm** or **m** (typically 0.5–3 m).
> - **Repeatability:** measured in **± mm** (typically ±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm for industrial).
> - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** a dimensionless integer count of independent joints.
> - **Joint Torque:** measured in **N·m**.
> - **Maximum Joint Speed:** measured in **rad/s** (rotational) or **m/s** (linear/prismatic).

### 1.3 Types of Manipulator Arm Configurations (KTU Classified)

| Configuration | Symbol | Typical DOF | Application |
|---------------|--------|-------------|-------------|
| Cartesian / Gantry | **L** (PPP) | 3 | Pick-and-place, CNC, 3D printing |
| Cylindrical | **C** (RPP) | 3 | Assembly, machine loading |
| Spherical / Polar | **S** (RRP) | 3 | Welding, painting |
| Articulated / Revolute | **A** (RRR) | 3–7 | Welding, assembly, painting |
| SCARA | **SC** (RRP) | 4 | PCB assembly, pick-and-place |
| Parallel | **D** (Delta) | 3–6 | High-speed sorting, packaging |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU examinations, always draw the **arm configuration diagram** and label the **joint types** (R = Revolute, P = Prismatic) to earn full marks. Examiners specifically look for this in the kinematic sketch.

### 1.4 ROS Context — Where Actuation Fits

In the ROS (Robot Operating System) framework, actuation is abstracted through:
- **Hardware Interface Layer** (`ros_control` package)
- **Joint State Publisher** (publishes `/joint_states`)
- **Joint Trajectory Controller** (subscribes to `/joint_trajectory_action`)
- **MoveIt!** motion planning framework that outputs joint trajectories to the controllers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** The manipulator arm in ROS is represented as a **URDF (Unified Robot Description Format)** file containing `<joint>` and `<link>` tags. Each `<joint>` defines a transmission element that physically maps actuator commands to link motion.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Manipulator Arm

A manipulator arm consists of the following structural and functional components:

1. **Base (Pedestal):** Anchors the arm to the floor/table; provides first joint (J1) reference.
2. **Links (L1, L2, …, Ln):** Rigid mechanical segments (typically aluminum alloy, steel, or carbon fiber) with mass $m_i$ and length $a_i$.
3. **Joints (J1, J2, …, Jn):** Either **revolute (R)** rotating about an axis or **prismatic (P)** translating linearly. Each joint is powered by an actuator.
4. **End-effector (Tool/EOAT):** Gripper, suction cup, welding torch, or specialized tool mounted on the last link.
5. **Sensors:** Encoders (position), resolvers, tachometers (velocity), torque sensors, IMU.
6. **Actuators:** DC servomotors, stepper motors, BLDC motors, hydraulic cylinders, pneumatic pistons.
7. **Transmission Elements:** Harmonic drives, planetary gearboxes, ball screws, timing belts, cable drives — they reduce speed and increase torque.

### 2.2 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Explained

The **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a manipulator is the number of independent joint variables required to completely specify its configuration in 3D space.

$$n_{DOF} = n_{links} - n_{constraints} + n_{actuators}$$

For spatial positioning and orientation in 3D (position = 3 DOF, orientation = 3 DOF):
$$n_{DOF} = 6 \text{ (full task-space mobility)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Gruebler's Equation** (Planar mechanisms):
> $$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$
> where $n$ = number of links, $j_1$ = number of lower pairs (1 DOF joints), $j_2$ = number of higher pairs (2 DOF joints).

For a **6-DOF Articulated Arm** (like the KUKA KR 16 or Universal Robots UR5e):
- J1: Base rotation (waist)
- J2: Shoulder pitch
- J3: Elbow pitch
- J4: Wrist roll
- J5: Wrist pitch
- J6: Wrist yaw

### 2.3 Types of Actuators — The "Why" and "How"

| Actuator Type | Energy Source | Key Equation | Pros | Cons | Typical Use |
|---------------|---------------|--------------|------|------|-------------|
| **DC Servo Motor** | Electrical | $\tau = K_t \cdot I$ | Precise, controllable | Brush wear | Mid-size industrial arms |
| **BLDC Motor** | Electrical | $\tau = K_t \cdot I$ | High power density, no brushes | Needs ESC | Modern collaborative arms |
| **Stepper Motor** | Electrical | $\theta_{step} = \frac{360°}{N_{steps}}$ | Open-loop positioning | Loses steps at high load | Low-cost SCARA, 3D printers |
| **Hydraulic Cylinder** | Hydraulic | $F = P \cdot A$ | High force/torque | Leakage, noisy | Heavy payload (>500 kg) |
| **Pneumatic Cylinder** | Pneumatic | $F = P \cdot A - F_{friction}$ | Fast, cheap, clean | Low precision | Pick-and-place, grippers |
| **Series Elastic Actuator (SEA)** | Electrical + spring | $\tau = k(\theta_m - \theta_l)$ | Compliant, safe | Complex | Humanoid robots, cobots |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Favourite:** Most examination questions focus on **DC Servo Motors** and **Harmonic Drive transmissions** for manipulator arms.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---|---------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\tau = K_t \cdot I$ | Motor torque constant $K_t$ (N·m/A), current $I$ (A) | DC/BLDC motor torque |
| 2 | $V = K_e \cdot \omega + I \cdot R$ | Back-EMF voltage; $K_e$ (V·s/rad), $\omega$ (rad/s), $R$ (Ω) | Motor voltage equation |
| 3 | $J_{eff}\ddot{\theta} + B\dot{\theta} + \tau_{load} = \tau_{motor}$ | Effective inertia, damping, motor torque | Joint dynamics |
| 4 | $P = \tau \cdot \omega$ | Power (W) | Power sizing |
| 5 | $G = \frac{\omega_{in}}{\omega_{out}} = \frac{\tau_{out}}{\tau_{in}}$ | Gear ratio (assuming ideal) | Harmonic / planetary gearbox |
| 6 | $\theta_{resolution} = \frac{2\pi}{N \cdot G}$ | Encoder resolution | Position sensing |
| 7 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Force from pressure | Hydraulic/pneumatic |
| 8 | $\tau = r \times F = F \cdot d \cdot \sin\alpha$ | Torque from force (perpendicular distance) | Joint torque calculation |
| 9 | $\theta = \int_0^t \omega \, dt$ | Angular position from velocity | Trajectory integration |
| 10 | $\omega = \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \dot{\theta}$ | Angular velocity | Control loop |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Critical Exam Rule:** When calculating **reflected inertia** through a gearbox with ratio $G$: $n = 1 \div G$, then $J_{reflected} = J_{motor} \div G^2$ (inertia reduces by the square of the ratio). This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Manufacturing:** 6-DOF arms weld car chassis (e.g., FANUC ARC Mate).
- **Surgical Robotics:** da Vinci Surgical System uses 7-DOF arms for minimally invasive surgery.
- **Space Exploration:** Canadarm2 on the ISS uses DC servo actuators with harmonic drives.
- **Humanoid Robotics:** Boston Dynamics Atlas uses hydraulic actuators for dynamic balancing.
- **Warehouse Automation:** Amazon Kiva robots use differential drive (mobile) + arm for picking.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: DC Servo Motor Torque Equation

Starting from the fundamental Lorentz force law for a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field:

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot L$$

where $B$ = magnetic flux density (T), $I$ = current (A), $L$ = conductor length (m).

For a motor with $N$ conductors at radius $r$ from the axis, the torque is:

$$\tau = F \cdot r = (B \cdot I \cdot L) \cdot r$$

Combining all motor-specific geometric and magnetic constants into a single constant $K_t$:

$$\tau_m = K_t \cdot I$$

This is the **motor torque equation** — current flowing through the armature produces torque proportional to the current.

Now, from Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the back-EMF generated when the motor rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ is:

$$V_b = K_e \cdot \omega$$

where $K_e$ = back-EMF constant (V·s/rad). In SI units, $K_t = K_e$.

Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law** to the armature circuit (resistance $R_a$, inductance $L_a$):

$$V_a = I \cdot R_a + L_a \frac{dI}{dt} + K_e \cdot \omega$$

This is the **armature voltage equation** of the DC motor.

Combining the **mechanical equation** (Newton's 2nd law for rotation) with the electrical equation, and including load torque $\tau_L$ and damping $B$:

$$J_m \frac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega + \tau_L = \tau_m = K_t I$$

Together, these form the **state-space model** of the DC servo motor that ROS controllers (typically PID) operate on.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Reflected Inertia Through a Harmonic Drive

Consider a motor with rotor inertia $J_m$ driving a harmonic drive (gear ratio $G : 1$, typically $G = 80:1$ to $160:1$ for industrial arms). The link inertia on the output side is $J_L$.

**Step 1: Kinetic Energy Equivalence**

The kinetic energy stored in the motor's rotation at speed $\omega_m$:

$$KE_m = \frac{1}{2} J_m \omega_m^2$$

The kinetic energy of the link rotating at output speed $\omega_L = \omega_m / G$:

$$KE_L = \frac{1}{2} J_L \omega_L^2 = \frac{1}{2} J_L \left(\frac{\omega_m}{G}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \frac{J_L}{G^2} \omega_m^2$$

**Step 2: Equivalent Inertia at Motor Shaft**

For equivalent dynamic behavior at the motor shaft, total kinetic energy must be equal:

$$\frac{1}{2} J_{eq} \omega_m^2 = \frac{1}{2} J_m \omega_m^2 + \frac{1}{2} \frac{J_L}{G^2} \omega_m^2$$

Dividing through by $\frac{1}{2}\omega_m^2$:

$$J_{eq} = J_m + \frac{J_L}{G^2}$$

**Step 3: Reflected Torque at the Motor Shaft**

If the load requires torque $\tau_L$ at the output, by energy conservation:

$$\tau_m \cdot \omega_m = \tau_L \cdot \omega_L = \tau_L \cdot \frac{\omega_m}{G}$$

Therefore:

$$\tau_m = \frac{\tau_L}{G}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip (14-Mark Question Favourite):** This means the **torque reduces by factor $G$** at the motor, but the **inertia reduces by factor $G^2$** — a major advantage of harmonic drives for high-DOF manipulator arms.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Forward Kinematics of a 2-DOF Planar Arm

Consider a 2-link planar arm with link lengths $a_1$ and $a_2$, joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ (both measured from the previous link, revolute joints).

**Step 1: Define homogeneous transformation matrices**

For revolute joint about z-axis:
$$A_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\sin\theta_i \\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

For our planar case, $\alpha_i = 0$ and $d_i = 0$:

$$A_1 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 & 0 & a_1\cos\theta_1 \\ \sin\theta_1 & \cos\theta_1 & 0 & a_1\sin\theta_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & 0 & a_2\cos\theta_2 \\ \sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & 0 & a_2\sin\theta_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Compute the overall transformation $T = A_1 \cdot A_2$**

Multiplying:

$$T = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 & a_1\cos\theta_1 + a_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 & a_1\sin\theta_1 + a_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Extract End-Effector Position**

$$x = a_1\cos\theta_1 + a_2\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$
$$y = a_1\sin\theta_1 + a_2\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$
$$\phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2$$

This is the **forward kinematics** solution — given joint angles, we can compute the end-effector pose. The **actuators at joints J1 and J2** physically realize these angles under closed-loop control.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU-style 7-mark sub-question):**
>
> A 2-DOF planar manipulator has link lengths $a_1 = 0.5$ m, $a_2 = 0.4$ m. The current joint angles are $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = 45°$. A harmonic drive with ratio $G = 100:1$ drives joint J2. The motor at J2 has rotor inertia $J_m = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ kg·m². Find (i) the end-effector position, and (ii) the equivalent inertia at the motor shaft (assuming link inertia $J_L = 5.0$ kg·m²).
>
> **Solution:**
>
> **(i) End-effector position:**
> $x = 0.5 \cos 30° + 0.4 \cos(75°)$
> $x = 0.5(0.866) + 0.4(0.2588) = 0.433 + 0.1035 = 0.5365$ m
>
> $y = 0.5 \sin 30° + 0.4 \sin(75°)$
> $y = 0.5(0.5) + 0.4(0.9659) = 0.25 + 0.3864 = 0.6364$ m
>
> $\phi = 30° + 45° = 75°$
>
> **(ii) Equivalent Inertia:**
> $J_{eq} = J_m + \dfrac{J_L}{G^2} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + \dfrac{5.0}{100^2} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 5.0 \times 10^{-4} = 6.0 \times 10^{-4}$ kg·m²

### 3.5 Joint Torque Requirement (Static Analysis)

For a 2-DOF planar arm holding a payload $W$ (N) at the end-effector, the joint torques are:

$$\tau_1 = (W \cdot a_1 \cos\theta_1) + (W \cdot a_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2))$$

$$\tau_2 = W \cdot a_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

**Derivation (brief):** The torque at J1 must support the moment arm of the entire weight about J1. The torque at J2 supports only the weight beyond J2 (link 2 + payload). The cosine factors project gravity perpendicular to the link.

This determines the **minimum actuator torque rating** required to be selected for the manipulator — a critical design step.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define the term **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a robotic manipulator arm. State the typical DOF required for full spatial positioning and orientation.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of a robotic manipulator arm is defined as the **number of independent joint variables** required to completely specify the position and orientation of the end-effector in 3D Cartesian space. For a manipulator to perform general tasks in 3D space (arbitrary position = 3 DOF + arbitrary orientation = 3 DOF), it requires a **minimum of 6 DOF**. Industrial manipulators typically have 6 DOF, while collaborative robots may have 7 DOF for redundancy and singularity avoidance. **(3 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between a **Revolute Joint** and a **Prismatic Joint** in a manipulator arm. Give one example of an industrial robot for each.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Revolute Joint (R) | Prismatic Joint (P) |
> |---------|-------------------|---------------------|
> | Motion Type | Rotational about a fixed axis | Linear translation along an axis |
> | Variable | Angle $\theta$ (rad) | Displacement $d$ (m) |
> | DOF | 1 (rotation) | 1 (translation) |
> | Example | KUKA KR 6 R700 (articulated) | Cartesian gantry robot used in CNC |
> | Workspace | Spherical / arc-shaped | Box / rectangular |
> **(3 marks)**

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — DC Servo Motor and Harmonic Drive

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **closed-loop position control of a single joint** of a manipulator arm using a **DC servo motor with harmonic drive transmission**. Derive the **armature voltage equation** and the **motor torque equation**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DC servo motor driving joint J2 of a manipulator has the following specifications:
- Armature resistance: $R_a = 2 \, \Omega$
- Motor constant: $K_t = K_e = 0.05$ N·m/A
- Rotor inertia: $J_m = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ kg·m²
- Harmonic drive ratio: $G = 80:1$
- Link inertia at output: $J_L = 4.0$ kg·m²
- Load torque at output: $\tau_L = 40$ N·m
- No-load armature voltage: $V_a = 24$ V

Calculate:
1. The **equivalent inertia** reflected at the motor shaft.
2. The **motor torque** required.
3. The **steady-state motor current** and the **back-EMF** at a speed of $\omega_m = 150$ rad/s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Derivation (7 marks):**

The closed-loop position control of a single manipulator joint consists of:
- **Reference input** $\theta_{ref}$ (desired joint angle from MoveIt planner)
- **Error detector** computing $e(t) = \theta_{ref} - \theta_{actual}$
- **PID Controller** generating voltage command $V_a(t)$
- **Motor Driver (H-Bridge)** providing current to the armature
- **DC Servo Motor** producing torque $\tau_m = K_t I$
- **Harmonic Drive** stepping down speed and stepping up torque
- **Joint Output** rotating the link
- **Encoder** providing $\theta_{actual}$ feedback

The **armature voltage equation** is derived from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law:
$$V_a = I R_a + L_a \frac{dI}{dt} + K_e \omega_m$$

The **motor torque equation** is derived from Lorentz force on armature conductors:
$$\tau_m = K_t I$$

The **mechanical equation** (Newton's 2nd law for rotation) at the motor shaft:
$$J_{eq} \frac{d\omega_m}{dt} + B\omega_m + \tau_{L,reflected} = \tau_m$$

where $\tau_{L,reflected} = \tau_L / G$ and $J_{eq} = J_m + J_L / G^2$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Equivalent Inertia**

$$J_{eq} = J_m + \frac{J_L}{G^2} = 2 \times 10^{-4} + \frac{4.0}{(80)^2}$$

$$J_{eq} = 2 \times 10^{-4} + \frac{4.0}{6400} = 2 \times 10^{-4} + 6.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$J_{eq} = 8.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Step 2: Motor Torque**

$$\tau_m = \frac{\tau_L}{G} = \frac{40}{80} = 0.5 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3: Motor Current**

$$I = \frac{\tau_m}{K_t} = \frac{0.5}{0.05} = 10 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4: Back-EMF**

$$V_b = K_e \omega_m = 0.05 \times 150 = 7.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5: Voltage Verification (Kirchhoff's at steady state, $dI/dt = 0$)**

$$V_a = I R_a + V_b = (10)(2) + 7.5 = 20 + 7.5 = 27.5 \text{ V}$$

Since applied voltage is 24 V < 27.5 V, the motor cannot reach 150 rad/s at the given load. The actual steady-state speed must be recalculated by setting $V_a = 24$ V:

$$24 = I R_a + K_e \omega_m \quad \text{and} \quad I = \frac{\tau_m}{K_t} = 0.5 / 0.05 = 10 \text{ A (assuming constant load)}$$

This yields $24 = 20 + 0.05 \omega_m \Rightarrow \omega_m = 80$ rad/s. **(7 marks — full marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Kinematics of a 2-DOF Planar Manipulator

**(a)** Define **forward kinematics** and **inverse kinematics** of a manipulator arm. With neat diagrams, derive the **forward kinematics** of a **2-DOF planar manipulator arm** with link lengths $a_1$ and $a_2$ and joint angles $\theta_1$, $\theta_2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 2-DOF planar manipulator arm with $a_1 = 0.6$ m, $a_2 = 0.5$ m, the joint angles are $\theta_1 = 60°$ and $\theta_2 = 30°$. Calculate:
1. The end-effector **position** $(x, y)$.
2. The **orientation** $\phi$ of the end-effector.
3. The **workspace reach** (maximum radial distance from base).
4. The **joint velocities** $\dot{\theta}_1$ and $\dot{\theta}_2$ if the end-effector is moving at $v_x = 0.2$ m/s along the x-axis and $v_y = 0.1$ m/s along the y-axis. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Forward Kinematics (FK):** The process of computing the **end-effector pose** $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ given the **joint variables** $(\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_n)$. FK is unique and straightforward for any given joint configuration.

**Inverse Kinematics (IK):** The process of computing the **joint variables** required to achieve a **desired end-effector pose**. IK may have **multiple solutions, no solution, or singularities**.

For the 2-DOF planar arm, the Denavit-Hartenberg (D-H) parameters are:
- Link 1: $a_1$ (length), $\alpha_1 = 0$, $d_1 = 0$, $\theta_1$ (variable)
- Link 2: $a_2$ (length), $\alpha_2 = 0$, $d_2 = 0$, $\theta_2$ (variable)

Using the homogeneous transformation matrices $A_1$ and $A_2$:

$$A_1 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 & 0 & a_1\cos\theta_1 \\ \sin\theta_1 & \cos\theta_1 & 0 & a_1\sin\theta_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & 0 & a_2\cos\theta_2 \\ \sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & 0 & a_2\sin\theta_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiplying $T = A_1 A_2$ gives the end-effector position as:

$$x = a_1\cos\theta_1 + a_2\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$
$$y = a_1\sin\theta_1 + a_2\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$
$$\phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2$$

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: End-Effector Position**

$\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 60° + 30° = 90°$

$$x = 0.6 \cos 60° + 0.5 \cos 90° = 0.6(0.5) + 0.5(0) = 0.3 \text{ m}$$

$$y = 0.6 \sin 60° + 0.5 \sin 90° = 0.6(0.866) + 0.5(1) = 0.5196 + 0.5 = 1.0196 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: End-Effector Orientation**

$$\phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2 = 90°$$

**Step 3: Maximum Workspace Reach**

$$R_{max} = a_1 + a_2 = 0.6 + 0.5 = 1.1 \text{ m}$$

(The end-effector can reach anywhere within an annulus of inner radius $|a_1 - a_2| = 0.1$ m and outer radius $1.1$ m from the base.)

**Step 4: Joint Velocities (using Jacobian inverse)**

The Jacobian matrix $J$ relates joint velocities to end-effector velocities:

$$\begin{bmatrix} v_x \\ v_y \end{bmatrix} = J \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1\sin\theta_1 - a_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -a_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ a_1\cos\theta_1 + a_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & a_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

Substituting values:
$$J = \begin{bmatrix} -0.6\sin 60° - 0.5\sin 90° & -0.5\sin 90° \\ 0.6\cos 60° + 0.5\cos 90° & 0.5\cos 90° \end{bmatrix}$$

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5196 - 0.5 & -0.5 \\ 0.3 + 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.0196 & -0.5 \\ 0.3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Determinant: $\det(J) = (-1.0196)(0) - (-0.5)(0.3) = 0 + 0.15 = 0.15$

$$J^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.15}\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0.5 \\ -0.3 & -1.0196 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix} = J^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{0.15}\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0.5 \\ -0.3 & -1.0196 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0.2 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.15}\begin{bmatrix} 0.05 \\ -0.06 - 0.10196 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{0.15}\begin{bmatrix} 0.05 \\ -0.16196 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.333 \\ -1.080 \end{bmatrix} \text{ rad/s}$$

So $\dot{\theta}_1 \approx 0.333$ rad/s and $\dot{\theta}_2 \approx -1.080$ rad/s. **(7 marks — full marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the D-H convention:** Many students confuse $\alpha_i$ (twist) with $\theta_i$ (joint angle) and lose **2–3 marks** in FK derivation.
> 2. **Reflected inertia mistake:** Students write $J_L / G$ instead of $J_L / G^2$. This is a **3-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Mixing units:** Using degrees in trigonometric functions and radians in derivatives. **Always convert degrees to radians** before using $\sin/\cos$ in calculus, OR use degrees consistently in the formula.
> 4. **Forgetting encoder feedback loop:** In block diagrams, missing the **feedback arrow** from encoder to controller loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Not labeling the configuration:** In kinematics questions, students forget to mention the **arm configuration** (Cartesian, Cylindrical, Articulated, SCARA) — 1 mark lost.
> 6. **Singularity not mentioned:** In inverse Jacobian calculation, students should mention what happens when $\det(J) = 0$ (singular configuration). Examiners award extra marks for this insight.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] A **manipulator arm** is a serial kinematic chain of links and actuated joints, typically with **6 DOF** for full 3D positioning and orientation.
- [ ] **Joint types:** **R** (Revolute, rotational) and **P** (Prismatic, linear). Notation: RRR, RRP, PPP, etc.
- [ ] **Actuator types:** DC Servo, BLDC, Stepper, Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Series Elastic Actuator (SEA). Selection depends on **payload, speed, precision, and environment**.
- [ ] **DC Servo Motor equations (must memorize):**
  - $\tau_m = K_t I$ (motor torque)
  - $V_a = I R_a + L_a (dI/dt) + K_e \omega$ (armature voltage)
  - $K_t = K_e$ in SI units
- [ ] **Harmonic Drive advantages:** High reduction ratio (80:1 to 160:1) in single stage, zero backlash, compact, coaxial input-output.
- [ ] **Reflected inertia rule:** $J_{eq} = J_m + J_L / G^2$ — inertia reduces by **square** of gear ratio.
- [ ] **Reflected torque rule:** $\tau_m = \tau_L / G$ — torque reduces by **linear** gear ratio.
- [ ] **Forward Kinematics (FK):** Joint variables → End-effector pose. Uses **Denavit-Hartenberg (D-H)** parameters.
- [ ] **Inverse Kinematics (IK):** End-effector pose → Joint variables. Uses **Jacobian inverse** or **closed-form solutions**. Subject to **singularities** where $\det(J) = 0$.
- [ ] **Workspace:** Annular region for 2-DOF planar arm; inner radius $= \vert a_1 - a_2 \vert$, outer radius $= a_1 + a_2$.
- [ ] **Common industrial arm configurations:** Cartesian (PPP), Cylindrical (RPP), Spherical (RRP), Articulated (RRR), SCARA (RRP), Parallel/Delta.
- [ ] **ROS Integration:** Manipulator arm described in **URDF**; controlled by **MoveIt!** + **ros_control**; trajectory commands sent to **`/joint_trajectory_controller`**.
- [ ] **Performance metrics to remember:** Payload (kg/N), Reach (m), Repeatability (±mm), DOF, Joint Torque (N·m), Max Speed (rad/s).
- [ ] **Hydraulic vs Pneumatic:** Hydraulic = high force, oil-based, precise; Pneumatic = low force, air-based, fast, low precision.
- [ ] **SCARA robots** (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm) are ideal for **vertical assembly** tasks (PCB insertion) because they are **compliant in X-Y but rigid in Z**.
- [ ] **Industrial safety:** Modern collaborative arms use **torque sensing at each joint** to detect collisions and stop within milliseconds (force/torque limiting per ISO/TS 15066).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robotic Operating System (ROS)** and modern robotics, a **Sensor** is a physical or virtual transducer device that measures a physical quantity (such as light, distance, temperature, force, or motion) from the robot's environment or its own internal state, and converts it into a standardized electrical signal (analog or digital) that can be processed, interpreted, and published over a ROS network as a **ROS Message** via topics, services, or actions.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBRAT504 – Module 3)** syllabus, sensors in ROS are studied under three primary classifications:
- **Internal Sensors (Proprioceptive)**: Measure the robot's internal state (e.g., encoders, IMU, gyroscopes).
- **External Sensors (Exteroceptive)**: Measure the environment (e.g., LiDAR, camera, ultrasonic).
- **Virtual Sensors**: Software-generated data from sensor fusion or simulation (e.g., Gazebo plugins).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A sensor in ROS is never a "bare device." It is always modeled as a **node** that publishes data on a **topic** using a standardized **message type** (e.g., `sensor_msgs/LaserScan`, `sensor_msgs/Image`, `sensor_msgs/Imu`).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a human being walking through a crowded room. You constantly use your **eyes** (vision sensors) to detect obstacles, your **ears** (microphones/ultrasonic sensors) to hear approaching vehicles, your **skin** (tactile/force sensors) to feel collisions, and your **inner ear** (IMU) to keep your balance. Your brain acts like the **ROS Master**, processing all this "published" sensory data and sending "subscribed" motor commands to your legs.

A robot equipped with sensors behaves identically:
- Each sensor is a **publisher node**.
- The ROS Master **routes** the data.
- The controller node **subscribes** to these topics and makes decisions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensors = The Five Senses of the Robot.** Without sensors, a robot is a "blind" mechanical structure with no situational awareness. In ROS, every physical sensor has a corresponding **driver package** (e.g., `urg_node` for Hokuyo LiDAR, `realsense2_camera` for Intel RealSense).

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants in ROS Sensor Data

- **Frequency / Sampling Rate (Hz)**: The rate at which a sensor publishes data. Common values: LiDAR = **10–40 Hz**, Camera = **30–60 Hz**, IMU = **100–1000 Hz**.
- **Field of View (FOV)**: Measured in **radians** or **degrees**; for a camera, horizontal FOV is typical ~**60°–90°**.
- **Range (m)**: Maximum measurable distance (e.g., LiDAR = **0.1 m to 30 m**).
- **Resolution (bits)**: ADC precision, typically **8-bit, 12-bit, or 16-bit**.
- **Covariance Matrix**: A 6x6 or 3x3 matrix quantifying sensor **uncertainty**, mandatory in `sensor_msgs/Imu` and `nav_msgs/Odometry`.
- **Standard Reference Frame**: `REP-103` and `REP-105` define `x-forward`, `y-left`, `z-up` for all ROS-compatible sensors.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

A ROS sensor pipeline operates in **four structured layers**:

### Layer 1 — Hardware Transduction
The physical phenomenon (light, sound, pressure) is converted into an analog voltage or current signal.
- *Why?* Because microcontrollers/SoCs only understand electrical signals.
- *How?* Using transducers such as **photodiodes** (light), **piezoelectric crystals** (pressure), and **Hall-effect ICs** (magnetic fields).

### Layer 2 — Signal Conditioning & ADC
The raw analog signal is amplified, filtered, and converted to digital values using an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**.
- *Why?* ROS topics carry discrete `float32`, `int16`, or `bool` values, not continuous voltages.
- *How?* ADC resolution $N$ bits gives $2^N$ discrete levels.

### Layer 3 — ROS Driver Node
A ROS package (e.g., `rosserial`, `velodyne_driver`) reads digital data (often over USB, UART, or Ethernet) and publishes it on a topic.
- *Why?* Standardization allows any subscriber to consume the data.
- *How?* Using the publisher–subscriber communication model.

### Layer 4 — Consumer Nodes
Nodes such as SLAM, Navigation, or perception pipelines subscribe to the topic and act on the data.

## 2.2 Sensor Classification Matrix

| Classification Type | Sensor Examples | ROS Message Type | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Proprioceptive** | Wheel Encoder, IMU, Gyroscope | `sensor_msgs/JointState`, `sensor_msgs/Imu` | Odometry, balance |
| **Exteroceptive – Range** | LiDAR, Ultrasonic, Radar | `sensor_msgs/LaserScan`, `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2` | Obstacle detection, mapping |
| **Exteroceptive – Vision** | RGB Camera, Stereo Camera, Depth Camera | `sensor_msgs/Image`, `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2` | Object recognition, SLAM |
| **Tactile / Force** | Force-Sensitive Resistor, Tactile Skin | Custom `geometry_msgs/WrenchStamped` | Grasping, collision detection |
| **Position / Orientation** | GPS, Magnetometer, Compass | `sensor_msgs/NavSatFix`, `sensor_msgs/MagneticField` | Outdoor navigation |
| **Environmental** | Temperature, Humidity, Gas | Custom messages | Service robotics, agriculture |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | Sampling frequency from sampling period | **Hz** |
| 2 | $\Delta x = \dfrac{c}{2 f_s} \cdot \dfrac{1}{N}$ | LiDAR radial resolution (c = speed of light $\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s) | **m** |
| 3 | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{\Delta \phi}{N_{beams}}$ | Angular resolution of a laser scanner | **rad** |
| 4 | $R_{max} = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | Maximum measurable range (Time-of-Flight) | **m** |
| 5 | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | **dB** |
| 6 | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2$ | Variance of sensor noise (used in covariance) | **unit²** |
| 7 | $\mathbf{z}_k = h(\mathbf{x}_k) + \mathbf{v}_k$ | Sensor measurement model in EKF-SLAM (h = nonlinear function) | **m, rad** |
| 8 | $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{J}_h \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{J}_h^T$ | Propagation of uncertainty (J = Jacobian) | **unit²** |
| 9 | $v = \dfrac{\Delta \phi \cdot r}{\Delta t}$ | Wheel encoder velocity (Δφ = rotation, r = wheel radius) | **m/s** |
| 10 | $v_{robot} = \dfrac{(v_R + v_L)}{2}$ | Differential drive forward velocity | **m/s** |
| 11 | $\omega = \dfrac{(v_R - v_L)}{L}$ | Differential drive angular velocity (L = wheelbase) | **rad/s** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Syntax:** All absolute value symbols have been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking the KTU-compatible markdown table structure, per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** Sensor fusion of LiDAR + Camera + Radar + GPS in ROS 2 (Autoware.Auto).
- **Industrial Robotics:** Force/torque sensors in assembly arms (ROS-Industrial).
- **Medical Robotics:** Tactile sensors in surgical robots (e.g., da Vinci Research Kit).
- **Aerial Robotics (Drones):** IMU + GPS + barometer fusion in PX4-Autopilot (ROS-integrated).
- **Disaster Response:** Multi-sensor mapping robots (DARPA SubT Challenge).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Time-of-Flight (ToF) Range Measurement for LiDAR

A LiDAR emits a laser pulse and measures the round-trip time $\Delta t$ until the reflection returns. Derive the range equation $R$.

**Step 1 — Identify the physical principle.**
Light travels at speed $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s in vacuum (and approximately in air).

**Step 2 — Express the total distance traveled.**
The light covers a distance $d_{total}$ equal to the round-trip distance: $d_{total} = c \cdot \Delta t$.

**Step 3 — Relate to the target range.**
Since the light travels to the target and back, the one-way range is half of the total distance:

$$
R = \dfrac{d_{total}}{2} = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Add the refractive index correction for atmospheric propagation.**
In practice, light travels slower in air by a factor of the refractive index $n \approx 1.0003$:

$$
R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2n}
$$

**Step 5 — Real-world example.**
A typical automotive LiDAR reports a maximum range of **200 m**. Compute the maximum measurable time-of-flight:

$$
\Delta t_{max} = \dfrac{2 \cdot R_{max}}{c} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 200}{3 \times 10^8} = 1.33 \, \mu s
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why LiDAR systems require **picosecond-precision timers** — the time difference between a 1 m and 200 m target is only **1.33 μs**, demanding extremely high-speed electronics.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Differential Drive Odometry from Wheel Encoders

A differential drive robot has two wheels with encoder ticks $T_L$ and $T_R$. Derive the linear and angular velocities.

**Step 1 — Encoder count to wheel rotation.**
Let $N$ = encoder ticks per revolution. The wheel rotations are:

$$
\phi_L = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot T_L}{N}, \quad \phi_R = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot T_R}{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Arc length covered by each wheel.**
With wheel radius $r$, the linear displacement at each wheel is:

$$
d_L = r \cdot \phi_L = \dfrac{2\pi r \cdot T_L}{N}, \quad d_R = \dfrac{2\pi r \cdot T_R}{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Individual wheel velocities.**
Divide by sampling time $\Delta t$:

$$
v_L = \dfrac{d_L}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{2\pi r \cdot T_L}{N \cdot \Delta t}, \quad v_R = \dfrac{2\pi r \cdot T_R}{N \cdot \Delta t}
$$

**Step 4 — Robot's forward (linear) velocity.**
The robot's center moves at the average of both wheels:

$$
v_{robot} = \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \dfrac{\pi r (T_R + T_L)}{N \cdot \Delta t}
$$

**Step 5 — Robot's angular velocity.**
With wheelbase $L$ (distance between wheels), the angular velocity is:

$$
\omega = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \dfrac{2\pi r (T_R - T_L)}{N \cdot \Delta t \cdot L}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Example.**
Given: $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.30$ m, $N = 360$ ticks/rev, $\Delta t = 0.1$ s, $T_R = 200$, $T_L = 180$.

$$
v_{robot} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 0.05 \cdot (200 + 180)}{360 \cdot 0.1} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 0.05 \cdot 380}{36} = 1.658 \, m/s
$$

$$
\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 0.05 \cdot (200 - 180)}{360 \cdot 0.1 \cdot 0.30} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 0.05 \cdot 20}{10.8} = 0.582 \, rad/s
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sensor Noise Propagation in a 2D Position Estimate

Suppose a robot estimates position $(x, y)$ from two range sensors with independent Gaussian noise $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ at angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$. Derive the combined position covariance.

**Step 1 — Measurement model.**
Each range sensor gives $z_i = \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2} + v_i$, where $v_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_i^2)$.

**Step 2 — Linearize via Jacobian.**
The Jacobian of $h(x, y)$ w.r.t. position is:

$$
\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{x - x_1}{z_1} & \dfrac{y - y_1}{z_1} \\[6pt] \dfrac{x - x_2}{z_2} & \dfrac{y - y_2}{z_2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine noise covariance.**

$$
\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1^2 & 0 \\ 0 & \sigma_2^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Propagate to position covariance.**

$$
\mathbf{\Sigma}_{xy} = (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{H})^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Closed form for two range sensors in 2D:**

$$
\mathbf{\Sigma}_{xy} = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_1^{-2} \sin^2 \alpha + \sigma_2^{-2} \cos^2 \alpha} \cdot \mathbf{I}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the bearing angle between the two sensors. Higher bearing diversity → lower position uncertainty.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sensor Selection Trade-offs

| Parameter | LiDAR | Camera (Stereo) | Ultrasonic | IMU |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Range** | **30–200 m** | **0.5–80 m** | **0.02–5 m** | N/A (internal) |
| **Accuracy** | ± **2 cm** | ± **5 cm** (depth) | ± **1 cm** | Drift ~ **0.1°/s** |
| **Cost (USD)** | **$500–$75,000** | **$30–$500** | **$2–$20** | **$10–$500** |
| **Weather Robustness** | Low (rain/fog) | Low (glare) | **High** | **High** |
| **Data Rate** | ~**1.5 M points/s** | ~**30 MB/s** | ~**20 Hz** | ~**1 kHz** |
| **ROS Driver Available** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: ROS Sensor Integration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Environment] --> B[Sensor Hardware Layer]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning and ADC]
    C --> D[ROS Driver Node]
    D --> E[ROS Master]
    E --> F[SLAM Node]
    E --> G[Perception Node]
    E --> H[Navigation Node]
    F --> I[Robot Decision Engine]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Actuator Commands]
    J --> K[Motor Controller]
    K --> L[Physical Motion]
    L --> A
```

> **Diagram Reading Guide:**
> - The outer loop (A → L → A) represents the **Perception–Action Cycle**.
> - Node **D** (ROS Driver) is where the physical sensor becomes a **ROS topic publisher**.
> - The **ROS Master (E)** is the central routing hub for all `publish/subscribe` communication.
> - This is a **closed-loop feedback system**, fundamental to autonomous robotics.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Sensor Fusion Architecture (EKF)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[IMU Sensor] --> A1[Preprocessing and Bias Removal]
    B[Wheel Encoder] --> B1[Tick to Velocity Conversion]
    C[LiDAR] --> C1[Scan to Point Cloud]
    A1 --> D[Extended Kalman Filter EKF Core]
    B1 --> D
    C1 --> D
    D --> E[State Estimate x y theta]
    D --> F[Uncertainty Covariance Matrix P]
    E --> G[Navigation Stack]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Motion Controller]
```

> **Engineering Note:** In KTU 2024, the **robot_localization** package (commonly used in exam problems) implements exactly this EKF structure, fusing IMU + Odometry + GPS.

## 4.3 Sensor Data Flow Timeline (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant HW as Sensor Hardware
    participant DRV as ROS Driver Node
    participant TOP as ROS Topic
    participant SUB as Consumer Node
    HW->>DRV: Raw bytes (USB/UART)
    DRV->>DRV: Parse and timestamp
    DRV->>TOP: Publish sensor_msgs/LaserScan
    TOP->>SUB: Deliver message via callback
    SUB->>SUB: Process (e.g., obstacle detection)
    SUB-->>DRV: Optional service call (e.g., reset)
```

## 4.4 Topic & Message Type Reference Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[LiDAR] --> M1[sensor_msgs LaserScan]
    S2[Stereo Camera] --> M2[sensor_msgs Image]
    S3[IMU] --> M3[sensor_msgs Imu]
    S4[GPS] --> M4[sensor_msgs NavSatFix]
    S5[Wheel Encoder] --> M5[sensor_msgs JointState]
    S6[Force Torque Sensor] --> M6[geometry_msgs WrenchStamped]
    M1 --> T1[/scan topic/]
    M2 --> T2[/camera/image_raw topic/]
    M3 --> T3[/imu/data topic/]
    M4 --> T4[/gps/fix topic/]
    M5 --> T5[/joint_states topic/]
    M6 --> T6[/wrench topic/]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a sensor in the context of ROS. Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A sensor in ROS is a device (or software module) that measures a physical quantity from the robot's environment or internal state and publishes it as a standardized ROS message on a topic.
- **Proprioceptive (1 mark):** Sensors that measure the robot's *internal* state. *Example:* Wheel encoder (measures wheel rotation), IMU (measures robot orientation).
- **Exteroceptive (1 mark):** Sensors that measure the *external* environment. *Example:* LiDAR (measures distance to obstacles), camera (measures ambient light).

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the role of the `sensor_msgs/Imu` message in ROS? List any four fields it contains.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Role (1 mark):** The `sensor_msgs/Imu` message is a standardized ROS message used to publish Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) data — orientation, angular velocity, and linear acceleration — typically at high frequencies (100–1000 Hz) for use in odometry, balance, and state estimation.
- **Fields (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
  1. `std_msgs/Header header` — Timestamp and frame_id (e.g., `imu_link`).
  2. `geometry_msgs/Quaternion orientation` — Robot orientation in 3D.
  3. `geometry_msgs/Vector3 angular_velocity` — Angular velocity in rad/s.
  4. `geometry_msgs/Vector3 linear_acceleration` — Linear acceleration in m/s².
  5. `float64[9] orientation_covariance` — 3x3 uncertainty matrix.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Module 3]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a ROS-based sensor data acquisition system. Describe the function of each layer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A differential drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.04$ m, wheelbase $L = 0.25$ m, and encoder resolution $N = 500$ ticks/rev. Over a sampling interval $\Delta t = 0.05$ s, the right and left encoders report $T_R = 300$ and $T_L = 280$ ticks respectively. Compute the linear velocity, angular velocity, and the change in robot pose $(\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta \theta)$ using forward kinematics.

#### Model Solution (a):
**Block diagram (3 marks):**

```
Physical Environment → Sensor Hardware → Signal Conditioning & ADC
            → ROS Driver Node → ROS Topic → Consumer Nodes → Decision
```

**Explanation of each layer (4 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Sensor Hardware Layer:** Physical transducers (LiDAR, camera, IMU) detect environmental stimuli and convert them into analog electrical signals.
2. **Signal Conditioning & ADC Layer:** Amplifies weak signals, removes noise via filters, and converts analog signals to digital using an ADC.
3. **ROS Driver Node Layer:** Software package that reads digital sensor data (via USB, UART, or Ethernet) and publishes it on a ROS topic using a standardized message type (e.g., `sensor_msgs/LaserScan`).
4. **Consumer Nodes Layer:** High-level nodes such as SLAM, Navigation, or perception algorithms subscribe to sensor topics and process the data for decision-making.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1 — Wheel angular displacements (1 mark):**

$$
\phi_L = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot T_L}{N} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 280}{500} = 3.519 \, rad
$$

$$
\phi_R = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot T_R}{N} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 300}{500} = 3.770 \, rad
$$

**Step 2 — Wheel velocities (1 mark):**

$$
v_L = \dfrac{r \cdot \phi_L}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{0.04 \cdot 3.519}{0.05} = 2.815 \, m/s
$$

$$
v_R = \dfrac{0.04 \cdot 3.770}{0.05} = 3.016 \, m/s
$$

**Step 3 — Linear and angular velocity of robot (1 mark):**

$$
v_{robot} = \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \dfrac{3.016 + 2.815}{2} = 2.916 \, m/s
$$

$$
\omega = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \dfrac{3.016 - 2.815}{0.25} = 0.804 \, rad/s
$$

**Step 4 — Pose update using forward kinematics (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta x = v_{robot} \cdot \Delta t \cdot \cos(\theta) = 2.916 \cdot 0.05 \cdot \cos(\theta) = 0.1458 \cos\theta \, m
$$

$$
\Delta y = v_{robot} \cdot \Delta t \cdot \sin(\theta) = 0.1458 \sin\theta \, m
$$

$$
\Delta \theta = \omega \cdot \Delta t = 0.804 \cdot 0.05 = 0.0402 \, rad
$$

**Step 5 — If initial heading $\theta = 0$ (1 mark):**
$\Delta x = 0.1458$ m, $\Delta y = 0$, $\Delta \theta = 0.0402$ rad.

**Final Answer (1 mark):** $v = 2.916$ m/s, $\omega = 0.804$ rad/s, $\Delta\theta = 0.0402$ rad.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Module 3 — Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a LiDAR sensor using the Time-of-Flight (ToF) method. Derive the range equation and compute the range for a measured time-of-flight of $\Delta t = 1.5 \, \mu s$. Assume $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the concept of sensor fusion in ROS. With a block diagram, explain how an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) combines IMU, wheel encoder, and GPS data to estimate robot pose. Mention the `robot_localization` package.

#### Model Solution (a):

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
A LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) sensor emits a narrow laser pulse (typically at 905 nm or 1550 nm wavelength) using a laser diode. The pulse travels through the air, hits an object in the environment, and a portion of the light is reflected back to a photodetector in the LiDAR. The internal timer measures the round-trip time $\Delta t$ with sub-nanosecond precision. Using $c$ (speed of light), the distance to the object is computed.

**Derivation of Range Equation (3 marks):**

Light covers total distance $d = c \cdot \Delta t$ in time $\Delta t$. One-way range:

$$
R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

**Numerical Computation (1 mark):**

$$
R = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8 \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \dfrac{450}{2} = 225 \, m
$$

#### Model Solution (b):

**Sensor Fusion Concept (2 marks):**
Sensor fusion is the process of combining data from multiple sensors to produce a more accurate, reliable, and complete estimate of the robot's state than any single sensor could provide alone. In ROS, the most common implementation is the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**, which handles nonlinear measurement models.

**EKF Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
IMU ──► [Preprocess] ──┐
Wheel Enc ──► [Tick-to-vel] ──┼──► [EKF Core] ──► Pose (x, y, θ) + Covariance P
GPS ──► [Lat/Lon-to-UTM] ──┘
```

**Explanation of robot_localization (2 marks):**
The `robot_localization` package in ROS provides the `ekf_localization_node` and `ukf_localization_node`. It accepts multiple sensor inputs via topics (`/imu/data`, `/odom`, `/gps/fix`) and outputs a fused odometry message on `/odometry/filtered`. The package uses the EKF to recursively predict and update the state, with proper handling of covariance matrices, outlier rejection (Mahalanobis distance thresholding), and configurable sensor input masking.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the ToF equation (students often write $R = c \cdot \Delta t$ instead of $c \cdot \Delta t / 2$) — **Lose up to 2 marks**.
> 2. **Not converting units** properly (e.g., writing $\Delta t = 1.5$ instead of $1.5 \times 10^{-6}$) — **Lose 1 mark**.
> 3. **In differential drive problems**, confusing $L$ (wheelbase) with $r$ (wheel radius) — **Lose 1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention `frame_id`** in the header of ROS sensor messages — **Lose 1 mark** in definition-type questions.
> 5. **Not writing the covariance matrix** in EKF-based questions — partial credit of 1 mark is lost.
> 6. **Missing the Jacobian $\mathbf{H}$** in sensor fusion derivations — **Lose 2 marks** if asked explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sensor = Publisher Node** in ROS. Every physical sensor needs a corresponding driver node.
- **`sensor_msgs/`** package contains the standardized message types: `LaserScan`, `Image`, `Imu`, `NavSatFix`, `PointCloud2`, `JointState`, `MagneticField`, `FluidPressure`, `Temperature`, `RelativeHumidity`.
- **REP-103 (Standard Units)** and **REP-105 (Coordinate Frames)** are mandatory for KTU exam answers involving sensor data interpretation.
- **Two main sensor classes:** Proprioceptive (internal) and Exteroceptive (external).
- **LiDAR equation:** $R = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$; remember the **factor of 2**.
- **Wheel odometry equations:** $v = (v_R + v_L)/2$ and $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/L$.
- **IMU publishes 100–1000 Hz**, Camera ~30 Hz, LiDAR ~10–40 Hz, GPS ~1–10 Hz.
- **Covariance matrices** are *not optional* in KTU answers for `Imu`, `Odometry`, or `PoseWithCovariance` — always include them.
- **TF (Transform) tree** must correctly link sensor `frame_id` to the robot's `base_link` (e.g., `base_link → imu_link`, `base_link → laser_frame`).
- **Sensor fusion in ROS** is typically done with `robot_localization` package using EKF/UKF.
- **The three pillars of ROS sensor integration:** Driver node (publishing), TF tree (transforms), and RViz (visualization) — all three should appear in any KTU diagram-based answer.
- **Cost vs. accuracy trade-off:** LiDAR is most accurate but expensive; ultrasonic is cheapest but noisy; cameras are versatile but weather-sensitive; IMUs are essential but suffer from drift.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computation Complete Robots - PR2
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Computation Complete Robots - PR2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Computation Complete Robot (CCR)** is defined as an autonomous robotic platform that possesses **sufficient on-board computational, sensory, and actuation resources** to execute the entire perception–planning–action loop independently, without relying on an off-board workstation or tethered external computer. The **PR2 (Personal Robot 2)**, developed by **Willow Garage**, is the canonical reference implementation of a CCR in the ROS ecosystem. It was engineered to host the **complete ROS middleware**, message-passing infrastructure, perception pipelines, motion planners, and high-level decision-making modules entirely on embedded multi-core computers housed within its mobile base.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** *A Computation Complete Robot is a self-contained mobile manipulator in which the on-board compute stack (CPU cores, RAM, storage, and ROS master) is powerful enough to perform real-time SLAM, perception, planning, and control — making the robot operationally independent of any external computing infrastructure.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two cars:

- **Car A** is a modern **Tesla** with powerful in-car computers that handle autopilot, navigation, music, and climate — fully self-sufficient even without internet.
- **Car B** is an old race car that needs a **pit crew laptop** plugged into the dashboard to even adjust the fuel map.

PR2 is the **Tesla of robots** in the ROS world. It carries two full **Intel Xeon/i7-class workstations inside its base**, runs a complete **Ubuntu Linux + ROS distribution**, and can perceive the world, plan motions, and manipulate objects **autonomously** while rolling around your office. Before PR2, most research robots were "thin clients" sending sensor data to a giant lab computer. PR2 flipped that paradigm — the **robot IS the computer**.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Computational Metrics

- **Height:** ~**1.65 m** (adjustable spine)
- **Weight:** ~**227 kg** (with batteries)
- **On-board computers:** **2 × Intel Xeon servers**, each with 8 cores, **24 GB RAM**, plus a third auxiliary PC
- **DOF (Degrees of Freedom):** **2 × 7-DOF arms** + **1-DOF gripper** (prismatic) + **2-DOF head** + **3-DOF mobile base** = **20+ DOF total**
- **Base footprint:** **Ø ~67 cm**
- **Battery:** 1.3 kWh Li-ion, ~2 hr operation
- **Sensors:** Stereo pairs (color + texture projector), **5 MP tilting laser**, IMU, force-torque at wrists, encoders on every joint

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The "computation complete" property does **not** mean the robot cannot use cloud/cluster computing for heavy tasks. It means the robot is **architecturally capable of running the complete ROS graph on-board** without any external server being mandatory for basic operation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why PR2 Qualifies as a "Computation Complete" Robot — The Five Pillars

PR2's claim to being **computation complete** rests on five architectural pillars. Each is essential; missing any one would demote the robot to a "tethered" or "thin-client" category.

1. **Self-Hosted ROS Master & Nodes**
   The PR2 launches the `roscore` (ROS Master) on one of its on-board servers. All nodes — `move_base`, `amcl`, `MoveIt!`, image pipelines — register and discover each other through this local master. No LAN connection to a lab server is required.

2. **Deterministic On-Board Compute Budget**
   Real-time control loops (e.g., 1 kHz joint controllers) demand bounded latency. PR2's two Xeon servers provide isolated CPU cores reserved for real-time kernels (`PREEMPT_RT` Linux patches), guaranteeing the **WCET (Worst-Case Execution Time)** is respected.

3. **Sensor Bandwidth Saturation In-Situ**
   The PR2 generates roughly **1.6 GB/s** of raw sensor data (stereo cameras at 30 Hz, tilting laser, joint states, force-torque). Offloading this to Wi-Fi would saturate the network. On-board processing filters and computes only the necessary topics for transmission.

4. **Power-Aware Compute Allocation**
   With a finite 1.3 kWh battery, every watt matters. PR2's compute stack is profiled so that perception runs on the **low-power auxiliary PC** and motion control on the **real-time cores**, balancing the thermal envelope with the energy budget.

5. **Fail-Operational Autonomy**
   If the Wi-Fi link drops, the PR2 continues operating safely because perception, planning, and control are all local. The robot can pause, replan, and re-execute without external intervention — a hallmark of **computation completeness**.

### 2.2 PR2 Hardware Stack — Logical View

| Subsystem | Component | Function | ROS Interface |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Compute** | 2× Xeon servers (24 GB RAM each) | Perception, planning, ROS Master | Internal Ethernet |
| **Real-time control** | 1× auxiliary PC + EtherCAT master | 1 kHz joint loop | `pr2_controller_manager` |
| **Mobile base** | 3-DOF (x, y, θ) differential drive | Navigation | `/base_controller` |
| **Manipulators** | 2× 7-DOF arms | Object manipulation | `/arm_controller` |
| **End-effectors** | 2× parallel-jaw grippers | Grasping | `/gripper_controller` |
| **Head** | 2-DOF pan-tilt + stereo | Visual perception | `/wide_stereo/left/right` |
| **Tilting laser** | Hokuyo 2D laser + servo | 3D obstacle map | `/base_scan` + `/tilt_scan` |
| **IMU** | 3-axis accelerometer/gyro | State estimation | `/imu_data` |
| **Power** | 1.3 kWh Li-ion + BMS | Sustained mobility | `/power_state` |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total DOF of PR2 | $n_{DOF} = 2(7) + 2(1) + 2 + 3 = 20$ | dimensionless | 2 arms × 7-DOF + 2 grippers + head + base |
| 2 | Kinematic chain (D-H) | $A_i = \text{Rot}_{z,\theta_i} \cdot \text{Trans}_{z,d_i} \cdot \text{Trans}_{x,a_i} \cdot \text{Rot}_{x,\alpha_i}$ | homogeneous 4×4 | Standard Denavit–Hartenberg |
| 3 | Forward kinematics | $T_{EE}^{base} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i(\theta_i)$ | 4×4 transform | Composed of link transforms |
| 4 | Sensor data rate | $R_{sensor} = f_{fps} \times W \times H \times 3$ bytes | MB/s | For RGB camera at $f_{fps}$ Hz |
| 5 | Power budget (PR2) | $P_{total} = P_{compute} + P_{motion} + P_{sensors}$ | Watts | Typically ~$400\text{ W}$ peak |
| 6 | Battery runtime | $t = \dfrac{C_{batt}}{P_{total}} \cdot \eta$ | hours | $C_{batt}=1.3$ kWh, $\eta \approx 0.85$ |
| 7 | Workspace volume (sphere approx.) | $V_w = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r_{reach}^3$ | m³ | With $r_{reach} \approx 0.85$ m per arm |
| 8 | Real-time period | $T_{RT} = \dfrac{1}{f_{ctrl}}$ | seconds | $f_{ctrl}=1$ kHz $\Rightarrow T_{RT}=1$ ms |
| 9 | Computational completeness metric | $C_{CCR} = \dfrac{P_{onboard}}{P_{required}} \ge 1$ | dimensionless | Must hold for all planned tasks |
| 10 | Communication latency budget | $\tau_{net} \le \tau_{loop} - \tau_{compute}$ | seconds | Ensures deadline is met |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "Why is PR2 a Computation Complete Robot?" the examiner expects an answer that touches **(a) on-board ROS master**, **(b) sufficient compute for real-time control**, **(c) sensor bandwidth handled locally**, and **(d) autonomy without tether**. Cover all four for full marks.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Research Acceleration:** PR2 enabled the **TUM Rosie**, **Berkeley BRETT**, and **CMU HERB** manipulation systems to run entirely on-board, eliminating the "tethered lab" failure mode.
- **Industrial lineage:** The architecture inspired the **Fetch Robotics Freight** and **Toyota HSR** mobile manipulators.
- **Telepresence & healthcare:** On-board computation allowed PR2 to perform semi-autonomous fetch-and-carry in hospitals (e.g., **Jacob Eisenstein's** trials).
- **Benchmark platform:** PR2 remains the **gold-standard benchmark** for evaluating SLAM, grasping, and whole-body motion planning algorithms in ROS.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Deriving the PR2 Arm's Kinematic Reach & Workspace

The PR2's 7-DOF arm is kinematically redundant. Its workspace can be derived from a simplified D-H model. We approximate the base of the arm as a 7-link serial chain with the following **modified D-H parameters** (simplified for analysis):

| Joint $i$ | $a_i$ (m) | $\alpha_i$ (rad) | $d_i$ (m) | $\theta_i$ (rad) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (shoulder pan) | 0.10 | $\pi/2$ | 0.0 | $\theta_1$ |
| 2 (shoulder lift) | 0.0 | $\pi/2$ | 0.0 | $\theta_2$ |
| 3 (upper arm roll) | 0.40 | $-\pi/2$ | 0.0 | $\theta_3$ |
| 4 (elbow flex) | 0.0 | $\pi/2$ | 0.0 | $\theta_4$ |
| 5 (forearm roll) | 0.40 | $-\pi/2$ | 0.0 | $\theta_5$ |
| 6 (wrist flex) | 0.0 | $\pi/2$ | 0.0 | $\theta_6$ |
| 7 (wrist roll) | 0.10 | 0.0 | 0.0 | $\theta_7$ |

**Step 1 — Define the homogeneous transform for a single link:**

$$
A_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\sin\theta_i \\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute forward kinematics as the product of all link transforms:**

$$
T_{EE}^{base} = A_1(\theta_1) \cdot A_2(\theta_2) \cdot A_3(\theta_3) \cdot A_4(\theta_4) \cdot A_5(\theta_5) \cdot A_6(\theta_6) \cdot A_7(\theta_7)
$$

> This is a $4\times 4$ matrix; the upper-left $3\times 3$ is the end-effector orientation $R_{EE}^{base}$, and the right-most $3\times 1$ column is the end-effector position $\mathbf{p}_{EE}^{base}$.

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum reach $r_{reach}$ (fully extended arm):**

$$
r_{reach} = \sum_{i=1}^{7} \lvert a_i \rvert + d_{gripper} = 0.10 + 0.40 + 0.40 + 0.10 + 0.15 = 1.15 \text{ m}
$$

But the *practical* reach (excluding the wrist tube) used in PR2 datasheets is:

$$
r_{reach}^{practical} \approx 0.85 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Approximate the spherical workspace volume:**

$$
V_w = \frac{4}{3}\pi \left(r_{reach}^{practical}\right)^3 = \frac{4}{3}\pi (0.85)^3 \approx 2.57 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the redundancy resolution (since 7-DOF > 6 spatial DOF):**

The arm has $n = 7$ joints for $m = 6$ Cartesian DOF, so the **degree of redundancy** is:

$$
r_{red} = n - m = 7 - 6 = 1
$$

The null space of the Jacobian $J(q)$ has dimension 1, meaning the elbow can swivel through a 1-DOF self-motion manifold while keeping the gripper fixed — crucial for obstacle avoidance.

**Step 6 — Singularity condition (Jacobian rank drop):**

$$
\det\!\left(J(q) J(q)^T\right) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{singular configuration}
$$

For the PR2 arm, the most common singularity is the **elbow singularity**, occurring when joints 2 and 4 are collinear.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — PR2 vs Other ROS Robots

| Property | PR2 | TurtleBot 2 | Fetch | Husky |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Computational Completeness** | **Full** (2× Xeon servers) | Partial (1× laptop) | Full (Intel i7) | Partial (NUC) |
| **On-board ROS Master** | Yes | Yes (optional) | Yes | Yes (with NUC) |
| **Manipulator** | 2× 7-DOF arms | None | 1× 7-DOF arm | None |
| **Mobile Base DOF** | 3 | 2 | 2 | 2 |
| **Stereo Vision** | Yes (2 pairs) | Optional (Kinect) | Yes | Optional |
| **Force/Torque Sensing** | Yes (wrists) | No | Yes | No |
| **Battery Capacity (Wh)** | 1300 | 220 | 600 | 250 |
| **Typical Use Case** | Research benchmark | Education | Logistics research | Outdoor research |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Only **PR2** and **Fetch** in this table are truly *Computation Complete*. TurtleBot and Husky, despite being ROS-native, rely on external computing for serious perception.

### 3.3 Computational Load Estimate for PR2

A typical PR2 on-board workload includes:

- **SLAM (gmapping/Cartographer):** ~1.5 CPU cores
- **MoveIt! motion planning:** ~2.0 CPU cores
- **Perception (point cloud + image proc):** ~3.5 CPU cores
- **Real-time joint control (1 kHz):** ~1.0 core (pinned, RT priority)
- **ROS Master + utility nodes:** ~0.5 cores

$$
C_{total} = 1.5 + 2.0 + 3.5 + 1.0 + 0.5 = 8.5 \text{ cores}
$$

With PR2's **16 physical cores** (2 servers × 8 cores):

$$
C_{CCR} = \frac{P_{onboard}}{P_{required}} = \frac{16}{8.5} \approx 1.88 \ge 1 \;\;\checkmark
$$

This satisfies the **computation completeness condition**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PR2 High-Level Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid

    subgraph PWR["Power Subsystem"]
```

### 4.2 ROS Computation Graph on PR2

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PERC["Perception Layer"]
        A1["/camera/left/image_raw"]
        A2["/camera/right/image_raw"]
        A3["/scan"]
        A4["/imu"]
    end

    subgraph PROC["Processing Layer"]
        B1["stereo_image_proc"]
        B2["laser_filters"]

    end

    subgraph PLAN["Planning Layer"]

    end

    subgraph CTRL["Control Layer"]
        D1["pr2_controller_manager"]

        D3["base_controller"]
    end

    subgraph HW["Hardware"]
        E1["Arms"]
        E2["Grippers"]
        E3["Mobile Base"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    A4 --> B3
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B1 --> C3
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D2 --> E1
    D2 --> E2
    D3 --> E3
```

### 4.3 Computation Completeness Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start: Task Request"] --> check1{"On-board ROS Master running?"}
    check1 -- No --> fail["Not CCR - tethered mode"]
    check1 -- Yes --> check2{"Sufficient CPU cores available?"}
    check2 -- No --> fail
    check2 -- Yes --> check3{"Sensor data processable locally?"}
    check3 -- No --> fail
    check3 -- Yes --> check4{"Real-time deadlines met?"}
    check4 -- No --> fail
    check4 -- Yes --> check5{"Battery supports task duration?"}
    check5 -- No --> fail
    check5 -- Yes --> ccr["CCR Verified - PR2 executes task autonomously"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the term **"Computation Complete Robot"** with reference to the PR2 platform.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A Computation Complete Robot (CCR) is a robotic system that hosts **sufficient on-board computational resources** — including CPU, memory, storage, and the full ROS middleware stack — to execute the complete **perception–planning–action loop** without dependence on external computing infrastructure. (1 mark)
> The PR2 qualifies as a CCR because it carries **two Intel Xeon servers** and an auxiliary real-time PC inside its base, runs the **ROS Master locally**, and processes all sensor data, motion planning (MoveIt!), and SLAM **on-board**. (1 mark)
> This makes the PR2 **operationally independent** of any lab workstation, satisfying the $C_{CCR} \ge 1$ condition for all standard research tasks. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List **four sensors** integrated on the PR2 and state the ROS topic each one typically publishes to.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Wide stereo cameras** → topic: `/wide_stereo/left/image_raw` and `/wide_stereo/right/image_raw` (0.75)
> 2. **Hokuyo 2D tilting laser** → topic: `/base_scan` (with `/tilt_scan` from the servo) (0.75)
> 3. **IMU** → topic: `/imu_data` (0.75)
> 4. **Force/Torque sensor at wrist** → topic: `/l_wrist_ft_controller/` (or `/r_wrist_ft_controller/`) (0.75)
>
> *(Full 3 marks for 4 correct sensor–topic pairs.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **hardware architecture** of the PR2 and justify why it is classified as a **Computation Complete Robot**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **forward kinematic transformation matrix** for a 2-link simplified PR2 arm (shoulder lift + elbow flex only), given the following D-H parameters: $a_1=0$, $d_1=0.30$ m; $a_2=0.40$ m, $\alpha_2=0$, $d_2=0$. Then find the end-effector position for $\theta_1 = 45°$ and $\theta_2 = 30°$. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**
>
> **Block Diagram (4 marks):**
> The PR2 architecture consists of:
> - **Compute cluster:** 2× Xeon servers + 1 auxiliary PC, interconnected by a Gigabit Ethernet switch.
> - **Sensor layer:** stereo cameras, tilting laser, IMU, force-torque sensors, joint encoders.
> - **Actuator layer:** 3-DOF mobile base, 2× 7-DOF arms, 2× 1-DOF grippers, 2-DOF head.
> - **Power layer:** 1.3 kWh Li-ion battery feeding all subsystems.
>
> **Justification (3 marks):**
> 1. The ROS Master runs locally on Server 1 → no external orchestrator required. ✓
> 2. Total of 16+ CPU cores provide $C_{CCR} \approx 1.88$ against a typical 8.5-core demand. ✓
> 3. All sensor bandwidth (~1.6 GB/s raw) is consumed on-board, preventing Wi-Fi saturation. ✓
> 4. Real-time EtherCAT control at 1 kHz is handled by a dedicated auxiliary PC with PREEMPT_RT Linux. ✓
> 5. Battery supports ~2 hours of autonomous operation. ✓
> Hence the PR2 satisfies all five pillars of **computation completeness**.

> **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**
>
> **Step 1 — Write the D-H transform for each link.**
>
> For link 1: $a_1=0$, $d_1=0.30$ m, $\alpha_1=\pi/2$, $\theta_1=45°=\pi/4$.
>
> $$
> A_1 = \begin{bmatrix}
> \cos\theta_1 & 0 & \sin\theta_1 & 0 \\
> \sin\theta_1 & 0 & -\cos\theta_1 & 0 \\
> 0 & 1 & 0 & d_1 \\
> 0 & 0 & 0 & 1
> \end{bmatrix}
> = \begin{bmatrix}
> 0.7071 & 0 & 0.7071 & 0 \\
> 0.7071 & 0 & -0.7071 & 0 \\
> 0 & 1 & 0 & 0.30 \\
> 0 & 0 & 0 & 1
> \end{bmatrix}
> $$
>
> For link 2: $a_2=0.40$ m, $d_2=0$, $\alpha_2=0$, $\theta_2=30°=\pi/6$.
>
> $$
> A_2 = \begin{bmatrix}
> \cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & 0 & a_2\cos\theta_2 \\
> \sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & 0 & a_2\sin\theta_2 \\
> 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
> 0 & 0 & 0 & 1
> \end{bmatrix}
> = \begin{bmatrix}
> 0.8660 & -0.5 & 0 & 0.3464 \\
> 0.5 & 0.8660 & 0 & 0.2 \\
> 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
> 0 & 0 & 0 & 1
> \end{bmatrix}
> $$
>
> **Step 2 — Multiply $A_1 \cdot A_2$ to obtain $T_{EE}^{base}$.**
>
> $$
> T_{EE}^{base} = A_1 \cdot A_2 = \begin{bmatrix}
> (0.7071)(0.8660) + (0.7071)(0.5) & (0.7071)(-0.5) + (0.7071)(0.8660) & 0 & 0.3464(0.7071) \\
> (0.7071)(0.8660) + (-0.7071)(0.5) & (0.7071)(-0.5) + (-0.7071)(0.8660) & 0 & 0.3464(0.7071) \\
> 0.5 & 0.8660 & 1 & 0.30 \\
> 0 & 0 & 0 & 1
> \end{bmatrix}
> $$
>
> Computing entry-by-entry:
> - $R_{11} = 0.7071 \times 0.8660 + 0.7071 \times 0.5 = 0.6124 + 0.3536 = 0.9660$
> - $R_{12} = 0.7071 \times (-0.5) + 0.7071 \times 0.8660 = -0.3536 + 0.6124 = 0.2588$
> - $p_x = 0.3464 \times 0.7071 = 0.2449$ m
> - $R_{21} = 0.7071 \times 0.8660 - 0.7071 \times 0.5 = 0.6124 - 0.3536 = 0.2588$
> - $R_{22} = 0.7071 \times (-0.5) - 0.7071 \times 0.8660 = -0.3536 - 0.6124 = -0.9660$
> - $p_y = 0.3464 \times 0.7071 = 0.2449$ m
> - $R_{31} = 0.5$, $R_{32} = 0.8660$, $p_z = 0.30$ m
>
> Final homogeneous transform:
>
> $$
> T_{EE}^{base} = \begin{bmatrix}
> 0.9660 & 0.2588 & 0 & 0.2449 \\
