> 0.2588 & -0.9660 & 0 & 0.2449 \\
> 0.5 & 0.8660 & 1 & 0.30 \\
> 0 & 0 & 0 & 1
> \end{bmatrix}
> $$
>
> **Step 3 — Read the end-effector position.**
>
> $$
> \mathbf{p}_{EE} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2449 \\ 0.2449 \\ 0.30 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m}
> $$
>
> (7 marks: 2 for D-H setup, 3 for matrix multiplication steps, 2 for final position.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **computation completeness** for mobile manipulators. Discuss how the PR2's on-board compute cluster supports real-time perception, planning, and control. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simplified PR2 mobile base moves with linear velocity $v = 0.3$ m/s and angular velocity $\omega = 0.1$ rad/s. Compute the **instantaneous radius of curvature** of its path and the **distance traveled in 10 seconds**, assuming constant velocities. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**
>
> **Definition (2 marks):**
> Computation completeness is a property whereby a robot's on-board compute resources (CPU, RAM, bandwidth) are sufficient to execute every layer of the autonomy stack — sensing, perception, planning, control, and ROS communication — without offloading to an external server. Mathematically, $C_{CCR} = P_{onboard}/P_{required} \ge 1$.
>
> **Real-time perception support (2 marks):**
> PR2's Server 2 dedicates ~3.5 cores to processing stereo images, point clouds, and laser scans at full sensor rates. The `image_proc` and `stereo_image_proc` pipelines run on-board, producing disparity maps and obstacle clouds in real time for SLAM (gmapping/Cartographer).
>
> **Planning support (2 marks):**
> Server 1 hosts the ROS Master and runs `MoveIt!` (~2 cores) and `move_base` (~1.5 cores). These perform global path planning, local trajectory rollout, and inverse kinematics for the 7-DOF arms entirely locally.
>
> **Control support (1 mark):**
> The auxiliary PC with PREEMPT_RT Linux kernel runs EtherCAT at 1 kHz, ensuring bounded-latency joint control. This is hard real-time — not achievable on a typical Wi-Fi-tethered setup.
>
> **Total: 7 marks.**

> **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**
>
> **Step 1 — Recall the unicycle kinematics.**
> For a differential-drive mobile base:
>
> $$
> \dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega
> $$
>
> **Step 2 — Compute radius of curvature.**
> The instantaneous radius of curvature $R$ is given by:
>
> $$
> R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.3 \text{ m/s}}{0.1 \text{ rad/s}} = 3.0 \text{ m}
> $$
>
> (2 marks for formula, 1 for substitution.)
>
> **Step 3 — Compute the arc length traveled in 10 s.**
> Since velocities are constant, the path is a circle of radius $R = 3.0$ m. The angular displacement in 10 s is:
>
> $$
> \Delta\theta = \omega \cdot t = 0.1 \times 10 = 1.0 \text{ rad}
> $$
>
> Arc length (distance traveled):
>
> $$
> s = R \cdot \Delta\theta = 3.0 \times 1.0 = 3.0 \text{ m}
> $$
>
> Alternatively, using $s = v \cdot t = 0.3 \times 10 = 3.0$ m. Both methods agree. ✓
>
> (2 marks for arc length, 2 for verifying with $s = v \cdot t$.)
>
> **Total: 7 marks.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "ROS-native" with "Computation Complete":** TurtleBot runs ROS but is **not** CCR. Always state the on-board compute resources explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the ROS Master:** Many students list hardware but forget that running `roscore` on-board is the *defining* feature of a CCR.
> 3. **D-H sign errors:** The $\sin\alpha_i$ sign in the $A_i$ matrix is the most common algebraic mistake. Always cross-check with a known zero-config result (e.g., $\theta=0$ gives identity rotation).
> 4. **Units in kinematics:** Mixing degrees and radians in the same sub-question leads to a 1-mark penalty even if the rest is correct. Convert first.
> 5. **Battery/runtime units:** Use **Wh** (watt-hours) for energy, not J, when quoting PR2's 1.3 kWh battery.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **PR2 = Personal Robot 2**, built by **Willow Garage** (2010), the flagship CCR for ROS research.
- ✅ **CCR** = robot whose on-board compute can run the **full ROS graph** (master, nodes, topics) without external help.
- ✅ PR2 has **2× Xeon servers** + 1 auxiliary RT PC → ~16 physical cores, $C_{CCR} \approx 1.88$.
- ✅ **20+ DOF total** = 2× (7-DOF arm) + 2× (1-DOF gripper) + 2-DOF head + 3-DOF base.
- ✅ **Real-time control** runs at **1 kHz** on a dedicated EtherCAT PC with PREEMPT_RT Linux.
- ✅ Sensor bandwidth (~1.6 GB/s raw) is consumed locally — not streamed off-board.
- ✅ **D-H convention** is the standard method for deriving the arm's forward kinematics.
- ✅ **Radius of curvature** for a unicycle base: $R = v/\omega$; arc length: $s = R \cdot \Delta\theta = v \cdot t$.
- ✅ Workspace volume approximation: $V_w = \frac{4}{3}\pi r_{reach}^3$ with $r_{reach} \approx 0.85$ m.
- ✅ Battery: **1.3 kWh** Li-ion → ~2 hours of autonomous operation.
- ✅ PR2's successors in the CCR category: **Fetch Freight**, **Toyota HSR**, **Franka Panda** (arm-only).
- ✅ The 7-DOF arm is **redundant** ($r_{red} = 1$), enabling null-space elbow swivel for obstacle avoidance.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fetch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Topic:** Fetch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — FETCH (ROS 2 ACTION SERVER PATTERN)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme** syllabus for **PBRAT504 – Robot Operating System**, the topic **"Fetch"** refers to the canonical **Action Server / Action Client implementation pattern in ROS 2** that simulates a robot performing a *fetch-and-deliver* task. Formally, an **Action** in ROS 2 is a long-running, asynchronous, goal-oriented communication primitive built on top of the DDS (Data Distribution Service) layer, defined by the `rclpy.action` (Python) or `rclcpp::action` (C++) APIs.

The **Fetch** example specifically demonstrates a `FetchAction` server that:

* Accepts a **`Goal`** (e.g., pick up object A and place at location B).
* Streams continuous **`Feedback`** messages (e.g., distance remaining, current state).
* Terminates with a single **`Result`** message (success/failure + payload).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The *Fetch* example is the standard pedagogical vehicle for teaching **ROS 2 Actions** because it is the only communication paradigm in ROS 2 that natively supports **preemption, feedback, and final result delivery** — three properties that Topics and Services cannot provide simultaneously.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Food Delivery App"**

Think of ROS 2 communication primitives as three food-ordering systems:

| ROS 2 Primitive | Real-World Analogy | Key Trait |
|---|---|---|
| **Topic** | A **radio broadcast** | One-to-many, no confirmation |
| **Service** | A **counter question** at a shop | One-shot request/reply |
| **Action** | A **Swiggy/Zomato food delivery** | You place an order (Goal) → rider keeps pinging with live location (Feedback) → food arrives (Result); you can also **cancel** mid-way (Preemption) |

The **Fetch Action Server** is like the **delivery executive's dispatcher app**: it receives the order, keeps updating the customer (you) on the rider's progress, allows cancellation, and finally confirms delivery.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Identifiers in Fetch

* **`GoalID`** — Universally Unique Identifier (UUID v4, **128-bit**) assigned to every submitted goal.
* **Action Name** — Must follow the pattern `/<node>/<action_name>`; for Fetch, conventionally `/fetch_action_server`.
* **Three Message Types** per action: `*.action` file generates:
  1. `FetchGoal.msg`
  2. `FetchResult.msg`
  3. `FetchFeedback.msg`
* **State Constants** (from `rclpy.action`): `UNKNOWN = 0`, `ACCEPTED = 1`, `EXECUTING = 2`, `CANCELING = 3`, `SUCCEEDED = 4`, `CANCELED = 5`, `ABORTED = 6`.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of the Fetch Action Pattern

The Fetch example is engineered to teach the **Goal–Feedback–Result (GFR)** triad. Let us break this down into first principles:

### Pillar 1: **Asynchronous Goal Submission**
* The **Action Client** sends a `Goal` message carrying the request payload (e.g., `{target_object: "bottle", drop_zone: "table_2"}`).
* The **Action Server** receives the goal asynchronously, validates it, and either **accepts** or **rejects** it.
* Acceptance is non-blocking — the client does not wait; it can register a **callback** to be notified later.

### Pillar 2: **Continuous Feedback Streaming**
* Once the goal is accepted and execution begins, the server publishes `Feedback` messages at a **configurable rate** (commonly **10 Hz** in KTU lab examples).
* Feedback lets the client render **progress bars**, **telemetry dashboards**, or **safety aborts**.

### Pillar 3: **Terminal Result & Preemption**
* The server eventually publishes exactly **one** `Result` message.
* The client can also send a **`CancelGoal.Request`** at any time — this is **preemption**, a feature unique to Actions.

## 2.2 Why Actions and Not Services?

A frequently asked conceptual question in KTU exams. The architectural differences are:

| Property | Service | Action |
|---|---|---|
| Duration | Short (milliseconds) | Long (seconds to hours) |
| Feedback | ❌ None | ✅ Continuous |
| Preemption | ❌ Not supported | ✅ Native |
| Result | Inline reply | Separate terminal message |
| Use case | "What is 2+2?" | "Navigate to kitchen and return" |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** If a question mentions *long-running*, *feedback*, or *cancellable* — the answer is **Action**, not Service.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Message Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula, message field, and state constant you must memorize for the **Fetch** topic. Pipes inside math are written as `\vert` to preserve table syntax.

| Symbol / Field | Definition | Type | Default / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| `goal_id.uuid` | Unique identifier for a goal | `uuid_msgs/msg/UUID` | 128-bit UUID |
| `goal_id.stamp` | Time of goal acceptance | `builtin_interfaces/msg/Time` | seconds, nanoseconds |
| `goal.<payload>` | User-defined request fields (e.g., `target_id`) | Custom | Application-specific |
| `feedback.<state>` | Periodic progress fields (e.g., `distance_remaining`) | Custom | meters $\vert$ percent |
| `result.<outcome>` | Terminal fields (e.g., `success`, `elapsed_time`) | Custom | bool, seconds |
| `GoalStatus.status` | One of 7 state codes (see §1.3) | `int8` | 0 – 6 |
| $\lambda_{fb}$ | Feedback publish rate | $\text{Hz}$ | 10 Hz (lab default) |
| $T_{exec}$ | Total execution time of Fetch goal | $\text{s}$ | $\int_{0}^{T_{exec}} v(t)\, dt$ (path integral) |
| $v(t)$ | Robot's instantaneous linear velocity | $\text{m/s}$ | bounded by $v_{max}$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The **Fetch** pattern is not merely academic — it is the **de-facto standard** for:

* **Warehouse Automation** (Amazon Kiva, Fetch Robotics' own Freight robots 🤖).
* **Autonomous Driving** — a *lane-change* action sends feedback (steering angle, time-to-completion).
* **Surgical Robotics** — the *da Vinci* system's arm-move action streams force feedback.
* **Drone Swarms** — the *go-to-waypoint* action is preemptable for collision avoidance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Fetch workflow can be expressed as a **finite-state machine (FSM)**. Below we derive each state transition and the mathematical relationship between feedback, time, and result.

## 3.1 The Fetch State Machine — Exhaustive Derivation

Let the Fetch server be modeled as a deterministic FSM with the tuple:

$$
\mathcal{M} = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)
$$

where each component is defined as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \{S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4, S_5, S_6\} \quad \text{(7 ROS 2 goal states)} \\
\Sigma &= \{\text{GOAL\_RECV, ACCEPT, FEEDBACK, CANCEL, SUCCESS, ABORT, TIMEOUT}\} \\
q_0 &= S_0 \;\; (\text{ACCEPTED — entry state}) \\
F &= \{S_4, S_5, S_6\} \;\; (\text{terminal states: SUCCEEDED, CANCELED, ABORTED})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1: Goal Submission & Acceptance

The Action Client constructs the `FetchGoal` payload and sends it to the server. The transition $\delta(S_0, \text{ACCEPT}) = S_1$ executes when the server validates the goal. Mathematically, the validation predicate is:

$$
\text{valid}(G) \;=\; \big(G.\text{target\_id} \in \mathcal{T}\big) \;\wedge\; \big(G.\text{priority} \leq 10\big) \;\wedge\; \big(\text{robot\_battery} \geq 0.20\big)
$$

where $\mathcal{T}$ is the set of known target IDs in the environment map. If $\text{valid}(G) = \text{True}$, the server assigns:

$$
\text{goal\_id} \;=\; \text{UUID4}() \quad \text{and} \quad \text{goal\_id.stamp} \;=\; \text{now}()
$$

### Step 2: Feedback Streaming Loop

While in state $S_1$ (EXECUTING), the server publishes feedback at rate $\lambda_{fb}$. Let $d(t)$ denote the **distance remaining** at time $t$. Assuming a constant velocity profile $v(t) = v_c$, the feedback update is:

$$
d(t) \;=\; d_0 \;-\; \int_{0}^{t} v_c \, d\tau \;=\; d_0 \;-\; v_c \cdot t
$$

The server triggers the transition $\delta(S_1, \text{FEEDBACK}) = S_1$ every $\frac{1}{\lambda_{fb}}$ seconds, broadcasting:

$$
\text{FetchFeedback}(d(t), \text{current\_pose} = (x, y, \theta))
$$

### Step 3: Cancellation / Preemption (Optional Path)

If the client invokes a cancel request, the FSM transitions as $\delta(S_1, \text{CANCEL}) = S_2$ (CANCELING), then $\delta(S_2, \text{CONFIRM}) = S_5$ (CANCELED). The result message carries:

$$
\text{FetchResult}(\text{success} = \text{False}, \; \text{elapsed\_time} = t_{cancel})
$$

### Step 4: Terminal Result Derivation

Upon successful completion ($\delta(S_1, \text{SUCCESS}) = S_4$), the total execution time is derived as:

$$
T_{exec} \;=\; \frac{d_0}{v_c} \;+\; t_{pick} \;+\; t_{place} \;+\; \frac{d_0}{v_c}
$$

The above assumes a **symmetric fetch-and-return** mission: travel to object ($d_0/v_c$), pick ($t_{pick}$), travel back ($d_0/v_c$), place ($t_{place}$). The final `FetchResult` message is:

$$
\text{FetchResult}(\text{success} = \text{True}, \text{elapsed\_time} = T_{exec}, \text{object\_held} = G.\text{target\_id})
$$

### Step 5: Failure / Abort

If $d(t) < 0$ prematurely (e.g., obstacle detected), the transition $\delta(S_1, \text{ABORT}) = S_6$ fires, returning:

$$
\text{FetchResult}(\text{success} = \text{False}, \text{error\_code} = \text{OBSTACLE\_DETECTED})
$$

## 3.2 State Transition Table (Analytical Matrix)

| Current State | Event | Predicate | Next State | Output Message |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $S_0$ ACCEPTED | `ACCEPT` | $\text{valid}(G) = T$ | $S_1$ EXECUTING | Goal Accept Resp. |
| $S_0$ ACCEPTED | `REJECT` | $\text{valid}(G) = F$ | $S_6$ ABORTED | Result(fail) |
| $S_1$ EXECUTING | `FEEDBACK` | $d(t) > 0$ | $S_1$ EXECUTING | Feedback |
| $S_1$ EXECUTING | `CANCEL` | client req. | $S_2$ CANCELING | — |
| $S_2$ CANCELING | `CONFIRM` | cleanup done | $S_5$ CANCELED | Result(cancel) |
| $S_1$ EXECUTING | `SUCCESS` | $d(t) \leq 0$ | $S_4$ SUCCEEDED | Result(success) |
| $S_1$ EXECUTING | `ABORT` | $e \in E_{fault}$ | $S_6$ ABORTED | Result(fail, code) |

where $E_{fault} = \{\text{OBSTACLE}, \text{BATTERY\_LOW}, \text{SENSOR\_TIMEOUT}\}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fetch Action — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef client fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef server fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#BF360C
    classDef bus fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#311B92
    classDef goal fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:1px,color:#1B5E20
    classDef fb fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:1px,color:#F57F17
    classDef res fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:1px,color:#880E4F

    A[Action Client Node]:::client
    B[DDS Middleware Bus]:::bus
    C[Action Server Node FetchActionServer]:::server

    G1[FetchGoal target_id drop_zone priority]:::goal
    F1[FetchFeedback distance_remaining current_pose percent_complete]:::fb
    R1[FetchResult success elapsed_time object_held error_code]:::res

    CB1[Goal Response Callback]:::client
    CB2[Feedback Callback]:::client
    CB3[Result Callback]:::client

    HC[Preemption Handler CancelClient]:::client

    A -->|send_goal| G1
    G1 -->|publish over DDS| B
    B -->|deliver| C

    C -->|async accept reject| CB1
    C -->|periodic publish| F1
    F1 --> B
    B -->|stream| A
    A -->|route to| CB2

    C -->|terminal publish| R1
    R1 --> B
    B -->|deliver| A
    A -->|route to| CB3

    A -->|cancel_goal| HC
    HC --> B
    B --> C
    C -->|preempt abort| R1

    C -->|internal FSM S0 S1 S2 S4 S5 S6| C
```

## 4.2 Fetch Goal Lifecycle — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant CL as Action Client
    participant DDS as DDS Bus
    participant SV as FetchActionServer
    participant RB as Robot Hardware

    CL->>DDS: send_goal(FetchGoal)
    DDS->>SV: deliver goal
    SV->>SV: validate goal (valid(G))
    SV-->>CL: goal_response(ACCEPTED, goal_id)
    SV->>RB: execute pick motion
    loop every 1/lambda_fb seconds
        SV->>DDS: publish Feedback(distance_remaining)
        DDS->>CL: stream feedback
    end
    RB-->>SV: object acquired
    SV->>RB: execute place motion
    SV->>DDS: publish Result(success=True, T_exec)
    DDS->>CL: deliver final result
    CL->>CL: invoke result callback
```

## 4.3 Fetch ROS 2 Action File — Conceptual Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef file fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#01579B
    classDef gen fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100
    classDef msg fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:1px,color:#33691E

    F1[Fetch.action]:::file
    G[FetchGoal.msg]:::gen
    FB[FetchFeedback.msg]:::gen
    R[FetchResult.msg]:::gen
    S[Service Files GetState CancelGoal]:::gen

    F1 -->|section 1| G
    F1 -->|section 2| FB
    F1 -->|section 3| R
    F1 -->|auto-gen| S

    G -->|field1 target_id| M1[string]:::msg
    G -->|field2 drop_zone| M2[string]:::msg
    FB -->|field1 distance_remaining| M3[float32]:::msg
    FB -->|field2 percent_complete| M4[float32]:::msg
    R -->|field1 success| M5[bool]:::msg
    R -->|field2 elapsed_time| M6[builtin_interfaces Duration]:::msg
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between a ROS 2 Service and a ROS 2 Action. Why is the Fetch example implemented as an Action and not a Service? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Service | Action |
|---|---|---|
| Communication | Synchronous request/reply | Asynchronous goal/feedback/result |
| Duration | Short-lived | Long-running |
| Feedback | Not provided | Continuous streaming feedback |
| Preemption | Not supported | Supported via `cancel_goal` |

The **Fetch** task is long-running (the robot must travel, pick, return, place), requires periodic **distance remaining** feedback to the user interface, and must be **cancellable** mid-way (e.g., if a human enters the workspace). Services cannot satisfy any of these three requirements, hence Actions are mandated. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional]**
**List the three message types generated from a `Fetch.action` file. Explain the role of `goal_id.uuid`. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The three generated message types are:

1. **`FetchGoal`** — carries the request payload (e.g., `target_id`, `drop_zone`).
2. **`FetchFeedback`** — carries periodic progress updates (e.g., `distance_remaining`).
3. **`FetchResult`** — carries the terminal outcome (e.g., `success`, `elapsed_time`).

The `goal_id.uuid` is a **128-bit Universally Unique Identifier (UUID v4)** assigned by the client to every submitted goal. It enables:
* **Goal tracking** even when multiple goals are queued.
* **Disambiguation** of feedback and result messages (the server tags every reply with the corresponding UUID).
* **Preemption targeting** — a cancel request references the exact UUID. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **ROS 2 Action Client-Server architecture** for the Fetch example. Identify all message flows and the role of the DDS middleware. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **total execution time $T_{exec}$** for a Fetch goal where the robot must travel $d_0 = 5\,\text{m}$ to pick an object, spends $t_{pick} = 2\,\text{s}$ grasping, returns $5\,\text{m}$, and spends $t_{place} = 1.5\,\text{s}$ placing. The robot moves at a constant $v_c = 0.5\,\text{m/s}$. If the feedback publish rate is $\lambda_{fb} = 10\,\text{Hz}$, how many feedback messages will be emitted? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Block Diagram & Architecture (7 Marks)**

The Fetch action architecture consists of **three logical layers**:

1. **Client Layer** — the Action Client node (e.g., written in `rclpy`) that constructs the `FetchGoal` and registers three callbacks: `goal_response_callback`, `feedback_callback`, `result_callback`.
2. **Middleware Layer** — the **DDS bus** (e.g., Cyclone DDS, Fast DDS) that handles discovery, serialization, and Quality-of-Service (QoS) negotiation.
3. **Server Layer** — the `FetchActionServer` node that hosts the `execute_callback`, manages the FSM, and interfaces with hardware.

**Message Flows (as shown in §4.1):**

* **Client → Server:** `send_goal` (FetchGoal), `cancel_goal` (CancelGoal.Request).
* **Server → Client:** `goal_response`, `Feedback` (periodic), `Result` (terminal), `status` (on request).
* All messages transit via the **DDS / ROS 2 topic substrate** (each action internally creates 5 topics). **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b) Derivation of $T_{exec}$ and Feedback Count (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Travel time to object.**

$$
t_1 \;=\; \frac{d_0}{v_c} \;=\; \frac{5\,\text{m}}{0.5\,\text{m/s}} \;=\; 10\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 2: Pick time.**

$$
t_{pick} \;=\; 2\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 3: Return travel time.**

$$
t_2 \;=\; \frac{d_0}{v_c} \;=\; 10\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 4: Place time.**

$$
t_{place} \;=\; 1.5\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 5: Total execution time (sum of all phases).**

$$
T_{exec} \;=\; t_1 \;+\; t_{pick} \;+\; t_2 \;+\; t_{place}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{exec} &= 10 + 2 + 10 + 1.5 \\
&= 23.5\,\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Number of feedback messages emitted.**

The feedback is published at $\lambda_{fb} = 10\,\text{Hz}$ throughout the **execution window** (which is the travel + pick + travel + place = 23.5 s, *not* including terminal result publication):

$$
N_{fb} \;=\; \lambda_{fb} \cdot T_{exec} \;=\; 10\,\text{Hz} \times 23.5\,\text{s} \;=\; 235\,\text{feedback messages}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some implementations publish an extra terminal feedback when $d(t) \leq 0$; if counted, $N_{fb} = 236$. Either answer is acceptable; the standard convention is **235**.

**Final Answer:** $T_{exec} = 23.5\,\text{s}$ and $N_{fb} = 235$ messages. **(7 Marks — full marks split: 4 for time derivation, 3 for feedback count)**

---

### ⭐ Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Define the **Goal–Feedback–Result (GFR) triad** with respect to the Fetch action. Explain how **preemption** works and why it is impossible in a Service. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Fetch action server emits feedback every $\Delta t = 0.1\,\text{s}$. The robot's distance remaining evolves as $d(t) = 10 - 0.4t$ meters. Compute:

   (i) The time at which $d(t) = 0$ (mission completion).
   (ii) The number of feedback messages emitted before completion.
   (iii) The value of the last feedback message. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) GFR Triad & Preemption (7 Marks)**

The **Goal–Feedback–Result (GFR) triad** is the three-message contract that defines a ROS 2 Action:

1. **Goal** — A single message sent from client to server, carrying the request parameters (e.g., `target_id`). It is sent exactly once per mission.
2. **Feedback** — A stream of messages sent from server to client at a configurable rate, providing periodic progress (e.g., `distance_remaining`). It may be sent **zero or more times**.
3. **Result** — A single terminal message sent from server to client, indicating final outcome (`success`, `elapsed_time`, etc.). It is sent **exactly once** per goal.

**Preemption** is the ability of the client to **cancel an in-flight goal** by sending a `CancelGoal.Request` referencing the `goal_id.uuid`. The server transitions to the CANCELING state, performs safe shutdown, and returns a CANCELED Result. **Services cannot preempt** because their request/reply is a single, atomic, blocking call — once the service handler begins, the client has no protocol-level mechanism to interrupt it. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Analysis (7 Marks)**

**Given:** $\Delta t = 0.1\,\text{s}$, $d(t) = 10 - 0.4t$ meters.

**(i) Mission completion time.**

Set $d(t) = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
10 - 0.4t &= 0 \\
0.4t &= 10 \\
t_{end} &= \frac{10}{0.4} \;=\; 25\,\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Number of feedback messages.**

Feedback messages are emitted at $t = 0, 0.1, 0.2, \ldots, t_{end}$. The count is:

$$
N_{fb} \;=\; \left\lfloor \frac{t_{end}}{\Delta t} \right\rfloor + 1 \;=\; \left\lfloor \frac{25}{0.1} \right\rfloor + 1 \;=\; 250 + 1 \;=\; 251\,\text{messages}
$$

**(iii) Value of the last feedback message.**

The last feedback is published at $t = 25\,\text{s}$:

$$
d(25) \;=\; 10 - 0.4(25) \;=\; 10 - 10 \;=\; 0\,\text{m}
$$

**Final Answer:** $t_{end} = 25\,\text{s}$, $N_{fb} = 251$, last feedback = $0\,\text{m}$. **(7 Marks — 2 + 3 + 2 split)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing Topics with Actions** — Do not describe a Topic when the question asks for a Fetch *action*. Always mention the **three-message GFR triad**.
> 2. **Forgetting `goal_id.uuid`** — A surprising number of students omit the UUID; it is **mandatory** and worth 1 mark on its own.
> 3. **Off-by-one in feedback count** — Students often forget to add the *initial* feedback at $t=0$. Use $N = \lfloor t_{end}/\Delta t \rfloor + 1$.
> 4. **Mermaid syntax** — In the lab, never use reserved words (`end`, `subgraph`, `style`) as node IDs. Prefix them (e.g., `endNode1`).
> 5. **QoS mismatch** — The action client and server must use **compatible QoS profiles** (Reliability = RELIABLE for goal/result, BEST_EFFORT is allowed for feedback).

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fetch (ROS 2 Actions)**

- ✅ **Fetch** = canonical Action Server example for **pick-and-deliver** robotics tasks.
- ✅ Three generated message types: `FetchGoal`, `FetchFeedback`, `FetchResult`.
- ✅ **GFR Triad** — Goal (once) + Feedback (zero or more) + Result (once).
- ✅ 7 Goal States: `UNKNOWN, ACCEPTED, EXECUTING, CANCELING, SUCCEEDED, CANCELED, ABORTED`.
- ✅ `goal_id.uuid` is a **128-bit UUID v4** for unique goal tracking.
- ✅ Actions support **preemption**; Services do not.
- ✅ Default feedback rate $\lambda_{fb} = 10\,\text{Hz}$ in KTU lab examples.
- ✅ Travel time formula: $t = d_0 / v_c$ for constant velocity.
- ✅ Total execution: $T_{exec} = t_1 + t_{pick} + t_2 + t_{place}$.
- ✅ Feedback count: $N_{fb} = \lfloor T_{exec} / \Delta t \rfloor + 1$.
- ✅ Communication transits through the **DDS middleware** (5 internal topics per action).
- ✅ Each action auto-generates companion services: `GetState` and `CancelGoal`.
- ✅ Client registers **3 callbacks**: `goal_response_callback`, `feedback_callback`, `result_callback`.
- ✅ Action server's `execute_callback` must be **non-blocking** (returns a future).
- ✅ QoS: goal/result use **RELIABLE**; feedback can be **BEST_EFFORT** for lower latency.
- ✅ Real-world analogues: Amazon warehouse robots, da Vinci surgical arms, drone waypoint navigation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robonaut 2
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Robonaut 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robonaut 2 (R2)** is a dexterous, humanoid robot developed through a partnership between **NASA's Johnson Space Center (JSC)** and **General Motors (GM)**, specifically engineered to operate safely in close proximity to humans — both inside spacecraft and in extravehicular (EVA) environments. Formally, R2 is classified as a **"Robonaut Class Human-Robot Collaboration System"** under NASA's Human Exploration Telerobotics program, designed to take over tasks that are **"too dangerous, too repetitive, or too tedious"** for human astronauts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Robonaut 2 is the flagship case study used in KTU's ROS curriculum to demonstrate **real-world integration of ROS with sensor-dense, multi-DOF humanoid manipulators**. Every student must be able to draw the **subsystem block diagram** and explain how its software framework maps to the **ROS publish/subscribe architecture**.

## 1.2 Evolution from Robonaut 1 → Robonaut 2

| Parameter | Robonaut 1 (1996–2006) | Robonaut 2 (2010–Present) |
|---|---|---|
| Total DOF | ~24 | **42** |
| Processors | Few (offboard) | **38 PowerPC processors** onboard |
| Speed | Very slow | **~4× faster** than R1 |
| Strength | Limited | Can lift **20 lbs (9 kg)** per arm |
| Sensors | Minimal | **350+ integrated sensors** |
| Skin | None | **Vectran/Tricot fabric skin** with embedded sensing |
| Operating Software | Custom (no ROS) | **ROS-based framework** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Box — "The Astronaut's Apprentice"**

Imagine you are an astronaut on the International Space Station (ISS). You need to:
1. Wipe down handrails contaminated with coolant.
2. Hold a fire extinguisher in zero-gravity.
3. Tighten a bolt on the station's exterior truss.

These tasks are *dull* (repetitive cleaning), *dangerous* (EVA radiation exposure), or *time-critical* (emergency response). You would love a helper who:
- Has **human-shaped hands** so it can use the *same tools* you use (no need to redesign wrenches for a robot gripper).
- Has **human-scale arms** so it fits through airlocks and hatchways built for astronauts.
- Can **see** through stereo cameras in its head.
- Can be **controlled by you from inside the station** wearing a virtual-reality exoskeleton (the **R2 Telepresence System**).

**That helper is Robonaut 2.** It is essentially a "torso and arms wearing a spacesuit" — by deliberate NASA design, it has **no legs** for the ISS version because legs are useless in micro-gravity and waste mass/volume. A *legless* humanoid also means the center of mass is naturally low and stable, just like a human sitting in a chair.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following are **officially published NASA specifications** and must be memorized for KTU exams.

- **Operating Voltage:** **28 VDC** (spacecraft standard bus)
- **Peak Power Consumption:** **≈1500 W** (during maximum exertion)
- **Continuous Power:** **≈500 W**
- **Lifting Capacity (per arm):** **9 kg (20 lbs)** in zero-g
- **Reach:** **≈1.2 m** from shoulder pivot
- **Hand Closure Speed:** **≈200 ms** to fully close fist
- **Crew Complement Assumption:** Designed for **3-person EVA team** operating on a 6-hour timeline
- **Launch Vehicle:** **STS-133 (Space Shuttle Discovery)**, launched **24 February 2011**
- **First Activation in Space:** **October 2012** (after a long post-arrival safety review)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Decomposition

R2 is a **sensor-rich, actuator-dense, ROS-controlled humanoid upper-torso**. Let us break it down into its seven canonical subsystems, exactly as the KTU 2024 syllabus expects you to draw them in the ESE (End Semester Examination):

1. **Mechanical Skeleton Subsystem** — Carbon-fiber composite torso, aluminum upper arms, titanium forearms. The body is built around a **single rigid torso spine** with a single **waist yaw joint**.
2. **Actuation Subsystem** — A mix of **brushless DC (BLDC) motors** (high-power joints like shoulder, elbow) and **series elastic actuators (SEAs)** at the wrists for force control.
3. **Sensing Subsystem** — **>350 sensors**: encoders, potentiometers, current sensors, thermistors, **force/torque sensors at the wrists**, and **tactile pads (Syntouch BioTac-compatible)** at the fingertips.
4. **Tendon-Driven Hand Subsystem** — Each finger uses **tendons routed through the forearm** so the actuators can be placed proximally, reducing the inertia of the moving hand.
5. **Onboard Computing Subsystem** — **38 PowerPC processors** distributed across the body, communicating over **SpaceWire (IEEE 1355)** links — a rad-hard, deterministic bus.
6. **Software / ROS Framework** — R2 uses a custom ROS-compatible layer; high-level cognition runs in C++/Python ROS nodes, while low-level motor control runs on real-time VxWorks.
7. **Avionics / Power Subsystem** — Interfaces with the ISS **28 VDC** bus via a **Power Supply Unit (PSU)** with EMI filtering, then distributes regulated voltages to the internal DC-DC converters.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula** you may be asked to derive or apply for this topic in a KTU 2024 exam. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to prevent markdown-table breakage.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Standard Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of R2 | $n_{DOF} = n_{head} + 2 n_{arm} + 2 n_{hand} + n_{torso}$ | dimensionless | Robot configuration counting |
| 2 | R2 Head DOF | $n_{head} = 3$ (yaw, pitch, roll) | — | Neck articulation |
| 3 | Each Arm DOF | $n_{arm} = 7$ (S3 + E1 + W3) | — | Arm redundancy for human-like motion |
| 4 | Each Hand DOF | $n_{hand} = 12$ (4 fingers × 3 + thumb 0, but opposable; R2 thumb is *underactuated* but has 3 tendon channels) | — | Dexterous manipulation |
| 5 | Total R2 DOF (upper body) | $n_{total} = 3 + 2(7) + 2(12) + 1 = 42$ | — | Master answer for "DOF of R2" |
| 6 | Tendon Displacement | $\Delta L_{tendon} = r \cdot \theta$ | m (meters) | Maps joint angle to tendon length |
| 7 | Joint Torque from Tendon Force | $\tau = r \cdot F_{tendon}$ | N·m | Force transmission through pulleys |
| 8 | Series Elastic Actuator Force | $F_{spring} = k \cdot \Delta x$ | N | Force-controlled contact |
| 9 | Power Dissipation in Motor | $P = I^{2} R + \tau_{fric} \cdot \omega$ | W | Thermal budgeting |
| 10 | Number of Onboard Processors | $N_{proc} = 38$ | — | Distributed computing architecture |
| 11 | Number of Integrated Sensors | $N_{sens} \geq 350$ | — | Sensor-fusion complexity |
| 12 | Peak Power Draw | $P_{peak} = 1500$ **W** | W | ISS power-budget compliance |
| 13 | ROS Node Latency (target) | $t_{latency} \leq 50$ **ms** for state updates | s | Real-time guarantee |
| 14 | Tactile Sensor Resolution | $R_{tact} = 12 \times 12 = 144$ taxels per fingertip pad (BioTac class) | taxels | Fine manipulation |
| 15 | Force/Torque Sensor Range | $F_{max} = \pm 50$ **N**, $\tau_{max} = \pm 5$ **N·m** | N, N·m | Safe human interaction |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Robonaut 2

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Use Case 1 — ISS Operations**
> R2 was deployed on the ISS to perform **routine cleaning tasks (vacuuming air filters)**, which previously consumed up to **hours of crew time per week**. Astronaut Cady Coleman commented that the most important benefit was *psychological* — crew morale improved when they had a "teammate" to interact with.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Use Case 2 — GM Manufacturing**
> GM's role in the R2 partnership was to ensure the **dexterous hand and arm** could be re-purposed on Earth for **soft, compliant manipulation in car assembly** (e.g., handling deformable airbag cushions, which traditional rigid-jaw grippers cannot do).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Use Case 3 — Disaster Response (Centuar Project)**
> The "**Robonaut 2 + Leg System**" project (the *Centaur* configuration) attaches a **four-wheeled mobility base** to R2, allowing it to navigate nuclear disaster sites (Fukushima-class) or collapsed buildings for search-and-rescue.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Design Choices

- **Why no legs on the ISS version?** In micro-gravity, **legs serve no propulsion function**, and removing them slashes mass, volume, and joint-count failure modes by ~30%. A *claw-on-rail* mobility system was later added so R2 could slide along ISS handrails.
- **Why tendon-driven hands?** The forearm has only ~**7 cm diameter** — too small to embed 12 BLDC motors. Tendons let the **motors live in the upper arm**, reducing hand inertia and improving *manipulability*.
- **Why 38 processors?** A **centralized processor** would create a single point of failure. Distributed processing also lets **each joint's low-level controller** run at **1 kHz** on its dedicated chip, while the *high-level task planner* runs at **~50 Hz** on the head processor.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total DOF Count of Robonaut 2 (Master Derivation)

This is the **single most important derivation** in the KTU module on R2. The examiner expects you to show every term.

**Step 1 — Start with the general formula for a serial kinematic chain:**

$$
n_{DOF,chain} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{joints}} f_i
$$

where $f_i$ is the **number of degrees of freedom of the *i*-th joint** (1 for revolute, 1 for prismatic, etc.). For R2, every joint is revolute, so $f_i = 1$ for all $i$.

**Step 2 — Decompose R2 into five sub-chains:**

$$
n_{DOF,R2} = n_{head} + n_{torso} + 2 \cdot n_{arm} + 2 \cdot n_{hand}
$$

The factor of **2** appears because R2 has **two** arms and **two** hands (left and right), which are independent kinematic chains.

**Step 3 — Plug in the published NASA DOF counts.**

- Head: $n_{head} = 3$ (yaw, pitch, roll — note: yaw and pitch are actuated, roll is a passive compliant neck)
- Torso: $n_{torso} = 1$ (single waist yaw)
- Each arm: $n_{arm} = 7$ (shoulder 3 + elbow 1 + wrist 3 = **3+1+3 = 7**)
- Each hand: $n_{hand} = 12$ (4 fingers × 3 joints + thumb 0 passive joints, but 12 independent *tendon channels* = 12 effective DOF)

**Step 4 — Substitute into the formula:**

$$
n_{DOF,R2} = 3 + 1 + 2(7) + 2(12)
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate term by term:**

$$
n_{DOF,R2} = 3 + 1 + 14 + 24
$$

**Step 6 — Final summation:**

$$
n_{DOF,R2} = 42
$$

This is the **canonical DOF count for Robonaut 2** and is what the KTU answer key expects.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Tendon Displacement and Force Transmission

The tendon system in each R2 finger maps a **joint rotation** to a **linear tendon pull**. This is a classic *pulley and cable* problem.

**Step 1 — Geometric model.** Consider a single finger joint (e.g., the MCP joint of the index finger) modeled as a cylindrical pulley of radius $r$ around which the tendon is wrapped.

**Step 2 — Arc-length relation.** When the joint rotates by an angle $\theta$ (in radians), the tendon must pay out a length equal to the arc length:

$$
\Delta L_{tendon} = r \cdot \theta
$$

**Step 3 — Force-torque equivalence.** A tendon pulled with tension $F_{tendon}$ exerts a torque on the joint given by the lever-arm relation:

$$
\tau_{joint} = r \cdot F_{tendon}
$$

**Step 4 — Work conservation (the cable is massless and inextensible):** The work done by the tendon at the joint equals the work done by the motor on the other end:

$$
\tau_{joint} \cdot \theta = F_{tendon} \cdot \Delta L_{tendon}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
F_{tendon} \cdot (r \cdot \theta) = r \cdot F_{tendon} \cdot \theta
$$

Both sides are equal — confirming the **conservative nature of the tendon transmission**. This is the property that allows the motor to be placed far from the joint without loss of mechanical authority.

**Step 6 — Final mechanical-advantage statement:** If the motor-side pulley has radius $R_{m}$ and the joint pulley has radius $r_j$, then the *mechanical advantage* is:

$$
MA = \frac{R_{m}}{r_{j}}
$$

and the effective joint torque is:

$$
\tau_{joint} = MA \cdot F_{motor}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Power Budget for a Single Peak-Motion Event

When R2's arm lifts a payload, the **peak electrical power** drawn from the ISS bus can be derived from first principles.

**Step 1 — Mechanical power required to lift mass $m$ at velocity $v$ against gravity $g$:**

$$
P_{mech} = m \cdot g \cdot v
$$

**Step 2 — Account for actuator efficiency $\eta$:**

$$
P_{electrical} = \frac{P_{mech}}{\eta}
$$

**Step 3 — Account for the 28 VDC bus limit.** The bus can deliver a maximum current $I_{max}$. Therefore the peak arm power is:

$$
P_{arm,peak} = V_{bus} \cdot I_{max} = 28 \cdot I_{max}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum across all 14 arm joints (worst case all active simultaneously):**

$$
P_{total,peak} = \sum_{i=1}^{14} P_{arm,i,peak} \leq 1500 \; W
$$

This is the **published peak power budget** for R2, and it tells the ISS power system in advance what to expect.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Subsystem vs. Failure Mode

| Subsystem | Primary Function | Critical Component | Dominant Failure Mode | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical Skeleton | Structural support | Carbon-fiber spine | Stress fracture from vibration | **Redundancy** in load path |
| Actuation | Move joints | BLDC motors + SEAs | Motor burnout | Current limiting & thermal sensors |
| Tendon System | Hand dexterity | Spectra tendons | Tendon fray/rupture | Pre-tension monitoring |
| Sensing | Feedback | Force/Torque sensor | Drift / calibration loss | Periodic auto-calibration |
| Onboard Computing | High-level control | 38 PowerPCs | Single-chip SEU (Single Event Upset from cosmic rays) | **Rad-hardened chips + watchdog timers** |
| Avionics/Power | Power distribution | 28 VDC PSU | Over-current on ISS bus | Inrush limiters |
| Software (ROS) | Task planning | C++/Python nodes | Node crash | **Launch files with respawn = true** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — R2 Subsystem Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of Robonaut 2, exactly as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus. Every node is **alphanumeric-only** (the prefix `r2_` avoids all reserved-keyword collisions) and every label is **clean uppercase text** with no bold/markdown formatting inside the quotes.

```mermaid
graph TD
    r2_hsrc[POWER_AND_AVIONICS] --> r2_psu[28VDC_POWER_SUPPLY_UNIT]
    r2_hsrc[POWER_AND_AVIONICS] --> r2_emi[EMI_FILTER_BANK]
    r2_emi --> r2_dcdc[DC_DC_CONVERTERS]
    r2_dcdc --> r2_cpu[COMPUTING_SUBSYSTEM_38_POWERPC]
    r2_cpu --> r2_real[REALTIME_LOWLEVEL_VXWORKS]
    r2_cpu --> r2_high[HIGHLEVEL_ROS_CPP_PYTHON_NODES]
    r2_high --> r2_plan[TASK_PLANNER_50_HZ]
    r2_real --> r2_mot[BRUSHLESS_DC_MOTOR_CONTROLLERS_1KHZ]
    r2_mot --> r2_act[ACTUATION_SUBSYSTEM]
    r2_act --> r2_armL[LEFT_ARM_7_DOF]
    r2_act --> r2_armR[RIGHT_ARM_7_DOF]
    r2_act --> r2_hdL[LEFT_HAND_12_DOF_TENDON]
    r2_act --> r2_hdR[RIGHT_HAND_12_DOF_TENDON]
    r2_act --> r2_head[HEAD_3_DOF_WITH_STEREO_CAMERAS]
    r2_act --> r2_torso[TORSO_WAIST_YAW_1_DOF]
    r2_sens[SENSING_SUBSYSTEM_350_SENSORS] --> r2_enc[ENCODERS_POTENTIOMETERS]
    r2_sens --> r2_ft[FORCE_TORQUE_WRIST_SENSORS]
    r2_sens --> r2_tac[TACTILE_BIOTAC_FINGERTIPS]
    r2_sens --> r2_ther[THERMAL_AND_CURRENT_MONITORS]
    r2_enc --> r2_fus[SENSOR_FUSION_NODE]
    r2_ft --> r2_fus
    r2_tac --> r2_fus
    r2_ther --> r2_fus
    r2_fus --> r2_plan
    r2_iss[ISS_TELEPRESENCE_OPERATOR] --> r2_tele[R2_TELEPRESENCE_EXOSKELETON]
    r2_tele --> r2_high
    r2_high --> r2_iss
    r2_act --> r2_skin[VECTRAN_TRICOT_PROTECTIVE_SKIN]
    r2_act --> r2_claw[ISS_RAIL_MOBILITY_CLAW]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Data Flow from Operator to Joint

The following diagram traces the **command flow** when a human operator (in a virtual-reality suit) moves R2's right arm. This is a *classic KTU exam-style "explain the data path" question*.

```mermaid
graph LR
    opA[HUMAN_OPERATOR_GLOVE_AND_EXOSKELETON] --> opB[SIGNAL_CONDITIONER_AMP]
    opB --> opC[ISS_LAPTOP_ROS_MASTER_NODE]
    opC -->|ROS_TOPIC_ARM_CMD| opD[R2_HEAD_PROCESSOR_HIGH_LEVEL]
    opD -->|GOAL_JOINT_ANGLES| opE[R2_ARM_PROCESSOR_LOW_LEVEL_1KHZ]
    opE -->|PWM_SIGNALS| opF[BLDC_MOTOR_DRIVER]
    opF --> opG[BRUSHLESS_DC_MOTOR_IN_UPPER_ARM]
    opG -->|TENDON_PULL| opH[FOREFINGER_MCP_JOINT]
    opH -->|FEEDBACK_ENCODER| opE
    opE -->|JOINT_STATE| opD
    opD -->|TF_BROADCAST_AND_JOINT_STATE| opC
    opC -->|HAPTIC_FEEDBACK| opA
```

## 4.3 Text-Based Schematic of the Tendon Hand

For examiners who want a *physical* representation, here is the **ASCII schematic** of how tendons route through the R2 right hand. (Mermaid is unsuitable for this kind of anatomical drawing, so a text schematic is the cleanest fallback.)

```
   SHOULDER (3 DOF) ----> ELBOW (1 DOF) ----> WRIST (3 DOF)
                                                 |
                                                 v
                                          [FOREARM TENDON SHEATH]
                                          |  |  |  |  |  |
                                          T1 T2 T3 T4 T5 T6
                                          |  |  |  |  |  |
                                          v  v  v  v  v  v
                                         THMB INDX MIDD RING PNKY
                                          (3) (3) (3) (3) (3)   <-- Joints per finger
                                          === DRIVE =============
                                          12 BLDC motors in upper
                                          arm driving 12 tendons
                                          via 12 capstan pulleys.
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Guide for KTU Students**
> In the ESE, when asked to "draw the block diagram of Robonaut 2," the examiner expects **at least these blocks**:
> 1. Power Source (28 VDC)
> 2. Central Computing (ROS nodes)
> 3. Low-Level Real-Time Controller (VxWorks/1 kHz)
> 4. Actuators (BLDC + SEA)
> 5. Sensors (Encoder, F/T, Tactile)
> 6. Mechanical Body (Torso, Arms, Hands, Head)
> 7. Skin and Mobility Add-ons
> 8. Telepresence Interface (Operator exoskeleton)

The Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 contains **all 8** of these blocks — it is therefore an "exemplar" answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**: Part A is 3 marks (no choice), Part B is 14 marks (internal choice between two questions, each with two 7-mark sub-parts).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]
**List the three primary design objectives of NASA's Robonaut 2 project and identify its two principal development partners.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three primary design objectives of Robonaut 2 are:

1. **Operate safely in close proximity to humans** — both inside a spacecraft cabin and during extravehicular activities (EVA) — without the need for a safety cage.
2. **Use the same tools and interfaces designed for human astronauts**, so no spacecraft redesign is needed.
3. **Reduce astronaut exposure to dangerous, repetitive, or tedious tasks** by serving as an autonomous or teleoperated assistant.

The two principal development partners are:
- **NASA's Johnson Space Center (JSC), Houston, Texas** — providing spaceflight systems engineering, rad-hardened electronics, and ISS integration.
- **General Motors (GM)** — providing dexterous manipulation technology, automotive-grade manufacturing rigor, and ground-based applications.

**(3 marks: 1 for objective 1, 1 for objectives 2 & 3, 1 for partners)**

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the total number of degrees of freedom, onboard processors, and integrated sensors in Robonaut 2.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Total Degrees of Freedom:** **42 DOF** (3 head + 1 torso + 2×7 arms + 2×12 hands)
- **Onboard Processors:** **38 PowerPC processors** distributed across the robot
- **Integrated Sensors:** **350 or more sensors** (encoders, current, thermal, force/torque, tactile, vision)

**(3 marks: 1 mark per correct value)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The internal choice below is the **exact KTU pattern**: students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each long question has two 7-mark sub-parts (a) and (b).

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Choose this OR Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**
**With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the seven major subsystems of Robonaut 2. Briefly describe the role of ROS in its software architecture.**

**Model Answer:**

The seven major subsystems of Robonaut 2 are:

**1. Power & Avionics Subsystem:** Receives **28 VDC** from the ISS bus, filters EMI, and provides regulated rails to all subsystems. Peak power **1500 W**.

**2. Onboard Computing Subsystem:** Contains **38 PowerPC processors** communicating over **SpaceWire (IEEE 1355)**. Split into real-time (low-level, 1 kHz) and high-level (50 Hz) tiers.

**3. Sensing Subsystem:** More than **350 sensors** — joint encoders, current sensors, thermistors, force/torque sensors at the wrists, and tactile (BioTac-class) sensors in the fingertips.

**4. Actuation Subsystem:** **Brushless DC motors** drive most joints; **Series Elastic Actuators (SEAs)** at the wrists provide force-controlled compliance for safe human contact.

**5. Mechanical Body Subsystem:** Carbon-fiber torso spine, single waist yaw joint, two 7-DOF arms, two 12-DOF hands, 3-DOF head with stereo cameras. **42 DOF** in total. A **Vectran/Tricot fabric skin** covers the body.

**6. Tendon-Transmission Subsystem (Hands):** Routes **12 tendons per hand** from motors in the upper arm to the fingers, allowing low-inertia manipulation.

**7. Telepresence & Software (ROS) Subsystem:** Human operators wear an exoskeleton whose joint angles are published as **ROS topics** (`/r2/right_arm/cmd` etc.). R2's onboard processors subscribe to these topics, compute inverse kinematics, and command the motors. The ROS master node runs on a crew laptop; high-level planners use **MoveIt!** for motion planning; sensor data is published to `/r2/joint_states` and visualized in **RViz**.

**Block Diagram (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for the exemplar).**

**(7 marks: 1 per subsystem = 6 marks; 1 mark for ROS role explanation)**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set C]**
**Calculate the total degrees of freedom of Robonaut 2. Show the formula, list each contributing chain, and state the standard number of onboard processors and sensors.**

**Model Answer:**

Using the general formula for a serial kinematic chain:

$$
n_{DOF} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{joints}} f_i
$$

For R2, every joint is revolute so $f_i = 1$. Decomposing the robot into independent chains:

| Sub-chain | DOF per chain | Number of chains | Subtotal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Head (yaw, pitch, roll) | 3 | 1 | 3 |
| Torso (waist yaw) | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| Arm (shoulder 3 + elbow 1 + wrist 3) | 7 | 2 | 14 |
| Hand (4 fingers × 3 + thumb underactuated) | 12 | 2 | 24 |
| **Total** | — | — | **42** |

$$
n_{DOF,R2} = 3 + 1 + 2(7) + 2(12) = 42
$$

**Number of onboard processors:** **38 PowerPC processors** (distributed).
**Number of integrated sensors:** **≥ 350 sensors** (encoders, current, thermal, F/T, tactile).

**(7 marks: 2 for formula, 3 for table & calculation, 1 for final answer, 1 for processors/sensors)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set D]**
**Explain the tendon-driven hand mechanism of Robonaut 2. Why is a tendon system used instead of placing motors directly inside the hand? Derive the relationship between tendon displacement and joint rotation.**

**Model Answer:**

The R2 hand is a **tendon-driven, underactuated, anthropomorphic gripper** with 5 fingers. Each finger has 3 joints (MCP, PIP, DIP), and each joint is moved by tendons routed through the wrist and forearm up to the BLDC motors in the upper arm.

**Why tendons instead of in-hand motors?**
1. **Space constraint:** The forearm is only **~7 cm** in diameter — too narrow for 12 BLDC motors. Placing motors proximally keeps the hand small and human-shaped.
2. **Reduced inertia:** Hand mass must be low so the wrist can accelerate rapidly without saturating motor torque.
3. **Compliance:** Tendons provide a small amount of passive elasticity, helping the hand conform to irregular objects.
4. **Tool compatibility:** A human-shaped, low-inertia hand can use the *same tools* astronauts use.

**Derivation of tendon-joint relationship:**

Let a joint be modeled as a pulley of radius $r$. When the joint rotates by $\theta$ (radians), the tendon must pay out:

$$
\Delta L_{tendon} = r \cdot \theta
$$

The corresponding force-torque equivalence is:

$$
\tau_{joint} = r \cdot F_{tendon}
$$

The mechanical advantage, when the motor-side pulley has radius $R_m$, is:

$$
MA = \frac{R_{m}}{r_{joint}}
$$

such that the effective joint torque becomes:

$$
\tau_{joint} = MA \cdot F_{motor}
$$

**(7 marks: 2 for explanation, 2 for "why tendons" with 4 sub-points, 3 for the derivation)**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]**
**Discuss the role of Robonaut 2 in ISS operations. What are the limitations of the original (legless) design, and how does the Centaur configuration overcome them?**

**Model Answer:**

**Role in ISS Operations:**

- R2 was launched aboard **STS-133 (Space Shuttle Discovery)** on **24 February 2011** and became the **first humanoid robot in space**.
- It performs **routine, repetitive, and tedious tasks** such as **cleaning air filters, vacuuming handrails, and assisting with experiment setup**.
- It serves as a **telerobot** for astronauts inside the station using the **R2 Telepresence System** (an exoskeleton interface).
- It acts as a **technology demonstration platform** for future deep-space humanoids that will support Artemis lunar and Mars missions.

**Limitations of the Legless Design:**

1. **No independent mobility** — R2 must be carried to its work site or attached to a fixed base.
2. **Limited workspace** — it can only reach what is within ~1.2 m of its mounting point.
3. **Cannot navigate between ISS modules** without human assistance.
4. **Cannot perform EVA** because it has no leg-like limbs to anchor itself to the station's exterior truss.
5. **Single point of placement** — once mounted, it cannot reposition.

**How the Centaur Configuration Overcomes These:**

The **Centaur** (sometimes called **"R2 + Leg"**) project attaches a **four-wheeled mobility base** (and optionally **two articulated climbing legs**) to the legless R2 torso. Benefits:

- **Self-locomotion** over flat ground and rough terrain (disaster sites, lunar surface analogs).
- **Climbing capability** on ladders and station trusses.
- **Expanded workspace** — no longer confined to a single mounting point.
- **Disaster-response use case** (Fukushima-style scenarios).
- The **legs can grasp handrails** like human legs, providing body stabilization for two-handed tool use.

**(7 marks: 2 for ISS role, 2 for limitations, 3 for Centaur discussion)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks Every Semester**

1. **Writing "32 DOF" or "37 DOF"** — The correct value is **42 DOF**. The 42 figure comes from `3 + 1 + 14 + 24`. If a student forgets the *torso* DOF, they get 41; if they forget the *head* DOF, they get 39; both are wrong.
2. **Confusing *Robonaut 1* and *Robonaut 2* specs** — R1 had ~24 DOF and *no* onboard processors (it was tethered to a workstation). Examiners love this trap.
3. **Forgetting the tendon hand in the DOF count** — Some students stop at "head + torso + 2 arms = 17" and forget that the **hands contribute 24 more DOF**, almost half the total.
4. **Saying "R2 has legs"** — The *original* ISS R2 has **no legs**. The Centaur version does. Read the question carefully.
5. **Writing "28 VAC"** instead of "**28 VDC**" — the ISS power bus is **Direct Current**, and confusing it with household AC loses a mark.
6. **Skipping the ROS layer** — A block diagram without a `ROS Master` / `ROS Topic` block is incomplete. The KTU syllabus is specifically about *Robot Operating System*, so ROS must be in the diagram.
7. **Wrong conversion of tendon units** — Tendon length is in **meters**, joint angle in **radians**. Mixing degrees into $\Delta L = r\theta$ will give a 57× error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- ✅ Robonaut 2 is a humanoid **upper-torso robot** developed by **NASA JSC + GM**.
- ✅ Launched to ISS on **STS-133, 24 Feb 2011**; first humanoid in space.
- ✅ **42 DOF total** = 3 (head) + 1 (torso) + 2×7 (arms) + 2×12 (hands).
- ✅ **38 PowerPC processors** onboard, communicating via **SpaceWire (IEEE 1355)**.
- ✅ **350+ sensors** (encoders, F/T, tactile BioTac, thermal, current).
- ✅ **28 VDC** power bus; **1500 W peak**, **500 W continuous**.
- ✅ **Lifting capacity:** 9 kg (20 lbs) per arm.
- ✅ **Reach:** ~1.2 m from shoulder.
- ✅ **Tendon-driven hands** with 12 tendons/hand; motors in upper arm.
- ✅ **Series Elastic Actuators (SEAs)** at wrists for force control.
- ✅ **Vectran/Tricot fabric skin** protects humans from pinch points.
- ✅ **No legs** in the ISS version; **Centaur** adds 4-wheel mobility + climbing legs.
- ✅ **ROS-based software**; high-level in C++/Python, low-level on VxWorks (1 kHz).
- ✅ Telepresence via exoskeleton; feedback via `/joint_states` and `/tf`.
- ✅ Tendon-joint relation: $\Delta L = r\theta$ and $\tau = r F$.
- ✅ Mechanical advantage: $MA = R_m / r_{joint}$.
- ✅ NASA motto behind R2: handle tasks that are **"too dangerous, too repetitive, or too tedious"** for astronauts.
- ✅ Three key use cases: **ISS ops, GM manufacturing, disaster response**.
- ✅ DOF must be quoted as **42** — not 32, not 37, not 39.
- ✅ ISS power bus is **DC**, not AC.
- ✅ R2's head DOF is **3**, not 2 (some students forget the roll joint).

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Section 5 — You have completed the full KTU 2024 Scheme study sheet for Robonaut 2 (Module 3 of PBRAT504).** Re-read the **Topic Recap** above, draw the **Mermaid block diagram** from Section 4.1 twice on a blank sheet, and you are exam-ready. All the best!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TurtleBot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** TurtleBot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**TurtleBot** is the world's most popular open-source, low-cost, personal **mobile robot platform** built on the Robot Operating System (ROS). It serves as the **de-facto standard reference platform** for ROS-based research, education, and prototyping. TurtleBot combines an **onboard computer**, a **mobile base (differential drive)**, a **depth sensor (RGB-D camera or LiDAR)**, and ROS 2 software stacks to enable **autonomous navigation**, **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)**, **teleoperation**, and **human-robot interaction**.

Mathematically, TurtleBot is classified as a **non-holonomic, differential-drive wheeled mobile robot (WMR)** governed by the kinematic model:

$$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}, \quad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$

where $v$ is linear velocity, $\omega$ is angular velocity, $r$ is wheel radius, $L$ is wheelbase, and $\omega_R, \omega_L$ are the right and left wheel angular velocities respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** TurtleBot is the **flagship reference platform** in Module 3 because it integrates ALL previously taught concepts — Nodes, Topics, Services, Actions, tf2, URDF, Gazebo, Navigation Stack (Nav2), and SLAM Toolbox — into one working physical robot.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine TurtleBot as a **"smart pet tortoise"** that:
- 🐢 **Moves around** your house on wheels (the mobile base).
- 👀 **Sees the world** through a 3D camera mounted on its back (the depth sensor).
- 🧠 **Thinks** using a small computer brain running ROS 2 (the onboard PC / SBC).
- 📡 **Talks** to your laptop wirelessly through Wi-Fi (ROS DDS communication).

> Just like how a student learns by practicing on real equipment in a lab rather than only reading theory, ROS developers learn by coding on TurtleBot — it is the **"Hello World" robot of the ROS ecosystem**.

## 1.3 Standard TurtleBot Hardware Specifications

| Generation | Processor | Sensor | Base | Cost (approx) |
|------------|-----------|--------|------|---------------|
| **TurtleBot 1 (2010)** | Netbook | Kinect | iRobot Create | \$500 |
| **TurtleBot 2 (2012)** | Laptop/PC | Kinect / Xtion | Kobuki | \$1200 |
| **TurtleBot 3 (2017)** | Raspberry Pi / Intel Joule | Intel RealSense / LDS-01 | OpenCR / Waffle / Burger | \$550 |
| **TurtleBot 4 (2022)** | Raspberry Pi 4 | OAK-D / RPLidar A1 | iRobot Create 3 | \$1100 |

> [!NOTE]
> **TurtleBot 4** is the **current 2024 KTU reference model** because it ships with **ROS 2 Humble/Iron** pre-installed and uses the **iRobot Create 3 base** (the same one used in the famous Roomba vacuum cleaner).

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics

- **Differential drive configuration** (two independently driven wheels + passive caster)
- **Operating System:** Ubuntu 22.04 LTS with **ROS 2 Humble Hawksbill** (LTS)
- **SLAM algorithm:** SLAM Toolbox / Cartographer
- **Navigation stack:** **Nav2 (Navigation 2)** in ROS 2
- **Battery life:** ~4–6 hours (TurtleBot 4 Standard)
- **Payload capacity:** ~9 kg (TurtleBot 3 Burger)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of TurtleBot (Layered Analysis)

TurtleBot is structured into **5 logical layers** (this is the "Why" and "How"):

### Layer 1 — Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)
- **Drivers** for wheels, IMU, battery, sensors run here.
- Publishes raw data as ROS 2 topics (e.g., `/battery_state`, `/imu`, `/scan`, `/cmd_vel`).
- *Why:* Separates hardware from algorithms so the same code works on different physical robots.

### Layer 2 — Sensor Data Acquisition
- **2D LiDAR** (e.g., RPLidar A1) → publishes `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` on `/scan`.
- **RGB-D Camera** (e.g., OAK-D Pro) → publishes `sensor_msgs/Image` and `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2` on `/oakd/rgb` and `/oakd/stereo/points`.
- **IMU** → publishes `sensor_msgs/Imu` for orientation.
- **Wheel encoders** → publish `nav_msgs/Odometry`.

### Layer 3 — Localisation & Mapping (SLAM)
- The **SLAM Toolbox node** subscribes to `/scan` and `/tf` to build a 2D occupancy grid map.
- Publishes the map on `/map` topic of type `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`.

### Layer 4 — Navigation (Nav2)
- **Nav2 stack** uses **four planners**:
  1. **Global Planner** — Computes A* / Dijkstra path from start to goal.
  2. **Local Planner** (DWB / TEB) — Avoids dynamic obstacles in real-time.
  3. **Recovery behaviours** — Rotation, clearing, waiting.
  4. **Behaviour tree** — Orchestrates the planners and recoveries.

### Layer 5 — Application Layer
- User writes **Python (rclpy)** or **C++ (rclcpp)** nodes for high-level tasks.
- Examples: go-to-coordinate, follow-person, patrol, object detection.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following are the **mathematically verified** kinematic and navigation formulas for TurtleBot's differential drive base.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbols | Unit |
|---|---------|---------|---------|------|
| 1 | **Forward kinematics** (linear vel.) | $v = \dfrac{r}{2}\left(\omega_R + \omega_L\right)$ | $v, r, \omega_R, \omega_L$ | $\text{m/s}, \text{m}, \text{rad/s}$ |
| 2 | **Forward kinematics** (angular vel.) | $\omega = \dfrac{r}{L}\left(\omega_R - \omega_L\right)$ | $\omega, L$ | $\text{rad/s}, \text{m}$ |
| 3 | **Inverse kinematics** | $\omega_R = \dfrac{2v + \omega L}{2r}, \quad \omega_L = \dfrac{2v - \omega L}{2r}$ | — | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| 4 | **Pose update (odometry)** | $x_{t+1} = x_t + v \Delta t \cos\theta_t$ | $x_t, y_t, \theta_t$ | $\text{m}$ |
| 5 | **Pose update (odometry)** | $y_{t+1} = y_t + v \Delta t \sin\theta_t$ | — | $\text{m}$ |
| 6 | **Pose update (heading)** | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \omega \Delta t$ | — | $\text{rad}$ |
| 7 | **ICC (Instantaneous Centre of Curvature)** | $R = \dfrac{L}{2}\dfrac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$ | $R$ (turn radius) | $\text{m}$ |
| 8 | **Special case: straight line** | $\omega_R = \omega_L \Rightarrow \omega = 0$ | — | — |
| 9 | **Special case: in-place rotation** | $\omega_R = -\omega_L \Rightarrow v = 0$ | — | — |
| 10 | **LiDAR range** (TurtleBot 4) | $r_{\max} = 12\,\text{m},\; \Delta\theta = 1^{\circ}$ | — | $\text{m}, \text{deg}$ |
| 11 | **SLAM map resolution** | $\text{res} \in [0.01, 0.05]\,\text{m/pixel}$ | — | $\text{m}$ |
| 12 | **Nav2 control frequency** | $f_{\text{ctrl}} = 20\,\text{Hz},\; \Delta t = 0.05\,\text{s}$ | — | $\text{Hz, s}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Formula Pitfall:** In equation (1)–(2), the **wheelbase $L$ is the distance between the TWO drive wheels**, not from a wheel to the caster wheel. The caster is non-driven and does NOT enter the kinematic equations.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

TurtleBot platforms are used in:
- **Warehouse logistics** (Amazon Robotics prototypes).
- **Hospital service robots** (medication delivery).
- **Agricultural scouting** (greenhouse monitoring).
- **Disaster response research** (DARPA SubT Challenge).
- **Academic teaching** (more than **500 universities** worldwide use TurtleBot in robotics labs).

## 2.4 ROS 2 Topics & Nodes Quick Map (TurtleBot 4)

| Topic / Node | Message Type | Publisher / Purpose |
|--------------|--------------|---------------------|
| `/cmd_vel` | `geometry_msgs/Twist` | Velocity commands to motors |
| `/odom` | `nav_msgs/Odometry` | Wheel-encoder integration |
| `/scan` | `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` | 2D LiDAR data |
| `/map` | `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` | SLAM-generated map |
| `/tf`, `/tf_static` | `tf2_msgs/TFMessage` | Coordinate transforms |
| `/cmd_vel_nav` | `geometry_msgs/Twist` | Nav2 output |
| `/goal_pose` | `geometry_msgs/PoseStamped` | Navigation goal input |
| `bt_navigator` | Node | Behaviour-tree planner |
| `planner_server` | Node | Global path planner |
| `controller_server` | Node | Local controller (DWB) |
| `slam_toolbox` | Node | Mapping & localization |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Differential-Drive Kinematic Model of TurtleBot

We will now derive the **forward kinematics** of TurtleBot's two-wheel differential-drive base **from first principles**. This is a high-frequency 14-mark KTU question.

### Step 1 — Define the System Geometry

Consider a robot with:
- Two **active wheels** of radius $r$ on the left and right.
- A **passive caster** wheel for balance.
- The contact points of the two drive wheels are separated by the **wheelbase $L$** along the robot's lateral axis (the $y$-axis of the body frame).
- The body frame $\mathcal{F}_B$ has its origin at the midpoint of the wheelbase.

### Step 2 — Wheel Velocity in World Frame

For the right wheel, the velocity at the contact point in the body frame is:

$$v_R = r \cdot \omega_R \quad (\text{along } +x_B)$$

Similarly for the left wheel:

$$v_L = r \cdot \omega_L \quad (\text{along } +x_B)$$

### Step 3 — Definition of Linear and Angular Velocities

The **linear velocity $v$** of the robot is the average of the two wheel velocities:

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$

The **angular velocity $\omega$** is computed from the differential. Since each wheel is at distance $L/2$ from the centre, the tangential contribution to rotation is $r\,\omega / 1$ (rotation about the ICC), and the relative velocity between wheels is $v_R - v_L$, which spans the wheelbase $L$:

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$

### Step 4 — Matrix Form (Clean Compact Representation)

The two equations can be elegantly written as a matrix multiplication:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{r}{2} & \dfrac{r}{2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{r}{L} & -\dfrac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_R \\ \omega_L \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **forward-kinematic Jacobian $J$** of TurtleBot. It is **invertible** as long as $L \neq 0$ (which is always true).

### Step 5 — Inverse Kinematics (Solving for Wheel Speeds)

Inverting the matrix above:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \omega_R \\ \omega_L \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{r} & \dfrac{L}{2r} \\[6pt] \dfrac{1}{r} & -\dfrac{L}{2r} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

Expanding:

$$\omega_R = \frac{v}{r} + \frac{L \cdot \omega}{2r}, \qquad \omega_L = \frac{v}{r} - \frac{L \cdot \omega}{2r}$$

### Step 6 — Pose Integration (Discrete-Time Odometry)

Given current pose $(x_t, y_t, \theta_t)$ and control inputs $(v, \omega)$ over time $\Delta t$:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &= x_t + v \cdot \Delta t \cdot \cos\theta_t \\
y_{t+1} &= y_t + v \cdot \Delta t \cdot \sin\theta_t \\
\theta_{t+1} &= \theta_t + \omega \cdot \Delta t
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **dead-reckoning update rule** used by TurtleBot's `/odom` topic.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Behaviour of TurtleBot Under Specific Inputs

| Case | $\omega_R$ | $\omega_L$ | Resulting $v$ | Resulting $\omega$ | Robot Behaviour |
|------|------------|------------|---------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $\omega_0$ | $\omega_0$ | $r\omega_0$ | $0$ | Moves **straight forward** |
| 2 | $-\omega_0$ | $-\omega_0$ | $-r\omega_0$ | $0$ | Moves **straight backward** |
| 3 | $\omega_0$ | $-\omega_0$ | $0$ | $\dfrac{2r\omega_0}{L}$ | **In-place clockwise** rotation |
| 4 | $\omega_0$ | $0$ | $\dfrac{r\omega_0}{2}$ | $\dfrac{r\omega_0}{L}$ | **Curved arc** (left turn) |
| 5 | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | **Stops** (no motion) |
| 6 | $2\omega_0$ | $\omega_0$ | $\dfrac{3r\omega_0}{2}$ | $\dfrac{r\omega_0}{L}$ | Wide left arc |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Question:** A TurtleBot 3 Burger has wheel radius $r = 0.033\,\text{m}$ and wheelbase $L = 0.16\,\text{m}$. The right wheel spins at $\omega_R = 2.5\,\text{rad/s}$ and the left wheel at $\omega_L = 1.5\,\text{rad/s}$. Compute linear velocity $v$, angular velocity $\omega$, turning radius $R$, and the pose change after $\Delta t = 1\,\text{s}$ starting from origin.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Linear velocity:**

$$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} = \frac{0.033 \times (2.5 + 1.5)}{2} = \frac{0.033 \times 4.0}{2} = 0.066\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Angular velocity:**

$$\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} = \frac{0.033 \times (2.5 - 1.5)}{0.16} = \frac{0.033 \times 1.0}{0.16} = 0.20625\,\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Turning radius (ICC):**

$$R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L} = \frac{0.16}{2} \cdot \frac{2.5 + 1.5}{2.5 - 1.5} = 0.08 \times 4 = 0.32\,\text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Pose change after 1 second** (starting from $x_0 = y_0 = \theta_0 = 0$):

$$x_1 = 0 + 0.066 \times 1 \times \cos(0) = 0.066\,\text{m}$$

$$y_1 = 0 + 0.066 \times 1 \times \sin(0) = 0.000\,\text{m}$$

$$\theta_1 = 0 + 0.20625 \times 1 = 0.20625\,\text{rad} \approx 11.82^{\circ}$$

> ✅ **Verification:** The robot moves slightly forward and turns by about $11.82^{\circ}$ in 1 second — consistent with a sharp right turn at low speed (turning radius 32 cm). 

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TurtleBot 4 Complete System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSORS["SENSOR LAYER"]
        lidarA["RPLidar A1"]
        camA["OAK-D Pro RGBD Camera"]
        imuA["IMU 9DOF"]
        odomA["Wheel Encoders"]
        bumperA["Cliff & Bumper Sensors"]
    end

    subgraph HAL["HARDWARE ABSTRACTION LAYER - ROS 2 DRIVERS"]
        lidarNode["scan_node"]
        camNode["oakd_node"]
        imuNode["imu_node"]
        odomNode["create3_base_node"]
    end

    subgraph TF["TF2 TRANSFORM TREE"]
        tfMap["map frame"]
        tfOdom["odom frame"]
        tfBase["base_footprint frame"]
        tfLidar["laser_frame"]
        tfCam["oakd_frame"]
    end

    subgraph SLAM["SLAM LAYER"]
        slamNode["slam_toolbox"]
        mapOut["/map OccupancyGrid"]
    end

    subgraph NAV["NAV2 NAVIGATION LAYER"]
        globalNode["planner_server - NavFn"]
        localNode["controller_server - DWB"]
        btNode["bt_navigator"]
        recoveryNode["recovery_server"]
    end

    subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        userNode["user_node rclpy"]
        goalPub["/goal_pose publisher"]
        cmdPub["/cmd_vel publisher"]
    end

    lidarA --> lidarNode
    camA --> camNode
    imuA --> imuNode
    odomA --> odomNode
    bumperA --> odomNode

    lidarNode --> TF
    camNode --> TF
    imuNode --> TF
    odomNode --> TF

    TF --> slamNode
    slamNode --> mapOut
    mapOut --> globalNode

    userNode --> goalPub
    goalPub --> btNode
    btNode --> globalNode
    btNode --> localNode
    localNode --> cmdPub
    cmdPub --> odomNode

    slamNode -.-> tfOdom
    odomNode -.-> tfBase
```

## 4.2 Differential-Drive Geometric Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TB["TURTLEBOT TOP VIEW"]
        wheelR["RIGHT WHEEL radius r"]
        wheelL["LEFT WHEEL radius r"]
        caster["CASTER passive"]
        center["C origin"]
        icc["ICC INSTANTANEOUS CENTER"]
    end

    wheelR --- center
    wheelL --- center
    caster --- center
    center -.->|"turn radius R"| icc
```

## 4.3 ROS 2 Navigation Goal Flow (Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start(["User Issues Goal x,y,theta"]) --> pub1["Publish on /goal_pose PoseStamped"]
    pub1 --> btNav["bt_navigator receives goal"]
    btNav --> globalPlan["planner_server computes global path"]
    globalPlan --> localPlan["controller_server DWB local plan"]
    localPlan --> cmdVel["Publish /cmd_vel Twist"]
    cmdVel --> motor["Create 3 base drives wheels"]
    motor --> odom["Wheel encoders publish /odom"]
    odom --> localPlan
    odom --> globalPlan
    localPlan --> check{"Reached goal?"}
    check -- no --> localPlan
    check -- yes --> done(["Success / Exit behaviour tree"])
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define TurtleBot. List any two hardware sensors used in TurtleBot 4 and state the ROS 2 topic on which each publishes data.**

**Model Answer:**

> **TurtleBot** is an open-source, low-cost, ROS-based mobile robot platform used as a standard reference for robotics research and education. It uses a differential-drive base, an onboard computer running ROS 2, and various sensors for perception.

| Sensor | ROS 2 Topic | Message Type |
|--------|-------------|--------------|
| 2D LiDAR (RPLidar A1) | `/scan` | `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` |
| RGB-D Camera (OAK-D) | `/oakd/rgb/preview/image_raw` | `sensor_msgs/Image` |
| IMU | `/imu` | `sensor_msgs/Imu` |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for sensor+topic pairs)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of the SLAM Toolbox and the Nav2 stack in TurtleBot's autonomous navigation pipeline.**

**Model Answer:**

> **SLAM Toolbox** simultaneously builds a 2D occupancy grid map of the environment (mapping) and estimates the robot's pose within that map (localization), using LiDAR scans and odometry. It publishes the map on the `/map` topic.
>
> **Nav2 (Navigation 2)** is the ROS 2 navigation stack that consumes the SLAM-built map, accepts a goal pose, plans a global path, executes local obstacle avoidance using a controller (DWB), and coordinates everything through a **behaviour tree**. It outputs velocity commands on `/cmd_vel`.
>
> Together, **SLAM provides the "where" (map + pose)** and **Nav2 provides the "how" (path + motion)** — enabling full autonomy.

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B is **Module Internal Choice**. You must answer **either Q(A) or Q(B)**.

---

### **Q(A). `[KTU University Exam — Module 3 Internal Choice]`**
**(a)** Derive the forward and inverse kinematic equations of a **differential-drive mobile robot** with wheel radius $r$, wheelbase $L$, right-wheel angular velocity $\omega_R$ and left-wheel angular velocity $\omega_L$. Show the Jacobian matrix form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A TurtleBot 3 Burger has $r = 0.033\,\text{m}$, $L = 0.16\,\text{m}$. To move in a circle of radius $R = 0.5\,\text{m}$ at a linear velocity $v = 0.2\,\text{m/s}$, compute (i) the required $\omega_R$ and $\omega_L$, (ii) the angular velocity $\omega$, and (iii) the pose $(x, y, \theta)$ after 2 seconds starting from origin. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to the derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key equations to write:

$$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}, \quad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$

$$\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r/2 & r/2 \\ r/L & -r/L \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_R \\ \omega_L \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\omega_R = \frac{v}{r} + \frac{L\omega}{2r}, \quad \omega_L = \frac{v}{r} - \frac{L\omega}{2r}$$

**Mark split:** 1 mark for each equation, 1 mark for Jacobian, 1 mark for inverse form, 1 mark for special cases.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step (i) — Find $\omega$ first** using $v = \omega R$:

$$\omega = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{0.2}{0.5} = 0.4\,\text{rad/s}$$

**Step (ii) — Compute $\omega_R$ and $\omega_L$** from inverse kinematics:

$$\omega_R = \frac{0.2}{0.033} + \frac{0.16 \times 0.4}{2 \times 0.033} = 6.0606 + 0.9697 = 7.0303\,\text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega_L = \frac{0.2}{0.033} - \frac{0.16 \times 0.4}{2 \times 0.033} = 6.0606 - 0.9697 = 5.0909\,\text{rad/s}$$

**Step (iii) — Pose update over $\Delta t = 2\,\text{s}$:**

$$x = 0 + 0.2 \times 2 \times \cos(0) = 0.4\,\text{m}$$

$$y = 0 + 0.2 \times 2 \times \sin(0) = 0\,\text{m}$$
