# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Real-Time Factor (RTF) Calculation in Multi-Robot Simulation

The **Real-Time Factor (RTF)** is the most fundamental performance metric for any simulator. KTU may present a numerical scenario asking you to compute RTF or determine simulator feasibility.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A research team uses **Stage** to simulate **50 mobile robots** with 2D LiDAR, running for a wall-clock duration of $T_{\text{wall}} = 60\,\text{s}$. The simulation advances to a simulated time of $T_{\text{sim}} = 180\,\text{s}$. Compute the **Real-Time Factor (RTF)** and the **simulation speedup**.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Recall the definition of Real-Time Factor (RTF).**

$$\text{RTF} = \frac{T_{\text{sim}}}{T_{\text{wall}}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values.**

$$\text{RTF} = \frac{180\,\text{s}}{60\,\text{s}}$$

**Step 3: Simplify.**

$$\boxed{\text{RTF} = 3.0}$$

> **Interpretation:** The simulator runs at **3× real time** — it produces 3 seconds of simulated time for every 1 second of wall-clock time. This is typical for lightweight 2D simulators running many agents.

**Step 4: Compute the Speedup (inverse of RTF in some contexts).**

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{\text{wall}}}{T_{\text{sim}}} = \frac{60\,\text{s}}{180\,\text{s}} = 0.33$$

> **Note:** Some textbooks define Speedup $= T_{\text{sim}}/T_{\text{wall}}$ — always verify the convention. Here, **RTF = 3.0** is the canonical measure.

### 3.1.3 Analytical Insight

| Simulator | Typical RTF (single robot) | Typical RTF (10+ robots) | Bottleneck |
|-----------|---------------------------|--------------------------|------------|
| Stage | 5–50 | 2–10 | CPU, 2D rasterization |
| Gazebo | 0.5–1.5 | 0.1–0.5 | Physics solver, GPU |
| Webots | 0.5–1.2 | 0.3–0.8 | ODE step size |
| CoppeliaSim | 0.8–1.5 | 0.4–1.0 | Bullet/Vortex step |
| CARLA | 0.3–1.0 (UE4) | 0.1–0.4 | Unreal rendering |
| AirSim | 0.4–1.0 | N/A (single agent) | UE4 + physics |
| Isaac Sim | 0.2–0.8 | 0.1–0.3 (parallel via Isaac Gym) | GPU ray tracing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** If RTF < 0.5, the simulator is slower than real-time — **regression testing** in CI/CD pipelines may still be feasible, but **closed-loop hardware-in-the-loop** (HIL) testing becomes problematic.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Sensor Sampling Budget for Multi-Robot Simulation

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A Webots simulation runs $N = 4$ robots. Each robot carries:
- 1 × RGB camera at $f_c = 30\,\text{Hz}$
- 1 × 2D LiDAR at $f_l = 20\,\text{Hz}$
- 1 × IMU at $f_i = 200\,\text{Hz}$

Each message has a fixed ROS message size (excluding headers): $S_c = 1.5\,\text{MB}$ (compressed image), $S_l = 25\,\text{kB}$ (LaserScan), $S_i = 0.5\,\text{kB}$ (Imu).

Compute the **total data throughput** $D_{\text{total}}$ in MB/s.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute per-robot throughput per sensor.**

$$D_c = f_c \times S_c = 30 \times 1.5 = 45\,\text{MB/s}$$

$$D_l = f_l \times S_l = 20 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} = 0.5\,\text{MB/s}$$

$$D_i = f_i \times S_i = 200 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.1\,\text{MB/s}$$

**Step 2: Sum per-robot throughput.**

$$D_{\text{robot}} = D_c + D_l + D_i = 45 + 0.5 + 0.1 = 45.6\,\text{MB/s}$$

**Step 3: Multiply by the number of robots.**

$$D_{\text{total}} = N \times D_{\text{robot}} = 4 \times 45.6 = \boxed{182.4\,\text{MB/s}}$$

**Step 4: Convert to Gigabits per second (Gbps) for network bandwidth analysis.**

$$D_{\text{total}} = 182.4 \times 8 = 1459.2\,\text{Mbps} \approx 1.46\,\text{Gbps}$$

> **Practical Insight:** This is the **internal ROS bus load**. If using multi-machine ROS 2 with `CycloneDDS` or `Fast DDS`, the network must support this throughput. Most modern GigE networks suffice; Wi-Fi may saturate.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Sim-to-Real Gap Quantification

The **Sim-to-Real Gap** $G$ is a critical concept for KTU. It measures how well a policy trained in simulation transfers to a real robot.

### 3.4.1 Definition

$$G = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert x_{\text{real}}^{(i)} - x_{\text{sim}}^{(i)} \vert$$

where $x^{(i)}$ is the $i$-th observation (e.g., joint angle, position, image).

### 3.4.2 Worked Example

A manipulator trained in **PyBullet** shows the following end-effector positions in 5 test trials:

| Trial | Real (m) | Sim (m) |
|-------|---------|---------|
| 1 | (0.52, 0.31, 0.10) | (0.50, 0.30, 0.12) |
| 2 | (0.61, 0.22, 0.15) | (0.60, 0.20, 0.16) |
| 3 | (0.48, 0.35, 0.08) | (0.49, 0.34, 0.09) |
| 4 | (0.55, 0.28, 0.12) | (0.53, 0.27, 0.13) |
| 5 | (0.59, 0.30, 0.11) | (0.58, 0.29, 0.12) |

**Step 1: Compute per-trial Euclidean error.**

$$e_1 = \sqrt{(0.52-0.50)^2 + (0.31-0.30)^2 + (0.10-0.12)^2} = \sqrt{0.0004 + 0.0001 + 0.0004} = \sqrt{0.0009} = 0.0300\,\text{m}$$

$$e_2 = \sqrt{(0.01)^2 + (0.02)^2 + (0.01)^2} = \sqrt{0.0006} = 0.0245\,\text{m}$$

$$e_3 = \sqrt{(0.01)^2 + (0.01)^2 + (0.01)^2} = \sqrt{0.0003} = 0.0173\,\text{m}$$

$$e_4 = \sqrt{(0.02)^2 + (0.01)^2 + (0.01)^2} = \sqrt{0.0006} = 0.0245\,\text{m}$$

$$e_5 = \sqrt{(0.01)^2 + (0.01)^2 + (0.01)^2} = \sqrt{0.0003} = 0.0173\,\text{m}$$

**Step 2: Compute mean error (Sim-to-Real Gap).**

$$G = \frac{0.0300 + 0.0245 + 0.0173 + 0.0245 + 0.0173}{5} = \frac{0.1136}{5} = \boxed{0.0227\,\text{m} \approx 22.7\,\text{mm}}$$

> **Interpretation:** The trained policy deviates from the real trajectory by ~22.7 mm on average. For pick-and-place tasks with 50 mm tolerance, this is acceptable; for precision insertion (<5 mm), **domain randomization** or **fine-tuning** is required.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any three "Other Simulators" used in the ROS ecosystem (other than Gazebo) and state one key feature of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Webots** — Open-source (Apache 2.0) 3D simulator with high-quality rendering and a large library of pre-built robot models (TurtleBot, NAO, PR2); supports both ROS 1 and ROS 2 natively.

2. **CoppeliaSim (formerly V-REP)** — 3D simulator with a **distributed control architecture** where each object can be controlled by embedded Lua/Python scripts, a ROS node, or a remote API. Excellent for manipulation research.

3. **CARLA** — Open-source autonomous driving simulator built on Unreal Engine 4, providing photorealistic urban environments, realistic vehicle dynamics, and rich sensor suites (RGB, Depth, Semantic Segmentation, LiDAR, Radar).

> [!TIP]
> **Alternative valid answers:** Stage (2D multi-robot), AirSim (UAV + photorealism), PyBullet (RL contact-rich), Isaac Sim (GPU + RL), Morse (academic multi-robot).

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between 2D and 3D simulators in ROS. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | 2D Simulators | 3D Simulators |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| **Representation** | Robots move on a 2D plane (x, y, θ) | Full 6-DoF motion in 3D space |
| **Physics** | Simple kinematic, no rigid-body dynamics | Full rigid-body dynamics with gravity, friction, contact |
| **Sensors** | 2D LiDAR, odometry | 3D LiDAR, RGB-D cameras, IMU, force/torque |
| **Computational Cost** | Very low (can simulate hundreds of robots) | High (limited to ~10–50 robots) |
| **Use Case** | SLAM, multi-agent coordination, teaching | Manipulation, autonomous driving, aerial robotics |
| **Example** | **Stage** | **Webots / CoppeliaSim / CARLA** |

**Conclusion:** 2D simulators like **Stage** prioritize speed and scalability for algorithms such as SLAM, while 3D simulators like **Webots** or **CoppeliaSim** provide realistic physics and sensor emulation for full robot validation.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has an **internal choice** — you may answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part is typically **7 marks**. Provide comprehensive model solutions below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture and features of **CoppeliaSim** (formerly V-REP). Discuss its ROS integration mechanism in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Stage**, **Webots**, and **CARLA** simulators with respect to: (i) physics engine, (ii) photorealism, (iii) multi-robot support, (iv) ROS 2 compatibility, and (v) typical application domain.

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**CoppeliaSim Architecture (V-REP's successor):**

CoppeliaSim employs a **distributed control architecture** — its defining feature. Unlike monolithic simulators, **each scene object** can be controlled independently through one of five mechanisms:

1. **Embedded scripts** — Lua or Python code running inside the simulator.
2. **Plugin** — Compiled C/C++ code linked into the simulator.
3. **ROS / ROS 2 node** — External ROS controller (e.g., `ros2_control`).
4. **Remote API** — TCP-based control from Python, C++, MATLAB, Java, Octave.
5. **Custom TCP/UDP socket** — User-defined protocol.

**Key Features:**

- **Multiple physics engines** (switchable per scene): ODE, Bullet, Vortex, Newton. This is rare among simulators and allows the user to choose the most stable solver for their application.
- **Mesh and CAD import** — STL, OBJ, COLLADA, glTF.
- **Vision sensors** — RGB, depth, optical flow, with realistic noise models (Gaussian, salt-and-pepper).
- **Force/torque sensors** — Useful for contact-rich manipulation.
- **Path/Motion planning module** — Built-in OMPL integration.
- **Cross-platform** — Windows, macOS, Linux.

**ROS Integration Mechanism:**

CoppeliaSim supports both ROS 1 and ROS 2 through a built-in interface plugin:

- **ROS 1:** Enable the `rosInterface` plugin in the simulator. ROS topics, services, and parameters are exposed via the `ros_talker` / `ros_subscriber` child scripts attached to scene objects.
- **ROS 2:** Use the `CoppeliaSimRosInterface` plugin (from CoppeliaSim 4.5+), which integrates natively with ROS 2 via a real-time extension.
- **Workflow:** A ROS 2 node (e.g., `moveit2` controller) publishes joint commands to a topic like `/joint_command`; CoppeliaSim's plugin subscribes, advances the simulation, and publishes sensor data back to topics like `/camera/rgb/image_raw`.

> **Conclusion:** CoppeliaSim's **multi-physics** and **multi-controller** architecture make it the most **flexible** among ROS-compatible simulators, particularly suited to **manipulation research** and **complex multi-robot** scenarios.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

| Aspect | **Stage** | **Webots** | **CARLA** |
|--------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| **(i) Physics Engine** | Custom 2D kinematic (no full rigid body) | ODE (default), Bullet (optional) | Unreal Engine 4 internal (PhysX-based) |
| **(ii) Photorealism** | None (2D grid + simple shapes) | Medium (OpenGL 4.x, PBR materials) | **High** (Unreal Engine 4 ray tracing, HDR lighting) |
| **(iii) Multi-Robot Support** | **Excellent** (hundreds of robots, low CPU) | Good (up to ~10–20 with reasonable RTF) | Moderate (~5–10 vehicles per server, multiple clients) |
| **(iv) ROS 2 Compatibility** | Partial (community packages only) | **Native** (built-in ROS 2 controller templates) | Bridge-based (`carla-ros-bridge` package) |
| **(v) Typical Application** | SLAM, multi-agent swarm research, teaching swarm algorithms | Education, manipulation, mobile robotics, industry pre-deployment | **Autonomous driving**, ADAS, vision-based ML training, sim-to-real for self-driving cars |

> **Inference:** For **algorithm-heavy swarm research**, Stage is optimal. For **balanced educational / research use**, Webots is the most pedagogically friendly. For **autonomous vehicle perception and policy learning**, CARLA's photorealism is unmatched.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of **CARLA** simulator. Describe its sensor stack and ROS 2 integration.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the sim-to-real transfer workflow using **AirSim** and **ROS 2**. Discuss the role of **domain randomization** in this process.

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**CARLA Architecture:**

CARLA (Car Learning to Act) follows a **client-server architecture**:

- **CARLA Server:** Runs the Unreal Engine 4 simulation. Manages world state, vehicles, sensors, traffic, weather, and physics. Exposes a **TCP/IP API** (port 2000 by default) using a custom protocol.
- **CARLA Client(s):** Multiple clients can connect simultaneously (e.g., one for the autonomous driving stack, one for scenario generation, one for data recording). Clients can be written in **Python** (the primary API), C++, or ROS 2.
- **Traffic Manager:** A built-in module that simulates background traffic using a finite-state machine with realistic lane-changing and overtaking behaviour.
- **Scenario Runner:** Python module to define OpenSCENARIO / OpenDRIVE-based driving scenarios for testing.

**Sensor Stack:**

CARLA provides a rich sensor suite via the `Sensor` actor class:

| Sensor | Output | Typical Rate | ROS 2 Message |
|--------|--------|--------------|---------------|
| **RGB Camera** | `Image` (BGR8) | 10–30 Hz | `sensor_msgs/Image` |
| **Depth Camera** | 32-bit float depth | 10–30 Hz | `sensor_msgs/Image` (encoding `32FC1`) |
| **Semantic Segmentation** | Per-pixel class ID | 10 Hz | `sensor_msgs/Image` (encoding `32SC1`) |
| **Instance Segmentation** | Per-instance mask | 10 Hz | `sensor_msgs/Image` |
| **3D LiDAR** | 64 or 32 vertical channels | 10–20 Hz | `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2` |
| **Radar** | 2D detections with velocity | 20 Hz | Custom `RadarDetection` |
| **GNSS** | Lat/Lon/Alt | 50 Hz | `sensor_msgs/NavSatFix` |
| **IMU** | Accel + Gyro | 100–200 Hz | `sensor_msgs/Imu` |
| **Collision Sensor** | Boolean on impact | Event-driven | `carla_msgs/CarlaCollisionEvent` |
| **Lane Invasion** | Boolean on crossing | Event-driven | `carla_msgs/CarlaLaneInvasionEvent` |

**ROS 2 Integration:**

The `carla-ros-bridge` package (Python-based) connects CARLA's TCP API to ROS 2 topics. Typical workflow:

1. Launch CARLA server (`CarlaUE4.sh`).
2. Launch `carla_ros_bridge` (specifying role, town, weather, sensors).
3. Launch `rviz2` to visualize sensor data.
4. Launch the autonomous driving stack (e.g., Autoware.auto, Autoware Universe, or custom `nav2` + `perception` nodes).
5. The driving stack publishes `autoware_auto_control_msgs/AckermannControlCommand` or `geometry_msgs/Twist` to control the vehicle.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Sim-to-Real Transfer Workflow (AirSim + ROS 2):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Define Mission Objective] --> B[Build AirSim Unreal Environment]
    B --> C[Configure Sensors and Weather]
    C --> D[Launch ROS 2 Bridge]
    D --> E[Train RL Policy in AirSim]
    E --> F[Apply Domain Randomization]
    F --> G[Validate in Simulation]
    G --> H{Sim to Real Gap Acceptable?}
    H -->|No| I[Increase Randomization or Fine Tune]
    I --> E
    H -->|Yes| J[Deploy Policy to Real Drone]
    J --> K[Real World Validation]
    K --> L[Iterate if Needed]

    classDef step fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef dec fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000

    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,J,K,L step
    class H,I dec
```

**Workflow Steps:**

1. **Mission definition** — e.g., autonomous drone landing on a moving platform.
2. **Environment setup** — AirSim Unreal project with the landing pad, obstacles, and lighting.
3. **Sensor configuration** — RGB camera, depth camera, IMU, GPS via AirSim's `settings.json`.
4. **ROS 2 bridge** — Use `airsim_ros_pkgs` to expose sensor data as ROS 2 topics.
5. **Policy training** — Reinforcement learning (PPO, SAC) using AirSim's Python API; rewards shaped for safety and accuracy.
6. **Domain randomization** (see below).
7. **Validation** — Run the trained policy in unseen AirSim environments.
8. **Deployment** — Transfer the policy (e.g., a TensorRT-optimized neural network) to the real drone's onboard computer (Jetson, Qualcomm Flight).
9. **Real-world test** — Validate on a real drone; iterate if gap is too large.

**Role of Domain Randomization:**

Domain randomization (DR) is a **sim-to-real technique** where training-time variability is deliberately introduced to make the policy robust to real-world unknowns. For AirSim drone training, DR includes:

- **Visual randomization:** Random textures, lighting intensity, fog density, shadows, camera exposure.
- **Dynamics randomization:** Random mass, motor constants, drag coefficients, wind disturbances.
- **Sensor noise:** Gaussian noise on IMU, random dropout in camera frames, varying LiDAR returns.
- **Environmental:** Moving obstacles, varied background scenery, time-of-day variation.

> **Why DR works:** By training on a **wide distribution** of simulated conditions, the policy **cannot overfit** to specific simulator artifacts. When deployed on a real drone, the real world appears as just another sample from this distribution, and the policy generalizes successfully.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse **domain randomization** with **domain adaptation**:
> - **Domain Randomization** = *No real data needed*; train on diverse sim data only.
> - **Domain Adaptation** = *Uses real data* (even unlabeled) to bridge the gap via fine-tuning or feature alignment.
>
> In the exam, explicitly state which one you are using and **why**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU**

### **Core Concepts**
- ☐ "Other Simulators" = all ROS-compatible simulators **except** Gazebo.
- ☐ **Stage** = 2D/2.5D, lightweight, ideal for **multi-robot SLAM**; uses Worldfile (`.world`).
- ☐ **Webots** = 3D, Apache 2.0, uses ODE/Bullet, excellent **educational** simulator with rich model library.
- ☐ **CoppeliaSim (V-REP)** = 3D, distributed control architecture, **switchable physics engines** (ODE/Bullet/Vortex/Newton), ideal for **manipulation**.
- ☐ **CARLA** = 3D, **Unreal Engine 4**, photorealistic, **autonomous driving** specialist.
- ☐ **AirSim** = 3D, Unreal Engine 4, **UAV + ML training**, Microsoft Research origin.
- ☐ **PyBullet** = Python module on **Bullet Physics**, ideal for **RL and contact-rich** tasks.
- ☐ **Isaac Sim** = **NVIDIA Omniverse**, GPU-accelerated, Isaac Gym for **parallel RL**, photorealistic.
- ☐ **Morse** = LAAS-CNRS academic simulator, Python-based, multi-robot.
- ☐ **ARGoS** = Swarm robotics specialist (not always ROS-native, but has plugins).

### **Numerical / Formula**
- ☐ $\text{RTF} = T_{\text{sim}}/T_{\text{wall}}$ — **RTF > 1** = faster than real-time.
- ☐ **Sensor throughput** = $f_s \times S$ per sensor; total = $N \times \sum (f_s \times S)$.
- ☐ **Sim-to-Real Gap** = mean absolute error between sim and real trajectories.

### **Comparison Traps**
- ☐ Gazebo uses **ODE / Bullet / DART**; Webots uses **ODE**; CoppeliaSim uses **ODE / Bullet / Vortex / Newton**; CARLA uses **Unreal's internal PhysX**; PyBullet uses **Bullet**; Isaac Sim uses **PhysX 5**.
- ☐ **Photorealism ranking (highest to lowest):** Isaac Sim > CARLA ≈ AirSim > Webots ≈ CoppeliaSim > Stage > PyBullet.
- ☐ **Multi-robot scalability ranking (most to least):** Stage > Isaac Gym (parallel) > ARGoS > CoppeliaSim > Webots > CARLA.
- ☐ **Open-source vs commercial:** Stage, Webots, CARLA, AirSim, PyBullet = open-source. Isaac Sim = commercial with free tier. CoppeliaSim = free for education, paid for industry.

### **ROS Integration Pattern**
- ☐ **Native ROS 2:** Webots, CoppeliaSim (4.5+), Isaac Sim, Stage (partial).
- ☐ **Bridge-based ROS 2:** CARLA (`carla-ros-bridge`), AirSim (`airsim_ros_pkgs`), PyBullet (`pybullet_ros`).

### **Exam Strategy Tips**
- ☐ Always mention the **physics engine** when comparing simulators.
- ☐ Always mention **licensing** for open-source vs. commercial questions.
- ☐ When asked "which simulator to use," justify with **at least 3 criteria** (fidelity, ROS compatibility, computational cost).
- ☐ Know the **sim-to-real gap** formula — it appears frequently as a 3-mark short answer.
- ☐ RTF and sensor throughput calculations are common in Part B 7-mark sub-questions.

> **Final Mantra:** *Gazebo is the default; "Other Simulators" are the specialists — choose by domain, fidelity, budget, and ROS version.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maps in ROS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maps in ROS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the **Robot Operating System (ROS)** architecture, a **Map** is a structured, spatially-indexed data representation of the robot's operating environment. It is published on a dedicated topic (typically `/map`) as a `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` message (for 2D) or `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2` / `octomap_msgs/Octomap` (for 3D). Maps are the foundational data structure that enables **localization, path planning, obstacle avoidance**, and **autonomous navigation** within the ROS Navigation Stack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A map in ROS is a probabilistic grid-based or feature-based geometric model of the environment, generated either a-priori (offline using `map_server`) or online (using SLAM algorithms like GMapping, Karto, or Cartographer), and consumed by the navigation stack for global and local planning.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are exploring a **new shopping mall** for the very first time. Before you can find the food court, you need one of two things:

1. **A pre-printed directory map** handed to you at the entrance (this is a **static map** loaded by `map_server`).
2. **You walking around and drawing your own sketch** on a piece of paper as you discover each store (this is a **dynamically built map** using **SLAM**).

In both cases, the map answers the question: *"Where am I, and what is around me?"* — the two fundamental questions of mobile robotics.

| ROS Component | Mall Analogy |
|---|---|
| `map_server` | The information desk that hands you a pre-made map |
| `OccupancyGrid` | The paper grid (each square = occupied/free/unknown) |
| SLAM Algorithm | You walking around and sketching the layout |
| `/map` topic | The announcement system broadcasting your current map |
| Costmap | A "danger heat-map" overlaying the floor plan |
| `amcl` node | You checking the directory every few seconds to know "where am I" |

## 1.3 Key Physical / System Constants & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard ROS Map Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Resolution:** Typically **0.05 m** (5 cm) per cell for indoor robots.
> - **Cell Values:** Three discrete states — `0` (Free), `100` (Occupied), `-1` (Unknown).
> - **TF Frame:** Maps are published in the `map` frame of the TF tree.
> - **Update Rate:** Static maps → published once; SLAM maps → typically **1–5 Hz**.
> - **Standard Map File Formats:** `.pgm` (image) + `.yaml` (metadata).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Core Map Types in ROS 2D Navigation:**
> 1. **Occupancy Grid Map** — 2D grid-based probabilistic map
> 2. **Costmap** — Layered map used by local/global planners (inflation layer included)
> 3. **Feature / Landmark Map** — Sparse map of distinct features (used by AMCL)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Maps Work in ROS

A ROS map is **not a single file or single node** — it is an *ecosystem* of topics, services, and TF frames. Let us break it down step-by-step.

### Step 1: Map Data Structure — `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`

The `OccupancyGrid` message is the standardized 2D map format. It contains three critical fields:

- **`info`** (of type `MapMetaData`): Holds the map's geometry.
  - `width`, `height`: Dimensions in cells.
  - `resolution`: Meters per cell (e.g., 0.05).
  - `origin`: The 2D pose $(x, y, \theta)$ of cell (0,0) in the `map` frame.
- **`data`**: A 1D `int8[]` array of length `width × height`, row-major ordered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Map Coordinate Transformation Formula:**
>
> Given cell coordinates $(i, j)$ in the grid and the map origin $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0)$:
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> x_{world} &= x_0 + (j + 0.5) \cdot \text{resolution} \cdot \cos(\theta_0) \\
> &- (i + 0.5) \cdot \text{resolution} \cdot \sin(\theta_0) \\
> y_{world} &= y_0 + (j + 0.5) \cdot \text{resolution} \cdot \sin(\theta_0) \\
> &+ (i + 0.5) \cdot \text{resolution} \cdot \cos(\theta_0)
> \end{aligned}
> $$

### Step 2: The `map_server` Node

The `map_server` node is responsible for serving a **pre-built static map** to the rest of the system. It does this by:

- **Reading** a `.pgm` image file and a `.yaml` metadata file from disk.
- **Publishing** the converted map on the `/map` topic (latched — published once per connection).
- **Offering** two services:
  - `static_map` (`nav_msgs/GetMap`): Returns the map on demand.
  - (No more `set_map` in ROS 2; dynamic maps use `map_saver` to write back.)

### Step 3: YAML Metadata Format

The `.yaml` file describes the map. Standard fields:

| Field | Meaning | Example Value |
|---|---|---|
| `image` | Path to the occupancy image | `my_map.pgm` |
| `resolution` | Meters per pixel | `0.050000` |
| `origin` | 2D pose of cell (0,0) as `[x, y, yaw]` | `[-10.0, -10.0, 0.0]` |
| `negate` | Invert white/black semantics | `0` |
| `occupied_thresh` | Pixels ≥ this are occupied | `0.65` |
| `free_thresh` | Pixels ≤ this are free | `0.196` |

### Step 4: SLAM — Building Maps Online

**Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** is the process of constructing a map while simultaneously tracking the robot's location within it. KTU syllabus covers three popular 2D SLAM packages:

1. **GMapping** (`gmapping` package) — Rao-Blackwellized particle filter, best for indoor with odometry + laser.
2. **Hector SLAM** (`hector_mapping`) — No odometry required, scan-matching only, suitable for aerial/UGV.
3. **Karto SLAM** (`slam_karto`) — Graph-based Sparse Pose Adjustment (SPA).
4. **Cartographer** (Google) — Loop closure capable, used by TurtleBot 4 in 2024.

### Step 5: Costmaps — The "Safety Overlay"

The **costmap** is a derived, layered map used by the **navigation stack** planners. It exists in two layers:

- **Global Costmap** — Used by `GlobalPlanner` / `NavFn` for long-range path planning.
- **Local Costmap** — Used by `LocalPlanner` (e.g., `DWAPlanner`, `TEB`) for reactive obstacle avoidance.

Each costmap has three core layers:
1. **Static Layer** — Loaded directly from the static map.
2. **Obstacle Layer** — Inflates real-time sensor readings (laser/pointcloud).
3. **Inflation Layer** — Adds a safety buffer around obstacles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Costmap Inflation Formula:**
>
> The cost of a cell at distance $d$ from the nearest obstacle is computed as:
>
> $$
> \text{cost}(d) = \begin{cases} \text{LETHAL\_OBSTACLE} & \text{if } d < \text{inscribed\_radius} \\ \text{inscribed\_cost} \cdot \frac{\text{inscribed\_radius} - 0.5 \cdot r}{d - 0.5 \cdot r} & \text{if } \text{inscribed\_radius} \le d < \text{inscribed\_radius} + \text{weight} \cdot r \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
> $$
>
> where $r$ is the robot's circumscribed radius.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Syntax | Definition / Value | KTU Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Occupied cell value | $p_{occ}$ | `100` | High — Direct exam question |
| Free cell value | $p_{free}$ | `0` | High |
| Unknown cell value | $p_{unk}$ | `-1` | High |
| Map resolution | $r$ | meters/cell (typ. 0.05) | High |
| World-to-grid transform | $T_{w \to g}$ | $(i,j) = f(x,y,r,\theta_0)$ | Medium |
| Costmap inscribed radius | $r_{ins}$ | Robot's inner circle radius | Medium |
| Costmap inflation radius | $r_{inf}$ | Safety buffer (e.g., 0.55 m) | Medium |
| Occupancy probability update | $p(o \mid z)$ | Bayes' filter — log-odds form | High |
| Log-odds update | $L_t$ | $L_t = L_{t-1} + \log\frac{p(z \mid o)}{1 - p(z \mid o)}$ | High |
| Map topic name | `/map` | `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` | High |
| Static map service | `/static_map` | `nav_msgs/GetMap` | High |
| Map frame | `map` → `odom` → `base_link` | TF tree of navigation | High |
| Map saving tool | `map_saver_cli` | Saves to `.pgm` + `.yaml` | Medium |
| Map loading tool | `map_server` | Loads and publishes map | High |
| 3D Map type | `octomap_msgs/Octomap` | Probabilistic 3D voxel grid | Medium |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Maps in ROS power a massive range of real-world applications:

- **Warehouse Automation** — Amazon Kiva robots use SLAM-built maps for navigation.
- **Autonomous Vehicles** — Multi-layered costmaps for real-time obstacle avoidance.
- **Disaster Response** — Hector SLAM on rescue drones mapping collapsed buildings.
- **Domestic Robotics** — Roomba/iRobot use occupancy grids for room segmentation.
- **Agricultural Robotics** — 3D OctoMaps for crop-row detection and weeding.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: World Coordinates to Grid Cell Coordinates

This is the **most critical derivation** for KTU — examiners love asking for the coordinate transformation logic.

**Problem Setup:** Given a world-frame point $P_{world} = (X, Y)$ in meters, derive the corresponding cell index $(i, j)$ on an occupancy grid of resolution $r$ and origin pose $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0)$.

**Step 1 — Translate to the map's local frame.**
Subtract the origin translation to convert the world point into the map's local coordinate frame.

$$
X' = X - x_0
$$

$$
Y' = Y - y_0
$$

**Step 2 — Inverse-rotate by the origin yaw.**
The map's origin may be rotated by $\theta_0$ relative to the world. We apply the inverse 2D rotation matrix $\begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta_0 & \sin\theta_0 \\ -\sin\theta_0 & \cos\theta_0 \end{pmatrix}$ to express the point in the grid's intrinsic axes.

$$
X_{local} = X' \cos\theta_0 + Y' \sin\theta_0
$$

$$
Y_{local} = -X' \sin\theta_0 + Y' \cos\theta_0
$$

**Step 3 — Convert meters to cells.**
Divide by the resolution $r$ to get fractional cell indices.

$$
j = \left\lfloor \frac{X_{local}}{r} \right\rfloor
$$

$$
i = \left\lfloor \frac{Y_{local}}{r} \right\rfloor
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why floor()?** Because cell indices must be integers, and the floor function rounds down to the nearest cell, ensuring points slightly outside a cell are still mapped consistently.

**Step 4 — Validate with bounds.**
A valid cell must satisfy:

$$
0 \le i < \text{height}, \quad 0 \le j < \text{width}
$$

If either condition fails, the point lies **outside the map**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Log-Odds Occupancy Update (Bayesian SLAM)

This derivation is the **probabilistic heart** of every occupancy grid SLAM algorithm and is a frequent KTU 14-mark question.

**Setup:** Each cell stores an estimate of the probability that it is occupied, $p(m_i)$. Measurements $z_{1:t}$ update this probability via Bayes' rule.

**Step 1 — Define the odds form.**

$$
\text{odds}(m_i) = \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}
$$

**Step 2 — Take the log to get the log-odds.**

$$
L(m_i) = \log \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}
$$

This is the **log-odds representation**, which is computationally efficient because multiplication becomes addition.

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' rule with the inverse sensor model $p(z_t \mid m_i)$.**

$$
p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid m_i) \cdot p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1})
$$

where $\eta$ is the normalizing constant.

**Step 4 — Take the log of both sides.**

$$
\log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t})} = \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1})} + \log \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i)}{1 - p(z_t \mid m_i)}
$$

**Step 5 — Express in compact form using the log-odds update rule.**

$$
L_t(m_i) = L_{t-1}(m_i) + \log \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i)}{1 - p(z_t \mid m_i)} - L_0
$$

where $L_0$ is the prior log-odds (typically $0$ for $p(m_i) = 0.5$, meaning "unknown").

**Step 6 — Convert back to probability for ROS display.**

ROS scales $[0, 1]$ to $[0, 100]$ for display, with $-1$ reserved for unknown:

$$
p_{occ} = \frac{\exp(L_t)}{1 + \exp(L_t)} \times 100
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Map Type vs. ROS Package

| Map Type | ROS Package | Topic | Message | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Static Occupancy Grid | `map_server` | `/map` | `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` | Pre-built maps |
| Live 2D SLAM | `gmapping` | `/map` | `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` | Indoor mapping |
| 2D SLAM (no odom) | `hector_mapping` | `/map` | `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` | Drones / unstable odom |
| 2D SLAM (graph) | `slam_karto` | `/map` | `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` | Mid-size environments |
| 3D SLAM | `cartographer_ros` | `/map` | `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` | Robust, loop closure |
| 3D Voxel Map | `octomap_server` | `/occupied_cells_vis` | `visualization_msgs/MarkerArray` | 3D navigation / drones |
| Costmap | `costmap_2d` | `/move_base/global_costmap` | `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` | Path planning |
| Localization Map | `amcl` | `/particlecloud` | `geometry_msgs/PoseArray` | Pose tracking |
| Point-Cloud Map | `pcl_ros` | `/cloud_map` | `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2` | Dense 3D reconstruction |
| Semantic Map | `semantic_mapping` | `/semantic_map` | Custom | Object-level understanding |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Hint:** When asked to "explain how a robot navigates using a map", the examiner expects you to mention: **(1) Map → (2) Localization (AMCL) → (3) Global Plan → (4) Costmap → (5) Local Plan → (6) Velocity commands**. Missing any link costs 2–3 marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Map Ecosystem in ROS

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Sensor Inputs] --> B1[LaserScan /scan]
    A --> B2[Odometry /odom]
    A --> B3[PointCloud2 /cloud]
    B1 --> C1{SLAM Node}
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[/map topic nav_msgs OccupancyGrid/]
    C1 --> D2[TF map to odom]
    E1[map_server] --> D1
    E1 --> E2[/static_map service/]
    D1 --> F1[Global Costmap]
    D1 --> F2[Local Costmap]
    B1 --> F2
    G1[AMCL Node] --> H1[Particle Cloud /particlecloud]
    G1 --> D2
    F1 --> I1[move_base Global Planner]
    F2 --> I2[move_base Local Planner]
    H1 --> I2
    I1 --> J1[/cmd_vel Velocity Output/]
    I2 --> J1
    D1 --> K1[rviz Visualization]
    F1 --> K1
    F2 --> K1
    H1 --> K1
    L1[map_saver_cli] --> M1[(my_map.pgm)]
    L1 --> M2[(my_map.yaml)]
    M1 -. Load .- E1
    M2 -. Load .- E1
```

## 4.2 Diagram Explanation — Data Flow Logic

The above architecture diagram represents the **complete map ecosystem** in a typical ROS-enabled mobile robot. The flow is logically divided into four phases:

| Phase | Components | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Sensing** | `A → B1, B2, B3` | Raw sensor data (LiDAR, wheel encoders, depth cameras) enters the system. |
| **2. Map Building** | `B1, B2, B3 → C1 → D1, D2` | SLAM node fuses sensors to publish the `/map` topic and broadcast the `map → odom` TF. |
| **3. Planning** | `D1 → F1, F2 → I1, I2` | The `move_base` server builds global and local costmaps and plans a path. |
| **4. Acting & Saving** | `I1, I2 → J1`, `L1 → M1, M2` | Velocity commands drive the robot; `map_saver_cli` writes the map to disk. |

## 4.3 State Diagram: Map Cell Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unknown
    Unknown --> Free : Sensor ray passes through cell, log_odds decreases
    Unknown --> Occupied : Sensor endpoint lands in cell, log_odds increases
    Free --> Occupied : Repeated hits raise log_odds above threshold
    Occupied --> Free : Repeated misses lower log_odds below threshold
    Free --> Unknown : Only if log_odds reset, rare
    Occupied --> Unknown : Only if log_odds reset, rare
```

## 4.4 Sequence Diagram: Loading a Pre-Built Map

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant Launch
    participant MapServer as map_server
    participant AMCL
    participant MoveBase as move_base
    participant RViz
    User->>Launch: ros2 launch my_robot navigation.launch.py
    Launch->>MapServer: Spawn with map.yaml path
    MapServer->>MapServer: Load .pgm, parse .yaml
    MapServer-->>RViz: Publish /map latched
    MapServer-->>MoveBase: Publish /map latched
    AMCL->>MapServer: Service call /static_map
    MapServer-->>AMCL: Returns nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid
    AMCL-->>MoveBase: Publishes /amcl_pose
    MoveBase-->>User: Robot navigates to goal
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw a block diagram of map-based navigation in ROS", reproduce the architecture from Section 4.1 and label each arrow with the topic name and message type. This alone is worth 5–7 marks in a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define an Occupancy Grid Map in ROS. What do the cell values 0, 100, and -1 represent?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4 | CO4 | Bloom: Remember]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Occupancy Grid Map** in ROS is a 2D probabilistic representation of the environment, published as a `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` message on the `/map` topic. The environment is discretized into a uniform grid of cells, each storing a probability of being occupied by an obstacle.

The cell values are standardized as:
- **`0`** → Cell is **Free** (no obstacle detected).
- **`100`** → Cell is **Occupied** (obstacle confirmed by sensor).
- **`-1`** → Cell is **Unknown** (no sensor data has reached it yet).

This standardization is critical because it allows different SLAM algorithms (GMapping, Hector, Karto) and the `map_server` to produce interoperable maps. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. List the standard fields of a ROS map YAML file with example values.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4 | CO4 | Bloom: Remember]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A ROS map YAML metadata file contains the following six standard fields:

| Field | Example | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `image` | `my_map.pgm` | Path to the grayscale image |
| `resolution` | `0.050000` | Meters per pixel |
| `origin` | `[-10.0, -10.0, 0.0]` | 2D pose of cell (0,0) |
| `negate` | `0` | Invert color semantics (0/1) |
| `occupied_thresh` | `0.65` | Occupied probability cutoff |
| `free_thresh` | `0.196` | Free probability cutoff |

These fields are mandatory for the `map_server` to correctly load and interpret the `.pgm` image. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q3 (a)** Explain the architecture of the ROS map_server node. How does it load and publish a static map? Include the role of the `.pgm` and `.yaml` files. **(7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4 | CO4 | Bloom: Understand]`

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The `map_server` is a ROS node from the `nav2_map_server` package (ROS 2) that provides the static map of the environment to the navigation stack. It reads the map from disk and publishes it as a `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` message on the `/map` topic.

**Loading Process (3 marks):**
1. On startup, `map_server` is given the path to a `.yaml` metadata file as a parameter (`yaml_filename`).
2. It reads the YAML, extracts the image path (`image` field), resolution, origin, and threshold values.
3. It opens the `.pgm` file (Portable Gray Map) — a binary grayscale image where each pixel is 0–255.
4. For each pixel, it applies the thresholds:
   - If pixel value $\le \text{free\_thresh} \cdot 255$ → cell = `0` (Free).
   - If pixel value $\ge \text{occupied\_thresh} \cdot 255$ → cell = `100` (Occupied).
   - Otherwise → cell = `-1` (Unknown).

**Publishing Process (2 marks):**
1. The map is packaged into a `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` message, with `info` holding the geometry and `data` holding the row-major cell array.
2. It is published on `/map` as a **latched topic** (or **transient local QoS** in ROS 2), meaning new subscribers immediately receive the last message.
3. It also provides the `/static_map` service, allowing on-demand retrieval of the map.

**Role of `.pgm` and `.yaml` (1 mark):**
- `.pgm` → Holds the actual visual data (pixels).
- `.yaml` → Holds the geometric metadata (resolution, origin, thresholds) needed to interpret the pixels.

**Conclusion (0 marks):** A clean separation of data and metadata, allowing the same `.pgm` to be re-used with different YAMLs for varied resolutions and origins.

---

#### **Q3 (b)** Compare the working principles of GMapping, Hector SLAM, and Cartographer. Which is most suitable for a TurtleBot 4 in an indoor environment and why? **(7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4 | CO4 | Bloom: Analyze]`

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | GMapping | Hector SLAM | Cartographer |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Algorithm** | Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter | Scan-Matching (Gauss-Newton) | Graph-based SLAM |
| **Odometry Required** | Yes | No | Optional |
| **Loop Closure** | No | No | Yes (SPA) |
| **Computational Cost** | Low–Medium | Low | High |
| **Map Output** | 2D OccupancyGrid | 2D OccupancyGrid | 2D + 3D |
| **Best For** | Small indoor | Drones, fast motion | Large environments |
| **ROS Package** | `gmapping` | `hector_mapping` | `cartographer_ros` |

**Working Principles (2 marks):**

- **GMapping** maintains a particle filter where each particle represents a possible robot trajectory and a corresponding map. It fuses laser scans with odometry to update the most likely trajectory. It does **not** perform loop closure, so drift accumulates in large environments.
- **Hector SLAM** uses **scan-to-map matching** with a multi-resolution grid. It can operate without odometry, making it robust to wheel slip, but it requires a high-frequency, accurate LiDAR (e.g., 40 Hz).
- **Cartographer** uses **Sparse Pose Adjustment (SPA)** for both local scan matching and global loop closure. It divides the map into submaps and performs periodic optimization, achieving very low drift over large areas.

**Suitability for TurtleBot 4 (1 mark):**

For a **TurtleBot 4 in an indoor environment**, **GMapping** is the most suitable choice. Reasons:
- TurtleBot 4 has reliable wheel odometry.
- Indoor environments are small-to-medium (single room or apartment).
- GMapping's computational cost is low, fitting TurtleBot 4's onboard Raspberry Pi / Jetson.
- Loop closure is unnecessary at this scale.

If the environment were a **large office building**, **Cartographer** would be preferred for its loop closure.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q4 (a)** Explain the role of costmaps in the ROS navigation stack. Differentiate between global and local costmaps with respect to their layers and update rates. **(7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4 | CO4 | Bloom: Understand]`

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Costmaps (2 marks):**
A **costmap** is a 2D grid used by the ROS navigation stack to represent not just the static map but also **real-time sensor data and safety inflation**. It transforms a binary free/occupied map into a multi-valued "cost" map where:
- `0` = Free
- `1–252` = Inflated cost (closer to obstacle = higher cost)
- `253` = Inscribed (robot center cannot enter)
- `254` = Possibly circumscribed
- `255` = Lethal (collision)

This cost representation allows planners to find **collision-free yet safe** paths.

**Global vs. Local Costmap (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Global Costmap | Local Costmap |
|---|---|---|
| **Used By** | `GlobalPlanner` (e.g., NavFn) | `LocalPlanner` (e.g., DWA, TEB) |
| **Coverage** | Entire map | Rolling window around robot |
| **Update Rate** | Slow (1–2 Hz) | Fast (10–20 Hz) |
| **Layers** | Static + Obstacle + Inflation | Obstacle + Inflation (static often omitted) |
| **Purpose** | Compute long-range optimal path | Reactive obstacle avoidance |
| **Size** | Full map (e.g., 2000×2000 cells) | Window (e.g., 100×100 cells) |

**Layers in Detail (1 mark):**
1. **Static Layer** — Loads the static `/map` (only in global costmap).
2. **Obstacle Layer** — Reads `/scan` and `/pointcloud` to mark observed obstacles.
3. **Inflation Layer** — Expands obstacles by `inflation_radius` to keep the robot at a safe distance.

---

#### **Q4 (b)** Derive the mathematical transformation from world coordinates $(X, Y)$ to occupancy grid cell indices $(i, j)$, given map resolution $r$ and origin pose $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0)$. **(7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4 | CO4 | Bloom: Apply]`

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- World point $P = (X, Y)$ in meters.
- Map resolution $r$ (meters/cell).
- Map origin pose $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0)$ in the `map` frame.

**To Find:** Cell index $(i, j)$.

**Step 1 — Translation (1 mark):**
Subtract the origin to convert into the map's local frame.

$$
X' = X - x_0, \quad Y' = Y - y_0
$$

**Step 2 — Inverse rotation (2 marks):**
Apply the inverse 2D rotation by $\theta_0$ to align with grid axes.

$$
X_{local} = X' \cos\theta_0 + Y' \sin\theta_0
$$

$$
Y_{local} = -X' \sin\theta_0 + Y' \cos\theta_0
$$

**Step 3 — Cell discretization (2 marks):**
Divide by resolution and floor to get integer indices.

$$
j = \left\lfloor \frac{X_{local}}{r} \right\rfloor
$$

$$
i = \left\lfloor \frac{Y_{local}}{r} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 4 — Validation (1 mark):**
The cell is valid only if $0 \le i < \text{height}$ and $0 \le j < \text{width}$; otherwise the point is **outside the map**.

**Step 5 — Final combined equation (1 mark):**

$$
i = \left\lfloor \frac{-(X - x_0)\sin\theta_0 + (Y - y_0)\cos\theta_0}{r} \right\rfloor
$$

$$
j = \left\lfloor \frac{(X - x_0)\cos\theta_0 + (Y - y_0)\sin\theta_0}{r} \right\rfloor
$$

This is the **canonical ROS `mapToWorld → worldToMap` transformation** used in the `nav2_costmap_2d` and `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` packages.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing cell value conventions:** Many students write "0 = occupied, 100 = free". This is **reversed** and costs 1 full mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the YAML's `negate` field:** Some maps use inverted conventions. Always mention this nuance in long answers.
> 3. **Confusing `map` and `odom` frames:** The map frame is the **global, world-fixed** frame; `odom` is **local and drifts**. Conflating them costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the rotation step in the transformation:** Many students do only translation + division, missing the $\theta_0$ term. Examiner deducts 2 marks.
> 5. **Omitting log-odds in SLAM answers:** If the question says "explain probabilistic mapping", you **must** include the log-odds update formula. A verbal answer alone gets ≤ 50% marks.
> 6. **Not mentioning the costmap inflation layer:** When asked about costmaps, examiners expect a mention of the inflation formula or at least the concept of safety buffer.
> 7. **Forgetting `map_saver_cli`:** When asked "how do you save a map in ROS 2", the answer is `ros2 run nav2_map_server map_saver_cli -f my_map` — not the obsolete ROS 1 `map_server` command.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Read Before Exam:**

- ✅ A ROS map is a **`nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`** on the **`/map`** topic, with cell values `0` (Free), `100` (Occupied), `-1` (Unknown).
- ✅ Maps are stored as a **`.pgm` image + `.yaml` metadata** pair on disk.
- ✅ The **`.yaml`** file has **6 fields**: `image`, `resolution`, `origin`, `negate`, `occupied_thresh`, `free_thresh`.
- ✅ The **`map_server`** node loads and publishes the static map; **`map_saver_cli`** saves it back to disk.
- ✅ **SLAM** = Simultaneous Localization & Mapping. Algorithms: **GMapping** (RBPF), **Hector** (scan-match), **Karto** (graph), **Cartographer** (loop closure).
- ✅ **Costmap** = layered safety overlay with **Static + Obstacle + Inflation** layers.
- ✅ **Global costmap** = long-range, slow update; **Local costmap** = rolling window, fast update.
- ✅ **Costmap inflation** uses the **inscribed radius** and **inflation radius** parameters.
- ✅ **Log-odds update** is the mathematical core of every SLAM algorithm: $L_t = L_{t-1} + \log \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i)}{1 - p(z_t \mid m_i)}$.
- ✅ The **TF tree** for navigation is: `map → odom → base_link → sensors`. The `map → odom` transform is published by the localization node (AMCL or EKF).
- ✅ **AMCL** (`amcl` package) is the standard 2D localization node, using a particle filter over the static map.
- ✅ **3D maps** use `octomap_msgs/Octomap` from the `octomap_server` package (probabilistic 3D voxels).
- ✅ **Standard map resolution** = **0.05 m** (5 cm) for indoor robots.
- ✅ For **TurtleBot 4 indoor navigation**, the recommended stack is: `map_server` + `amcl` + `nav2_bt_navigator` + `nav2_dwb_controller`.
- ✅ **Coordinate Transformation** = Translate → Inverse-Rotate by $\theta_0$ → Divide by $r$ → Floor.
- ✅ **`rviz`** is the primary visualization tool for displaying `/map`, `/costmap`, `/particlecloud`, and the TF tree.
- ✅ **Occupancy probability to ROS integer** scaling: $p_{occ} = \text{round}(p \times 100)$, with `p < 0` mapped to `-1`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recording Data with rosbag
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recording Data with rosbag

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — Recording Data with rosbag

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**rosbag** (in ROS 1) and **ros2 bag** (in ROS 2) is the official data recording and playback framework of the Robot Operating System. It is a command-line utility that subscribes to one or more ROS topics, captures the published **messages** along with their **timestamps** and **metadata**, and serializes them into a portable database file (a *bag file*). The same tool can later deserialize the file and re-publish the messages, enabling reproducible experimentation, offline analysis, debugging, and dataset creation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim-style):**  
> *rosbag is a tool for recording and playing back ROS topics by capturing message data into a file (.bag) and republishing it later, facilitating offline analysis, debugging, and testing without requiring the original sensor or hardware to be active.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Black Box" of a Robot

Imagine a fighter jet's **Flight Data Recorder (Black Box)**. Throughout the flight, the black box silently subscribes to dozens of data streams — altitude, speed, control surface positions, pilot commands — and stores them with precise timestamps. After the flight, investigators "play back" the data to reconstruct exactly what happened.

A robot is no different:

- The **robot's sensors and controllers** are constantly publishing data on **ROS topics**.
- **rosbag** "taps" these topics, like attaching a black box to a robot's nervous system.
- The recorded **.bag file** can be replayed later on the same — or even a completely different — machine, perfectly reproducing the original message stream.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary to Anchor in Memory:**
> - **Topic** → a named bus over which messages flow (e.g., `/cmd_vel`, `/camera/image_raw`).
> - **Message** → the typed data packet published on a topic (e.g., `sensor_msgs/msg/Image`).
> - **Bag file** → the serialized database on disk (extension `.db3` or `.mcap` in ROS 2; `.bag` in ROS 1).
> - **Serialization** → converting in-memory message objects into a byte stream for storage.
> - **Deserialization** → the reverse — converting stored bytes back into message objects on playback.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

The following parameters are central to rosbag operations and are frequently tested in KTU examinations:

| Metric | Typical Default | KTU-Relevant Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Default file format (ROS 2 Humble+) | **`.mcap`** or **`.db3`** (SQLite3) | MCAP is the modern default; `.db3` is the legacy SQLite3 format. |
| Storage plugin selectable via | `-s` / `--storage` flag | Plugins: `sqlite3`, `mcap`. |
| Compression plugin selectable via | `-z` / `--compression-mode` / `--compression-format` | Plugins: `zstd`, `bz2`, `lz4`. |
| Default recording mode | All advertised topics | Use `-a` for *all*, or list topics explicitly. |
| Message rate | Determined by publisher frequency | Uncompressed throughput may exceed **50 MB/s** on a multi-camera rig. |
| Loop playback | `-l` / `--loop` | Replays bag indefinitely — useful for live demos. |
| Rate scaling on playback | `-r <factor>` | E.g., `-r 0.5` = half speed; `-r 2.0` = double. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The rosbag Ecosystem — Three Sub-Commands

The rosbag tool is logically partitioned into **three orthogonal sub-commands**, each addressing a distinct phase of the data lifecycle. This separation is a recurring KTU question pattern (e.g., *"Explain the different sub-commands of ros2 bag"*).

1. **`ros2 bag record`** — Capture phase
   - Subscribes to specified topics.
   - Serializes incoming messages.
   - Writes them to disk at fixed intervals (default **5 seconds** per chunk in `.db3`).

2. **`ros2 bag play`** — Reproduction phase
   - Opens a previously recorded bag.
   - Publishes each message to its original topic at the original (or scaled) timestamp.

3. **`ros2 bag info`** — Inspection phase
   - Reads bag metadata without unpacking all messages.
   - Reports duration, message count, topic list, types, and storage format.

### 2.2 Operational Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

The recording pipeline follows a deterministic four-stage contract:

- **Stage 1 — Topic Discovery:** The recorder queries the ROS Discovery layer (DDS participant → `ros2 topic list`) to validate requested topic names. If `-a` is supplied, *every* currently advertised topic is captured.
- **Stage 2 — Subscription Establishment:** A DDS DataReader (subscriber) is created for each topic. A separate subscriber per topic enables parallel, non-blocking ingestion.
- **Stage 3 — Serialization & Buffering:** Each message is converted via the generated *CDR (Common Data Representation)* serializer into a binary record: `[timestamp | topic_name | serialization_length | payload_bytes]`.
- **Stage 4 — Chunked Write to Storage:** Records are accumulated in memory; on reaching a chunk boundary (or on `Ctrl-C`), the chunk is flushed to disk via the storage plugin (SQLite3 transaction or MCAP chunk).

### 2.3 Recording Modes and Options

ros2 bag provides a rich set of flags. The following are the **highest-yield** for KTU exams:

| Flag (long / short) | Purpose | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `-o <name>` / `--output` | Set output bag name/directory. | Naming convention: `ros2 bag record -o session1 /scan /imu` |
| `-a` | Record **all** advertised topics. | Quick-and-dirty full session capture. |
| `<topic1> <topic2> ...` | Record specific topics. | Sensor-specific recording. |
| `-s <plugin>` | Select storage plugin. | `-s mcap` for modern, queryable format. |
| `-z` / `--compression-mode file` | Enable compression. | Reduces disk usage by 60–80 %. |
| `--compression-format zstd` | Choose codec. | zstd offers best speed/ratio trade-off. |
| `-d <seconds>` | Set maximum recording duration. | Auto-stop after fixed time. |
| `--include-hidden-topics` | Include hidden (`_`-prefixed) topics. | Captures diagnostics and parameter events. |
| `-b <bytes>` | Set max bag size (auto-rotation). | Prevents disk-fill on long missions. |

### 2.4 Playback Engineering

Playback reverses the record process with additional controls:

- **Rate scaling:** `-r 0.5` slows playback; `-r 2.0` accelerates it.
- **Looping:** `-l` restarts the bag from the beginning on completion — used for **CI testing and regression demos**.
- **Topic remapping:** `--remap /old_topic:=/new_topic` redirects a topic on the fly — useful when testing algorithms on a different bus.
- **Start offset:** `--start-offset <sec>` begins playback at a timestamp *n* seconds in.

### 2.5 Why rosbag Matters — Engineering Utility

rosbag is the backbone of **reproducible robotics research** because it decouples the *data-generation environment* (often a real or expensive physical robot) from the *data-consumption environment* (a laptop, a cloud server, a CI runner). Concrete use cases:

- **Algorithm benchmarking** without re-running expensive SLAM or perception pipelines.
- **Dataset creation** for machine learning (e.g., ROSBag → ROS2 → image extraction → training).
- **Regression testing** in continuous integration: a fixed bag is replayed and outputs are compared.
- **Debugging distributed multi-node systems**: replay a frozen state to reproduce a rare race-condition bug.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** When asked *"Why is rosbag important?"*, always link the answer to **reproducibility** and **decoupling of hardware from software**. Marks are awarded for these two keywords.

### 2.6 KTU Formula / Parameter Cheat Sheet

Since rosbag is a tool rather than a physical law, the "formula sheet" below is a **parameter reference** of equivalents engineers must memorize:

$$
\text{Disk Usage} \;\approx\; \frac{(\text{message size in bytes}) \times (\text{message rate in Hz}) \times (\text{duration in s})}{\text{compression ratio}}
$$

$$
\text{Compression Ratio} \;=\; \frac{\text{uncompressed size}}{\text{compressed size}}
$$

$$
\text{Playback Time} \;=\; \frac{\text{Bag Duration}}{\text{Rate Factor } r}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> All three equations above are **order-of-magnitude estimators** for KTU problems — exact values depend on serializer overhead and codec efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs

Because rosbag is a **software-engineering topic**, the analytical content here takes the form of **command-syntax derivations, workflow walkthroughs, and worked numerical examples** rather than algebraic derivations. Every step is written out in full, with explicit logic rows.

### 3.1 Walkthrough — A Standard Recording Session

**Scenario:** A student wants to record the `/turtle1/cmd_vel` and `/turtle1/pose` topics from a turtlesim simulation into a bag named `turtle_session`, using MCAP storage and zstd compression, with a 30-second auto-stop.

**Step 1 — Identify running nodes and topics.**

$$
\texttt{\$ ros2 node list} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \texttt{/turtlesim},\;\texttt{/teleop\_twist\_keyboard}
$$

$$
\texttt{\$ ros2 topic list} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \texttt{/turtle1/cmd_vel},\;\texttt{/turtle1/pose},\;\texttt{/turtle1/color\_sensor},\;\ldots
$$

*Logic row:* The user inspects the live topic graph to know which names to record. Topics not listed cannot be subscribed to.

**Step 2 — Issue the record command.**

$$
\texttt{\$ ros2 bag record -o turtle\_session -s mcap -z --compression-format zstd -d 30 /turtle1/cmd\_vel /turtle1/pose}
$$

*Logic row:*
- `-o turtle_session` → output directory name.
- `-s mcap` → use the MCAP storage plugin (queryable, modern).
- `-z` + `--compression-format zstd` → enable file-level zstd compression.
- `-d 30` → stop after **30 seconds** automatically.
- The two trailing arguments are the topic names to record.

**Step 3 — Allow the recorder to run until auto-stop or `Ctrl-C`.**

*Logic row:* The recorder subscribes via DDS, serializes messages, and flushes chunks to disk. A new directory `turtle_session/` is created containing metadata plus chunk files.

**Step 4 — Inspect the produced bag.**

$$
\texttt{\$ ros2 bag info turtle\_session}
$$

*Logic row:* This sub-command reads only the metadata file, so it is **fast** and **non-destructive**. It prints: path, duration, start/end times, message count, storage identifier, compression identifier, and per-topic message counts.

**Step 5 — Play back the bag at half speed, looping.**

$$
\texttt{\$ ros2 bag play -r 0.5 -l turtle\_session}
$$

*Logic row:* `-r 0.5` halves the playback tempo (useful for slower visual inspection); `-l` ensures turtlesim continues to receive messages after the bag "ends."

### 3.2 Walkthrough — Numerical Disk-Usage Estimation

**Problem:** A depth camera publishes `sensor_msgs/msg/Image` at **30 Hz**. Each serialized message is approximately **614,400 bytes** (640 × 480 × 2 bytes/pixel + header). The robot runs for **15 minutes** with `zstd` compression achieving a ratio of **2.5×**. Estimate the disk footprint.

**Step 1 — Compute uncompressed raw data rate.**

$$
\text{rate}_{\text{raw}} \;=\; (\text{message size}) \times (\text{message rate})
$$

$$
\text{rate}_{\text{raw}} \;=\; 614{,}400 \;\text{B} \times 30 \;\text{Hz} \;=\; 18{,}432{,}000 \;\text{B/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to MB/s for intuition.**

$$
18{,}432{,}000 \;\text{B/s} \;\div\; 1{,}048{,}576 \;\approx\; 17.58 \;\text{MB/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total uncompressed size over 15 minutes.**

$$
\text{duration} \;=\; 15 \times 60 \;=\; 900 \;\text{s}
$$

$$
\text{size}_{\text{raw}} \;=\; 18{,}432{,}000 \times 900 \;=\; 16{,}588{,}800{,}000 \;\text{B}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply compression ratio.**

$$
\text{size}_{\text{compressed}} \;=\; \frac{16{,}588{,}800{,}000}{2.5} \;=\; 6{,}635{,}520{,}000 \;\text{B}
$$

**Step 5 — Express in GB.**

$$
6{,}635{,}520{,}000 \;\div\; 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \;\approx\; 6.18 \;\text{GB}
$$

*Logic row:* A single 15-minute depth-camera session consumes ~6 GB even with zstd. This justifies the `-b` (max-size) auto-rotation flag in long missions.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — ros2 bag vs rosbag (ROS 1)

| Feature | ROS 1 `rosbag` | ROS 2 `ros2 bag` |
|---|---|---|
| Default storage | `.bag` (custom binary) | `.mcap` (modern) or `.db3` (SQLite3) |
| Plugin architecture | Limited, monolithic | **Pluggable** (storage + compression plugins) |
| QoS awareness | N/A (TCP) | **Native** (records publisher's QoS profile) |
| Multi-machine recording | Manual coordination | Improved via DDS discovery |
| Metadata query | Slow on large bags | Fast (MCAP has indexes) |
| Loop playback | `-l` | `-l` |
| Topic remap on play | `--topic` or remap args | `--remap` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024, always use the **ROS 2 (`ros2 bag`)** form, since the syllabus specifies the modern architecture. Mentioning QoS awareness explicitly scores high marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Recording Workflow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1["Sensor Publisher Node"] -->|"sensor_msgs/msg/Image"| nodeT1["Topic /camera/image_raw"]
    nodeS2["IMU Publisher Node"] -->|"sensor_msgs/msg/Imu"| nodeT2["Topic /imu/data"]
    nodeS3["Cmd Vel Publisher Node"] -->|"geometry_msgs/msg/Twist"| nodeT3["Topic /cmd_vel"]
    nodeT1 --> nodeR["ros2 bag record node"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeR
    nodeT3 --> nodeR
    nodeR -->|"Stage 3 Serialize"| nodeBuf["In-Memory Message Buffer"]
    nodeBuf -->|"Stage 4 Chunk Flush"| nodeStore["Storage Plugin: sqlite3 or mcap"]
    nodeStore -->|"Compress zstd or bz2"| nodeDisk["Bag File on Disk .db3 or .mcap"]
    nodeInfo["ros2 bag info"] -->|"Read Metadata"| nodeDisk
    nodePlay["ros2 bag play"] -->|"Deserialized Replay"| nodeRePub["Republish on Original Topics"]
```

*Reading the diagram:* Three sensor/control nodes publish to their topics. The `ros2 bag record` process subscribes to all of them, serializes each message, buffers, chunks, and writes to disk. The `info` and `play` sub-commands are decoupled consumers of the same file.

### 4.2 The Playback & Loop Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[Playback Engine]
        nodeBag["Bag File .mcap or .db3"]
        nodeDecode["Deserializer CDR to Message Object"]
        nodeClock["Virtual Clock original or scaled r"]
        nodeScale["Rate Factor r = 0.5 or 2.0"]
    end
    nodeBag --> nodeDecode
    nodeScale --> nodeClock
    nodeDecode --> nodeClock
    nodeClock -->|"Re-publish"| nodeAlg["Downstream Algorithm Node SLAM or Perception"]
    nodeAlg -->|"Visualize"| nodeRVIZ["RViz"]
    nodeClock -->|"Loop or Stop"| nodeLoop["Loop Controller -l flag"]
    nodeLoop --> nodeBag
```

*Reading the diagram:* A virtual clock decouples the bag's wall-clock timestamps from real time, allowing rate scaling. The loop controller can restart the bag from the beginning when `-l` is set, ensuring the downstream algorithm node (e.g., a SLAM stack) receives a continuous stream.

### 4.3 Data Record Internals (Per-Message Structure)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Message Object e.g. sensor_msgs/msg/Image"] --> nodeB["CDR Serializer"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Header 16 bytes time_recv nanoseconds + topic_id"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Length Field 4 bytes uint32"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Serialized Payload N bytes"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Append to Active Chunk"]
    nodeF --> nodeG{"Chunk Full or Time Elapsed"}
    nodeG -->|"Yes"| nodeH["Flush to Disk with Compression"]
    nodeG -->|"No"| nodeA
```

*Reading the diagram:* Each message is wrapped in a tiny header (timestamp + topic) plus a length prefix, then appended to a chunk. When the chunk threshold is reached, the chunk is compressed (if enabled) and flushed atomically.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing `rosbag` (ROS 1) with `ros2 bag` (ROS 2).** ROS 2 syntax uses a space: `ros2 bag record`, not `rosbag2 record` (the latter is a different, older project). Lose **1 mark** for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify topics** after `record`. The command will hang waiting for input if launched in a non-interactive shell without topics and without `-a`. Lose **1 mark** if you do not mention this.
> 3. **Omitting QoS awareness** when comparing ROS 1 vs ROS 2 bag. This is the *single highest-value keyword* in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Storing on a full disk.** Always mention the `-b` (max bag size) flag when answering long-mission questions.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]`**  
*Define rosbag. List its three main sub-commands in ROS 2 and state the purpose of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*rosbag (or `ros2 bag` in ROS 2) is a command-line tool provided by the Robot Operating System to record messages published on ROS topics into a file on disk, and to later replay those messages for offline analysis, debugging, and testing.*

*Three main sub-commands:*

- **`ros2 bag record`** — subscribes to one or more topics and writes the received messages into a bag file (1 mark).
- **`ros2 bag play`** — reads a bag file and republishes its messages on their original topics, optionally with rate scaling and looping (1 mark).
- **`ros2 bag info`** — reads the metadata of a bag file and prints its duration, message counts, topic list, types, and storage format (1 mark).

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]`**  
*Mention any three command-line options used with `ros2 bag record` and explain their use.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **`-o <name>`** — specifies the output bag directory name. Without it, ros2 bag creates a timestamped default name (1 mark).
- **`-a`** — records *all* currently advertised topics on the ROS graph, useful for full-session capture (1 mark).
- **`-z` with `--compression-format zstd`** — enables compression of the bag file on disk, reducing storage footprint by typically 60–80 % (1 mark).

*(Alternative correct options: `-s mcap` for storage plugin, `-d <seconds>` for auto-stop duration, `-b <size>` for max-size rotation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module-Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** *Each Part-B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each. Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Sample]`**

**(a)** *Explain the working of `ros2 bag record` with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the storage plugin and the compression plugin. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A LiDAR node publishes `sensor_msgs/msg/LaserScan` messages at 20 Hz. Each serialized message is 30 KB. Estimate the disk usage for a 45-minute recording session, assuming zstd compression with a ratio of 3.2×. Express the answer in MB. (7 marks)*

##### **Model Solution**

**(a) Working of `ros2 bag record` (7 marks):**

1. **Topic Acquisition (1 mark):** The `record` sub-command parses its arguments. If `-a` is provided, it queries the DDS discovery layer to enumerate every advertised topic. If specific topic names are passed, it validates them against the current graph.
2. **Subscription Creation (1 mark):** A DDS DataReader (subscriber) is instantiated for each topic, using the publisher's Quality-of-Service (QoS) profile to ensure no message is silently dropped due to incompatible durability or reliability settings.
3. **Serialization (1 mark):** Each incoming message is converted from its in-memory ROS object representation into a binary byte stream using the **CDR (Common Data Representation)** serializer. The record is then wrapped in a small internal header: `[receive_time_ns | topic_id | payload_length | payload_bytes]`.
4. **Buffering & Chunking (1 mark):** Records are accumulated in an in-memory buffer. The default chunk size is **5 seconds** of recording, configurable via plugin parameters.
5. **Storage Plugin Write (1.5 marks):** The chunk is handed to the configured **storage plugin** — either `sqlite3` (writing a `.db3` file via transactions) or `mcap` (writing indexed chunks for fast random-access queries). The plugin determines the on-disk format, indexing strategy, and atomic-flush semantics.
6. **Compression Plugin (1.5 marks):** If `-z` is specified, the **compression plugin** (`zstd`, `bz2`, or `lz4`) compresses the chunk *before* the storage plugin writes it. This reduces I/O bandwidth and disk footprint.
7. **Termination:** The recorder flushes its final chunk and writes a metadata file (`.yaml` and `.db3`/`.mcap` summary) when the user issues `Ctrl-C` or when the `-d` duration expires.

*Block diagram (already shown in Section 4.1) — reference it explicitly for full marks.*

**(b) Numerical Estimation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Raw data rate.**

$$
\text{rate}_{\text{raw}} \;=\; 30 \;\text{KB} \times 20 \;\text{Hz} \;=\; 600 \;\text{KB/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Total duration in seconds.**

$$
t \;=\; 45 \times 60 \;=\; 2700 \;\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Uncompressed total size.**

$$
\text{size}_{\text{raw}} \;=\; 600 \;\text{KB/s} \times 2700 \;\text{s} \;=\; 1{,}620{,}000 \;\text{KB}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to MB (1 MB = 1024 KB).**

$$
\text{size}_{\text{raw, MB}} \;=\; \frac{1{,}620{,}000}{1024} \;\approx\; 1582.03 \;\text{MB}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply zstd compression with ratio 3.2×.**

$$
\text{size}_{\text{compressed}} \;=\; \frac{1582.03}{3.2} \;\approx\; 494.4 \;\text{MB}
$$

**Final Answer:** Approximately **494 MB** of disk space is required.

*(Accept ±2 % numerical tolerance due to header overhead.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Sample]`**

**(a)** *With the help of suitable examples, explain the `ros2 bag play` sub-command. Discuss the use of rate-scaling, looping, and topic remapping. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Compare and contrast the recording capabilities of ROS 1 `rosbag` and ROS 2 `ros2 bag`. Highlight at least four differences. (7 marks)*

##### **Model Solution**

**(a) `ros2 bag play` Explained (7 marks):**

The `play` sub-command is the **reproduction** stage of the rosbag lifecycle. Given a previously recorded bag directory, it deserializes each message in the order of its original receive-time and republishes it on the corresponding topic.

**Basic usage (1 mark):**

$$
\texttt{\$ ros2 bag play turtle\_session}
$$

*Example:* After recording turtlesim's `/turtle1/cmd_vel` and `/turtle1/pose`, the user can play back the bag in a fresh terminal (with turtlesim running) to *re-enact* the turtle's previous motion.

**Rate scaling (2 marks):**

The `-r <factor>` option scales the temporal pace of the replay.

$$
\texttt{\$ ros2 bag play -r 0.5 turtle\_session}
$$

Here, `r = 0.5` means messages are published at **half** their original rate — useful for slow-motion debugging of a fast-moving robot. Conversely, `-r 2.0` accelerates 2×.

**Looping (2 marks):**

The `-l` flag causes the bag to *restart from the beginning* once the end is reached.

$$
\texttt{\$ ros2 bag play -l turtle\_session}
$$

This is the standard idiom for **continuous-integration demos** and **live exhibition booths**, where a recorded session must replay indefinitely without manual restart.

**Topic remapping (2 marks):**

The `--remap` flag redirects a topic on the fly:

$$
\texttt{\$ ros2 bag play turtle\_session --remap /turtle1/cmd\_vel:=/new\_cmd\_vel}
$$

This is essential when the consuming algorithm expects a *different topic name* than the one originally recorded — a common scenario when migrating bags between robots.

**(b) ROS 1 `rosbag` vs ROS 2 `ros2 bag` (7 marks — at least 4 differences required):**

1. **Storage Architecture (2 marks):** ROS 1 uses a single custom binary `.bag` format. ROS 2 introduces a **pluggable storage architecture** with officially supported plugins `sqlite3` (`.db3`) and `mcap` (`.mcap`), enabling fast indexed queries in MCAP and transactional integrity in SQLite3.

2. **Quality-of-Service Awareness (2 marks):** ROS 1 runs over TCP-ros and has a single implicit QoS. ROS 2's `ros2 bag` records the **publisher's exact QoS profile** (reliability, durability, history, deadline) so that on playback, the algorithm subscribes with a *compatible* profile — preventing silent message loss.

3. **Multi-Machine & Discovery Coordination (1 mark):** ROS 1 required manual XMLRPC coordination for multi-machine recording. ROS 2 leverages **native DDS discovery** for simpler cross-host recording.

4. **Compression Model (1 mark):** Both support compression, but ROS 2's compression is **plugin-based and configurable per file** (`zstd`, `bz2`, `lz4`), whereas ROS 1 has a fixed `bz2` lumped into the writer.

5. **Metadata Inspection Speed (1 mark):** `ros2 bag info` on MCAP bags uses pre-built indexes and is **orders of magnitude faster** than ROS 1's `rosbag info` on equivalent large bags.

*(Any 4 of the above earn full 7 marks; 5+ earns distinction.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Definition** → rosbag = record / play / info framework; decouples data generation from consumption.
- **Three sub-commands** → `record`, `play`, `info`. Remember the lifecycle: capture → inspect → reproduce.
- **Default storage in modern ROS 2** → MCAP (`.mcap`); legacy fallback → SQLite3 (`.db3`).
- **Compression** → enable via `-z`, choose codec via `--compression-format` (zstd recommended).
- **Key record flags** → `-o` (output name), `-a` (all topics), `-s` (storage), `-z` (compression), `-d` (duration), `-b` (max size).
- **Key play flags** → `-r` (rate), `-l` (loop), `--remap` (topic redirect), `--start-offset` (begin later).
- **QoS awareness** → ROS 2 bag records the *publisher's QoS profile*; consuming nodes must subscribe with a compatible profile.
- **Disk-size formula** →
  $$
  \text{Disk} \;\approx\; \frac{\text{msg\_size} \times \text{rate} \times \text{duration}}{\text{compression\_ratio}}
  $$
- **Loop playback formula** →
  $$
  \text{Playback Time} \;=\; \frac{\text{Bag Duration}}{\text{Rate Factor } r}
  $$
- **Why it matters** → reproducibility, debugging, dataset creation, regression testing.
- **Common examiner traps** → ROS 1 vs ROS 2 syntax confusion; forgetting QoS; omitting `-b` for long missions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 30-Second Mnemonic — "R-P-I":**  
> **R**ecord topics → **P**lay them back → **I**nspect with `info`. Master these three verbs and you have mastered Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building Maps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Building Maps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Map Building** (also called **Mapping**) in the Robot Operating System (ROS) is the computational process by which a mobile robot constructs a spatial representation of an unknown environment by fusing sequential sensor measurements (LiDAR, depth cameras, odometry, IMU) with its estimated pose trajectory, producing a persistent map that can be reused for navigation, localization, and path planning.

In ROS, a **Map** is a 2D occupancy grid (nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid) where each cell stores a value in the range [-1, 100]:
- **-1** → Unknown / Unobserved
- **0** → Free space
- **100** → Occupied

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme explicitly focuses on building **2D occupancy grid maps** using the ROS Navigation Stack and the *map_server* / *gmapping* toolchains. Students must distinguish between **mapping** (creating the map) and **localization** (finding the robot within an already-built map).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blindfolded Cartographer"

Imagine you are blindfolded and placed in an unfamiliar room. You are given only a **flashlight** (a LiDAR/laser rangefinder) and a **step counter** (wheel odometry). Every time you take a step, you:

1. Sweep the flashlight and record where the beam stops (free or occupied).
2. Mark the path you walked as *free* on a mental notepad.
3. Update your notepad using a pencil that can be erased (probabilistic reasoning).

After thousands of such steps, your notepad becomes a complete floor plan — this is exactly what the ROS mapping node does: it converts streams of `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` + `nav_msgs/Odometry` into a stitched `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`.

### 1.3 Taxonomy of Maps in ROS

| Map Type | Data Structure | ROS Message | Typical Use |
|----------|----------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Metric Grid Map** | 2D array of cells | `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` | Indoor navigation (gmapping) |
| **3D Point Cloud Map** | Unordered 3D points | `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2` | RGB-D SLAM (RTAB-Map) |
| **Topological Map** | Graph of nodes/edges | Custom (move_base_flex) | Large-scale warehouses |
| **Feature Map** | List of landmarks | Custom | Visual SLAM (ORB-SLAM) |
| **Vector Map** | Polylines, polygons | `autoware_msgs/LaneletMap` | Outdoor autonomous driving |

### 1.4 Key ROS Nodes & Topics for Map Building

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Vocabulary for KTU Board Exams:** You are expected to know the following node names, topic names, and launch files. Marks are routinely lost when these are confused.

- **`slam_gmapping`** — Online 2D SLAM using Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filters.
- **`map_server`** — Loads a pre-built map from a `.yaml` + `.pgm` file pair and serves it on the `/map` topic.
- **`hector_slam`** — Scan-match based SLAM, **does not require odometry** (ideal for UAVs).
- **`cartographer_node`** — Google's graph-based SLAM, supports 2D and 3D.
- **`map_saver`** — Converts the current `/map` topic into `.pgm` + `.yaml` files.

Standard topics:
- `/map` (nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid)
