- `/scan` (sensor_msgs/LaserScan)
- `/tf`, `/tf_static` (tf2_msgs/TFMessage)
- `/initialpose` (geometry_msgs/PoseWithCovarianceStamped)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Occupancy Grid Mapping Problem

The classical formulation, due to **Moravec & Elfes (1985)**, decomposes the continuous environment into a discrete grid of $N = M \times M$ cells. Let $m_i$ denote the binary state of cell $i$:

$$m_i = 1 \iff \text{cell } i \text{ is occupied}$$
$$m_i = 0 \iff \text{cell } i \text{ is free}$$

The mapping problem seeks the posterior:

$$p(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$$

where:
- $z_{1:t} = \{z_1, z_2, \dots, z_t\}$ — history of sensor measurements.
- $x_{1:t} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_t\}$ — history of robot poses (estimated via odometry/SLAM).

A standard independence assumption reduces the joint to a product of marginals:

$$p(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$$

This assumption is computationally essential — without it, the joint distribution over $2^N$ binary variables is intractable.

### 2.2 Log-Odds Representation (The Engine of ROS Mapping)

Probabilities in $[0, 1]$ suffer from **numerical underflow** when multiplied thousands of times. The standard solution is the **log-odds** representation, which is used internally by every ROS mapping package:

$$\text{odds}(m_i) = \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}$$

$$l(m_i) = \log \left( \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)} \right)$$

The inverse transform is:

$$p(m_i) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{\,l(m_i)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why log-odds?** Multiplication of probabilities becomes **addition** of log-odds. This is exactly what the inverse sensor model update does — it *adds* a constant to the cell's log-odds. This is computationally O(1) per cell per scan and is the key reason ROS mapping runs in real time.

### 2.3 The Recursive Bayesian Update

Given the Markov assumption, the posterior updates as:

$$l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + \underbrace{\text{inverse\_sensor}(z_t, i)}_{\text{additive evidence}} - l_0$$

where $l_0 = 0$ is the prior log-odds (typically 0, meaning $p = 0.5$ — *unknown*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Favorite:** The inverse sensor model for a single laser ray hitting a cell $i$ at range $r_i$ from the sensor, while the measured range is $z_t$, is:
>
> - $p_{\text{occ}} = 0.9$ if $r_i \approx z_t$ and $i$ is the **endpoint** of the beam.
> - $p_{\text{free}} = 0.3$ if $0 < r_i < z_t$ (cells **along** the beam, free).
> - $p_{\text{prior}} = 0.5$ if $r_i > z_t$ (cells **beyond** the beam, unobserved).
>
> These probabilities correspond to log-odds:
> $l_{\text{occ}} = \log(9) \approx 2.197$, $l_{\text{free}} = \log(0.3/0.7) \approx -0.847$, $l_0 = 0$.

### 2.4 Resolution vs. Coverage Trade-off

A map of resolution $\rho$ (meters/cell) covering an area $A \times B$ square meters requires:

$$\text{Number of cells} = \frac{A \cdot B}{\rho^2}$$

A typical indoor map of $50\,\text{m} \times 30\,\text{m}$ at $5\,\text{cm}$ resolution has:

$$\frac{50 \times 30}{(0.05)^2} = 600{,}000 \text{ cells}$$

This is why 2D SLAM is feasible but dense 3D voxel maps quickly become memory-bound.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Forbidden character rule:** All vertical bars are rendered as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| Log-odds definition | $l(m_i) = \log\!\big(p(m_i) / (1 - p(m_i))\big)$ | dimensionless | $l \in (-\infty, +\infty)$ |
| Log-odds → probability | $p(m_i) = 1 - 1/(1 + e^{l(m_i)})$ | $[0, 1]$ | Sigmoid inverse |
| Recursive update | $l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + l_{\text{sens}} - l_0$ | dimensionless | $l_0 = 0$ |
| Occupied log-odds ($p=0.9$) | $l_{\text{occ}} = \log(9) \approx 2.197$ | dimensionless | Endpoint of beam |
| Free log-odds ($p=0.3$) | $l_{\text{free}} = \log(3/7) \approx -0.847$ | dimensionless | Along the beam |
| Map cell count | $N_{\text{cells}} = (L \cdot W) / \rho^{2}$ | cells | $\rho$ in m/cell |
| Pose uncertainty growth | $\sigma_{x}^{2}(t) = \sigma_{x,0}^{2} + D \cdot t$ | $\text{m}^{2}$ | $D$ = drift coefficient |
| Ray length budget | $L_{\text{ray}} = \lfloor z_{t} / \rho \rfloor$ | cells | Cells to traverse per beam |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse Robotics (Amazon Kiva):** Maps the entire warehouse, then localizes against it for sub-centimetre shelf retrieval.
- **Autonomous Vacuum Cleaners (Roomba):** Builds a partial grid to plan efficient coverage paths.
- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo):** Combines 2D grid maps for routing with 3D point-cloud maps for obstacle detection.
- **Disaster Response Robots:** Map collapsed buildings where no prior map exists — the *de novo* mapping case.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Recursive Log-Odds Update

**Goal:** Show that the Bayes update for a single cell can be written as an *additive* log-odds update.

**Step 1 — Bayes' Rule for a single cell:**

We want $p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$. Applying Bayes:

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

The denominator is a normalization constant independent of $m_i$, so for ratio computations it cancels.

**Step 2 — Form the odds ratio (free vs. occupied):**

$$\frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})} = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)}{p(z_t \mid \lnot m_i, x_t)} \cdot \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

This expresses the *posterior odds* as the product of *likelihood ratio* and *prior odds*.

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm (definition of log-odds):**

$$l_t(m_i) = l_{\text{sens}}(m_i, z_t, x_t) + l_{t-1}(m_i)$$

where

$$l_{\text{sens}}(m_i, z_t, x_t) = \log\!\left( \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)}{p(z_t \mid \lnot m_i, x_t)} \right)$$

**Step 4 — The standard ROS inverse sensor model:**

For a ray that travels from the sensor to range $z_t$:

$$l_{\text{sens}}(m_i) = \begin{cases} l_{\text{occ}} \approx 2.197 & \text{if } r_i \approx z_t \text{ (endpoint)} \\ l_{\text{free}} \approx -0.847 & \text{if } 0 < r_i < z_t \text{ (along beam)} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise (unobserved)} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The posterior log-odds is simply the **prior** plus a **fixed** value depending only on which geometric zone cell $i$ lies in. This is the closed-form rule that the `slam_gmapping` node uses to update every grid cell in O(1) time per ray.

**Step 5 — Clamping to avoid saturation:**

Without clamping, repeated measurements would drive $l_t$ to $\pm\infty$, freezing the cell. The practical fix used in ROS:

$$l_t(m_i) = \text{clip}\!\left( l_{t-1}(m_i) + l_{\text{sens}} - l_0,\; l_{\min},\; l_{\max} \right)$$

with $l_{\min} = \log(0.05/0.95) \approx -2.94$ and $l_{\max} = \log(0.95/0.05) \approx 2.94$.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Mapping Algorithms

| Algorithm | ROS Package | Odometry Required? | Loop Closure | Computational Cost | Use Case |
|-----------|-------------|--------------------|--------------|-------------------|----------|
| **RBPF SLAM (Grid-based)** | `gmapping` | Yes | Particle-filter based | Medium (1–30 particles) | Indoor floor plans |
| **Scan-Match SLAM** | `hector_slam` | **No** | No | Low | UAVs, light robots |
| **Graph SLAM** | `cartographer` | Yes (optional) | **Yes** (sparse pose graph) | High | Large indoor / multi-floor |
| **Visual SLAM** | `rtabmap`, `ORB-SLAM2` | Optional | Yes (bag-of-words) | High (GPU helpful) | RGB-D / stereo cameras |
| **RTK + LiDAR** | `lio_sam` | IMU required | Yes | High | Outdoor / autonomous driving |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A robot with a 2D LiDAR (angular resolution $1^{\circ}$, range $0.1$–$30\,\text{m}$) takes one scan in a $10 \times 10\,\text{m}$ map of resolution $\rho = 0.1\,\text{m}$ per cell. The robot is at pose $(0, 0, 0)$ and one beam measures range $z = 4.5\,\text{m}$ at angle $\theta = 90^{\circ}$.
>
> **Task:** Compute (a) the endpoint cell coordinates, (b) the number of cells traversed by the beam, (c) the log-odds update for the endpoint cell.

**Solution:**

(a) Endpoint in world frame:

$$x_{\text{end}} = 0 + 4.5 \cdot \cos(90^{\circ}) = 0\,\text{m}$$
$$y_{\text{end}} = 0 + 4.5 \cdot \sin(90^{\circ}) = 4.5\,\text{m}$$

Cell index (assuming origin at corner):

$$i = \lfloor 0 / 0.1 \rfloor = 0,\quad j = \lfloor 4.5 / 0.1 \rfloor = 45$$

(b) Number of cells along the beam (Bresenham traversal):

$$N_{\text{ray}} = \lfloor 4.5 / 0.1 \rfloor = 45 \text{ cells}$$

(c) Endpoint log-odds update (assuming prior $l_{t-1} = 0$):

$$l_t(m_{0,45}) = 0 + \log(0.9/0.1) - 0 = \log(9) \approx 2.197$$

Convert back to probability:

$$p_t(m_{0,45}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-2.197}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.111} \approx 0.900$$

Hence the endpoint cell is now labelled **occupied with 90% confidence** — exactly what we expect from a single LiDAR return.

### 3.4 Numerical Stability of Log-Odds

Compare raw probability update:

$$p_{\text{new}} = \frac{p(z\mid m)\cdot p_{\text{old}}}{p(z\mid m)\cdot p_{\text{old}} + p(z\mid \lnot m)\cdot (1 - p_{\text{old}})}$$

If $p_{\text{old}} = 10^{-6}$ and the likelihood ratio is $10^{6}$, naive arithmetic yields floating-point underflow. The log-odds update $l_{\text{new}} = l_{\text{old}} + 2.197$ **never** underflows because it is a simple addition. This is the central engineering reason every ROS mapping node uses log-odds internally.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — ROS Map Building Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensor Driver Node] -->|sensor_msgs/LaserScan| B[Scan Preprocessor]
    C[Wheel Encoder Node] -->|nav_msgs/Odometry| D[Pose Estimator]
    E[IMU Node] -->|sensor_msgs/Imu| D
    B -->|filtered scan| F[Scan Matcher]
    D -->|odometry pose| F
    F -->|corrected pose| G[Occupancy Grid Updater]
    B -->|raw scan rays| G
    G -->|log-odds fusion| H[Map Memory nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid]
    H -->|publish on /map| I[Navigation Stack]
    H -->|save request| J[map_saver Node]
    J -->|map.pgm| K[Disk Storage]
    J -->|map.yaml| K
    K -->|load on startup| L[map_server Node]
    L -->|publish /map| I
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture — slam_gmapping Internal Dataflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SLAM_gmapping_Node
        P1[Particle 1 - 30] --> P2[Scan Match per Particle]
        P3[Particle 3] --> P2
        Pn[Particle n] --> P2
        P2 --> P4[Weight Update]
        P4 --> P5[Resampling]
        P5 --> P6[Best Particle Map]
        P6 --> P7[Map Publisher /map]
        P6 --> P8[Pose Publisher /pose]
    end
    IN1[/scan topic/] --> P2
    IN2[/tf tree/] --> P2
    IN3[/initialpose/] --> P1
    P7 --> OUT1[/map - nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid/]
    P8 --> OUT2[/tf - odom to map/]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Occupancy Grid Update Step

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start] --> Q[Receive LaserScan z_t and pose x_t]
    Q --> R[For each beam in scan]
    R --> T[Bresenham ray tracing through grid]
    T --> U{Is cell along beam and within range?}
    U -->|Yes - before endpoint| V[Add l_free to cell log-odds]
    U -->|Yes - at endpoint| W[Add l_occ to cell log-odds]
    U -->|No - beyond range| X[Leave cell unchanged]
    V --> Y[Clamp log-odds to l_min l_max]
    W --> Y
    X --> Y
    Y --> Z[More beams?]
    Z -->|Yes| R
    Z -->|No| AA[Publish updated map on /map]
    AA --> AB[End]
```

### 4.4 Sequence Diagram — Saving and Loading a Map

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant SaveNode as map_saver
    participant MapTopic as /map topic
    participant Disk as File System
    participant LoadNode as map_server
    participant NavStack as move_base
    User->>SaveNode: rosrun map_server map_saver -f my_map
    SaveNode->>MapTopic: Subscribe (one snapshot)
    MapTopic-->>SaveNode: nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid
    SaveNode->>Disk: Write my_map.pgm (image)
    SaveNode->>Disk: Write my_map.yaml (metadata)
    Note over Disk: resolution, origin, negate, occupied_thresh, free_thresh
    User->>LoadNode: roslaunch map_server map_server.launch map_file:=my_map.yaml
    LoadNode->>Disk: Read .yaml and .pgm
    LoadNode->>MapTopic: Publish nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid on /map (latched)
    MapTopic->>NavStack: Continuous /map for AMCL localization and path planning
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*What is an Occupancy Grid Map in ROS? List the three possible values of an `OccupancyGrid` cell and explain what they mean.*

**Model Answer:**
An Occupancy Grid Map is a 2D probabilistic spatial representation of the robot's environment, stored as a discretized grid of cells. Each cell holds the probability that the area it represents is occupied by an obstacle. In ROS (`nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` message), the three values are:

- **-1 (Unknown):** The sensor has not yet observed this cell.
- **0 (Free):** The cell is confidently empty (probability ≈ 0).
- **100 (Occupied):** The cell is confidently blocked (probability ≈ 1).

The internal representation uses log-odds for numerical stability, with `0` mapped to a probability of `0.5` (truly unknown).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between `gmapping` and `hector_slam` ROS packages. When would you prefer one over the other?*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `slam_gmapping` | `hector_slam` |
|---------|-----------------|---------------|
| Algorithm | Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter | Scan-to-scan matching with Gauss-Newton optimization |
| Odometry required | **Yes** (wheel odom + TF) | **No** (uses LiDAR scan-match only) |
| Computational load | Medium (uses 30 particles by default) | Low |
| Best suited for | Wheeled ground robots, indoor | UAVs, handheld mapping, legged robots |

Prefer `hector_slam` for aerial robots where wheel odometry is unavailable. Prefer `gmapping` for indoor floor robots where robust pose tracking from wheel encoders is available.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 ESE):** Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the ROS map building pipeline. List the standard topics involved. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the recursive Bayesian update equation for an occupancy grid cell using the **log-odds** representation, starting from Bayes' theorem. Show why this leads to an *additive* update rule. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture (7 Marks):**

The ROS map building pipeline is a publisher–subscriber graph that fuses two asynchronous data streams (sensor scans and odometry) into a single 2D map.

**Required blocks (each worth ~1 mark):**
1. **Sensor Driver Node** (e.g., `rplidar_ros`, `realsense2_camera`) publishes `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` on `/scan`.
2. **Odometry Source** (e.g., `robot_pose_ekf`, `ros_arduino_bridge`) publishes `nav_msgs/Odometry` on `/odom`.
3. **TF Broadcaster** maintains the coordinate transform tree `map → odom → base_link → laser_frame`.
4. **SLAM Node** (`slam_gmapping` or `hector_slam`) consumes `/scan` + `/tf` and produces:
   - The corrected pose on `/tf` (as `map → odom`).
   - The live map on `/map` (`nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`).
5. **map_server** loads `.yaml` + `.pgm` files at startup and republishes them on `/map`.
6. **map_saver** dumps the current `/map` to disk on demand.
7. **Navigation Stack (`move_base`)** consumes the published `/map` for global planning.

**Standard topics (must be memorized, 1 mark):**
- `/scan`, `/map`, `/tf`, `/initialpose`, `/move_base/global_costmap`, `/map_metadata`.

**(b) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply Bayes' rule to a single cell $m_i$ given measurement $z_t$ and pose $x_t$:

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \, p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Write the equivalent expression for the *complement* (free cell) and form the ratio of the two. The denominator cancels:

$$\frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{p(\lnot m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})} = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)}{p(z_t \mid \lnot m_i, x_t)} \cdot \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(\lnot m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Take natural log of both sides and use the definition $l(m_i) = \log(p(m_i)/p(\lnot m_i))$:

$$l_t(m_i) = \log\!\left(\frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)}{p(z_t \mid \lnot m_i, x_t)}\right) + l_{t-1}(m_i)$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Identify the additive constant as the **inverse sensor model** $l_{\text{sens}}$:

$$l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + l_{\text{sens}}(m_i, z_t, x_t)$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** The *additivity* is the central insight: posterior log-odds = prior log-odds + a **fixed** evidence term. This avoids:
- Numerical underflow from $p^N$ products.
- O(N) operations per update (each cell is updated in O(1)).
- The need to store the full joint distribution $p(m, x_{1:t})$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently forget that the update is **clamped** to $[l_{\min}, l_{\max}]$ to prevent saturation. Failure to mention this loses 1 mark.
> - Do **not** confuse $l_0 = 0$ (prior log-odds) with the *occupied* log-odds $\log 9 \approx 2.197$. These are different quantities.
> - The derivation must start from **Bayes' rule**, not just state the final formula — examiners will not award full credit for an ungrounded formula.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the data format of the ROS message `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`. How is the `.pgm` file generated by `map_saver` related to this message? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A robot is at pose $(0, 0, 0)$ in a map of resolution $0.05\,\text{m}$. A LiDAR beam at angle $60^{\circ}$ returns range $5.0\,\text{m}$. Compute (i) the world-frame endpoint coordinates, (ii) the endpoint cell indices, (iii) the new occupancy probability at the endpoint cell after this single measurement, assuming uniform prior and $p_{\text{occ}} = 0.9$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Message Format (7 Marks):**

The `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` message consists of two fields:

1. **`MapMetaData info`** (3 marks):
   - `time_map` (Header stamp)
   - `resolution` (meters/cell, e.g., `0.05`)
   - `width`, `height` (in cells)
   - `origin` (`geometry_msgs/Pose` — pose of cell `(0,0)` in world frame)

2. **`int8[] data`** (3 marks):
   - Row-major flattening of the grid, length = `width × height`.
   - Each value ∈ `{-1, 0, 1, …, 100}`.
   - Index conversion: `idx = i + j * width`.

**.pgm ↔ OccupancyGrid mapping (1 mark):**
`map_saver` converts the `data[]` array to a greyscale **P5 binary PGM** image where pixel brightness maps inversely to occupancy (white = free, black = occupied, grey = unknown). The accompanying `.yaml` file stores `resolution`, `origin`, and the `negate` / `occupied_thresh` / `free_thresh` parameters required to reconstruct the original `OccupancyGrid` message.

**(b) Computation (7 Marks):**

(i) Endpoint in world frame (2 marks):

$$x_{\text{end}} = 0 + 5.0 \cdot \cos(60^{\circ}) = 5.0 \cdot 0.5 = 2.5\,\text{m}$$

$$y_{\text{end}} = 0 + 5.0 \cdot \sin(60^{\circ}) = 5.0 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 4.330\,\text{m}$$

(ii) Cell indices with origin $(0, 0)$ and $\rho = 0.05\,\text{m}$ (2 marks):

$$i = \lfloor 2.5 / 0.05 \rfloor = 50$$

$$j = \lfloor 4.330 / 0.05 \rfloor = \lfloor 86.6 \rfloor = 86$$

(iii) New occupancy probability (3 marks):

Prior is uniform: $l_{t-1} = 0$, $l_0 = 0$.
Inverse sensor model for the endpoint: $l_{\text{occ}} = \log(0.9 / 0.1) = \log 9 \approx 2.197$.

Apply the update:

$$l_t = 0 + 2.197 - 0 = 2.197$$

Convert back to probability:

$$p_t = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-2.197}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.1111} \approx 0.9001 \approx 90\%$$

Hence the endpoint cell $(50, 86)$ is marked **occupied with 90% confidence** after this single LiDAR return.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Sign convention in the log-odds formula** is frequently inverted. Re-derive $l_{\text{occ}}$ from $p_{\text{occ}} = 0.9$: it is $\log(0.9/0.1)$, **not** $\log(0.1/0.9)$.
> - Many students forget the **negative** sign in the sigmoid exponent. Memorize: $p = \sigma(l) = 1 / (1 + e^{-l})$.
> - The `.pgm` file is **not** a ROS message — it is a portable image format. The `.yaml` is what `map_server` reads first to interpret the image.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A ROS 2D **occupancy grid** uses three cell states: **-1 (unknown)**, **0 (free)**, **100 (occupied)**.
- ✅ The internal data structure uses **log-odds**, $l(m_i) = \log(p/(1-p))$, to avoid floating-point underflow.
- ✅ The **recursive update** is **additive**: $l_t = l_{t-1} + l_{\text{sens}} - l_0$, with clamping at $\pm 2.94$.
- ✅ Standard log-odds values: **$l_{\text{occ}} = \log 9 \approx 2.197$**, **$l_{\text{free}} \approx -0.847$**, **$l_0 = 0$**.
- ✅ `slam_gmapping` needs **odometry**; `hector_slam` does **not** (scan-match only).
- ✅ `cartographer` supports **loop closure** via sparse pose graphs (best for large maps).
- ✅ The two output files of `map_saver` are **`map.pgm`** (image) and **`map.yaml`** (metadata).
- ✅ `map_server` republishes a saved map on **`/map`** (latched topic) at startup.
- ✅ TF tree for SLAM: **`map → odom → base_link → laser_frame`**.
- ✅ Typical resolution: **5 cm/cell** for indoor (yields 600k cells for a 50×30 m area).
- ✅ The `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` data is stored in **row-major order** with index `i + j·width`.
- ✅ ROS 2 successor: `nav2` (Navigation2) uses the same occupancy grid concept but in `nav_msgs/msg/OccupancyGrid` (suffixed `.msg`).
- ✅ For 3D mapping, use **`pointcloud_to_laserscan`** (projection) or **RTAB-Map** / **Octomap** for full 3D voxels.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Starting a Map Server and Looking at a Map
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Starting a Map Server and Looking at a Map

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **map_server** is a ROS (Robot Operating System) node within the `map_server` package that provides map data to other nodes in the ROS computational graph. It serves two primary functions: **(i)** reading a map from a structured map file (a pair consisting of a **PGM image** and a **YAML metadata descriptor**) and publishing it as a **nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid** message on the `/map` topic, and **(ii)** offering two ROS services — `/static_map` (`nav_msgs/GetMap`) and `/map_metadata` (`nav_msgs/GetMapMetadata`) — for on-demand retrieval of map information by path planners and localization stacks (e.g., `amcl`, `move_base`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The *map_server* is the canonical data-source node for the **Navigation Stack**. The map it publishes is the *global costmap's* ground truth. Without a running `map_server`, the robot has no prior knowledge of its environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Box**
> | Term | Expansion | Meaning |
> |------|-----------|---------|
> | **PGM** | Portable Grey Map | A grayscale image format where pixel intensity encodes occupancy |
> | **YAML** | YAML Ain't Markup Language | A human-readable data serialization format used for map metadata |
> | **OccupancyGrid** | `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` | A 2-D grid ROS message where each cell holds a probability of occupancy |
> | **Resolution** | meters/pixel | The real-world distance represented by a single pixel in the PGM image |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **tourist guide** holding a large printed city map. You stand at the entrance of a museum and hand copies of this map to every visitor (other ROS nodes) who asks for one. You do not draw the map yourself; you simply *serve* a pre-existing map. You also keep a small **index card** with key information about the map (its scale, origin, legend rules) that anyone may request.

In this analogy:
- The **printed city map** = the PGM grayscale image of the environment.
- The **index card with the map's scale, origin, and legend** = the YAML metadata file.
- **You, the guide** = the `map_server` node.
- **Tourists requesting maps** = other ROS nodes subscribing to `/map` or calling `/static_map`.
- **The museum lobby** = the ROS Master + computational graph.

> [!TIP]
> Think of `map_server` as a **read-only librarian** of environmental geometry: it does *not* create maps, it only *distributes* them. Map *creation* is done separately (e.g., via `gmapping`, `cartographer`, or `hector_slam`), and the produced map is then handed to `map_server` for storage and distribution.

## 1.3 Physical / Engineering Metrics

- **Map Resolution** is typically in the range **0.01 m to 0.1 m** per pixel (i.e., 1 cm to 10 cm).
- **Occupancy Probability** values in the OccupancyGrid range from **-1** (unknown) to **0** (free) to **100** (occupied), mapped from the 0–255 grayscale range of the PGM using the YAML threshold parameters.
- The **default occupancy threshold values** are: `occupied_thresh = 0.65`, `free_thresh = 0.196`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of the `map_server` Package

The `map_server` package contains **two executable nodes**:

1. **`map_server`** — a service-and-topic server that loads a map from disk and serves it.
2. **`map_saver`** — a client utility that subscribes to a `/map` topic and writes the received OccupancyGrid back to disk as a `.pgm` + `.yaml` pair.

### 2.1.1 Internal Workflow of `map_server`

When the `map_server` node is launched with the argument `map_server <map.yaml>`, it executes the following operational logic:

1. **Parse YAML file** → extract the image filename, resolution, origin, and thresholds.
2. **Load PGM image** into memory as a 2-D byte array.
3. **Convert PGM bytes to OccupancyGrid values**:
   - Byte value **b** = 255 → occupancy **0** (definitely free).
   - Byte value **b** = 0   → occupancy **100** (definitely occupied).
   - Byte value **b** = **205 (occupancy threshold cross-over)** → ambiguous; resolved using `occupied_thresh` and `free_thresh`.
4. **Publish** the resulting `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` on `/map` (as a `latched` topic — the last message is held for late subscribers).
5. **Advertise services** `/static_map` and `/map_metadata`.

## 2.2 The OccupancyGrid Message Anatomy

The `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` message consists of three logical components:

| Field | Type | Purpose |
|-------|------|---------|
| `header` | `std_msgs/Header` | Frame ID (`map`) + timestamp |
| `info` | `nav_msgs/MapMetaData` | Width, height, resolution, origin pose |
| `data` | `int8[]` | Row-major grid of cell occupancies |

`MapMetaData` sub-fields:
- `map_load_time` — `time`
- `resolution` — `float32` (m/cell)
- `width`, `height` — `uint32` (cells)
- `origin` — `geometry_msgs/Pose` (real-world pose of cell [0,0])

## 2.3 YAML Metadata File Structure

A standard `map.yaml` file for `map_server` follows this exact schema:

```yaml
image: my_map.pgm
resolution: 0.050000
origin: [-10.0, -10.0, 0.0]
negate: 0
occupied_thresh: 0.65
free_thresh: 0.196
```

| YAML Key | Type | Meaning | KTU-Favored Range |
|----------|------|---------|-------------------|
| `image` | string | Path to the PGM file (relative or absolute) | — |
| `resolution` | float | Meters per pixel | **0.01 – 0.10 m** |
| `origin` | list[3] or list[7] | [x, y, yaw] or [x, y, z, qx, qy, qz, qw] of cell (0,0) | — |
| `negate` | int (0/1) | If 1, inverts white/black semantics | 0 or 1 |
| `occupied_thresh` | float | Pixels with occupancy $\geq$ this are considered occupied | **0.65 (default)** |
| `free_thresh` | float | Pixels with occupancy $\leq$ this are considered free | **0.196 (default)** |

> [!NOTE]
> The two thresholds exist because the raw PGM grayscale is mapped to a *continuous* probability (0–100). The `map_server` then *binarises* this into the discrete OccupancyGrid values using the threshold pair.

## 2.4 Conversion Logic: PGM Byte → Occupancy Probability

The standard formula used inside `map_server` to convert a PGM byte to an occupancy value is:

$$
\text{occupancy} \;=\; \text{occ}_{\text{prob}}(b) \;=\; \begin{cases} \vert 1 \;-\; \tfrac{b}{255.0} \vert \;\times\; 100 & \text{if } \text{negate} = 0 \\[8pt] \tfrac{b}{255.0} \;\times\; 100 & \text{if } \text{negate} = 1 \end{cases}
$$

Then, thresholding:

$$
\text{cell} \;=\; \begin{cases} 0   & \text{if } \text{occ}_{\text{prob}} \leq \text{free\_thresh} \\ 100 & \text{if } \text{occ}_{\text{prob}} \geq \text{occupied\_thresh} \\ -1  & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Cell value **-1 = Unknown** is a *first-class* state in ROS navigation. It is **not** a placeholder; it tells the planner "no information exists here, treat as untraversable by default."

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / Formula | Symbolic Form | Units / Values |
|---|-------------------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | Real-world distance per cell | $d = \text{resolution}$ | meters/pixel |
| 2 | Map width in meters | $W_m = W_c \times \text{resolution}$ | meters |
| 3 | Map height in meters | $H_m = H_c \times \text{resolution}$ | meters |
| 4 | Cell to world coordinates | $x_w = x_c \cdot r + x_o$ ; $y_w = y_c \cdot r + y_o$ | meters |
| 5 | PGM to occupancy (no negate) | $\text{occ} = \vert 1 - b/255 \vert \cdot 100$ | 0–100 |
| 6 | PGM to occupancy (negate=1) | $\text{occ} = (b/255) \cdot 100$ | 0–100 |
| 7 | Default occupied threshold | $\text{occ}_{th} = 0.65$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Default free threshold | $\text{fr}_{th} = 0.196$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Latched topic | `/map` | bool |
| 10 | Service call latency | $\sim 10$–$50$ ms | ms (typical) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The `map_server` is the **centralized ground-truth distribution hub** for:

- **Warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)** — the pre-built warehouse floor plan is served via `map_server` and consumed by `amcl` for localization.
- **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs)** in hospitals, airports, and factories.
- **Service robots** like vacuum cleaners and delivery bots that need a stable prior map.
- **Research drones** performing indoor SLAM — the resulting map is saved by `map_saver` and re-loaded by `map_server` on subsequent runs.

Without `map_server`, every localization and planning node would need to independently parse PGM files — a violation of the **single source of truth** engineering principle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Commands & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Procedure: Starting the Map Server

### Step 1 — Verify the map files exist
Before starting `map_server`, the user must have the `.pgm` + `.yaml` pair in a known directory (commonly `~/maps/`). A typical directory listing looks like:

```text
/home/ktu_student/maps/
    ├── my_office_map.pgm
    └── my_office_map.yaml
```

### Step 2 — Launch the `map_server` node

```bash
rosrun map_server map_server my_office_map.yaml
```

**Explanation of each token:**
- `rosrun` — the ROS package-runner command.
- `map_server` — the *package* name.
- `map_server` — the *node/executable* name (identical to the package by convention).
- `my_office_map.yaml` — the *map file* argument passed to the node.

### Step 3 — Verify the node is publishing
In a **second terminal**, run:

```bash
rostopic list
```

Expected output must include:

```text
/map
/map_metadata
/rosout
/rosout_agg
```

### Step 4 — Inspect the published map
Use the standard ROS introspection command:

```bash
rostopic echo /map -n1
```

This prints *one* message of type `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`. The first few lines should resemble:

```text
header:
  seq: 0
  stamp: ...
  frame_id: "map"
info:
  map_load_time: ...
  resolution: 0.050000
  width: 384
  height: 384
  origin:
    position: {x: -10.0, y: -10.0, z: 0.0}
    orientation: {x: 0.0, y: 0.0, z: 0.0, w: 1.0}
data: [0, 0, 0, 0, 100, 100, ...]
```

### Step 5 — Look at the map in RViz
Open RViz with the default configuration:

```bash
rosrun rviz rviz
```

Then in the RViz GUI:
1. Set the **Fixed Frame** to `map`.
2. Click **Add → By Topic** → select `Map` (under `/map`).
3. The OccupancyGrid renders automatically as a colored grid:
   - **Dark cells** → Occupied (value 100)
   - **Light cells** → Free (value 0)
   - **Grey cells** → Unknown (value -1)

### Step 6 — Call the `/static_map` service explicitly

```bash
rosservice call /static_map
```

This returns the full map data in the service response. The same can be done programmatically from a Python or C++ client.

### Step 7 — Save a map using `map_saver`
After running SLAM and producing a live `/map` topic, the user freezes the map and saves it:

```bash
rosrun map_server map_saver -f ~/maps/my_new_map
```

This produces `my_new_map.pgm` and `my_new_map.yaml` in `~/maps/`.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Cell-to-World Coordinate Transform

The `map_server` does not itself transform coordinates, but every node that consumes its map must. The KTU-board-expected derivation is:

**Given**:
- A cell index $(i, j)$ where $i$ is the *column* (x-direction) and $j$ is the *row* (y-direction).
- Resolution $r$ in meters/cell.
- Origin $(x_o, y_o)$ in meters (the world position of cell $(0, 0)$).

**Step 1** — Express the cell offset in meters:

$$
\Delta x = i \cdot r, \quad \Delta y = j \cdot r
$$

**Step 2** — Add the origin offset to get world coordinates:

$$
x_w = x_o + \Delta x
$$

$$
y_w = y_o + \Delta y
$$

**Step 3** — In matrix form (commonly used in KTU board answers):

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x_w \\ y_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} r & 0 & x_o \\ 0 & r & y_o \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i \\ j \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note the absence of rotation: the grid axes are *assumed* to be aligned with the world frame. If a non-zero `yaw` is present in the `origin` quaternion, an additional rotation matrix $R_z(\theta)$ must be pre-multiplied. The full transform is then:
> $$
> \begin{bmatrix} x_w \\ y_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} r\cos\theta & -r\sin\theta & x_o \\ r\sin\theta & r\cos\theta & y_o \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i \\ j \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
> $$

## 3.3 Numerical Example Worked Out (KTU Board Pattern)

> **Q:** A map has `resolution = 0.05 m`, `origin = [-2.0, -3.0, 0.0]`, and `width = 200`, `height = 150`. Find the world coordinates of cell `(100, 75)`.

**Solution**:

**Step 1** — Identify the given values:

$$
r = 0.05 \text{ m}, \quad x_o = -2.0 \text{ m}, \quad y_o = -3.0 \text{ m}, \quad i = 100, \quad j = 75
$$

**Step 2** — Compute the meter offset:

$$
\Delta x = 100 \times 0.05 = 5.0 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Delta y = 75 \times 0.05 = 3.75 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3** — Add the origin:

$$
x_w = -2.0 + 5.0 = 3.0 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y_w = -3.0 + 3.75 = 0.75 \text{ m}
$$

**Final Answer**: Cell `(100, 75)` corresponds to world coordinates **(3.0 m, 0.75 m)**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Map Server Behaviour Under Varying Configurations

| Scenario | `negate` | `occupied_thresh` | `free_thresh` | PGM Byte 100 | Resulting Cell Value |
|----------|:--------:|:-----------------:|:-------------:|:------------:|:-------------------:|
| Default (white = free) | 0 | 0.65 | 0.196 | 100 | 100 (occupied) |
| Inverted (black = free) | 1 | 0.65 | 0.196 | 100 | 39 (free) |
| Strict occupancy | 0 | 0.30 | 0.10 | 100 | 100 (occupied) |
| Lenient free | 0 | 0.90 | 0.50 | 100 | -1 (unknown) |
| Standard ROS bag replay | 0 | 0.65 | 0.196 | 205 | 19 (free) |

> [!TIP]
> When students inspect a map and see large **grey regions**, it usually means the PGM byte fell in the *ambiguous zone* (between the two thresholds), so the `map_server` marked the cells as **-1 (unknown)**. Lowering `free_thresh` or raising `occupied_thresh` will resolve this.

## 3.5 Service Call Latency Analysis

When a node calls `/static_map`, the round-trip time can be approximated by:

$$
T_{rt} = T_{req} + T_{proc} + T_{resp}
$$

For a typical 384×384 map (~147 KB of `int8` data), $T_{rt}$ is dominated by $T_{resp}$, the time to serialize and transmit the data over TCP/IP. For a 100 Mbps link, the theoretical lower bound is:

$$
T_{resp,\min} \;=\; \frac{147 \times 1024 \times 8}{100 \times 10^6} \;\approx\; 12 \text{ ms}
$$

In practice, observed values are **20–50 ms** due to overhead. The KTU board may ask students to estimate this minimum.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The `map_server` Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User / Terminal] -->|rosrun map_server map_server map.yaml| B[ROS Master]
    B --> C[map_server Node]
    
    D[map.yaml] -->|parses| C
    E[map.pgm] -->|loads and converts| C
    
    C -->|latched publish| F[/map topic nav_msgs OccupancyGrid/]
    C -->|advertises| G[/static_map service nav_msgs GetMap/]
    C -->|advertises| H[/map_metadata service nav_msgs GetMapMetadata/]
    
    F --> I[RViz Visualization]
    F --> J[amcl Localizer]
    F --> K[move_base Planner]
    
    G --> L[Service Clients]
    H --> M[Costmap 2D]
    
    N[map_saver Node] -->|subscribes /map| F
    N -->|writes| O[map_saved.pgm + map_saved.yaml]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Cell-to-World Coordinate Transform

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Pixel_Space [Pixel Space - OccupancyGrid]
        P1[Cell at i=100, j=75]
    end
    
    subgraph Metric_Offset [Metric Offset Calculation]
        M1[dx = i x resolution]
        M2[dy = j x resolution]
    end
    
    subgraph World_Space [World Space - map frame]
        W1[x_world = x_origin + dx]
        W2[y_world = y_origin + dy]
        W3[Final World Pose]
    end
    
    P1 --> M1
    P1 --> M2
    M1 --> W1
    M2 --> W2
    W1 --> W3
    W2 --> W3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: PGM-to-Occupancy Conversion Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PGM Image byte b 0 to 255] --> B{negate flag}
    B -->|negate=0| C[occ = abs 1 - b/255 x 100]
    B -->|negate=1| D[occ = b/255 x 100]
    C --> E{occ compared to thresholds}
    D --> E
    E -->|occ less than equal free_thresh| F[Cell = 0 FREE]
    E -->|occ greater than equal occupied_thresh| G[Cell = 100 OCCUPIED]
    E -->|in between| H[Cell = -1 UNKNOWN]
    F --> I[Final OccupancyGrid]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Navigation Stack Integration with `map_server`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[map_server] -->|publishes /map| B[Global Costmap]
    A -->|static_map service| C[amcl Localization]
    B --> D[move_base]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Local Planner]
    E --> F[Velocity Commands to Robot]
    G[Sensor Data /scan /odom] --> C
    G --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the Mermaid diagrams above, all node IDs are pure alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`-style) and labels contain only uppercase/plain alphanumeric text to comply with rendering safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: What is the role of the `map_server` node in the ROS Navigation Stack? List the two services it advertises.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
- The `map_server` node **serves pre-built map data** (a static map) to the rest of the navigation stack by publishing it as a `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` message on the `/map` topic. *(2 marks)*
- The two services advertised are:
  1. `/static_map` (of type `nav_msgs/GetMap`) — for full map retrieval.
  2. `/map_metadata` (of type `nav_msgs/GetMapMetadata`) — for retrieving only the metadata. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Explain the significance of cell value `-1` in a `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` map.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
- The value `-1` represents an **unknown** cell. *(1 mark)*
- It indicates that the SLAM algorithm or the map source had **no information** about that cell — neither free nor occupied. *(1 mark)*
- The navigation planner treats unknown cells as **untraversable by default** to ensure safe path planning, although `unknown` can be configured to be treated as free in `costmap_2d` parameters. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (Choice A) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the two executables provided by the `map_server` ROS package. With neat illustrations, explain the role of the YAML file and the PGM file in describing a saved map.

**(b) [7 marks]** A ROS map file has `resolution = 0.02 m`, `origin = [0.0, 0.0, 0.0]`, `width = 500`, `height = 500`, and the byte value at a particular cell in the PGM is 51. The `negate` flag is 0, and default thresholds apply. Determine the cell's occupancy value in the resulting OccupancyGrid.

---

#### Model Solution for B1(a) — 7 marks

**The `map_server` package contains two executables:**

1. **`map_server` node** *(2 marks)*:
   - Loads a map from a YAML file + PGM image pair provided on the command line.
   - Publishes the loaded map as a `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` on the latched topic `/map`.
   - Advertises the `/static_map` and `/map_metadata` services.

2. **`map_saver` node** *(2 marks)*:
   - Subscribes to a live `/map` topic (e.g., from `gmapping` or `amcl`).
   - On invocation (e.g., Ctrl-C or `rosrun map_server map_saver -f <name>`), it dumps the current grid to a `.pgm` image and a companion `.yaml` file.

**Role of YAML and PGM files** *(3 marks)*:

- **YAML file**: A small, human-readable metadata file containing `image`, `resolution`, `origin`, `negate`, `occupied_thresh`, and `free_thresh`. It tells `map_server` how to interpret the PGM file (its scale, where the origin is, and how to threshold the greyscale values into occupancy probabilities).
- **PGM file**: A grayscale image where each pixel represents a cell in the grid. A **white pixel (255)** typically represents free space, a **black pixel (0)** represents an obstacle, and **intermediate greys** represent partial occupancy. The `map_server` converts these bytes into the 0–100 occupancy range (and -1 for unknown) using the thresholds defined in the YAML.

---

#### Model Solution for B1(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Identify the given values** *(1 mark)*:
$$
r = 0.02 \text{ m}, \quad x_o = 0, \quad y_o = 0, \quad b = 51, \quad \text{negate} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the conversion formula with `negate=0`** *(2 marks)*:

$$
\text{occ}_{\text{prob}} \;=\; \left| 1 - \frac{b}{255} \right| \times 100
$$

$$
\text{occ}_{\text{prob}} \;=\; \left| 1 - \frac{51}{255} \right| \times 100
$$

$$
\text{occ}_{\text{prob}} \;=\; \vert 1 - 0.2 \vert \times 100 \;=\; 0.8 \times 100 \;=\; 80
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the default thresholds** *(2 marks)*:
- `occupied_thresh = 0.65` and `free_thresh = 0.196` (the values are in *probability*, not percentage).
- Converted to percentage: `occupied_thresh = 65`, `free_thresh = 19.6`.
- We have `occ_prob = 80`, which is **greater than 65** ⇒ cell is **occupied**.

**Step 4 — Final cell value** *(1 mark)*:
$$
\text{Cell value} \;=\; 100 \quad (\text{OCCUPIED})
$$

**Step 5 — Verification** *(1 mark)*: Since the cell value `80` is above `65`, thresholding logic correctly identifies it as occupied, yielding the discrete OccupancyGrid value of 100.

---

### Question B2 (Choice B) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a block diagram, explain the architecture of the ROS `map_server` and describe how it interacts with the navigation stack (specifically `amcl` and `move_base`).

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the steps to start a `map_server` and visualize the map using RViz. Also describe the role of `map_saver` and provide the command to save a map.

---

#### Model Solution for B2(a) — 7 marks

**Block Diagram** *(3 marks)*:

```
[map.yaml] + [map.pgm] → [map_server Node]
                                |
                +---------------+---------------+
                |               |               |
            [/map topic]   [/static_map]   [/map_metadata]
                |               |               |
                v               v               v
       [Global Costmap]   [amcl Loc.]     [Costmap 2D]
                                |               |
                                +-------+-------+
                                        |
                                  [move_base]
                                        |
                                  [Local Planner]
                                        |
                                  [cmd_vel → Robot]
```

**Explanation** *(4 marks)*:
- The `map_server` reads the YAML + PGM pair and **publishes the map on `/map`** as a latched `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`.
- `amcl` (Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization) subscribes to `/map` to localize the robot in the pre-built map using laser scans and odometry.
- `move_base` uses the `/map` data to construct the **global costmap** for path planning.
- The services `/static_map` and `/map_metadata` allow on-demand retrieval, e.g., when `costmap_2d` initializes, it may call `/static_map` once to obtain the full map and seed the global costmap.

---

#### Model Solution for B2(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Ensure pre-built map files exist** *(1 mark)*:
The `my_map.yaml` and `my_map.pgm` files must be present in a known directory.

**Step 2 — Launch the `map_server`** *(2 marks)*:
```bash
rosrun map_server map_server my_map.yaml
```
Verify with `rosnode list` and `rostopic list` that the `/map` and `/map_metadata` topics and `/static_map` service are present.

**Step 3 — Launch RViz** *(1 mark)*:
```bash
rosrun rviz rviz
```
In RViz, set the **Fixed Frame** to `map`, click **Add → By Topic → Map**, and the visualization appears.

**Step 4 — Role of `map_saver`** *(2 marks)*:
`map_saver` is a utility that subscribes to a live `/map` topic (e.g., during SLAM) and serializes the current OccupancyGrid into a `.pgm` + `.yaml` pair for later reuse. It is the inverse operation of `map_server`.

**Step 5 — Command to save a map** *(1 mark)*:
```bash
rosrun map_server map_saver -f ~/maps/my_saved_map
```

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the `latched` nature of `/map`**: Students often claim `map_server` *continuously* republishes the map. In reality, `/map` is a *latched* topic — the last message is held for any future subscribers. *(−1 mark)*
> 2. **Confusing the role of `map_server` vs `gmapping`**: `map_server` does *not* create maps; it only serves pre-existing ones. Mixing these up costs full marks on Part B (a) questions. *(−2 marks)*
> 3. **Threshold calculation error**: Conflating the percentage form (0–100) with the YAML form (0.0–1.0) of the thresholds. The YAML stores 0.65, but the comparison is done after multiplying by 100. *(−1 mark)*
> 4. **Missing the `frame_id` in the OccupancyGrid header**: The `header.frame_id` must be `"map"`. Forgetting this makes the map un-displayable in RViz. *(−1 mark)*
> 5. **Saving a map while `/map` is not latched in `map_saver`**: Students often press Ctrl-C too early, getting an empty PGM. Always let the SLAM node publish a complete map first. *(−2 marks)*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist**
> - `map_server` is a **read-only** node; it does *not* create maps. It serves them.
> - Two executables: **`map_server`** (loader + publisher + services) and **`map_saver`** (subscriber + writer).
> - Map file = **PGM image** + **YAML metadata**.
> - The `/map` topic uses message type **`nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`** and is **latched**.
> - Two services: `/static_map` (`nav_msgs/GetMap`) and `/map_metadata` (`nav_msgs/GetMapMetadata`).
> - OccupancyGrid cell values: **0** (free), **100** (occupied), **-1** (unknown).
> - Default thresholds: `occupied_thresh = 0.65`, `free_thresh = 0.196`.
> - PGM byte → occupancy formula (no negate): $\text{occ} = \vert 1 - b/255 \vert \times 100$.
> - PGM byte → occupancy formula (negate=1): $\text{occ} = (b/255) \times 100$.
> - Cell-to-world transform: $x_w = x_o + i \cdot r$ ; $y_w = y_o + j \cdot r$ (no rotation).
> - The `header.frame_id` of `/map` must be **`"map"`** for TF compatibility.
> - To visualize: launch `map_server` first, then open RViz, set Fixed Frame to `map`, and add the Map display.
> - To save: use `rosrun map_server map_saver -f <name>` after SLAM produces a stable map.
> - `map_server` is a **prerequisite** for `amcl` and `move_base` localization + planning.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Localizing the Robot in a Map
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Localizing the Robot in a Map # Module 4: Localizing the Robot in a Map ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Localization** is the process of determining the pose (position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$) of a mobile robot within a known reference frame, given a map of the environment and a stream of sensor observations. When the map is known a priori, the problem is termed **Map-Based Localization** (also called *position tracking* or *global localization* depending on initial uncertainty). ### Formal Academic Definition In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, localization is mathematically formalized as a **recursive Bayesian estimation problem**. Given: - A known map $m$ of the environment, - A sequence of control actions $u_{1:t} = \{u_1, u_2, \dots, u_t\}$ (odometry/velocities), - A sequence of sensor measurements $z_{1:t} = \{z_1, z_2, \dots, z_t\}$ (laser, camera, sonar), The robot must estimate its belief over all possible poses $x_t$: $$ bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m) $$ This posterior density completely captures the robot's *knowledge* about where it is. Localization algorithms differ primarily in **how they represent this belief** (Gaussian unimodal, multi-modal Gaussian mixture, or sample-based). ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Blindfolded Student in a Known House" Analogy** > > Imagine a blindfolded student is placed *somewhere inside a house whose blueprint they have memorized*. They carry a **cane (sensor)** that beeps when it hits a wall, and a **pedometer (odometry)** that counts their steps. Their goal is to figure out: *"Which room am I in, and which direction am I facing?"* > > - The **blueprint** = the *map* $m$. > - The **pedometer** = *control input* $u_t$ (noisy estimate of motion). > - The **canebeeps** = *measurement* $z_t$ (noisy estimate of distance to walls). > - The student's **belief about their location** = $bel(x_t)$. > > The student repeatedly does two things: **(1) predicts** where they should be after walking, and **(2) updates** that prediction using wall-beep measurements. This is exactly the **prediction–correction loop** at the heart of all Bayesian localization algorithms. ### Key Distinctions (KTU High-Yield) | Localization Type | Initial Belief | Difficulty | |---|---|---| | **Position Tracking** | Narrow, near true pose | Easy | | **Global Localization** | Uniform over the map | Harder (kidnapped robot problem is extreme case) | | **Monte Carlo / Particle Filter** | Sample-based, flexible | Standard ROS solution | Standard metric used in ROS: **Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization (AMCL)** with default parameters tuned for a **$5\text{ Hz}$** update rate. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Bayes Filter for Localization The mathematical backbone of *all* localization algorithms in ROS is the **Recursive Bayes Filter**, executed in two steps at every time step $t$: #### Step 1: Prediction (Motion Update) Using the **Markov assumption** (the next state depends only on the current state and the control input), the prior belief is propagated through the motion model $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$: $$ \overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1} $$ This integral says: *"To find where I might be now, sum/integrate my previous belief weighted by the probability that motion $u_t$ would have moved me from $x_{t-1}$ to $x_t$."* #### Step 2: Measurement Update (Correction) The sensor measurement $z_t$ is incorporated via Bayes' theorem using the observation model $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$: $$ bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t) $$ where $\eta$ is the normalizer: $$ \eta = \frac{1}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)} = \frac{1}{\int p(z_t \mid x_t, m)\, \overline{bel}(x_t)\, dx_t} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the Two-Step Structure?** > - The **prediction step** adds *uncertainty* (odometry is noisy → belief spreads out). > - The **measurement step** *reduces* uncertainty (sensor data sharpens the belief). > - The robot therefore alternates between *growing* and *shrinking* its confidence — a beautiful balancing act. ### 2.2 Map Representations in ROS | Map Type | ROS Message | Best For | Key Trade-off | |---|---|---|---| | **Occupancy Grid (2D)** | `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` | Flat indoor, laser-based | High memory: typically **$20\text{ MB}$** for a $200\text{ m} \times 200\text{ m}$ map at $5\text{ cm}$ resolution | | **3D Point Cloud** | `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2` | RGB-D, stereo | Needs OctoMap for memory efficiency | | **Feature Map** | Custom (landmarks) | Outdoor, vision-based | Requires robust feature extraction | | **Topological Map** | Custom (graph nodes/edges) | Large-scale navigation | Loses fine geometric precision | In `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`, each cell stores a value in $\{-1, 0, 100\}$: - $-1$ = **Unknown** - $0$ = **Free** - $100$ = **Occupied** ### 2.3 Three Implementations of the Bayes Filter #### A. Kalman Filter (KF) Localization - Represents $bel(x_t)$ as a **single multivariate Gaussian**: $\mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$. - $\mu_t$ is a 3-vector $(x, y, \theta)^T$; $\Sigma_t$ is the $3 \times 3$ covariance. - **Best for:** unimodal, smooth, position-tracking problems. - **Cannot handle** global localization (Gaussian cannot represent multiple hypotheses). **Prediction Equations:** $$ \mu_t = \mu_{t-1} + \Delta_t $$ $$ \Sigma_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t $$ where $\Delta_t$ is the odometry increment and $R_t$ is the **motion noise covariance** (typically 3×3 diagonal with translational/rotational variances $\sigma_x^2, \sigma_y^2, \sigma_\theta^2$). $A_t$ is the Jacobian of the motion model w.r.t. the state. **Update Equations:** $$ K_t = \Sigma_t H_t^T (H_t \Sigma_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1} $$ $$ \mu_t \leftarrow \mu_t + K_t (z_t - h(\mu_t)) $$ $$ \Sigma_t \leftarrow (I - K_t H_t) \Sigma_t $$ where $H_t$ is the Jacobian of the observation model $h(\cdot)$, $Q_t$ is **measurement noise covariance**, and $K_t$ is the **Kalman Gain** (decides how much to trust the sensor vs. the prediction). #### B. Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) Localization - Same Gaussian assumption, but handles **nonlinear** motion/observation models by linearizing via Jacobians. - Used in ROS packages like `robot_pose_ekf`. - **Limitation:** linearization errors can cause divergence, and it still cannot represent multimodal beliefs. #### C. Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) / Particle Filter - Represents $bel(x_t)$ as a **set of $N$ weighted samples** (particles): $\{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{N}$. - Each particle is a hypothesis pose; the weight encodes how well that pose explains the measurement. - **Only filter that can solve global localization** (multimodal) and the **kidnapped robot problem**. **The MCL Recursion:** 1. **Sampling:** Draw $x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$ for each particle. 2. **Importance Weighting:** Set $w_t^{[i]} = p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$. 3. **Resampling:** Draw $N$ particles with replacement, with probability $\propto w_t^{[i]}$. Particles with low weights die; high-weight ones replicate. 4. **Resulting empirical belief:** $bel(x_t) \approx \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \delta(x_t - x_t^{[i]})$. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) | # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$ | Posterior belief over pose | Probability density | | 2 | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$ | Prediction (Chapman–Kolmogorov) | Marginalize over previous state | | 3 | $bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | Bayes update | $\eta$ is normalizer | | 4 | $\eta = \left[ \int p(z_t \mid x_t, m)\, \overline{bel}(x_t)\, dx_t \right]^{-1}$ | Normalizing constant | Ensures total probability = 1 | | 5 | $\mu_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t$ | KF linear prediction | $\mu \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | | 6 | $\Sigma_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t$ | Covariance prediction | $R_t$ = process noise (m², rad²) | | 7 | $K_t = \Sigma_t H_t^T (H_t \Sigma_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain | Dimensionless gain matrix | | 8 | $\mu_t \leftarrow \mu_t + K_t (z_t - h(\mu_t))$ | State update | $h(\mu_t)$ = predicted measurement | | 9 | $w_t^{[i]} \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$ | Particle weight | From beam/ray model | | 10 | $N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2}$ | Effective particle count | Triggers resampling if $N_{\text{eff}} < N/2$ | | 11 | $p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = z_{\text{hit}} \cdot p_{\text{hit}} + z_{\text{short}} \cdot p_{\text{short}} + z_{\text{max}} \cdot p_{\text{max}} + z_{\text{rand}} \cdot p_{\text{rand}}$ | Beam model mixture | Weights sum to 1 | ### 2.5 Real-World Utility > [!NOTE] > - **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva):** Use AMCL on ROS to localize among shelves. > - **Autonomous Cars (Waymo, Baidu Apollo):** Use EKF + particle filters for lane-level localization against HD maps. > - **Vacuum Robots (Roomba, Xiaomi):** Use simplified MCL on occupancy grids. > - **Drone Swarms (DJI, Skydio):** Use EKF with visual landmarks for GPS-denied navigation. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Bayes Filter Update Equation We start from the definition of the posterior belief and apply the definition of conditional probability: $$ bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m) $$ By the definition of conditional probability: $$ p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)\, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)} $$ Apply the **Markov assumption** to the measurement (the current observation $z_t$ depends only on the current state $x_t$ and the map $m$): $$ p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m) $$ Substitute and recognize the normalizer $\eta$: $$ bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) $$ The remaining term $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)$ is by definition $\overline{bel}(x_t)$, yielding the update equation used in Section 2. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Kalman Filter Prediction Step The robot's motion model is: $$ x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t) $$ Linearize $f$ about the current estimate $\mu_{t-1}$ using the first-order Taylor expansion: $$ f(x_{t-1}, u_t) \approx f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) + F_{t-1}(x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1}) $$ where $F_{t-1} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\big|_{\mu_{t-1}, u_t}$ is the Jacobian. Then: $$ \mathbb{E}[x_t] = F_{t-1} \mu_{t-1} + f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) - F_{t-1}\mu_{t-1} = f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) $$ Define the predicted mean: $$ \bar{\mu}_t = f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) $$ The covariance propagates by the law of total variance, treating $x_{t-1}$ and $\varepsilon_t$ as independent: $$ \bar{\Sigma}_t = F_{t-1} \Sigma_{t-1} F_{t-1}^T + R_t $$ For the **2D differential-drive odometry** model with state $x = (x, y, \theta)^T$ and control $u = (\delta_{\text{rot1}}, \delta_{\text{trans}}, \delta_{\text{rot2}})^T$: $$ x' = x + \delta_{\text{trans}} \cos(\theta + \delta_{\text{rot1}}) $$ $$ y' = y + \delta_{\text{trans}} \sin(\theta + \delta_{\text{rot1}}) $$ $$ \theta' = \theta + \delta_{\text{rot1}} + \delta_{\text{rot2}} $$ The Jacobian $F_x = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ becomes: $$ F_x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -\delta_{\text{trans}} \sin(\theta + \delta_{\text{rot1}}) \\ 0 & 1 & \delta_{\text{trans}} \cos(\theta + \delta_{\text{rot1}}) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ The noise Jacobian $F_u$ maps the control noise into state noise, and the full process noise becomes $R_t = F_u Q_u F_u^T$ where $Q_u = \text{diag}(\sigma_{\text{rot1}}^2, \sigma_{\text{trans}}^2, \sigma_{\text{rot2}}^2)$. ### 3.3 Detailed Numerical Worked Example — EKF Localization > [!NOTE] > **Scenario:** A differential-drive robot at true pose $x_t = (2.0 \text{ m}, 1.0 \text{ m}, 30°)^T$. A landmark is at $m_1 = (5.0, 1.0)$. The robot drives with odometry $u_t$ that says it has translated $\delta_{\text{trans}} = 1.0 \text{ m}$ forward (no rotation). Initial estimate $\mu_{t-1} = (1.5, 1.0, 0°)^T$, $\Sigma_{t-1} = 0.01 \cdot I_3$. Process noise $R = 0.04 \cdot I_3$. Measurement returns range $r = 2.5 \text{ m}$, bearing $b = 25°$. Measurement noise $Q = \text{diag}(0.25, 0.01)$. #### Step 1: Prediction (Motion Update) Apply the motion model with $\delta_{\text{rot1}} = 0$, $\delta_{\text{trans}} = 1.0$, $\delta_{\text{rot2}} = 0$: $$ \bar{\mu}_t = \mu_{t-1} + \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 \cdot \cos(0°) \\ 1.0 \cdot \sin(0°) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 + 1.0 \\ 1.0 + 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 \\ 1.0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ The Jacobian $F_x$ at $\theta = 0$: $$ F_x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -1.0 \cdot \sin(0°) \\ 0 & 1 & 1.0 \cdot \cos(0°) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 1.0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Covariance prediction: $$ \bar{\Sigma}_t = F_x \Sigma_{t-1} F_x^T + R $$ $$ F_x \Sigma_{t-1} F_x^T = 0.01 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.02 & 0.01 \\ 0 & 0.01 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ \bar{\Sigma}_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.05 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.06 & 0.01 \\ 0 & 0.01 & 0.05 \end{bmatrix} $$ #### Step 2: Predicted Measurement $h(\bar{\mu}_t)$ From $\bar{\mu}_t = (2.5, 1.0, 0°)$ to landmark $m_1 = (5.0, 1.0)$: $$ \Delta x = 5.0 - 2.5 = 2.5, \quad \Delta y = 1.0 - 1.0 = 0 $$ $$ \hat{r} = \sqrt{2.5^2 + 0^2} = 2.5 \text{ m} $$ $$ \hat{b} = \text{atan2}(0, 2.5) - 0° = 0° = 0 \text{ rad} $$ #### Step 3: Measurement Jacobian $H_t$ $$ H_t = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{\Delta x}{\hat{r}} & -\frac{\Delta y}{\hat{r}} & 0 \\ \frac{\Delta y}{\hat{r}^2} & -\frac{\Delta x}{\hat{r}^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -0.4 & -1 \end{bmatrix} $$ #### Step 4: Innovation $$ \nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t) = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 \\ 25° \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 \\ 0° \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0.4363 \text{ rad} \end{bmatrix} $$ #### Step 5: Kalman Gain $$ S = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q = \begin{bmatrix} 0.30 & 0.004 \\ 0.004 & 0.1056 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T S^{-1} \approx \begin{bmatrix} -0.1667 & 0.0019 \\ 0 & -0.0403 \\ 0 & -0.0907 \end{bmatrix} $$ #### Step 6: State Update $$ \mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 \\ 1.0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.0008 \\ -0.0176 \\ -0.0396 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5008 \\ 0.9824 \\ -0.0396 \end{bmatrix} $$ The estimate of $\theta$ has shifted by $\approx -2.27°$, consistent with the bearing residual of $25°$ at the unrotated prediction. $\blacksquare$ ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | Kalman Filter | EKF | MCL (Particle Filter) | AMCL (Adaptive) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Belief Representation | Single Gaussian | Single Gaussian | Set of weighted samples | Sample set + KLD-sampling | | Computational Cost $O(\cdot)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(N \cdot M)$ | $O(N_{\text{var}} \cdot M)$ | | Handles Global Localization? | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | | Kidnapped Robot? | ❌ | ❌ | ⚠ with random particles | ✅ (injects samples) | | Nonlinear Models? | ❌ | ✅ (approx) | ✅ | ✅ | | Memory | Low (2 matrices) | Low | High ($N$ particles) | Adaptive | | ROS Package | `robot_pose_ekf` | `robot_pose_ekf` | `amcl` | `amcl` (default) | | Typical $N$ | — | — | 500–5000 | 500–5000 (adaptive) | ## 4.
