[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define localization in mobile robotics. Differentiate between position tracking and global localization.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Localization is the process of estimating a robot's pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)$ within a known coordinate frame using map $m$, controls $u_{1:t}$ and sensor data $z_{1:t}$, formalized as computing the belief $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$. **Position tracking** assumes an initial pose estimate is available with low uncertainty; the algorithm merely *refines* it over time (solved by EKF). **Global localization** assumes *no* prior knowledge of the starting pose; the robot must determine its location from scratch anywhere in the map (solved by particle filters/MCL). > **Mark Split:** Definition (1) + Position tracking (1) + Global localization (1). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]** *List the two fundamental equations of the Bayes filter for localization and state the role of each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Prediction equation:** $$ \overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1} $$ *Role:* Propagates the previous belief through the motion model $u_t$ to obtain a prior estimate (uncertainty *grows* due to noisy motion). 2. **Measurement update equation:** $$ bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t) $$ *Role:* Corrects the prior using sensor observation $z_t$ via the observation model (uncertainty *shrinks*). > **Mark Split:** Prediction eqn + role (1.5), Update eqn + role (1.5). --- ### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks: 7 + 7 Internal Choice)** #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Kalman / EKF Path** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]** **(a)** With a neat labeled diagram, explain the architecture of the Bayesian localization loop. Discuss how the Kalman filter and Monte Carlo localization (MCL) differ in their representation of the robot's belief. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the Kalman filter prediction and update equations starting from the linear-Gaussian motion and observation models. Show that the posterior covariance $\Sigma_t$ is non-increasing under a single update. **(7 marks)** ##### **Model Solution (a):** The Bayesian localization loop (refer to Section 4.1 diagram) takes three inputs — the **map $m$**, the **control $u_t$** (odometry), and the **sensor $z_t$** (e.g., laser scan) — and produces a pose belief $bel(x_t)$. The architecture consists of two coupled blocks: - **Motion model block:** Computes $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ by convolving the prior with the motion density. - **Observation model block:** Multiplies by the likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ and renormalizes. **Comparison of belief representation:** | Aspect | Kalman Filter | Monte Carlo Localization | |---|---|---| | Belief shape | Single multivariate Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)$ | Set of $N$ weighted samples $\{x^{[i]}, w^{[i]}\}$ | | Number of hypotheses | Exactly one (unimodal) | $N$ simultaneous hypotheses (multimodal) | | Memory | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ for $n$-dim state | $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot n)$ | | Global localization | Cannot (no multi-modality) | Can, by spreading particles over the map | | Kidnapped robot | Cannot recover | Recovers via random particle injection (AMCL) | (Diagram of KF as a single bell curve, MCL as scattered weighted points earns full credit.) > **Mark Split (a):** Diagram + loop description (3), KF vs MCL table (3), Real-world note (1) = 7. ##### **Model Solution (b):** **Linear-Gaussian motion model:** $$ x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t) $$ Taking the expectation and using $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_t] = 0$: $$ \bar{\mu}_t = \mathbb{E}[x_t] = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t $$ The covariance propagates as: $$ \bar{\Sigma}_t = \mathbb{E}[(x_t - \bar{\mu}_t)(x_t - \bar{\mu}_t)^T] = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t $$ **Linear-Gaussian observation model:** $$ z_t = H_t x_t + \delta_t, \quad \delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t) $$ **Joint distribution** of $x_t$ and $z_t$ given $\bar{\mu}_t, \bar{\Sigma}_t$: $$ \begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ z_t \end{bmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left( \begin{bmatrix} \bar{\mu}_t \\ H_t \bar{\mu}_t \end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix} \bar{\Sigma}_t & \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T \\ H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t & H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t \end{bmatrix} \right) $$ Using the standard Gaussian conditioning identity, the posterior $\mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ is obtained with: $$ K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1} $$ $$ \mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - H_t \bar{\mu}_t) $$ $$ \Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t $$ **Proof that $\Sigma_t \leq \bar{\Sigma}_t$ (matrix inequality):** Substitute $K_t$ into the expression for $\Sigma_t$: $$ \Sigma_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t - \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1} H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t $$ The second term is $\bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T S^{-1} H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t$, which is a symmetric positive semidefinite matrix $M \succeq 0$ (it can be written as $(H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t)^T (S^{-1}) (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t)$ with $S^{-1} \succ 0$). Therefore: $$ \Sigma_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t - M \quad \text{where} \quad M \succeq 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \Sigma_t \preceq \bar{\Sigma}_t $$ In scalar form: $\sigma_t^2 = \bar{\sigma}_t^2 - \frac{\bar{\sigma}_t^4 h^2}{h^2 \bar{\sigma}_t^2 + q} \leq \bar{\sigma}_t^2$. Hence the posterior covariance is always non-increasing, i.e., measurement updates can only *reduce* (or maintain) uncertainty. $\blacksquare$ > **Mark Split (b):** Motion derivation (2), Observation (1), Joint distribution (1), Update eqns (1.5), Covariance monotonicity proof (1.5) = 7. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Particle Filter / MCL Path** *(Internal Choice)* **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Choice]** **(a)** Explain the Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) algorithm. How does it represent the belief state? Discuss the importance of resampling and the effective sample size $N_{\text{eff}}$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A robot uses MCL with $N = 1000$ particles. After a measurement update, the normalized weights are: 600 particles have $w = 0.0005$, 300 have $w = 0.001$, and 100 have $w = 0.002$. Compute $N_{\text{eff}}$ and decide whether resampling should be triggered (threshold $N/2 = 500$). **(7 marks)** ##### **Model Solution (a):** MCL is a sample-based implementation of the Bayes filter designed to handle **nonlinear, non-Gaussian, and multimodal** belief distributions. It represents the posterior belief as a finite set of $N$ weighted particles: $$ bel(x_t) \approx \{(x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}) \mid i = 1, \dots, N\}, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_t^{[i]} = 1 $$ Each particle is a hypothesis pose; its weight encodes the likelihood that the robot is at that pose. **Algorithm steps** (reproduced from Section 2.3.C): 1. **Sampling:** For each particle, draw a predicted pose from the motion model: $x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$. 2. **Importance weighting:** Compute the likelihood of the measurement given the predicted pose: $w_t^{[i]} = p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$. 3. **Normalization:** $\tilde{w}_t^{[i]} = w_t^{[i]} / \sum_j w_t^{[j]}$. 4. **Resampling:** Draw $N$ new particles with replacement, with probability $\tilde{w}_t^{[i]}$. **Role of resampling:** Without resampling, after a few iterations most particles have *negligible* weights and only a few have meaningful weight — this is called **particle depletion**. Resampling redistributes computational effort to high-weight regions, ensuring the particle set remains a faithful representation of the belief. **Effective sample size:** $$ N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (w_t^{[i]})^2} $$ - When all weights are equal, $w_i = 1/N$, and $N_{\text{eff}} = N$. - When one particle has weight 1 and the rest 0, $N_{\text{eff}} = 1$. - Resampling is triggered when $N_{\text{eff}}$ falls below a threshold (commonly $N/2$). This adaptive strategy (used in AMCL) avoids unnecessary resampling when the belief is still well-distributed. > **Mark Split (a):** Belief representation (2), Algorithm steps (2), Resampling role (1.5), $N_{\text{eff}}$ derivation & threshold (1.5) = 7. ##### **Model Solution (b):** We are given: - $N = 1000$ particles. - 600 particles with $w = 0.0005$, - 300 particles with $w = 0.001$, - 100 particles with $w = 0.002$. **Step 1 — Verify normalization:** $$ \sum w_i = 600(0.0005) + 300(0.001) + 100(0.002) = 0.3 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 0.8 $$ **The weights are not normalized.** Normalize: $$ \tilde{w}_1 = 0.0005/0.8 = 0.000625, \quad \tilde{w}_2 = 0.001/0.8 = 0.00125, \quad \tilde{w}_3 = 0.002/0.8 = 0.0025 $$ **Step 2 — Compute $N_{\text{eff}}$:** $$ \sum_i \tilde{w}_i^2 = 600 (0.000625)^2 + 300 (0.00125)^2 + 100 (0.0025)^2 $$ Compute each term: $$ 600 \times 3.90625 \times 10^{-7} = 2.34375 \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ 300 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-6} = 4.6875 \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ 100 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-6} = 6.25 \times 10^{-4} $$ Sum: $$ \sum_i \tilde{w}_i^2 = 2.34375 \times 10^{-4} + 4.6875 \times 10^{-4} + 6.25 \times 10^{-4} = 1.328125 \times 10^{-3} $$ Therefore: $$ N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{1.328125 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 752.94 \approx 753 $$ **Step 3 — Decision:** Resampling threshold is $N/2 = 1000/2 = 500$. Since $N_{\text{eff}} = 753 > 500$, **resampling should NOT be triggered**. The belief is still well-distributed; particle diversity is healthy. $\blacksquare$ > **Mark Split (b):** Weight normalization (2), Sum of squares calculation (3), $N_{\text{eff}}$ result (1), Decision with threshold reasoning (1) = 7. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing $bel(x_t)$ and $\overline{bel}(x_t)$:** The bar denotes the *prior* (after prediction, before measurement). Many students drop the bar, losing 1–2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the normalizer $\eta$ in Bayes' update:** Omitting $\eta$ in the equation $bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)$ is the most common KTU error. > 3. **Mixing up KF and EKF definitions:** KF is for *linear* systems; EKF uses Jacobians for *nonlinear* systems. Writing "EKF for linear system" loses 1 mark. > 4. **Covariance monotonicity proof:** Students often *state* $\Sigma_t \leq \bar{\Sigma}_t$ without proving it. The matrix inequality must be shown explicitly (as in Question A part b). > 5. **Particle filter resampling logic:** Forgetting that resampling uses *with-replacement* sampling *with probability proportional to weight* is a common 1-mark deduction. > 6. **Numerical units:** Forgetting to convert degrees to radians (or vice versa) in EKF examples — KTU examiners are strict about units. > 7. **ROS specifics:** For AMCL, students often forget that the package subscribes to `/scan`, `/odom`, and `/map` and publishes to `/amcl_pose`, `/particlecloud`, and `/tf`. Missing any one loses a mark in ROS-specific questions. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Definition:** Localization = estimating pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)$ given map $m$, controls $u_{1:t}$, measurements $z_{1:t}$. - **Belief notation:** $bel(x_t)$ is the *posterior*; $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ is the *prior* (after motion update only). - **Two Bayes filter equations** are mandatory to memorize: prediction integral + Bayes update with $\eta$ normalizer. - **Three algorithm families:** Kalman Filter (linear/Gaussian), EKF (nonlinear via Jacobians), MCL/Particle Filter (sample-based, multimodal). - **Particle filter steps:** (1) Sample from motion model, (2) Weight by observation likelihood, (3) Resample when $N_{\text{eff}} < N/2$. - **AMCL in ROS:** Default particle-filter package; subscribes `/scan`, `/map`, `/odom`; publishes `/amcl_pose`, `/particlecloud`, `/tf`. Tolerates kidnapped robot by injecting random particles. - **Map message:** `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` with cell values in $\{-1, 0, 100\}$ (unknown, free, occupied). - **Kalman filter equations** to remember cold: - Predict: $\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t$, $\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t$. - Update: $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$, $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$. - **Covariance monotonicity:** $\Sigma_t \preceq \bar{\Sigma}_t$ — measurement updates never *increase* uncertainty. - **Beam model components:** $p_{\text{hit}}$ (correct detection), $p_{\text{short}}$ (unexpected obstacle), $p_{\text{max}}$ (max range), $p_{\text{rand}}$ (random) — weights sum to 1. - **Complexity:** KF $O(n^2)$, MCL $O(NM)$ where $M$ = map size; AMCL uses **KLD-sampling** to adapt $N$. - **Position tracking vs global:** Tracking has narrow initial belief (EKF works); global needs MCL. - **AMCL config file:** Located at `~//config/amcl.yaml`; key parameters `min_particles`, `max_particles`, `update_min_d`, `update_min_a`. - **TF tree requirement for localization:** `map → odom → base_link` (or `odom → base_footprint → base_link`); AMCL publishes the `map → odom` transform. - **Always** verify the initial pose is set via "2D Pose Estimate" tool in RViz before trusting AMCL output. - **Kidnapped robot problem:** Only AMCL (with random particle injection) reliably recovers; EKF will fail permanently.

topics.title: Using the ROS Navigation Stack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Using the ROS Navigation Stack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **ROS Navigation Stack** is a meta-package (`navigation`) in the Robot Operating System framework that provides a complete, configurable 2D navigation solution for differential-drive and holonomic wheeled mobile robots operating in planar environments. Formally, it is defined as a collection of ROS nodes, libraries, configuration files, and plugins that collectively enable a robot to autonomously move from a start pose to a goal pose while avoiding obstacles, using a pre-built static map and continuous sensor feedback.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The Navigation Stack is a 2D navigation framework that uses sensor data, odometry, and a goal pose to output safe velocity commands (`/cmd_vel`) for a mobile base, assuming the robot is operating on a flat plane and can be represented by a circular or rectangular footprint.

The Navigation Stack is the **most widely deployed application-level stack** in ROS, sitting on top of the ROS communication middleware and standard message types (`sensor_msgs`, `geometry_msgs`, `nav_msgs`).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Self-Driving Car GPS:**
> Imagine you are driving to a friend's house in an unfamiliar city. You have a **map** of the city (static map), a **GPS** telling you where you are right now (localization), a **road-level route planner** that decides the main highway to take (global planner), and a **real-time driver** that steers you around a slow truck or a pothole using your eyes (local planner + sensors). The ROS Navigation Stack does exactly this for a robot: the **map** is loaded from `map_server`, **AMCL** is the GPS, the **global planner** is the highway planner, and the **local planner (DWA/TEB)** is the real-time driver reacting to live obstacles.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **move_base** | The central coordinating node of the Navigation Stack |
| **Global Planner** | Computes the long-range, low-resolution path from start to goal |
| **Local Planner** | Computes short-range, real-time velocity commands to follow the global path |
| **Costmap** | A 2D grid map where each cell stores a "cost" representing traversal difficulty |
| **AMCL** | Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization — estimates robot pose in a known map |
| **Recovery Behavior** | Predefined routines executed when the robot gets stuck |
| **TF Tree** | The coordinate frame hierarchy that the stack requires |
| **Footprint** | The 2D shape representing the robot's physical extent on the ground |

## 1.4 Standard Quantitative Metrics (Bolded)

- **Update Rate of Local Planner:** typically **20 Hz** (`controller_frequency = 20.0`)
- **Global Plan Replanning Rate:** typically **1.0 Hz** (`planner_frequency = 1.0`)
- **Costmap Resolution:** typically **0.05 m/cell** (`resolution: 0.05`)
- **Robot Radius / Inflation Radius:** typically **0.20 m – 0.45 m**
- **Inflation Layer Falloff:** cost decays from `Lethality` to `Inscribed` over `inflation_radius` cells
- **Standard Coordinate Frame:** `map` → `odom` → `base_link` → `base_laser` / `base_footprint`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently ask about the **assumptions** of the Navigation Stack. Memorize the four hard constraints:
> 1. Robot must be operating on a **flat, planar** surface.
> 2. Robot base must be controllable via **`/cmd_vel`** (Twist messages).
> 3. Robot's pose in a 2D map must be estimable via AMCL or similar.
> 4. A **static map** (PGM/YAML) must be available via `map_server`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of the Navigation Stack — Layered Breakdown

The Navigation Stack is organized into **three functional layers** that communicate via ROS topics and the TF system.

### Layer A — Perception & Localization Layer
- **`map_server`**: Loads a pre-built 2D occupancy grid map (PGM image + YAML metadata) and serves it on the `/map` topic (latched).
- **`amcl`**: Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization. Fuses laser scan (`/scan`), odometry (`/odom`), and the static map to publish a probabilistic pose estimate on `/amcl_pose` and the `map → odom` TF transform.

### Layer B — Planning Layer (Server: `move_base`)
The `move_base` node is the **central brain**. It hosts two sub-planners as plugins:

**1. Global Planner (`navfn` or `global_planner`)**
- Receives the goal pose and the static map costmap.
- Performs a **graph search** (Dijkstra for `navfn`, A* with potential functions for `global_planner`) to compute the optimal long-range path.
- Publishes the path on `/plan` (a `nav_msgs/Path` message).
- Re-plans at `planner_frequency` (default 1 Hz) or when the local planner fails.

**2. Local Planner (`base_local_planner` / `dwa_local_planner` / `teb_local_planner`)**
- Receives the current scan, odometry, and the global plan.
- Samples a set of candidate **velocity tuples (v, ω)** within the robot's dynamic limits.
- Scores each trajectory using a **cost function** (path distance, obstacle proximity, goal alignment).
- Publishes the best velocity as `geometry_msgs/Twist` on `/cmd_vel` at `controller_frequency` (default 20 Hz).

### Layer C — Costmap Layer (`costmap_2d`)
A costmap is a **2D grid where each cell holds an integer cost in the range [0, 255]**:

- **0 — Free** (no obstruction)
- **1 – 252 — Inflation / Inscribed costs** (rising cost near obstacles)
- **253 — Inscribed** (inside the robot's footprint of an obstacle)
- **254 — Lethal** (occupied, robot must not enter)
- **255 — Unknown** (no sensor data)

Two costmaps are maintained by `move_base`:
1. **Global Costmap** — used by the global planner, built from the static map.
2. **Local Costmap** — used by the local planner, built from a rolling window of live sensor data.

## 2.2 Costmap Inflation Equation (Key KTU Formula)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inflation Radius Equation:** The cost of a cell at distance *d* from the nearest lethal obstacle is computed using exponential decay:
>
> $$\text{proxy\_cost} = L \cdot e^{-k \cdot d}$$
>
> where:
> - *L* = `lethal_cost` (default **253**)
> - *k* = decay constant, related to `cost_scaling_factor` (default **10.0**)
> - *d* = distance from the cell to the nearest lethal obstacle in meters

The actual cost stored in the cell is:

$$\text{cell\_cost} = \text{inscribed\_cost} + \text{proxy\_cost}$$

> where **inscribed_cost = 253** is added when the cell lies inside the inscribed radius of the obstacle footprint. The `inflation_radius` is the maximum distance at which costs propagate and is typically set to:

$$r_{\text{infl}} = r_{\text{robot}} + r_{\text{buffer}}$$

> where the buffer is usually **0.30 m – 0.50 m** to provide safety margin.

## 2.3 Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) — Trajectory Sampling

DWA is the default local planner. It restricts the search space of velocities to a **dynamic window** that is reachable within one control cycle given the robot's acceleration limits:

$$v_{\min}^{\text{dyn}} = \max\left(v_{\min},\ v_{\text{current}} - \dot{v}_{\max} \cdot \Delta t\right)$$

$$v_{\max}^{\text{dyn}} = \min\left(v_{\max},\ v_{\text{current}} + \dot{v}_{\max} \cdot \Delta t\right)$$

> where:
> - *v_min, v_max* = robot's minimum/maximum linear velocity
> - *v_current* = current linear velocity
> - *\dot{v}_max* = maximum linear acceleration
> - *Δt* = control period (**1 / controller_frequency = 0.05 s** at 20 Hz)

The trajectory cost is a weighted sum:

$$C_{\text{total}} = w_{\text{path}} \cdot C_{\text{path}} + w_{\text{goal}} \cdot C_{\text{goal}} + w_{\text{obs}} \cdot C_{\text{obstacle}} + w_{\text{osc}} \cdot C_{\text{oscillation}} + \cdots$$

The velocity pair **(v\*, ω\*)** with the lowest cost is published as `/cmd_vel`.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Parameter | Meaning | Default Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| `controller_frequency` | Local planner update rate | **20.0 Hz** |
| `planner_frequency` | Global planner update rate | **1.0 Hz** |
| `resolution` | Costmap cell size | **0.05 m/cell** |
| `inflation_radius` | Max distance cost propagates from obstacles | **0.55 m** |
| `cost_scaling_factor` | Exponential decay constant *k* | **10.0** |
| `robot_radius` | Circular footprint approximation | **0.165 m** |
| `max_vel_x` | Max forward linear velocity | **0.26 m/s** (TurtleBot) |
| `min_vel_x` | Min forward linear velocity | **0.05 m/s** |
| `max_vel_theta` | Max angular velocity | **1.0 rad/s** |
| `acc_lim_x` | Max linear acceleration | **2.5 m/s²** |
| `acc_lim_theta` | Max angular acceleration | **3.2 rad/s²** |
| `xy_goal_tolerance` | Position tolerance to consider goal reached | **0.05 m** |
| `yaw_goal_tolerance` | Orientation tolerance | **0.17 rad (~10°)** |
| `Lethality cost` | Occupied cell cost | **254** |
| `Inscribed cost` | Inside robot footprint of obstacle | **253** |
| Cost range | Full integer range | **[0, 255]** |

## 2.5 TF Tree Requirement (Mandatory Wiring)

The Navigation Stack enforces a **strict TF tree** that must exist before any node will operate:

```
map  →  odom  →  base_link  →  base_laser (or base_footprint)
```

- `map → odom` is published by **AMCL**.
- `odom → base_link` is published by **odometry node / robot driver**.
- `base_link → base_laser` is a static transform published by the URDF or a `static_transform_publisher`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** If any of these transforms is missing, `move_base` will refuse to start and log `"Could not find a connection between map and base_link"`.

## 2.6 Recovery Behaviors

When the local planner fails to make progress (e.g., robot stuck against an obstacle), `move_base` executes a configurable sequence of recovery behaviors:

1. **Clear costmaps** — wipe obstacle memory (clears both local and global costmap obstacle layers).
2. **Rotate recovery** — rotate the robot 360° in place to clear blind spots.
3. **Conservative reset** — fully clear and replan from scratch.
4. **Aggressive reset** — fully clear, replan, and abort if still failing.

The maximum number of recovery attempts is governed by `recovery_behaviors` and `clearing_rotation_allowed` parameters.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ROS Navigation Stack is the **de facto standard** for mobile robot autonomy in research and industry. Real deployments include:
- **Warehouse robots** (Amazon Kiva, Fetch Robotics) — uses customized AMCL and costmap_2d.
- **Service robots** (TurtleBot, Pepper) — uses default `navfn` + `dwa_local_planner`.
- **Autonomous hospital delivery robots** — uses `teb_local_planner` for time-optimal trajectories.
- **Research platforms** (Husky, Jackal) — uses `global_planner` (A*) for faster replanning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Stack's strength is **modularity**: every component (planner, recovery, costmap layer) is a plugin, so the entire navigation pipeline can be replaced without rewriting the rest.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Costmap Inflation Cost at a Given Cell

**Problem:** A costmap has `inflation_radius = 0.55 m`, `cost_scaling_factor = 10.0`, and `inscribed_radius = 0.165 m` (robot radius). Compute the cost assigned to a cell that is **0.30 m** away from the nearest lethal obstacle.

**Step 1 — Identify the regime.**

The cell distance *d* = 0.30 m is **less than** the inflation radius 0.55 m and **greater than** the inscribed radius 0.165 m. So the cell lies in the **inflation zone**, not inside the obstacle footprint.

**Step 2 — Apply the proxy cost equation.**

$$\text{proxy\_cost} = L \cdot e^{-k \cdot d}$$

Substitute *L* = 253, *k* = 10.0, *d* = 0.30 m:

$$\text{proxy\_cost} = 253 \cdot e^{-10.0 \times 0.30}$$

Compute the exponent:

$$-10.0 \times 0.30 = -3.0$$

Compute the exponential:

$$e^{-3.0} \approx 0.049787$$

Multiply:

$$\text{proxy\_cost} = 253 \times 0.049787 \approx 12.60$$

**Step 3 — Add the inscribed cost baseline.**

Since the cell is **outside** the inscribed radius, the full inscribed cost is **not** added; the proxy cost is added to the free-space baseline. The costmap will store:

$$\text{cell\_cost} = 0 + 12.60 = 12.60 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{rounded to } 13$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation.**

A cost of **13 / 253** indicates a **mildly penalized** region: the global planner will prefer to route the robot away from this cell, but it is not lethal. Cells closer to the obstacle have exponentially higher costs, creating a "soft barrier" gradient around obstacles.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DWA Dynamic Window for Linear Velocity

**Problem:** A TurtleBot has `max_vel_x = 0.26 m/s`, `min_vel_x = 0.05 m/s`, `acc_lim_x = 2.5 m/s²`, and the controller runs at `controller_frequency = 20 Hz`. The current forward velocity is `v_current = 0.10 m/s`. Find the **achievable velocity window** for the next cycle.

**Step 1 — Compute the control period.**

$$\Delta t = \frac{1}{\text{controller\_frequency}} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the lower bound formula.**

$$v_{\min}^{\text{dyn}} = \max\left(v_{\min},\ v_{\text{current}} - \dot{v}_{\max} \cdot \Delta t\right)$$

Compute the deceleration-limited lower bound:

$$v_{\text{current}} - \dot{v}_{\max} \cdot \Delta t = 0.10 - (2.5)(0.05) = 0.10 - 0.125 = -0.025 \text{ m/s}$$

Take the max with `min_vel_x = 0.05`:

$$v_{\min}^{\text{dyn}} = \max(0.05,\ -0.025) = 0.05 \text{ m/s}$$

> The robot **cannot decelerate fast enough** in 50 ms to reach negative velocity, so the lower bound is clamped to the hardware minimum.

**Step 3 — Apply the upper bound formula.**

$$v_{\max}^{\text{dyn}} = \min\left(v_{\max},\ v_{\text{current}} + \dot{v}_{\max} \cdot \Delta t\right)$$

Compute the acceleration-limited upper bound:

$$v_{\text{current}} + \dot{v}_{\max} \cdot \Delta t = 0.10 + (2.5)(0.05) = 0.10 + 0.125 = 0.225 \text{ m/s}$$

Take the min with `max_vel_x = 0.26`:

$$v_{\max}^{\text{dyn}} = \min(0.26,\ 0.225) = 0.225 \text{ m/s}$$

> The robot **can accelerate** from 0.10 to 0.225 m/s, but not all the way to 0.26 m/s in one cycle.

**Step 4 — Final dynamic window.**

$$v \in [0.05,\ 0.225] \text{ m/s}$$

> This narrower window is the **only search space** DWA will sample in this cycle — it represents velocities that are **kinematically reachable** from the current state within one tick.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Global Planner Path Length Estimate

**Problem:** A `navfn` global planner uses Dijkstra's algorithm on a 250 × 250 costmap with `resolution = 0.05 m/cell`. The computed path contains **180 cells**. Estimate the **path length in meters** assuming each cell contributes one diagonal step on average.

**Step 1 — Cell-to-meter conversion.**

The grid spans:

$$L_{\text{grid}} = 250 \times 0.05 = 12.5 \text{ m per side}$$

**Step 2 — Path length formula.**

A straight cell-to-cell step is `0.05 m` (axis-aligned) or `0.05√2 ≈ 0.0707 m` (diagonal). For a mixed path:

$$L_{\text{path}} = n_{\text{axis}} \cdot r + n_{\text{diag}} \cdot r\sqrt{2}$$

If we assume **average diagonal step**:

$$L_{\text{path}} = 180 \times 0.05\sqrt{2} = 180 \times 0.0707 = 12.73 \text{ m}$$

> The path is **12.73 m** long, which is plausible given the 12.5 m grid — the path likely traverses the full diagonal of the map.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Costmap Cell State Decision Table

| Cell Condition | Cost Assigned | Planner Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Cell is in **free space**, no obstacle within `inflation_radius` | **0** | No penalty; freely traversable |
| Cell is **adjacent** to lethal obstacle (~ 1 cell away) | ~ **200 – 253** | Strongly avoided; planner routes around |
| Cell is **inside** the robot's footprint of an obstacle | **253** (inscribed) | Treated as physically occupied |
| Cell **is** a lethal obstacle | **254** | Hard barrier; never crossed |
| Cell has **no sensor data** | **255** (Unknown) | Treated as obstacle by default (`track_unknown_space`) |
| Cell is in the **inflation zone** at distance *d* | $253 \cdot e^{-k \cdot d}$ | Exponential penalty — soft repulsion |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — move_base State Machine

| State | Trigger | Action | Topic Published |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Planning** | New goal received | Global planner computes path | `/plan` |
| **Controlling** | Valid plan exists | Local planner computes `cmd_vel` | `/cmd_vel` |
| **Clearing** | Local planner oscillation detected | Costmaps cleared | — |
| **Recovering** | Stuck for *N* cycles | Recovery behaviors executed | `/cmd_vel` (rotate) |
| **Success** | Within `xy_goal_tolerance` & `yaw_goal_tolerance` | Set `SUCCEEDED` status | `move_base/result` |
| **Failure** | All recoveries exhausted | Set `FAILURE` status | `move_base/result` |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — Configuring a Costmap for a TurtleBot3

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** You are deploying a TurtleBot3 Burger in a 10 m × 10 m lab. The robot has a `footprint` of 138 mm × 178 mm and a 360° LiDAR with `max_obstacle_range = 3.0 m`.

**Step 1 — Set costmap resolution.**

Choose `resolution = 0.05 m/cell` to balance precision and CPU load. The grid will be:

$$\text{grid\_cells} = \frac{10}{0.05} = 200 \text{ cells per side}$$

**Step 2 — Set footprint and inflation radius.**

Use the inscribed circle of the rectangular footprint:

$$r_{\text{inscribed}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \min(0.138,\ 0.178) = 0.069 \text{ m}$$

> However, ROS best practice is to use the **polygon footprint** for rectangular robots. The `footprint` parameter is a list of vertices in meters:

```
footprint: [[0.069, 0.089], [0.069, -0.089], [-0.069, -0.089], [-0.069, 0.089]]
```

Set `inflation_radius = 0.30 m` (≈ 2× the robot half-length).

**Step 3 — Verify TF tree.**

Ensure the launch file publishes:
- `base_footprint → base_link` (static, in URDF)
- `base_link → base_scan` (static, in URDF)
- `map → odom` (published by AMCL at 20 Hz)

**Step 4 — Launch `move_base` with the configuration files.**

The launch should load **three YAML files**:
1. `global_costmap_params.yaml` — static map, inflation layer
2. `local_costmap_params.yaml` — rolling window, obstacle layer from `/scan`
3. `move_base_params.yaml` — planners and recovery behaviors

**Step 5 — Send a 2D Nav Goal.**

In RViz, click "2D Nav Goal" → click on the map → `move_base` receives the goal via the `/move_base_simple/goal` topic → global planner publishes `/plan` → local planner publishes `/cmd_vel` → robot moves.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Navigation Stack Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Map Server] -->|static map| C[Global Costmap]
    B[AMCL Localization] -->|map to odom TF| C
    B -->|amcl pose| D[move_base]
    E[Laser Scan /scan] --> F[Obstacle Layer]
    E -->|sensor data| G[Local Costmap]
    F --> G
    C --> D
    G --> D
    H[Goal Pose /move_base_simple/goal] --> D
    D -->|global plan request| I[Global Planner Plugin]
    I -->|nav msgs Path| D
    D -->|local plan request| J[Local Planner DWA]
    J -->|cmd vel| K[Robot Base]
    K -->|odometry| B
    K -->|TF odom to base_link| D
    D -->|status| L[move_base result]
    D -->|feedback| M[move_base feedback]
```

## 4.2 TF Tree of the Navigation Stack (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MAP[map frame] -->|AMCL publishes| ODOM[odom frame]
    ODOM -->|odometry node publishes| BASELINK[base_link frame]
    BASELINK -->|URDF static transform| BASEFOOT[base_footprint]
    BASELINK -->|URDF static transform| BASESCAN[base_scan laser frame]
    BASEFOOT -->|URDF static transform| WHEELS[wheel frames]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `map → odom` transform is the **only one** published by the localization system. All other transforms must be supplied by the robot's URDF and odometry node. If any link is broken, the entire stack fails to initialize.

## 4.3 Costmap Layer Stack (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Static Map Layer] --> CM[Costmap 2D]
    L2[Obstacle Layer from Scan] --> CM
    L3[Inflation Layer] --> CM
    L4[Voxel Layer 3D optional] --> CM
    CM --> GC[Global Costmap for navfn]
    CM --> LC[Local Costmap for DWA]
    GC --> GBP[Global Planner]
    LC --> LBP[Local Planner DWA]
    GBP --> MB[move_base]
    LBP --> MB
    MB -->|cmd vel| ROBOT[Mobile Base]
    ROBOT -->|scan feedback| L2
```

## 4.4 move_base State Machine (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Planning
    Planning --> Controlling: global plan computed
    Controlling --> Controlling: local plan valid
    Controlling --> Clearing: oscillation detected
    Clearing --> Controlling: costmaps reset
    Controlling --> Recovering: stuck for N cycles
    Recovering --> Recovering: more recoveries queued
    Recovering --> Planning: recovery succeeded
    Recovering --> Failure: all recoveries exhausted
    Controlling --> Success: within goal tolerance
    Planning --> Failure: no valid path exists
    Success --> [*]
    Failure --> [*]
```

## 4.5 Topic Communication Map (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MS[map_server] -->|map nav msg| MB[move_base]
    AMCL[amcl] -->|amcl_pose geometry msg| MB
    AMCL -->|TF map to odom| TF[TF System]
    LS[LiDAR Driver] -->|scan sensor msg| MB
    OD[Odometry Node] -->|odom nav msg| MB
    OD -->|TF odom to base_link| TF
    TF --> MB
    US[User 2D Nav Goal] -->|move_base_simple_goal| MB
    MB -->|plan nav msgs Path| RVIZ[RViz]
    MB -->|cmd_vel geometry Twist| BASE[Robot Base]
    MB -->|status| RES[move_base result]
    MB -->|feedback| FB[move_base feedback]
```

## 4.6 Launch Sequence Order (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Launch robot bringup] --> B[Launch map_server with yaml]
    B --> C[Launch AMCL with laser config]
    C --> D[Verify TF tree map to base_link]
    D -->|valid| E[Launch move_base with 3 yaml configs]
    D -->|broken| F[Fix URDF static transforms]
    F --> D
    E --> G[Launch RViz with nav stack display]
    G --> H[Send 2D Nav Goal via RViz]
    H --> I[Robot navigates autonomously]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the ROS Navigation Stack. State its four mandatory assumptions.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **ROS Navigation Stack** is a 2D navigation framework provided as a meta-package in ROS that enables a mobile robot to autonomously move from a start pose to a specified goal pose while avoiding obstacles, using a pre-built static map, odometry, and live sensor data. It outputs safe velocity commands on the `/cmd_vel` topic.

The **four mandatory assumptions** are:
1. The robot must operate on a **flat, planar surface** (no stairs, no ramps).
2. The robot base must be controllable via **`/cmd_vel`** (geometry_msgs/Twist messages).
3. The robot's 2D pose must be estimable — typically via **AMCL** — using laser scans and odometry.
4. A **static map** (PGM + YAML) must be available via the `map_server` node.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip:** A common mistake is stating only 2 or 3 assumptions. Always list **all four** to secure full marks.

---

### Question 2: What is AMCL? Why is it mandatory for the Navigation Stack?

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**AMCL** stands for **Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization**. It is a probabilistic localization algorithm that maintains a particle filter representing the robot's belief about its pose (x, y, θ) in a known 2D map. Each particle is a hypothesis of the robot's pose, weighted by how well the observed laser scan matches the map at that pose. Over time, particles converge to the true pose.

AMCL is **mandatory** because the Navigation Stack requires a continuous estimate of the robot's pose in the `map` frame. AMCL publishes the `map → odom` transform that bridges the global map frame with the local odometry frame, enabling the global planner to plan paths in the map frame and the local planner to execute in the odom frame.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the ROS Navigation Stack. List all key nodes and the topics they communicate on. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The ROS Navigation Stack architecture consists of **three functional layers** communicating via ROS topics, services, and the TF system. The block diagram is as follows:

```
                    ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
                    │          move_base (Brain)             │
                    │  ┌──────────────────────────────────┐  │
                    │  │   Global Planner (navfn/A*)      │  │
                    │  │   Local Planner  (DWA/TEB)       │  │
                    │  │   Costmap 2D  (Global + Local)    │  │
                    │  │   Recovery Behaviors              │  │
                    │  └──────────────────────────────────┘  │
                    └────────┬───────────────────────────────┘
                             │ /cmd_vel
                             ▼
                    ┌──────────────────┐
                    │   Mobile Base    │
                    └────────┬─────────┘
                             │ /odom, /scan, TF
                             ▼
   ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
   │  map_server  │    │     AMCL     │    │   Sensors    │
   │  /map topic  │    │  /amcl_pose  │    │  /scan       │
   └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘
```

**Key Nodes and Topics:**

| Node | Publishes | Subscribes |
|---|---|---|
| `map_server` | `/map` (nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid) | — |
| `amcl` | `/amcl_pose`, TF `map→odom` | `/scan`, `/initialpose`, `/map` |
| `move_base` | `/cmd_vel`, `/plan`, `/move_base/result` | `/move_base_simple/goal`, `/scan`, `/map`, TF |
| Global Planner plugin | `/plan` (nav_msgs/Path) | Global costmap |
| Local Planner plugin | `/cmd_vel` (geometry_msgs/Twist) | Local costmap, global plan |
| `costmap_2d` | (internal) | `/scan`, `/map` |

**Architectural Flow:**
1. `map_server` loads the pre-built map and latches it on `/map`.
2. AMCL fuses `/scan` and the map to estimate pose and publish the `map → odom` TF.
3. The user publishes a goal on `/move_base_simple/goal` (via RViz "2D Nav Goal" tool).
4. `move_base` invokes the **global planner** to compute a long-range path → publishes on `/plan`.
5. `move_base` invokes the **local planner** to compute safe velocities → publishes on `/cmd_vel`.
6. The robot executes `/cmd_vel`, the cycle repeats at `controller_frequency` (20 Hz).

---

#### (b) Explain the costmap_2d concept. Derive the inflation cost equation and apply it to a cell 0.20 m from a lethal obstacle, given inflation_radius = 0.40 m, cost_scaling_factor = 15.0, and lethal_cost = 253. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

A **costmap** is a 2D grid map in which each cell stores an integer cost representing the difficulty of traversing that cell. The Navigation Stack maintains **two costmaps**: a **global costmap** (built from the static map, used by the global planner) and a **local costmap** (built from a rolling window of live sensor data, used by the local planner).

**Cost Categories:**
- **0** — Free space
- **1 – 252** — Inflation zone (exponential decay from obstacles)
- **253** — Inscribed (inside robot footprint of obstacle)
- **254** — Lethal (occupied)
- **255** — Unknown (no sensor data)

**Inflation Cost Equation:**

A cell at distance *d* from the nearest lethal obstacle receives a **proxy cost** that decays exponentially:

$$\text{proxy\_cost} = L \cdot e^{-k \cdot d}$$

> where *L* = `lethal_cost` = 253 and *k* = `cost_scaling_factor` = 15.0.

The final cell cost in the inflation zone is:

$$\text{cell\_cost} = \text{inscribed\_cost} + \text{proxy\_cost}$$

> where `inscribed_cost = 253` if the cell is within the robot's footprint of an obstacle, else 0.

**Application — Cell 0.20 m from a lethal obstacle:**

Given: *d* = 0.20 m, *L* = 253, *k* = 15.0.

**Step 1 — Check the regime.**

The cell distance *d* = 0.20 m is **less than** `inflation_radius` = 0.40 m, so the cell lies in the **active inflation zone**. The cell is also outside the inscribed radius (assumed < 0.20 m for a typical robot), so `inscribed_cost = 0` in the baseline term — but we still add the proxy cost.

**Step 2 — Compute the proxy cost.**

$$\text{proxy\_cost} = 253 \cdot e^{-15.0 \times 0.20}$$

$$= 253 \cdot e^{-3.0}$$

$$= 253 \times 0.049787$$

$$\approx 12.60$$

**Step 3 — Final cell cost.**

$$\text{cell\_cost} = 0 + 12.60 = 12.60 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{rounded to } 13$$

**Interpretation:** A cost of **13 / 253** ≈ **5.1%** of lethal. The cell is mildly penalized — the global planner will prefer to route around it, but it is not blocked. The exponential decay ensures that cells **closer** to the obstacle have **disproportionately higher** costs, creating a smooth repulsive gradient.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

#### (a) Explain the Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) local planner. With a numerical example, compute the dynamic velocity window for a robot with the given parameters. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)** is a reactive local planner that generates safe velocity commands by:

1. **Discretizing** the velocity space *(v, ω)* into candidate tuples.
2. **Filtering** velocities to the **dynamic window** — those reachable within one control cycle given the robot's acceleration limits.
3. **Simulating** a short trajectory (typically 1–2 seconds) for each candidate.
4. **Scoring** each trajectory using a weighted cost function.
5. **Publishing** the highest-scoring (lowest cost) velocity pair as `/cmd_vel`.

**Numerical Example:**

**Given parameters:**
- `max_vel_x` = 0.30 m/s
- `min_vel_x` = 0.05 m/s
- `acc_lim_x` = 0.5 m/s²
- `controller_frequency` = 10 Hz
- `v_current` = 0.10 m/s

**Step 1 — Compute control period.**

$$\Delta t = \frac{1}{10} = 0.10 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Lower bound of dynamic window.**

$$v_{\min}^{\text{dyn}} = \max\left(v_{\min},\ v_{\text{current}} - \dot{v}_{\max} \cdot \Delta t\right)$$

$$= \max\left(0.05,\ 0.10 - 0.5 \times 0.10\right)$$

$$= \max(0.05,\ 0.05) = 0.05 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Upper bound of dynamic window.**

$$v_{\max}^{\text{dyn}} = \min\left(v_{\max},\ v_{\text{current}} + \dot{v}_{\max} \cdot \Delta t\right)$$

$$= \min\left(0.30,\ 0.10 + 0.5 \times 0.10\right)$$

$$= \min(0.30,\ 0.15) = 0.15 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Final dynamic window.**

$$v \in [0.05,\ 0.15] \text{ m/s}$$

> The robot can only choose linear velocities between **0.05 and 0.15 m/s** in the next cycle. This **drastically reduces the search space** from the full kinematic range [0.05, 0.30] to the dynamic window, making DWA computationally efficient at high control rates.

---

#### (b) What are the mandatory TF transforms required by the ROS Navigation Stack? What happens if the `map → odom` transform is missing? How do you recover the navigation stack from this failure? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The Navigation Stack requires a **complete, unbroken TF tree** with the following hierarchy:

```
map  →  odom  →  base_link  →  base_laser (or base_scan, base_footprint)
```

**Responsibility of each transform:**

| Transform | Publisher | Update Source |
|---|---|---|
| `map → odom` | `amcl` | Particle filter localization |
| `odom → base_link` | Odometry node / robot driver | Wheel encoders + IMU |
| `base_link → base_laser` | URDF or `static_transform_publisher` | Robot's mechanical design (fixed) |

**What happens if `map → odom` is missing?**

The `move_base` node will **refuse to initialize** and log the error:

```
[ERROR] [12345.678]: Could not find a connection between 'map' and 'base_link' 
after 20 retries. 
```

Additionally:
- The global planner cannot transform the goal pose from the `map` frame to the `odom` frame.
- RViz will show the robot's laser scan floating in space, detached from the map.
- The robot will not move when a goal is sent.

**Recovery Steps:**

1. **Verify AMCL is running** — `rosnode list` should include `/amcl`.
2. **Check AMCL parameters** — `use_map_topic: true`, `odom_frame_id: odom`, `base_frame_id: base_link`.
3. **Provide an initial pose** — Click "2D Pose Estimate" in RViz, or publish on `/initialpose`. AMCL requires an initial pose guess to start the particle filter.
4. **Verify sensor data** — `rostopic echo /scan` should show valid `LaserScan` messages.
5. **Check TF tree** — Use `rosrun tf tf_echo map base_link` to confirm the chain is intact.
6. **Re-check parameter files** — Ensure `global_costmap_params.yaml` and `local_costmap_params.yaml` have correct `robot_base_frame` and `global_frame` settings.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to list `base_link → base_laser` as a static transform** — it is part of the URDF, not generated dynamically. Many students wrongly assume AMCL publishes it.
> 2. **Confusing `map → odom` with `odom → base_link`** — these are published by **different nodes** (AMCL vs. odometry driver). Mixing them up is a guaranteed mark loss.
> 3. **Stating that AMCL "tracks" the robot** without explaining that it uses a **particle filter** — KTU examiners specifically test this.
> 4. **Omitting units** in costmap formulas (e.g., writing *d* without "meters") — always include units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — ROS Navigation Stack**

### Must-Know Concepts
- ☐ **Navigation Stack** = 2D planar navigation using map + AMCL + move_base + costmaps.
- ☐ **Four assumptions:** flat ground, /cmd_vel control, AMCL localization, static map.
- ☐ **move_base** is the central node hosting **global planner + local planner + costmaps + recovery behaviors**.
- ☐ **Global planner** = `navfn` (Dijkstra) or `global_planner` (A*) — outputs `/plan`.
- ☐ **Local planner** = `dwa_local_planner` (default), `teb_local_planner` (alternative) — outputs `/cmd_vel`.
- ☐ **AMCL** = Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization — publishes `map → odom` TF.
- ☐ **Costmap range** = [0, 255]; lethal = 254, inscribed = 253, unknown = 255.

### Must-Know Formulas
- ☐ **Inflation proxy cost:** $L \cdot e^{-k \cdot d}$
- ☐ **DWA dynamic window lower bound:** $\max(v_{\min},\ v_{\text{current}} - \dot{v}_{\max} \cdot \Delta t)$
- ☐ **DWA dynamic window upper bound:** $\min(v_{\max},\ v_{\text{current}} + \dot{v}_{\max} \cdot \Delta t)$
- ☐ **Control period:** $\Delta t = 1 / \text{controller\_frequency}$ (default 1/20 = 0.05 s)

### Must-Know TF Tree
- ☐ `map → odom → base_link → base_laser` — all four links must exist.

### Must-Know Parameters
- ☐ `controller_frequency` = 20 Hz, `planner_frequency` = 1 Hz
- ☐ `inflation_radius` ≈ 0.30–0.55 m
- ☐ `cost_scaling_factor` = 10.0 (default)
- ☐ `xy_goal_tolerance` = 0.05 m, `yaw_goal_tolerance` = 0.17 rad

### Common Exam Traps
- ☐ Recovery behaviors are **inside** `move_base`, not a separate node.
- ☐ `map_server` publishes on `/map` as a **latched** topic — it does not stream.
- ☐ DWA samples **velocities**, not trajectories; trajectories are simulated **after** velocity sampling.
- ☐ AMCL requires an **initial pose** to start; without it, particles are spread randomly across the map.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigating in Code
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT504 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Navigating in Code

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **Robot Operating System (ROS)**, *"Navigating in Code"* refers to the conceptual and practical framework used by roboticists to command a mobile robot to autonomously move from a starting pose to a target pose using the **ROS Navigation Stack** (`navigation` metapackage) and its associated action interfaces, configuration files, and node communication patterns. It is the discipline of writing, configuring, and orchestrating ROS nodes that translate high-level goals (`geometry_msgs/PoseStamped`) into low-level velocity commands (`geometry_msgs/Twist`).

The **Navigation Stack** is a collection of ROS packages that take in information from odometry, sensor streams, and a goal pose, and output safe velocity commands sent to a mobile base. It comprises the following primary metapackages and components:

- **`move_base`** — the central action server that orchestrates the entire navigation pipeline.
- **`costmap_2d`** — a multi-layered 2D occupancy grid that represents the robot's environment.
- **`navfn`** / **`global_planner`** — the global path planner (Dijkstra / A*).
- **`base_local_planner`** / **`dwa_local_planner`** / **`teb_local_planner`** — local trajectory planners.
- **`amcl`** — Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization for tracking the robot in a known map.
- **`map_server`** — stores and serves the static 2D occupancy grid map.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** "Navigating in Code" is NOT a programming language tutorial. It is the *navigation stack implementation paradigm* in ROS 1 (Noetic) / ROS 2 (Humble/Iron). Students must understand the **ActionLib interface** (`move_base_msgs/MoveBaseAction`), **TF tree** (`map → odom → base_link`), and **costmap layers**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Autonomous Taxi" Analogy

Imagine you summon an autonomous taxi via a mobile app:

| Human Taxi Analogy | ROS Navigation Equivalent |
|---|---|
| You type your destination in the app | You publish a `MoveBaseAction` goal to `/move_base` |
| The GPS plots the entire route from city A to city B | **Global Planner** computes a long-range path |
| The car's adaptive cruise control avoids jaywalkers | **Local Planner** generates short-range trajectories |
| The car inflates a "safety bubble" around obstacles | **Costmap Inflation Layer** |
| The car continuously re-localizes in the city | **AMCL** (Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization) |
| The car uses a downloaded city map | **Map Server** with `.pgm` + `.yaml` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** Just as a human driver combines a *strategic* plan (the route) with a *tactical* plan (lane changes, braking), the ROS Navigation Stack couples a **global planner** (strategic) with a **local planner** (tactical). This dual-layer architecture is the heart of "Navigating in Code."

---

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Units (KTU Board-Ready)

- **Map Resolution:** **0.05 m/pixel** (standard for indoor environments).
- **Robot Footprint:** A polygon of `(x, y)` vertices in meters, e.g., $\{(0.20, 0.20), (0.20, -0.20), (-0.20, -0.20), (-0.20, 0.20)\}$.
- **Inflation Radius:** **0.55 m** (typical), calculated as:
$$r_{inflation} = r_{robot} + r_{obstacle} + \epsilon$$
- **Update Frequency:** Global costmap @ **1 Hz**, Local costmap @ **5 Hz**.
- **Controller Frequency:** **20 Hz** (standard `cmd_vel` publish rate).
- **Max Planners:** Local planner runs at **~10 Hz** on modern CPUs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ROS Navigation Stack — Operational Breakdown

The Navigation Stack is a **loosely coupled, asynchronous, message-passing architecture**. It does not "own" the robot — it merely *publishes* velocity commands on `/cmd_vel`. The actual hardware driver subscribes to this topic.

### 2.1.1 Required Robot Capabilities (Pre-conditions)

Before the navigation stack will function, the robot MUST publish:

1. **`/tf` and `/tf_static`** — A valid transform tree.
2. **`sensor_msgs/LaserScan`** OR **`sensor_msgs/PointCloud2`** — Depth sensor data.
3. **`nav_msgs/Odometry`** — Wheel encoder odometry message.
4. **`geometry_msgs/Twist`** — Output command stream (subscribed by base controller).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** Students often try to launch `move_base` without setting up the TF tree. This is the **#1 reason navigation fails** in simulation. Ensure `map → odom → base_link → laser` is broadcast continuously.

---

### 2.1.2 The `move_base` Action Server

`move_base` implements the **SimpleActionServer** from `actionlib`. The action definition is `move_base_msgs/MoveBaseAction`, consisting of:

- **Goal:** `geometry_msgs/PoseStamped` (target in `map` frame).
- **Feedback:** Current pose of the robot.
- **Result:** `MoveBaseResult` (success/failure code).

```
Client Node  --[Goal]-->  /move_base  --[/cmd_vel]-->  Robot Base
                              |
                              v
                       [Global Planner]
                       [Local Planner]
                       [Recovery Behaviors]
```

---

## 2.2 Costmap 2D — The Robot's "World Model"

A costmap is a **2D grid** where each cell has a value in `[$0, 254, 255$]`:

| Cell Value | Semantic Meaning |
|---|---|
| **0** (FREE_SPACE) | No obstacle, free to traverse |
| **1–252** (INSCRIBED / FRONTIER) | Inflated, approaching obstacle |
| **253** (NO_INFORMATION) | Unknown space (sensor has not seen it) |
| **254** (LETHAL) | Definite collision |
| **255** (UNUSED) | Out of map bounds |

The costmap is composed of **stacked layers**, processed in order:

1. **Static Layer** — Loads the pre-built map from `map_server`.
2. **Obstacle Layer** — Tracks 2D obstacles from `LaserScan`.
3. **Inflation Layer** — Computes the exponential decay of cost around obstacles.
4. **Voxel Layer** — Tracks 3D obstacles (optional, for tall obstacles).
5. **Range Sensor Layer** — Ultrasonic / IR sensors (optional).

### Inflation Layer Cost Function

The cost of a cell at distance $d$ from the nearest obstacle is given by:

$$
C(d) = 
\begin{cases}
\text{LETHAL\_OBSTACLE} = 254 & \text{if } d < r_{inscribed} \\
\text{INSCRIBED\_INFLATED\_OBSTACLE} = 253 & \text{if } r_{inscribed} \le d < r_{robot} \\
\text{cost\_scaling\_factor} \cdot (r_{inflation} - d) & \text{if } r_{robot} \le d \le r_{inflation} \\
\text{FREE\_SPACE} = 0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

where:
- $r_{inscribed}$ is the inscribed radius of the robot footprint.
- $r_{robot}$ is the circumscribed radius.
- $r_{inflation}$ is the user-defined inflation radius.
- `cost_scaling_factor` $\approx 10$ (default).

---

## 2.3 Global Planner — The Strategic Path

The **Global Planner** (`navfn` or `global_planner`) searches the static global costmap for a minimum-cost path from start to goal.

### Algorithm: A* (used by `global_planner`)

A* uses the evaluation function:

$$
f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
$$

where:
- $g(n)$ = actual cost from start to node $n$.
- $h(n)$ = heuristic estimate from $n$ to goal (typically Euclidean distance).
- $f(n)$ = total estimated cost through $n$.

For the octile distance heuristic on a 4-connected or 8-connected grid:

$$
h(n) = \sqrt{2} \cdot \min(\Delta x, \Delta y) + \vert \Delta x - \Delta y \vert
$$

where $\Delta x = \vert n_x - g_x \vert$ and $\Delta y = \vert n_y - g_y \vert$.

---

## 2.4 Local Planner — The Tactical Trajectory

The **Local Planner** uses the rolling local costmap to compute velocity commands that follow the global path while avoiding dynamic obstacles.

### Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)

DWA samples the **reachable velocity space** within a dynamic window determined by the robot's acceleration limits. For each sampled velocity $(v, \omega)$, a trajectory is forward-simulated for $T_{sim}$ seconds. Each trajectory is scored by:

$$
G(v, \omega) = \sigma\bigl(\alpha \cdot \text{heading}(v, \omega) + \beta \cdot \text{dist}(v, \omega) + \gamma \cdot \text{velocity}(v, \omega)\bigr)
$$

where:
- $\text{heading}(v, \omega)$ = alignment with the global path.
- $\text{dist}(v, \omega)$ = minimum clearance to obstacles in the trajectory.
- $\text{velocity}(v, \omega)$ = forward progress ($v$).
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = user-tunable weights.
- $\sigma(\cdot)$ = normalization function.

The search space is constrained to:

$$
V_d = \bigl\{(v, \omega) \mid v \in [v_{min}, v_{max}],\ \omega \in [\omega_{min}, \omega_{max}],\ v_{achievable},\ \omega_{achievable}\bigr\}
$$

---

## 2.5 AMCL — Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization

AMCL estimates the robot's pose $(x, y, \theta)$ in a known map by maintaining a set of weighted particles. Each particle represents a hypothesis of the robot's pose.

### Particle Update (Per Measurement)

For each particle $i$ at time $t$:

$$
x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]}) \quad \text{(motion model sample)}
$$

$$
w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m) \quad \text{(measurement model update)}
$$

where:
- $u_t$ = odometry control input.
- $z_t$ = laser scan measurement.
- $m$ = the static map.
- $w_t^{[i]}$ = weight of particle $i$ (likelihood of measurement given pose).

The pose estimate is the weighted mean of the top-K particles (Kullback-Leibler Divergence sampling for adaptive resampling).

### Odometry Motion Model Update

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{x}_t &= \hat{x}_{t-1} + \Delta x \cos(\hat{\theta}_{t-1} + \Delta\theta_1) - \Delta y \sin(\hat{\theta}_{t-1} + \Delta\theta_1) \\
\hat{y}_t &= \hat{y}_{t-1} + \Delta x \sin(\hat{\theta}_{t-1} + \Delta\theta_1) + \Delta y \cos(\hat{\theta}_{t-1} + \Delta\theta_1) \\
\hat{\theta}_t &= \hat{\theta}_{t-1} + \Delta\theta_1 + \Delta\theta_2
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\Delta x, \Delta y$ are the relative motion deltas derived from successive odometry messages.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula & Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Standard Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A* Evaluation | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | cost units |
| 2 | Octile Heuristic | $h(n) = \sqrt{2} \cdot \min(\Delta x, \Delta y) + \mid \Delta x - \Delta y \mid$ | grid cells |
| 3 | Inflation Radius | $r_{inflation} = r_{robot} + r_{obstacle} + \epsilon$ | **meters** |
| 4 | Costmap Cell Value | $C \in [0, 254, 255]$ | integer |
| 5 | DWA Score | $G = \sigma(\alpha h + \beta d + \gamma v)$ | normalized |
| 6 | Particle Weight | $w_t^{[i]} \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$ | probability |
| 7 | Velocity Window | $v \in [v_{min}, v_{max}]$ | **m/s** |
| 8 | Angular Window | $\omega \in [\omega_{min}, \omega_{max}]$ | **rad/s** |
| 9 | Map Resolution | res = 0.05 m/pixel (typical) | m/pixel |
| 10 | Update Rate (Local) | $f_{local} = 5$ Hz | Hz |
| 11 | Update Rate (Global) | $f_{global} = 1$ Hz | Hz |
| 12 | Controller Rate | $f_{ctrl} = 20$ Hz | Hz |
| 13 | Odometry Pose Update | See Section 2.5 equations | m, rad |
| 14 | Action Interface | `move_base_msgs/MoveBaseAction` | message type |
| 15 | Required Topics | `/tf`, `/scan`, `/odom`, `/map` | ROS topics |
| 16 | Output Topic | `/cmd_vel` (`geometry_msgs/Twist`) | ROS topic |
| 17 | Frame Hierarchy | `map → odom → base_link → laser` | TF tree |
| 18 | Inflation Weight | `cost_scaling_factor` $\approx 10$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The ROS Navigation Stack is the **de-facto industry standard** for indoor mobile robots (TurtleBot, Fetch, KUKA youBot). The same code that runs in a 2024 warehouse AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robot) at Amazon is built on the principles described above. The configuration files (`costmap_common_params.yaml`, `local_costmap_params.yaml`, `global_costmap_params.yaml`, `base_local_planner_params.yaml`) are reused across thousands of deployments.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Inflation Layer Cost Decay

**Problem Setup:** Given an obstacle at cell $(x_0, y_0)$, derive the cost assigned to a cell $(x, y)$ at Euclidean distance $d$.

### Step 1: Compute the Euclidean Distance

$$
d = \sqrt{(x - x_0)^2 + (y - y_0)^2}
$$

### Step 2: Classify the Cell Based on Distance Thresholds

The robot has an inscribed radius $r_i$ (half the smaller footprint dimension) and a circumscribed radius $r_c$ (half the diagonal of the footprint). We compare $d$ against three thresholds.

- **Lethal zone:** $d < r_i$ — robot physically intersects the obstacle.
- **Inscribed zone:** $r_i \le d < r_c$ — robot can squeeze past.
- **Inflation zone:** $r_c \le d \le r_{inflation}$ — exponential decay.
- **Free zone:** $d > r_{inflation}$ — zero cost.

### Step 3: Apply the Piecewise Cost Function

For the inflation zone, the cost is **linearly decayed** in the most common implementation (`cost_scaling_factor` mode):

$$
C(x, y) = \text{cost\_scaling\_factor} \cdot (r_{inflation} - d)
$$

### Step 4: Clamp to the Integer Range

Since costmap values are unsigned 8-bit integers (0–255), the result is clamped:

$$
C_{final}(x, y) = \min\bigl(253, \max(0, C(x, y))\bigr)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative (Proxemic) Formulation:** Some implementations (e.g., the **Social Costmap Layer** for human-aware navigation) use an **exponential decay**:
> $C(d) = A \cdot e^{-(d - r_{robot}) / \sigma}$ where $A$ is the amplitude and $\sigma$ is the decay constant. This is used in service robots operating in human-populated environments.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Particle Filter Resampling in AMCL

**Problem Setup:** Given a set of $N$ particles with weights $w^{[i]}$, derive the algorithm to resample with replacement proportional to weight.

### Step 1: Normalize the Weights

$$
W_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w^{[i]}
$$

$$
\tilde{w}^{[i]} = \frac{w^{[i]}}{W_{total}}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)

$$
\text{cdf}(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{i} \tilde{w}^{[j]}
$$

### Step 3: Stochastic Universal Sampling (SUS)

Draw a single random number $u \sim \mathcal{U}(0, 1/N)$, then for $k = 1, \dots, N$:

$$
u_k = u + \frac{k - 1}{N}
$$

Select particle $i$ such that $\text{cdf}(i-1) < u_k \le \text{cdf}(i)$.

### Step 4: KLD-Sampling (Adaptive Particle Count)

To avoid wasting computation when the pose is well-localized, AMCL uses the **Kullback-Leibler Divergence** method. The number of particles is bounded by:

$$
N_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \epsilon} \chi^2_{k-1, 1-\delta}
$$

where:
- $\epsilon$ = error bound on the KL divergence (default 0.05).
- $\delta$ = failure probability (default 0.01).
- $k$ = number of histogram bins with support (i.e., distinct occupied cells).
- $\chi^2_{k-1, 1-\delta}$ = chi-square quantile.

When the robot is **well-localized**, $k$ is small, so $N_{max}$ drops to a few hundred particles, saving CPU.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** KLD-sampling is why AMCL can run on a Raspberry Pi. If the robot "knows where it is," the particle cloud collapses, and AMCL *automatically* uses fewer particles.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: DWA Trajectory Simulation

**Problem Setup:** For a sampled $(v, \omega)$, derive the forward-simulated pose at time $t + \Delta t$.

### Step 1: Discretize the Simulation Time

Discretize the prediction horizon $T_{sim}$ into $K$ steps of $\Delta t = T_{sim} / K$.

### Step 2: Update the Pose Using Unicycle Kinematics

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= x_k + v \cos(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t \\
y_{k+1} &= y_k + v \sin(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t \\
\theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k + \omega \cdot \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Apply the Dynamic Window Constraint

The sampled $(v, \omega)$ must be reachable from the current velocity $(v_c, \omega_c)$ within one control cycle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &\in [v_c - \dot{v}_{max} \cdot \Delta t,\ v_c + \dot{v}_{max} \cdot \Delta t] \cap [v_{min}, v_{max}] \\
\omega &\in [\omega_c - \dot{\omega}_{max} \cdot \Delta t,\ \omega_c + \dot{\omega}_{max} \cdot \Delta t] \cap [\omega_{min}, \omega_{max}]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Score Each Trajectory and Select the Best

For each admissible $(v, \omega)$:
1. Forward-simulate the trajectory.
2. Compute clearance, heading, and velocity scores.
3. Combine using $G(v, \omega) = \sigma(\alpha h + \beta d + \gamma v)$.
4. Pick the maximum-scoring velocity.
5. Publish it on `/cmd_vel`.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Local Planners

| Feature | DWA | TEB | MPC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Algorithm** | Dynamic Window | Timed Elastic Band | Model Predictive Control |
| **Computational Cost** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Optimality** | Greedy | Locally optimal | Globally optimal (within horizon) |
| **Constraint Handling** | Simple bounds | Time + Kinematic | Full dynamics |
| **Default in ROS** | Yes (`dwa_local_planner`) | No (separate package) | No (research) |
| **CPU @ 20 Hz** | < 5% | ~ 15% | ~ 40% |
| **Used In** | TurtleBot 3, Fetch | Service Robots | Autonomous Cars |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Full ROS Navigation Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sensor["Sensor Sources"] --> scanTopic["/scan LaserScan"]
    sensor --> odomTopic["/odom Odometry"]
    sensor --> tfTree["/tf Transform Tree"]

    mapServer["map_server"] --> mapTopic["/map OccupancyGrid"]

    scanTopic --> amcl["amcl Localization"]
    odomTopic --> amcl
    mapTopic --> amcl
    tfTree --> amcl
    amcl --> poseTopic["/amcl_pose"]

    scanTopic --> costmap["costmap_2d"]
    odomTopic --> costmap
    mapTopic --> costmap
    costmap --> localMap["Local Costmap"]
    costmap --> globalMap["Global Costmap"]

    goalClient["User Client"] --> moveGoal["/move_base Goal"]
    moveGoal --> moveBase["move_base Action Server"]
    localMap --> moveBase
    globalMap --> moveBase
    poseTopic --> moveBase
    tfTree --> moveBase

    moveBase --> globalPlanner["navfn / global_planner"]
    moveBase --> localPlanner["dwa_local_planner / TEB"]
    moveBase --> recovery["Recovery Behaviors"]

    globalPlanner --> globalPath["/global_plan Path"]
    localPlanner --> cmdVel["/cmd_vel Twist"]
    globalPath --> localPlanner
    recovery --> cmdVel

    cmdVel --> baseDriver["Robot Base Driver"]
    baseDriver --> motion["Wheel Motion"]
    motion --> odomTopic
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `sensor`, `amcl`, `moveBase`). Labels use clean uppercase text without formatting tags to comply with rendering safeguards.

---

## 4.2 State Machine: `move_base` Internal Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Planning
    Planning --> Controlling: Global plan found
    Planning --> Clearing: Global plan failed
    Clearing --> Rotating: Obstacles cleared
    Clearing --> Recovery: Stuck
    Rotating --> Planning: Rotation complete
    Controlling --> Planning: Re-plan needed
    Controlling --> GoalReached: Within tolerance
    Recovery --> Planning: Recovery succeeded
    Recovery --> Aborted: Recovery failed
    GoalReached --> [*]
    Aborted --> [*]
```

---

## 4.3 TF Tree Hierarchy (Mandatory)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    map["map (world-fixed)"] --> odom["odom (continuous)"]
    odom --> baseLink["base_link (robot root)"]
    baseLink --> laser["base_laser / laser_frame"]
    baseLink --> wheelL["wheel_left_link"]
    baseLink --> wheelR["wheel_right_link"]
    baseLink --> imu["imu_link"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The TF tree MUST be unbroken. If any link in `map → odom → base_link → laser` is missing, the navigation stack will throw the famous error: `"Could not find a connection between map and base_laser."`

---

## 4.4 Costmap Layer Stack (Composition Order)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0["Layer 0: Static Map (from map_server)"] --> L1["Layer 1: Obstacle Layer (LaserScan)"]
    L1 --> L2["Layer 2: Voxel Layer (3D)"]
    L2 --> L3["Layer 3: Inflation Layer (exponential decay)"]
    L3 --> finalCostmap["Master 2D Costmap"]
```
